title: ChWDTA: Channel-wise Wavelet-Domain Transformer Attention and Entropy Modeling for Learned Image Compression
summary: State-of-the-art learned image compression (LIC) schemes are increasingly based on hybrid CNN-transformer architectures. To further improve rate-distortion performance, we introduce channel-wise wavelet transforms into both the transformer and entropy-coding components. First, we propose a channel-wise wavelet-domain transformer attention (ChWDTA) mechanism. ChWDTA keeps the efficient windowed spatial self-attention used in modern LIC backbones, but computes the Q/K/V projections on channel-wise wavelet-transformed features before mapping the attention output back with the inverse transform. The resulting Channel-wise Wavelet-Domain Transformer Block (ChWDTB) therefore preserves the spatial tokenization pattern of windowed attention while sparsifying the channel covariance seen by the attention projections. Second, in the entropy-coding stage, we introduce a channel-wise wavelet packet (ChWP) decomposition that produces four equal-sized subbands, which better fit channel-wise slice-based autoregressive entropy modeling. When each channel-wise subband is divided into two slices, we use eight slices for entropy coding. With this configuration, the proposed scheme obtains BD-rate reductions of -17.82%, -19.15%, and -22.56% on the Kodak, CLIC Professional Validation, and Tecnick test sets, respectively. Even when each channel-wise subband is coded as a single slice, the scheme still retains most of the coding gains with lower complexity. The results confirm the advantage of introducing wavelet transform in CNN-transformer-based LIC schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances learned image compression (LIC) by introducing channel-wise wavelet transforms into both the transformer attention mechanism and entropy coding, achieving significant BD-rate reductions (-17.82% to -22.56%) on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing hybrid CNN-transformer LIC schemes still have room for rate-distortion improvement, motivating the integration of channel-wise wavelet transforms to sparsify channel covariance and better structure entropy coding.
AI.method: Proposes (1) Channel-wise Wavelet-domain Transformer Attention (ChWDTA) that applies wavelet transform on channel dimension before Q/K/V projections in windowed self-attention, and (2) Channel-wise Wavelet Packet (ChWP) decomposition for entropy coding that produces four equal-sized subbands for improved slice-based autoregressive modeling.
AI.result: BD-rate reductions of -17.82% (Kodak), -19.15% (CLIC Professional Validation), and -22.56% (Tecnick). Using 8 slices (2 per subband) for entropy coding yields best results; single-slice per subband retains most gains with lower complexity.
AI.conclusion: Channel-wise wavelet transforms effectively boost CNN-transformer LIC performance, confirming their advantage in both attention computation and entropy modeling.

title: VLAMotor: Test-Guided Enhancement of Vision-Language-Action Models via Agent-BasedData Synthesis
summary:
Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models follow a data-driven paradigm and are constrained by the coverage of training data, making them prone to failure on edge-case configurations after deployment. To mitigate such risks, it is essential to expose high-quality failure modes and convert the resulting failures into supervisory data for model enhancement. Existing studies largely stop at failure detection and lack a mechanism for leveraging discovered failures for model repair.
  We propose VLAMotor, the first analysis framework for VLA enhancement, which integrates distance-aware model testing for failure exposure and agent-based data synthesis for model finetunning. First, VLAMotor estimates input uncertainty based on the distance to training samples, and combines uncertainty ranking with redundancy elimination to build compact test sets that expose diverse failures. Then, VLAMotor abstracts failure trajectories into structured semantic representations, and plans parameterized repair-skill sequences, which are then realized as executable trajectories through inverse kinematics and motion execution. The resulting successful trajectories are automatically labeled and used to fine-tune the original VLA model, yielding an enhanced VLA model. Evaluation on four representative robotic manipulation tasks shows that 92.33% of the in-simulation test cases generated by VLAMotor trigger VLA failures, and VLAMotor improves test coverage over the state-of-the-art tool by 18.93%. By fine-tuning VLA models with synthetic data derived from failed test cases, VLAMotor further enhances the overall success rate of VLA models by 49.25%. When deployed on real hardware, the simulation-enhanced models improve the success rate over the original VLA models by 57.50%, demonstrating an effective and low-cost direction for VLA enhancement.

AI.tldr: VLAMotor is the first framework for enhancing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by exposing failures through distance-aware testing and repairing them via agent-based synthetic data generation, achieving 49.25% improvement in simulation and 57.50% on real hardware.
AI.motivation: VLA models are limited by training data coverage, causing failures on edge-case configurations after deployment. Existing work stops at failure detection and lacks mechanisms for leveraging failures to repair the model.
AI.method: VLAMotor has two stages: (1) Distance-aware model testing: estimates input uncertainty via distance to training samples, ranks by uncertainty, eliminates redundancy to build compact test sets exposing diverse failures. (2) Agent-based data synthesis: abstracts failure trajectories into semantic representations, plans repair-skill sequences, executes via inverse kinematics, and uses successful trajectories to fine-tune the original VLA model.
AI.result: 92.33% of test cases trigger VLA failures; 18.93% improvement in test coverage over SOTA; fine-tuning with synthetic data improves overall success rate by 49.25% in simulation and 57.50% on real hardware.
AI.conclusion: VLAMotor provides an effective and low-cost direction for VLA enhancement by converting discovered failures into training data, bridging the gap between failure detection and model repair.

title: DefocusTrackerAI -- A Generalized Framework for the Automatic Detection of Defocused Particle Images
summary: The present work introduces DefocusTrackerAI, a generalized deep-learning framework for the automatic detection and position estimation of defocused particle images from any kind of optical configuration without compromising uncertainty and recall, intended as a follow-up of the open-source project DefocusTracker. We selected the deep neural network architecture from the direct comparison of two well-known object detection models, Faster R-CNN and YOLOv9, trained on a diverse and feature-rich synthetic image set containing astigmatic and non-astigmatic defocused particle images of varying diameters. The model evaluation on synthetic data showed that, first, YOLOv9 outperforms Faster R-CNN, achieving higher recall and lower uncertainty, particularly at high particle image densities; and second, that YOLOv9 provides enhanced spatial resolution, with uncertainty values between 0.1 and 0.4 pixels for particle image densities N_s up to 0.5, outperforming state-of-the-art algorithms. We demonstrated that our models are able to detect astigmatic and non-astigmatic defocused particle images in multiple optical setups with varying lighting conditions. In addition, we successfully applied our models on real DPT experiments, including fluorescence and shadowgraph data, showing that they can be used beyond conventional DPT applications, including the tracking of sprays and droplets. A pre-trained, ready-to-use version of DefocusTrackerAI based on YOLOv9 is available at https://gitlab.com/goncalo.coutinho/defocustrackerAI-main/-/tree/7e0f11f649ebad50e20dca5b9545f26ca303ebe0 and can be used for automatic detection of defocused particle images of any kind with high accuracy. In combination with a suitable calibration approach for the depth position, it can be used as an effective first step for three-dimensional defocusing particle tracking.

AI.tldr: DefocusTrackerAI is a deep-learning framework for detecting and estimating positions of defocused particle images across various optical setups, using YOLOv9 which outperformed Faster R-CNN in recall and uncertainty, achieving 0.1-0.4 pixel accuracy. It works for astigmatic/non-astigmatic images, different lighting conditions, and real DPT experiments (fluorescence, shadowgraph, sprays/droplets). Pre-trained model available on GitLab.
AI.motivation: To develop a generalized deep-learning framework for automatic detection and position estimation of defocused particle images from any optical configuration without compromising uncertainty and recall, serving as a follow-up to the open-source DefocusTracker project.
AI.method: Selected optimal deep neural network architecture by comparing Faster R-CNN and YOLOv9 models trained on diverse synthetic image sets containing astigmatic and non-astigmatic defocused particle images with varying diameters. Applied the best model to real DPT experiments including fluorescence and shadowgraph data.
AI.result: YOLOv9 outperformed Faster R-CNN with higher recall and lower uncertainty at high particle image densities. Achieved spatial resolution uncertainty between 0.1-0.4 pixels for densities up to N_s=0.5, outperforming state-of-the-art algorithms. Successfully detected particles across multiple optical setups, lighting conditions, and real experiments including sprays and droplets.
AI.conclusion: DefocusTrackerAI provides an effective, accurate, and generalized solution for 3D defocusing particle tracking across diverse optical configurations and applications, with a pre-trained YOLOv9 model publicly available.

title: CLSP-REQA: A Real-Time Quality-Aware Closed-Loop Seizure Prediction Framework with Mamba-BiLSTM and Confidence-Gated Intervention
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, submitted to Biomedical Signal Processing and Control
summary: Reliable seizure prediction is a prerequisite for closed-loop neurostimulation therapy, yet existing methods rarely account for the variability in EEG signal quality encountered in real-world deployment, and the overwhelming majority adopt non-strict evaluation protocols that overestimate generalisation performance. We propose CLSP-REQA (Closed-Loop Seizure Prediction with Real-time EEG Quality Assessment), a unified framework that embeds a lightweight signal quality estimator directly within the prediction pipeline. A Real-time EEG Quality Assessment (REQA) module runs in parallel with a Mamba-BiLSTM backbone, producing a scalar quality score q in [0,1] that modulates output confidence through a tiered non-linear fusion function (ECLO). Under strict cross-patient evaluation on the CHB-MIT Scalp EEG Database (n = 23 subjects, 198 seizures), CLSP-REQA achieves an AUC-ROC of 0.7426 +- 0.0199, outperforming the unadapted cross-patient baseline of 0.69 reported by Jemal et al., using only 16 EEG channels compared to 23 in prior work, and without requiring any target-patient data or domain adaptation. On the SIENA Scalp EEG Database (n = 14 subjects, 47 seizures), CLSP-REQA achieves AUC 0.7012 +- 0.0249, substantially surpassing the best domain-adapted cross-patient result of 0.61 on the same dataset, demonstrating strong cross-dataset generalisation. The framework outputs a structured four-tuple (p, q, c, Phi_SHAP) directly compatible with closed-loop neurostimulator interfaces.

AI.tldr: CLSP-REQA is a unified seizure prediction framework that integrates a real-time EEG quality assessment module with a Mamba-BiLSTM backbone. It achieves state-of-the-art cross-patient and cross-dataset performance under strict evaluation, without requiring target-patient data or domain adaptation.
AI.motivation: Existing seizure prediction methods neglect EEG signal quality variability in real-world deployment and use non-strict evaluation protocols that overestimate generalization, hindering reliable closed-loop neurostimulation.
AI.method: CLSP-REQA combines a lightweight Real-time EEG Quality Assessment (REQA) module (producing quality score q in [0,1]) with a Mamba-BiLSTM backbone, and uses a tiered non-linear fusion function (ECLO) to modulate output confidence.
AI.result: On CHB-MIT, AUC-ROC 0.7426±0.0199 (beats 0.69 baseline) using only 16 EEG channels without target-patient data. On SIENA, AUC 0.7012±0.0249 (beats 0.61 best domain-adapted result).
AI.conclusion: CLSP-REQA demonstrates strong cross-patient and cross-dataset generalization for seizure prediction, outputs a structured four-tuple (p,q,c,Phi_SHAP) compatible with closed-loop neurostimulators, and eliminates the need for domain adaptation or target-patient data.

title: BitsMoE: Efficient Spectral Energy-Guided Bit Allocation for MoE LLM Quantization
comment: 29 pages, 6 figures, 9 tables. Code and models are available at https://github.com/zjiayu064/BitsMoE
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models reduce per-token computation through sparse expert activation, but their deployment remains memory-intensive because all expert weights must be kept resident in memory. Existing MoE compression methods struggle in the ultra-low-bit regime: pruning irreversibly removes model capacity, while coarse-grained quantization fails to allocate bits according to heterogeneous expert and weight-direction importance. We propose BitsMoE, a spectral-energy-guided bit-allocation framework for MoE LLM quantization. BitsMoE decomposes each MoE layer by SVD into a shared basis and expert-specific spectral factors, retaining the shared basis without quantization to preserve common cross-expert structure and using the expert-specific factors as fine-grained quantization units. To determine the bit-width of each unit, BitsMoE formulates spectrum-wise mixed-precision quantization as an activation-aware reconstruction surrogate and solves an integer linear program that minimizes estimated reconstruction loss under a fixed bit budget. Experiments across multiple MoE LLMs show that BitsMoE substantially reduces downstream task accuracy degradation in ultra-low-bit regimes. Under 2-bit quantization on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base, BitsMoE accelerates quantization by 12.3$\times$, improves average accuracy by 27.83 percentage points, and increases decoding speed by 1.76$\times$ over GPTQ. Our model and code are publicly available at https://github.com/zjiayu064/BitsMoE.

AI.tldr: BitsMoE is a spectral-energy-guided bit-allocation framework that compresses MoE LLMs via SVD decomposition and mixed-precision quantization, achieving significant accuracy and speed improvements in ultra-low-bit regimes.
AI.motivation: MoE LLMs reduce computation via sparse expert activation but remain memory-intensive because all expert weights must reside in memory. Existing compression methods (pruning and coarse-grained quantization) perform poorly in ultra-low-bit regimes due to irreversible capacity loss and inability to allocate bits according to heterogeneous expert/weight-direction importance.
AI.method: BitsMoE decomposes each MoE layer via SVD into a shared basis (kept unquantized) and expert-specific spectral factors (fine-grained quantization units). It formulates spectrum-wise mixed-precision quantization as an activation-aware reconstruction surrogate and solves an integer linear program to minimize estimated reconstruction loss under a fixed bit budget.
AI.result: Under 2-bit quantization on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base, BitsMoE accelerates quantization by 12.3×, improves average accuracy by 27.83 percentage points, and increases decoding speed by 1.76× over GPTQ.
AI.conclusion: BitsMoE substantially reduces downstream task accuracy degradation in ultra-low-bit regimes for MoE LLMs, offering a practical and effective compression solution.

title: Position Paper: Post-Solve Robustness in Decision Engines: Feasible Regions and Smoothness Under Perturbations
summary: Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) decision engines routinely output nominally optimal plans for high-stakes industrial systems. Yet deployment rarely matches solve-time assumptions: small perturbations in costs, demands, or resource availability can invalidate feasibility or trigger discontinuous shifts to qualitatively different solutions. We argue that this post-solve robustness gap is a missing layer in today's optimization pipelines and a missing evaluation dimension for learning-enabled decision systems. Rather than replacing robust optimization or stochastic programming, the proposed layer audits a solved incumbent and returns solver-backed evidence about how far that solution can be trusted. We formalize two central objects: (i) an $ε$-near-optimal feasible neighborhood in parameter space, capturing when an incumbent remains feasible and near-optimal under perturbations, and (ii) solution smoothness in decision space, capturing whether nearby alternatives with small combinatorial edits remain competitive. We then synthesize the most relevant partial answers from sensitivity and stability analysis, robust optimization, neighborhood search, adversarial testing, and learning-based enhancements, and articulate an agenda for a unified post-solve robustness layer. Concretely, we call for certified inner approximations around the incumbent, probabilistic robustness estimation with calibrated uncertainty, adversarial robustness margins, and learning-based prediction and explanation aligned with solver-backed verification. We conclude with a compact reporting template and evaluation protocol that would make robustness a first-class output of decision engines.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a "post-solve robustness gap" in MILP-based decision engines, where solutions nominally optimal under solve-time assumptions break under real-world perturbations. It proposes a unified audit layer that provides solver-backed evidence about solution trustworthiness, formalized through ε-near-optimal feasible neighborhoods and solution smoothness, and synthesizes existing approaches into an actionable agenda for making robustness a first-class output.
AI.motivation: MILP solutions are deployed in high-stakes systems but often fail under small perturbations (costs, demands, resources) that were not modeled at solve time. This gap between solve-time optimality and post-solve robustness is underexamined in both optimization pipelines and evaluation of learning-enabled systems.
AI.method: The paper formalizes two key objects: (i) ε-near-optimal feasible neighborhood in parameter space, and (ii) solution smoothness in decision space. It then synthesizes partial answers from sensitivity analysis, stability analysis, robust optimization, neighborhood search, adversarial testing, and learning-based enhancements.
AI.result: A unified agenda for a post-solve robustness layer, including: certified inner approximations, probabilistic robustness estimation with calibrated uncertainty, adversarial robustness margins, and learning-based prediction with solver-backed verification. Also provides a reporting template and evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: Robustness should become a first-class output of decision engines, not an afterthought. The proposed layer complements (rather than replaces) robust optimization and stochastic programming by auditing a solved incumbent.

title: Beam-focusing Analysis for Modular XL-arrays: Effect of Time Synchronization Errors
comment: 5 pages, 7 figures, accepted by IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
summary: For near-field communications, it is a hardware-efficient means to form an extremely large-scale array (XL-array) by concatenating multiple modular arrays (also referred to as subarrays). In this letter, we aim to investigate the effect of time synchronization errors among transmissions of different subarrays on the beam-focusing performance. To this end, we first characterize the beam pattern function when the transmit beamforming is designed based on maximum ratio transmission (MRT) under the premise of perfect time synchronization. As this function is highly difficult for analysis, we then consider a typical case with two subarrays. Interestingly, we show that for this case, the beam-focusing effect still persists even in the presence of time synchronization errors, while the focused location is deviated from the user location with an angle offset upper-bounded by 1/M, where M denotes the number of antennas in each subarray. Subsequently, for the general case with multiple subarrays, despite analytical intractability, we numerically show that time synchronization errors give rise to an imbricated (instead of focused) beam pattern. This may significantly degrade multi-user communication performance in practice due to the reduced desired signal power and increased inter-user interference.

AI.tldr: This letter investigates how time synchronization errors among subarrays in XL-array near-field communications affect beam-focusing performance. It shows that with two subarrays, beam-focusing persists but with a deviation offset bounded by 1/M; with multiple subarrays, errors cause imbricated beam patterns, degrading multi-user performance.
AI.motivation: To investigate the effect of time synchronization errors among transmissions of different subarrays on beam-focusing performance in near-field XL-array communications, which is important for practical deployment of modular arrays.
AI.method: First characterizes beam pattern function under MRT with perfect synchronization. Then analytically studies a two-subarray case, deriving bounds on beam deviation. For the general multi-subarray case, numerical simulations are used due to analytical intractability.
AI.result: For two subarrays, beam-focusing persists but focused location deviates with angle offset upper-bounded by 1/M. For multiple subarrays, time synchronization errors cause imbricated (non-focused) beam patterns.
AI.conclusion: Time synchronization errors can significantly degrade multi-user communication performance by reducing desired signal power and increasing inter-user interference, especially in the general multi-subarray case.

title: Mathematical framework for perception-driven parameter choice in image denoising
summary: We approach image denoising from a perception-driven perspective: how can we select the parameters that are best suited for human visual perception? We combine research methods in mathematics and psychology to develop a mathematical framework for measuring perceived similarity. We construct a sample set of differently denoised photographs by using the same base image as input data and by tuning the parameter value in a total variation denoising algorithm. A comparison test is conducted with human participants to survey perceived differences between the images. Analyzing the results with psychometric scaling provides us with a HaarPSI value to use as a threshold in discretizing parameter grids. As a result, we obtain psychometrically scaled, openly available image sets that are ready to use in further experiments in perception-driven imaging, as well as a framework for ensuing experiments involving comparison tests.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a perception-driven framework for image denoising by combining mathematics and psychology. Using psychometric scaling from human comparison tests on total variation denoised images, they derive a HaarPSI threshold to discretize parameter grids, producing openly available, psychometrically scaled image sets.
AI.motivation: To select denoising parameters best suited for human visual perception rather than relying solely on mathematical error metrics.
AI.method: Combined mathematics (total variation denoising with parameter tuning) and psychology (human comparison tests analyzed via psychometric scaling) to derive a HaarPSI threshold for discretizing parameter grids.
AI.result: A psychometrically scaled, openly available image set and a framework for perception-driven imaging experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively bridges perceptual psychology and mathematical optimization for denoising, enabling parameter selection aligned with human visual preferences.

title: From Human Videos to Robot Manipulation: A Survey on Scalable Vision-Language-Action Learning with Human-Centric Data
comment: Accepted to IJCAI 2026 Survey Track. Project page: https://aaronfengzy.github.io/HumanCentricToVLA-Survey/
summary: Recent progress in generalizable embodied control has been driven by large-scale pretraining of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. However, most existing approaches rely on large collections of robot demonstrations, which are costly to obtain and tightly coupled to specific embodiments. Human videos, by contrast, are abundant and capture rich interactions, providing diverse semantic and physical cues for real-world manipulation. Yet, embodiment differences and the frequent absence of task-aligned annotations make their direct use in VLA models challenging. This survey provides a unified view of how human videos are transformed into effective knowledge for VLA models. We categorize existing approaches into four classes based on the action-related information they derive: (i) latent action representations that encode inter-frame changes; (ii) predictive world models that forecast future frames; (iii) explicit 2D supervision that extracts image-plane cues; and (iv) explicit 3D reconstruction that recovers geometry or motion. Beyond this taxonomy, we highlight three key open challenges in this area: structuring unstructured videos into training-ready episodes, grounding video-derived supervision into robot-executable actions under embodiment and viewpoint heterogeneity, and designing evaluation protocols that better predict real-world deployment performance and transfer efficiency, thereby informing future research directions. A curated list of papers and resources is available at https://github.com/AaronFengZY/HumanCentricToVLA-Survey.

AI.tldr: This survey categorizes methods for leveraging abundant human videos to train Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, identifying four approaches (latent actions, predictive world models, explicit 2D supervision, explicit 3D reconstruction) and three open challenges (structuring unstructured videos, grounding video supervision to robot actions, and designing better evaluation protocols).
AI.motivation: Human videos are abundant and rich in semantic/physical cues, but embodiment differences and lack of task annotations hinder their direct use in VLA models. The survey aims to unify how human videos can be transformed into effective knowledge for embodied control.
AI.method: The authors provide a taxonomy categorizing approaches into four classes: (i) latent action representations encoding inter-frame changes, (ii) predictive world models forecasting future frames, (iii) explicit 2D supervision extracting image-plane cues, and (iv) explicit 3D reconstruction recovering geometry or motion.
AI.result: Identified four categories of methods for human-to-robot video transfer and highlighted three open challenges: structuring unstructured videos into training-ready episodes, grounding video-derived supervision into robot-executable actions under embodiment/viewpoint heterogeneity, and designing evaluation protocols for real-world performance.
AI.conclusion: This taxonomy and challenge analysis informs future research directions for leveraging human video data to improve generalizable embodied control in VLA models, with a curated resource list provided.

title: Improved Belief-Attention in Vision Task
summary: Recently, Belief-Attention \cite{Guoqiang25BeliefAttention} has been proposed by first performing an orthogonal projection of the softmax-based weighted summation of $V$ vectors with respect to the original $V$ vectors and then taking the perpendicular component as the residual signal in Transformer for performance improvement. In this paper, we first conduct an ablation study showing the projected component also carries information about the token correlation, which should not be ignored. We then propose to extend Belief-Attention by making use of both the perpendicular and projected components. In particular, the projected component goes through certain activation function and then a linear mapping before merging with the considered token. Conceptually speaking, the neural block for the projected component can be viewed as a two-layer feedforward network (FFN) within the new attention block. It is also noted that standard attention captures the token correlation via the inner-product matrix $QK^T$. We propose to introduce an additional inner-product matrix $ZZ^T$ to $QK^T$ to capture richer token correlation. We refer to the new module as Belief2-Attention. It can be easily shown that Belief2-Attention is more expressive than standard Attention. We then verify the effectiveness of Belief2-Attention for vision tasks of image classification and segmentation.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Belief-Attention by incorporating both perpendicular and projected components, and introduces an additional inner-product matrix ZZ^T to capture richer token correlations, resulting in Belief2-Attention which is more expressive and validated on vision tasks.
AI.motivation: Previous Belief-Attention discarded the projected component during orthogonal projection; this paper shows that component also carries useful token correlation information.
AI.method: (1) Uses both perpendicular and projected components from Belief-Attention; (2) Processes projected component through activation + linear mapping (acts as 2-layer FFN); (3) Adds an extra inner-product matrix ZZ^T to QK^T for richer token correlation.
AI.result: Belief2-Attention is theoretically more expressive than standard attention; shows effectiveness on image classification and segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Belief2-Attention improves upon Belief-Attention by leveraging previously ignored information and enhancing token correlation modeling.

title: Towards Networked One Search Agent Systems: Multilateration of WiFi Fine Time Measurement Responders Using GNSS References
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, 1st Workshop on Robot Meets GNSS and Ranging for Seamless Autonomy, June 5, 2026 at IEEE ICRA in Vienna
summary: This paper presents a proof-of-concept system for localising ground-based WiFi access points, acting as IEEE~802.11mc Fine Time Measurement (FTM) responders, from an uncrewed aerial vehicle using FTM ranging and Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-referenced moving-baseline multilateration. Each associated GNSS-referenced FTM-initiator pose supplies a known reference point, turning the flight trajectory into a temporal multilateration problem. The real-time smartphone pipeline performs GNSS--ranging time association, robust outlier gating, a two-stage Gauss-Newton bootstrap, and sequential Bayesian filtering with bias tracking. Six measurement-noise configurations, including empirical and adaptive models, are evaluated on field data collected in unstructured, mountainous terrain. For a line-of-sight access point with \num{455} ranging measurements, the online Android pipeline achieves a final horizontal error of \SI{4.4}{\metre}, while offline replay of the same flight yields a time-weighted mean horizontal error of \SI{4.7}{\metre} and a best-case final horizontal error of \SI{1.1}{\metre} under the best noise model after a close flyby. For non-line-of-sight targets, the real-time pipeline does not converge because of limited measurement availability, weak geometry, and signal attenuation, although an offline robust least-squares solver recovers a coarse solution for the vegetation-only case. The system is intended as a building block for Networked One Search Agent architectures, and preliminary middleware tests demonstrate software-level interoperability, while quantitative multi-agent accuracy is left for future work.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a proof-of-concept system for localizing ground-based WiFi access points from a drone using IEEE 802.11mc Fine Time Measurement (FTM) ranging and GNSS-referenced moving-baseline multilateration. The real-time smartphone pipeline achieves a horizontal error of 4.4m for line-of-sight targets; offline replay improves this to 1.1m under the best noise model. Non-line-of-sight targets remain challenging.
AI.motivation: To enable localization of ground-based WiFi access points (FTM responders) from an uncrewed aerial vehicle using precise FTM ranging and GNSS positioning, as a building block for multi-agent search architectures.
AI.method: The system uses a smartphone-based real-time pipeline that performs GNSS-ranging time association, robust outlier gating, a two-stage Gauss-Newton bootstrap, and sequential Bayesian filtering with bias tracking. Six measurement-noise configurations (empirical and adaptive) are evaluated on field data collected in mountainous terrain.
AI.result: For a line-of-sight access point (455 measurements), the online Android pipeline achieves a final horizontal error of 4.4m; offline replay yields a time-weighted mean error of 4.7m and best-case final error of 1.1m under the best noise model. Non-line-of-sight targets fail to converge in real-time but offline robust least-squares can recover a coarse solution for vegetation-only cases.
AI.conclusion: The proof-of-concept demonstrates feasibility for line-of-sight WiFi AP localization from UAVs using FTM ranging, but non-line-of-sight scenarios remain problematic. The system is intended for Networked One Search Agent architectures; preliminary middleware tests show software-level interoperability.

title: DAStatFormer: A Hybrid Multibranch Transformer with Statistical Feature Integration for DAS-Based Pattern Recognitions
summary: Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) enables large-scale monitoring through optical fibers, but its high dimensionality and complex spatio-temporal patterns make event classification demanding. Existing deep learning approaches-CNNs, recurrent models, and Transformer variants-either fail to capture long-range dependencies or require processing raw DAS matrices at prohibitive cost. We propose DAStatFormer, a hybrid multibranch Transformer that combines compact multidomain statistical features with Gated Transformer Networks. Instead of raw signals, we extract 24 ANOVA-selected attributes per channel from the temporal, waveform, and spectral domains, reducing data size by orders of magnitude while preserving discriminative information. Each domain is processed via dedicated step-wise and channel-wise attention branches, fused by an adaptive gating mechanism. Experiments on the open $Φ$-OTDR benchmark and a real-scenario DAS dataset show that DAS-tatFormer achieves up to 99.4% accuracy and near-perfect real-world performance, while using significantly fewer parameters and lower inference cost than models such as DASFormer and DeepViT. These results demonstrate its suitability for scalable, real-time DAS-based monitoring. We release our code at https://github.com/MichelD-git/DAStatFormer

AI.tldr: DAStatFormer is a hybrid Transformer that uses compact statistical features (24 ANOVA-selected attributes) instead of raw DAS data, achieving up to 99.4% accuracy with significantly lower computational cost than existing models.
AI.motivation: DAS data is high-dimensional with complex spatio-temporal patterns; existing deep learning methods either fail to capture long-range dependencies or process raw data at prohibitive computational cost.
AI.method: Extracts 24 ANOVA-selected statistical attributes per channel from temporal, waveform, and spectral domains. Processes each domain via dedicated step-wise and channel-wise attention branches, fused by an adaptive gating mechanism in a hybrid multibranch Transformer architecture.
AI.result: Achieves up to 99.4% accuracy on Φ-OTDR benchmark and near-perfect real-world performance, with significantly fewer parameters and lower inference cost than DASFormer and DeepViT.
AI.conclusion: DAStatFormer is suitable for scalable, real-time DAS-based monitoring by dramatically reducing data dimensionality while preserving discriminative information and improving efficiency.

title: Emergent Collaborative Deliberation in Multi-Model AI Systems: A BFT-Derived Protocol for Epistemic Synthesis
summary: We present the Consilium Protocol, a Byzantine Fault Tolerance-derived architecture for structured multi-model AI deliberation that treats inter-model disagreement as epistemic signal rather than error. The protocol assigns engineered cognitive personas to language models -- separating what a model is from how it reasons -- and introduces an In-Sample/Out-of-Sample validation framework adapted from quantitative finance to distinguish training-data consensus from empirically grounded conclusions. Across 1,478 deliberation sessions spanning 32 topics in 10 domain categories, we demonstrate that (1) the cognitive persona, not the underlying model, determines epistemic behavior: free edge-inference models costing 0.0002 USD per batch produced comparable analytical output to frontier models costing 10.69 USD; (2) RLHF alignment training creates measurable, domain-specific epistemic blind spots -- contested policy topics exhibit 12.3 percentage points less adversarial challenge than settled science topics, and AI safety topics show asymmetric bias ($Δ$=11.6%) where models challenge claims that AI is dangerous far more vigorously than claims that AI risk is overstated; (3) the protocol exhibits no directional bias of its own (immigration $Δ$=2.3%, renewables $Δ$=1.2%); and (4) out-of-sample evidence retrieval validated 239 claims with 100% evidence retrieval and surfaced 167 blind-spot discoveries invisible to training-data deliberation. Run-to-run reproducibility across randomized model$\times$persona assignments averages $\pm$2.2% standard deviation. Total cost for the complete battery including all overhead: 217 USD. We release the protocol specification under MIT license to enable independent verification.

AI.tldr: The Consilium Protocol uses BFT-inspired multi-model AI deliberation with assigned cognitive personas and an In-Sample/Out-of-Sample validation framework to distinguish training-data consensus from empirical truth. Results show persona (not model) drives epistemic behavior, RLHF creates domain-specific blind spots, the protocol is unbiased, and out-of-sample retrieval validates claims with 100% evidence discovery — all for $217 total cost.
AI.motivation: To address the problem that inter-model disagreement in multi-AI systems is typically treated as error, whereas the authors propose it should be treated as epistemic signal for more robust AI deliberation.
AI.method: Byzantine Fault Tolerance-derived architecture with engineered cognitive personas (separating model identity from reasoning style) and an In-Sample/Out-of-Sample validation framework adapted from quantitative finance to distinguish training-data-based consensus from empirically grounded conclusions.
AI.result: (1) Cognitive persona, not the underlying model, determines epistemic behavior — cheap models ($0.0002/batch) comparable to frontier models ($10.69/batch). (2) RLHF creates measurable, domain-specific epistemic blind spots (12.3pp less challenge on contested policy vs. settled science; AI safety shows 11.6% asymmetric bias). (3) Protocol itself shows no directional bias. (4) Out-of-sample retrieval validated 239 claims at 100% rate, surfacing 167 blind-spot discoveries. Reproducibility: ±2.2% SD.
AI.conclusion: The Consilium Protocol enables cost-effective, structured multi-model deliberation that surfaces epistemic blind spots from alignment training while maintaining neutrality. Released under MIT license for independent verification.

title: Bounding Global and Local Compression Error of Signal Parameterizations
summary: Differentiable signal parameterizations such as implicit neural representations (INRs) and hybrid models are increasingly central to computational imaging, yet principled tools for evaluating reconstruction fidelity at finite model size remain limited when ground truth is unavailable. We introduce a framework for predicting the reconstruction error of compressive signal parameterizations, yielding non-asymptotic, signal-specific bounds that are both theoretically sound and efficiently computable without access to the ground truth signal. Specifically, we prove that when parameterization-based compression satisfies certain natural properties, the compression error at any compression level is bounded by a simple scaled difference between model predictions at different compression levels. We verify these properties for representative model families including interpolated grids, Fourier feature networks, multi-resolution hash encodings, and tensor factorizations, and show empirically that the resulting worst-case guarantees can be efficiently adapted into signal-specific error predictors that are tight and generalizable. Across direct fitting of synthetic and natural signals, and inverse problems including radiance field and MRI reconstruction, our method closely tracks global error curves and yields informative local error heatmaps without ground-truth access. Code is available at https://github.com/voilalab/global_error_bound.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework to predict reconstruction error of compressive signal parameterizations (like INRs) without ground truth, by bounding error using scaled differences between predictions at different compression levels.
AI.motivation: Principled tools for evaluating reconstruction fidelity at finite model size are limited when ground truth is unavailable, despite the growing use of differentiable signal parameterizations in computational imaging.
AI.method: The authors prove a bound where compression error at any level is bounded by a scaled difference between model predictions at different compression levels, assuming certain natural properties hold. They verify these properties for interpolated grids, Fourier feature networks, multi-resolution hash encodings, and tensor factorizations.
AI.result: The framework yields tight, generalizable error predictors that closely track global error curves and produce informative local error heatmaps without ground truth, demonstrated on synthetic/natural signal fitting, radiance field, and MRI reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides theoretically sound, efficiently computable, and signal-specific error bounds for compressive signal parameterizations, enabling principled evaluation without ground-truth access.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Optimal Experiment Design in Parameter Identification of Mechatronic Systems
summary: Informative excitation signals are critical for accurate system identification of mechatronic systems, yet classical system identification (SI) approaches require expert knowledge and hand-crafted signal design to respect hardware safety constraints, limiting their generalizability. We propose a reinforcement learning (RL) agent that learns optimal excitation signals for a Quanser Aero 2 testbed while autonomously enforcing safety constraints through reward shaping. Evaluated across 10 independent training seeds, our comprehensive agent achieves competitive estimation accuracy across all three identified parameters, outperforming classical baselines while incurring only 0.75% safety violations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a reinforcement learning agent that autonomously generates safe, optimal excitation signals for system identification of mechatronic systems, achieving competitive accuracy with only 0.75% safety violations on a Quanser Aero 2 testbed.
AI.motivation: Classical system identification requires expert knowledge and hand-crafted signal design to ensure hardware safety, limiting generalizability. There is a need for automated, safe excitation signal generation.
AI.method: A reinforcement learning agent is trained to learn optimal excitation signals using reward shaping to enforce safety constraints, evaluated on a Quanser Aero 2 testbed.
AI.result: The RL agent achieves competitive estimation accuracy across all three identified parameters, outperforming classical baselines with only 0.75% safety violations across 10 independent training seeds.
AI.conclusion: RL-based excitation signal generation can effectively automate safe system identification, reducing the need for expert intervention while maintaining high accuracy and safety.

title: Flow-Based Generative Modeling for Optimizing Sampling Policies in Compressed Sensing Applications
summary: Numerous modern applications in signal processing and medical imaging necessitate acquiring high-dimensional signals under tight resource constraints. Traditional sampling theory suggests that accurate signal reconstruction requires a number of measurements proportional to the signal's ambient dimension, a requirement often too expensive or impractical. Compressed sensing challenges this notion by demonstrating that sparse signals can be recovered with fewer measurements, provided the measurement operator meets certain conditions. This proof-of-concept study presents a task-aware flow-based generative framework -- a reformulation of the conventional Flow Matching training paradigm with a flow model trained to optimize subsampling in compressed sensing applications. We establish the fundamental feasibility of the proposed framework of learning subsampling masks that substantially enhance the performance of compressed sensing for image classification, image reconstruction, and MRI acceleration. For the image reconstruction task, our method demonstrated state-of-the-art performance, achieving Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio of 25.17 dB at the subsampling rate of 5\% on the CelebA dataset and 29.24 dB when reconstructing $8\times$ accelerated MRI measurements (fastMRI dataset) with the minimal computational overhead. These results highlight the effectiveness of task-conditioning within generative flow models and reveal a promising direction for representation learning strategies. Overall, the proposed framework offers a unified, flexible approach to designing data- and task-driven sensing schemes that can be potentially adapted to a broad range of inverse problems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a task-aware flow-based generative framework that learns optimized subsampling masks for compressed sensing, achieving state-of-the-art performance in image classification, reconstruction, and MRI acceleration with minimal computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Traditional compressed sensing requires measurement operators to meet specific conditions for sparse signal recovery. This work aims to learn data-driven subsampling masks that enhance reconstruction quality and task performance beyond hand-crafted designs.
AI.method: A flow-based generative framework reformulating Flow Matching training, where a flow model is trained to jointly optimize subsampling masks and downstream task performance (classification, reconstruction, MRI acceleration).
AI.result: Achieves PSNR of 25.17 dB at 5% subsampling on CelebA, 29.24 dB for 8x accelerated MRI on fastMRI dataset, with state-of-the-art performance and minimal computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Task-conditioning in generative flow models is effective for learning sensing schemes, offering a unified and flexible approach adaptable to various inverse problems.

title: A Methodological Framework for Explicit Control of the Speed-Accuracy Trade-off in Brain-Computer Interfaces
summary: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) are limited by low signal-to-noise ratio in modalities such as electroencephalography, which requires multiple trials to reliably decode user intentions. This induces a speed-accuracy trade-off, whereby higher accuracy comes at the cost of speed. The speed-accuracy balance is application-dependent, motivating controllable trade-offs. Conventional metrics, such as the Information Transfer Rate, combine speed and accuracy obscuring their dependence and potentially introducing biases. In this study, we propose an evaluation framework independent of classifier, paradigm, and early-stopping strategy that separates speed and accuracy. We employ two measures, Gain (relative speed improvement) and Conservation (relative accuracy preservation), and combine them into a tunable Gain-Cons Balance controlled by α, regulating the speed-accuracy trade-off. The parameter adjusts the operating point without modifying the classifier, facilitating deployment across scenarios. The framework was evaluated on P300 event-related potential paradigms using public recordings from 63 subjects as well as multiple classifiers and early-stopping strategies to achieve distinct operating points in speed-accuracy and bitrate. Results show that tuning α yields fast, accurate, or balanced BCI behaviours, demonstrating explicit control of the speed-accuracy trade-off. The method supports subject-level performance prediction and improves explainability of BCI behaviour. Further analysis of the Information Transfer Rate reveals a systematic bias toward speed, explained by the proposed framework through the Gain and Conservation measurements. Overall, this work establishes the speed-accuracy trade-off as a controllable design variable validated on public P300-based paradigms, enabling transparent evaluation and application-specific optimization of BCIs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new evaluation framework for BCIs that separates speed and accuracy (using Gain and Conservation measures) with a tunable parameter α to control the speed-accuracy trade-off, validated on P300 paradigms across 63 subjects, and reveals that Information Transfer Rate is biased toward speed.
AI.motivation: BCIs suffer from low SNR requiring multiple trials, creating a speed-accuracy trade-off that is application-dependent. Conventional metrics like Information Transfer Rate combine speed and accuracy, obscuring their dependence and introducing biases.
AI.method: Proposes a framework using Gain (relative speed improvement) and Conservation (relative accuracy preservation) combined via a tunable parameter α (Gain-Cons Balance) to control the speed-accuracy trade-off without modifying the classifier. Evaluated on P300 paradigms using 63 subjects with multiple classifiers and early-stopping strategies.
AI.result: Tuning α yields fast, accurate, or balanced BCI behaviors, demonstrating explicit control of the trade-off. The framework enables subject-level performance prediction and reveals that Information Transfer Rate is systematically biased toward speed.
AI.conclusion: Establishes speed-accuracy trade-off as a controllable design variable for BCIs, enabling transparent evaluation and application-specific optimization, validated on public P300-based paradigms.

title: Hoeffding Concept Bottleneck Models with Applications to Overhead Images
summary: Explainability of deep learning algorithms is critical for computer-vision applications with high-stake decisions. Concept bottleneck models (CBM) have recently shown promising performance to provide explainable and accurate predictions for classification problems, based on a bottleneck of high-level concepts. Existing CBM methods rely on a linear aggregation of the concept scores to compute predictions. However, a large number of concepts is often used in this linear approach, which undermines explainability and favors information leakage. In general, the underlying relation between concepts and output logits is not linear. Therefore, we introduce Hoeffding Concept Bottleneck Models (HCBM), which build on the Hoeffding functional decomposition of gradient-boosted trees to provide non-linear and sparse aggregations of concept scores, and generate compact predictions using prime implicants. HCBM are proved to be robust to interconcept leakage, and outperform standard linear CBM in practice, as shown in extensive experiments. Beyond classification, HCBM can be adapted to object detection, and we focus on a challenging case with overhead images to show the high performance of HCBM in these settings.

AI.tldr: HCBM replaces linear concept aggregation in Concept Bottleneck Models with a non-linear, sparse method using Hoeffding decomposition of gradient-boosted trees, improving robustness to information leakage and outperforming standard CBM in classification and object detection tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard CBM methods use linear aggregation of many concepts, which undermines explainability and suffers from information leakage, while the true concept-output relationship is often non-linear.
AI.method: HCBM leverages the Hoeffding functional decomposition of gradient-boosted trees to achieve non-linear and sparse aggregation of concept scores, then generates compact predictions via prime implicants.
AI.result: HCBM is robust to inter-concept leakage and outperforms standard linear CBM in extensive experiments; it also adapts well to object detection in challenging overhead image settings.
AI.conclusion: Non-linear, sparse concept aggregation improves explainability and performance over linear CBM, with demonstrated effectiveness beyond classification into object detection.

title: Deliberative Curation: A Protocol for Multi-Agent Knowledge Bases
comment: 29 pages, 1 figure, 6 tables. Open-source implementation available at https://github.com/StevenJohnson998/AIngram
summary:
As AI agents transition from isolated tools to collaborative participants in shared knowledge ecosystems, governing collective knowledge curation becomes a critical challenge. Human platform governance mechanisms do not transfer directly: agent statelessness undermines deterrence-based sanctions, model homogeneity violates independence assumptions underlying crowd wisdom, and sycophancy collapses deliberative consensus.
  We propose a deliberative curation protocol combining three governance layers: (1) a knowledge artifact lifecycle formalized as a labeled transition system; (2) reputation-weighted deliberative voting integrating Beta Reputation with EigenTrust amplification; and (3) graduated sanctions adapted for stateless agents, including broken agent handling distinguishing malfunction from adversarial behavior.
  We evaluate the protocol through agent-based simulation with 100 agents across seven behavioral archetypes under two adversity scenarios (30 seeds, paired t-tests). The protocol trades modest precision under benign conditions for substantially better resilience under adversity: 0.826 vs 0.791 for majority vote under moderate adversity (p<0.001), widening to 0.807 vs 0.740 under stress (p<0.001). The protocol degrades roughly three times more slowly than majority vote. Ablation analysis identifies commit-reveal vote concealment as the most impactful single component (8.2-8.6pp precision improvement, p<0.001), outperforming reputation weighting and deliberation combined. Graduated sanctions were not exercised in simulation and remain empirically unvalidated.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a deliberative curation protocol for AI agents governing shared knowledge, using commitment-reveal voting, reputation weighting, and graduated sanctions. It shows the protocol trades modest benign precision for significantly better resilience under adversarial conditions, degrading ~3x slower than majority voting.
AI.motivation: Human governance mechanisms fail for AI agents due to statelessness (undermining deterrence), model homogeneity (violating independence assumptions), and sycophancy (collapsing consensus). A new approach for collective knowledge curation by AI agents is needed.
AI.method: Three-layer deliberative curation protocol: (1) a labeled transition system for knowledge artifact lifecycle; (2) reputation-weighted voting combining Beta Reputation with EigenTrust amplification; (3) graduated sanctions for stateless agents. Evaluated via agent-based simulation with 100 agents across 7 behavioral archetypes under 2 adversity scenarios (30 seeds, paired t-tests).
AI.result: Protocol achieves 0.826 vs 0.791 precision over majority vote under moderate adversity (p<0.001), widening to 0.807 vs 0.740 under stress (p<0.001). Degrades ~3x slower. Commit-reveal vote concealment is the most impactful component (8.2-8.6pp improvement, p<0.001). Graduated sanctions remained unvalidated.
AI.conclusion: The deliberative curation protocol successfully trades some benign-case precision for substantially stronger adversarial resilience. Commit-reveal mechanisms are the key enabler. Graduated sanctions need empirical validation in future work.

title: Finite-Resolution Information from Collision Statistics
summary: Collision statistics provide a finite-resolution view of information by measuring how often a fixed number of independent samples fall on the same state. These directly countable quantities form the basis of integer-order Rényi entropies. Here, we use low-order Rényi entropies to approximate Shannon entropy and mutual information, while characterizing what is necessarily lost when only finitely many collision moments are used. We derive interpolation-error bounds showing that approximation error is controlled by the shape of the Rényi entropy path near the Shannon point. We also separate this deterministic error from finite-sample estimation error: for fixed collision order, increasing sample size improves estimation of the finite-resolution target but does not eliminate its deterministic difference from Shannon entropy or mutual information. Finally, we show that finite collision moments do not generally identify Shannon entropy, and that increasing collision order shifts sensitivity toward high-probability events. Numerical experiments illustrate the approximation--estimation tradeoff and compare collision-based approximations with plug-in and Miller--Madow estimators. The framework links collision counts, Rényi entropy, Shannon limits, and mutual information through a finite-resolution view of information, clarifying when low-order coincidence structure is informative and when irreducible information is lost.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how low-order Rényi entropies (derived from collision statistics) approximate Shannon entropy and mutual information, deriving error bounds and showing that finite-resolution collision moments cannot eliminate a deterministic gap from Shannon quantities.
AI.motivation: To understand the approximation-estimation tradeoff when using finite-resolution collision statistics (countable moments) to infer Shannon entropy and mutual information, and to characterize what information is fundamentally lost when only finitely many collision orders are available.
AI.method: Derives interpolation-error bounds connecting low-order Rényi entropies to Shannon entropy, separates deterministic approximation error from finite-sample estimation error, and analyzes the sensitivity of higher-order collisions to high-probability events. Numerical experiments compare collision-based approximations with plug-in and Miller-Madow estimators.
AI.result: Approximation error is controlled by the shape of the Rényi entropy path near the Shannon point; larger sample sizes improve estimation of the finite-resolution target but do not close the deterministic gap; finite collision moments do not generally identify Shannon entropy; increasing collision order shifts sensitivity toward high-probability events.
AI.conclusion: Collision statistics provide a finite-resolution view of information where low-order coincidences are informative up to a fundamental irreducible loss, clarifying the limits of estimating Shannon quantities from finite collision moments.

title: Multi-Contrast MRI Motion Correction via Parameter-Informed Disentanglement and Adaptive Experts
summary: Motion artifacts in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) degrade diagnostic reliability. Existing deep learning methods are typically contrast-specific and fail to generalize across diverse modalities and artifact severities. We propose a unified framework combining parameter-informed contrast disentanglement with severity-aware adaptive correction. ScanCLIP, pretrained on over 30,000 MRI text-image pairs, derives contrast embeddings from acquisition parameters to disentangle contrast style from anatomical content, yielding contrast-free features. A Vision Transformer then estimates motion severity and routes features through a Mixture-of-Experts network, enabling targeted artifact correction. A dual-pathway decoder reconstructs both the clean image and residual artifact map, enforcing image-space consistency. On IXI and HCP benchmarks, our method improves PSNR by 0.75 dB and SSIM by up to 0.0279 over state-of-the-art approaches, with larger gains at higher artifact severities. It further demonstrates robust zero-shot generalization on real-world clinical data acquired with unseen scanning parameters, where existing methods either fail to remove artifacts or introduce additional distortions.

AI.tldr: A unified MRI motion artifact correction framework using ScanCLIP contrast disentanglement, severity-aware Mixture-of-Experts, and dual-pathway reconstruction, achieving superior performance across diverse contrasts and artifact levels.
AI.motivation: Motion artifacts degrade MRI diagnostic reliability, and existing deep learning methods are contrast-specific and fail to generalize across diverse modalities and artifact severities.
AI.method: (1) ScanCLIP pretrained on 30,000+ MRI text-image pairs derives contrast embeddings from acquisition parameters to disentangle contrast from anatomy. (2) Vision Transformer estimates motion severity, routing features through Mixture-of-Experts for targeted correction. (3) Dual-pathway decoder reconstructs clean image and residual artifact map simultaneously.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA by 0.75 dB PSNR and up to 0.0279 SSIM on IXI/HCP; larger gains at higher severities; robust zero-shot generalization on real-world clinical data with unseen parameters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework achieves contrast-agnostic, severity-adaptive motion artifact correction with strong generalization to unseen clinical scenarios, outperforming existing methods.

title: Linear Motility Maps in Nonlinear Viscous Fluids
summary: Systems moving in low Reynolds number fluid regimes are known to be governed by a ``motility map'' which linearly relates their shape change rates to they body frame velocity moving through the fluid. A consequence of this is ``Purcell's Scallop Theorem'' -- a locomotion system that undergoes shape changes that follow the same path forward and backward in time (reciprocal body deformations) cannot achieve net displacement, regardless of pacing of those changes.We show that linear-in-velocity motility maps extend to any power law viscosity (a.k.a. Ostwald--de Waele fluid), and therefore to many biological fluids in intermediate shear ranges. We also show that the linear-in-velocity property can be violated in Carreau-Yasuda fluids to produce net motion using an ``inchworm'' model consisting of two unequal masses with unequal drag coefficients performing reciprocal motions. Interestingly, the direction of motion can be switched by changing speeds. Our results show that the linear motility map of geometric mechaincs can be used to analyze and design locomotion in power-law fluids, and that some nonlinear drag relationships such as Carreau-Yasuda can be exploited to generate net locomotion in seeming violation of the ``scallop theorem''.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that Purcell's Scallop Theorem (no net motion from reciprocal motions in low Reynolds number fluids) holds for power-law fluids but can be violated in Carreau-Yasuda fluids, where an inchworm model achieves net displacement and can even switch direction by changing speed.
AI.motivation: To understand whether the geometric mechanics framework (motility maps) and the Scallop Theorem extend beyond Newtonian fluids to non-Newtonian biological fluids, and to identify conditions where reciprocal motions can produce net locomotion.
AI.method: The authors analyze motility maps in power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) fluids and Carreau-Yasuda fluids, using an inchworm model with two unequal masses and unequal drag coefficients performing reciprocal motions.
AI.result: Linear-in-velocity motility maps hold for power-law fluids (Scallop Theorem applies), but are violated in Carreau-Yasuda fluids, enabling net motion from reciprocal deformations. Direction of motion can be switched by changing speed.
AI.conclusion: Geometric mechanics can be used for power-law fluid locomotion design, while Carreau-Yasuda fluids offer opportunities to break the Scallop Theorem constraints.

title: Planktonzilla: Multimodal dataset and models for understanding plankton ecosystems
summary: Marine plankton underpin aquatic food webs and play a key role in global CO2 sequestration, making reliable species identification critical for understanding ocean health and climate feedbacks. Existing classification models perform well on individual collections but fail to generalize across instruments and environments due to isolated training datasets and inconsistent labels. To address this, we introduce Planktonzilla-17M, a unified dataset consolidating publicly available plankton image collections spanning thirteen imaging systems. It comprises 17.4 million images with standardized taxonomy and geo-environmental metadata, including 3.74 million plankton images spanning over 602 taxonomic classes, of which 201 are identified at the species level, making it the largest and most comprehensive plankton image dataset to date. Using this large-scale dataset, we perform a controlled comparison between supervised and CLIP-style image--text training on a shared ViT backbone. We find that a supervised classifier matches or exceeds CLIP-style training when trained using taxonomic lineage as text. We further observe that BioCLIP and BioCLIP2 perform poorly on plankton in zero-shot and few-shot settings. Leveraging Planktonzilla-17M improves plankton classification performance, highlighting the limitations of current biological foundation models in marine imaging domains.

AI.tldr: The authors introduce Planktonzilla-17M, the largest plankton image dataset (17.4M images, 602 classes), and show supervised training with taxonomic lineage text works as well as CLIP-style methods, while existing biological foundation models (BioCLIP, BioCLIP2) perform poorly on plankton.
AI.motivation: Reliable plankton species identification is critical for understanding ocean health and climate feedbacks, but existing classification models fail to generalize across instruments and environments due to isolated training datasets and inconsistent labels.
AI.method: Consolidated 17.4M publicly available plankton images from 13 imaging systems into Planktonzilla-17M with standardized taxonomy and geo-environmental metadata; performed controlled comparison between supervised and CLIP-style image-text training on a shared ViT backbone.
AI.result: Supervised classifier matches/exceeds CLIP-style training when using taxonomic lineage as text; BioCLIP and BioCLIP2 perform poorly on plankton in zero-shot and few-shot settings; Planktonzilla-17M improves classification performance.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale unified datasets like Planktonzilla-17M significantly improve plankton classification, and current biological foundation models have critical limitations in marine imaging domains.

title: Motif-based morphology signatures for interpretable ECG screening and monitoring
comment: Accepted to the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Conference (EMBC) 2026
summary: Electrocardiography (ECG) remains central to cardiovascular screening, yet interpretation remains largely manual and episodic. Clinical practice relies on brief resting ECGs and, when required, long-duration ambulatory recordings, both generating data that require resource-intensive review. Consequently, subtle morphological changes or progressive drift preceding clinically apparent abnormalities may go unnoticed. We propose a motif-based framework that defines beat-aligned ECG motifs as interpretable cardiac signatures and quantifies morphological drift and deviation across short and long-term monitoring. Motifs are representative cardiac cycles capturing dominant morphology. We introduce three interpretable drift metrics: deviation from a normal sinus rhythm (NSR), deviation from a personalised baseline, and a motif instability index. Motifs are extracted by selecting beats that minimise Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) distance within fixed windows. We evaluate these metrics on short (PTB-XL) and long-duration (MIT-BIH Arrhythmia) ECG datasets. Interpretability is achieved through representative motif overlays and fiducial-based visualisations, enabling direct inspection of morphological changes. In MIT-BIH, the proposed metrics significantly separated predominantly normal from arrhythmic subjects (p<0.01). In PTB-XL, NSR deviation distinguished normal from abnormal ECGs across major diagnostic subtypes (p<1e-4, Cliff's delta up to 0.93). ECG motifs provide an interpretable representation of cardiac morphology, supporting scalable longitudinal monitoring and early detection of morphology-driven change.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a motif-based framework for ECG analysis that defines beat-aligned motifs as interpretable cardiac signatures, introducing three drift metrics (NSR deviation, personalised baseline deviation, motif instability index) to quantify morphological changes over time. Evaluated on PTB-XL and MIT-BIH datasets, the metrics significantly distinguish normal from abnormal ECGs (p<0.01), enabling scalable, interpretable longitudinal monitoring.
AI.motivation: ECG interpretation remains largely manual and episodic; subtle morphological changes or progressive drift preceding clinical abnormalities may go unnoticed in both brief resting ECGs and long-duration ambulatory recordings due to resource-intensive review.
AI.method: A motif-based framework defining beat-aligned ECG motifs (representative cardiac cycles captured via DTW distance minimization) with three interpretable drift metrics: NSR deviation, personalised baseline deviation, and motif instability index. Visualized via motif overlays and fiducial-based plots.
AI.result: On MIT-BIH, metrics significantly separated normal from arrhythmic subjects (p<0.01). On PTB-XL, NSR deviation distinguished normal from abnormal ECGs across major diagnostic subtypes (p<1e-4, Cliff's delta up to 0.93).
AI.conclusion: ECG motifs provide interpretable cardiac morphology representation supporting scalable longitudinal monitoring and early detection of morphology-driven change.

title: From Demonstrations to Rewards: Test-Time Prompt Optimization for VLM Reward Models
summary: Reinforcement learning relies on accurate reward functions, which are often hand-crafted or even unavailable in real-world applications, such as robotics. Recent work has explored the zero-shot reasoning capabilities of pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as reward models. However, without careful prompt engineering, these approaches tend to produce suboptimal rewards, where false positive predictions can severely degrade downstream policy learning. In robotics, limited datasets comprising expert demonstrations are often collected to bootstrap policy learning. This scenario provides an opportunity to optimize a reward model prior policy training. We propose Demo2Reward a test-time adaptation technique to optimize the language instruction of a reward model based on a few demonstrations (3-10 trajectories) to reduce false positives while preserving true positives. Crucially, this requires no additional model training or computation resources during policy learning. We show that Demo2Reward consistently outperforms existing zero- and few-shot VLM reward models across a range of simulated robotic tasks and policy backbones. Finally, we demonstrate that Demo2Reward effectively transfers to a real-world robotic learning scenario, enabling policy learning without manually engineering a reward function.

AI.tldr: Demo2Reward optimizes VLM-based reward model prompts using 3-10 expert demonstrations to reduce false positives, improving policy learning in robotics without additional training or reward engineering.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning requires accurate reward functions, which are often hand-crafted or unavailable. Existing zero-shot VLM reward models produce suboptimal rewards with false positives that degrade policy learning.
AI.method: Test-time adaptation technique that optimizes the language instruction of a VLM reward model using a few expert demonstrations (3-10 trajectories) to reduce false positives while preserving true positives, requiring no additional model training or computational resources during policy learning.
AI.result: Demo2Reward consistently outperforms existing zero- and few-shot VLM reward models across simulated robotic tasks and policy backbones, and effectively transfers to real-world robotic learning without manual reward engineering.
AI.conclusion: Few demonstrations can effectively optimize VLM reward model prompts for more reliable RL-based policy learning in robotics, eliminating the need for hand-crafted reward functions.

title: Agents on a Tree: Pathwise Coordination for Multi-Objective Molecular Optimization
summary: Multi-objective molecular optimization requires searching vast chemical spaces under conflicting objectives, where early design decisions strongly constrain downstream outcomes. Existing methods typically rely on a single policy or fixed scalarization, which limits their ability to represent diverse trade-offs and to explore multiple promising design trajectories. We propose ATOM, a multi-agent framework that formulates molecular optimization as a tree-structured search. Each node corresponds to an atomic operation and hosts an agent specialized for a particular objective or decision context. Agents coordinate along different paths of the tree rather than enforcing a global consensus, enabling the method to maintain and compare alternative molecular evolution trajectories. A global memory of past optimization behaviors further supports balanced exploration and exploitation across objectives. This tree-structured interaction enables reasoning over long-horizon dependencies inherent in molecular design. Experiments on challenging multi-objective benchmarks involving activity, synthesizability, and ADMET-related properties show that ATOM consistently achieves improved Pareto coverage and hypervolume over strong baselines. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of pathwise multi-agent coordination for molecular optimization. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ATOM-41CE.

AI.tldr: ATOM is a multi-agent framework that formulates molecular optimization as a tree-structured search, where each node hosts an agent specialized for a particular objective, enabling pathwise coordination for improved Pareto optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing molecular optimization methods rely on single policies or fixed scalarization, limiting their ability to represent diverse trade-offs and explore multiple promising design trajectories under conflicting objectives.
AI.method: ATOM formulates molecular optimization as a tree-structured search where each node (atomic operation) hosts a specialized agent for a particular objective. Agents coordinate along different tree paths rather than enforcing global consensus, with a global memory supporting balanced exploration and exploitation.
AI.result: On challenging multi-objective benchmarks (activity, synthesizability, ADMET-related properties), ATOM consistently achieves improved Pareto coverage and hypervolume over strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Pathwise multi-agent coordination is effective for molecular optimization, enabling reasoning over long-horizon dependencies and diverse trade-off exploration.

title: Upper Bounds on Multiple $b$-Burst Deletion-Correcting Codes
summary: Motivated by their applications in DNA-based storage systems, codes capable of correcting consecutive deletions have attracted significant attention. An important class of such codes consists of those that can correct multiple consecutive deletion errors, commonly referred to as multiple $b$-burst deletion-correcting codes. In this paper, we investigate the fundamental limits of multiple $b$-burst deletion-correcting codes. Specifically, we first characterize several structural properties of the associated deletion balls. Then, leveraging these properties, we derive several upper bounds and a combinatorial lower bound on the maximum size of such codes. As a consequence, our bounds improve upon the previously known results for general parameter regimes and are shown to be asymptotically optimal for certain cases.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates codes that correct multiple consecutive deletions (b-burst deletions) for DNA storage, deriving improved upper/lower bounds on maximum code size, with asymptotic optimality for certain cases.
AI.motivation: DNA-based storage systems require codes that can correct consecutive deletions, motivating the need to understand the fundamental limits of multiple b-burst deletion-correcting codes.
AI.method: Characterize structural properties of deletion balls, then derive upper bounds and a combinatorial lower bound on maximum code size.
AI.result: Improved bounds over previously known results for general parameter regimes; bounds are asymptotically optimal for certain cases.
AI.conclusion: The derived bounds advance the understanding of fundamental limits for multiple b-burst deletion-correcting codes, with implications for DNA storage.

title: A physics-informed foundation model for quantitative diffusion MRI
summary: Understanding the human brain requires access to its microscopic tissue architecture. Diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides the only noninvasive window into whole-brain microstructure in vivo, yet reliable quantitative mapping remains confined to specialized research settings requiring dense sampling and optimized acquisition protocols. To address this gap, we present a physics-informed generative microstructure network (PIGMENT) that learns a universal generative prior of human brain microstructure and adapts it zero-shot to each participant's measured data to recover subject-specific maps. Trained on 11375 scans spanning multiple sites, vendors, and field strengths, PIGMENT enabled reliable quantitative mapping for tensor, kurtosis, and NODDI models across external datasets from five independent centers. It remains effective where conventional fitting becomes unreliable, recovering meaningful maps from extremely sparse acquisitions while supporting downstream tractography and structural connectivity mapping. PIGMENT estimates demonstrated strong biological validity, preserving submillimeter cortical microarchitectural patterns and early-childhood white matter developmental trajectories from 10-fold accelerated scans. Furthermore, PIGMENT enables reliable quantitative tensor mapping on cost-efficient low-field systems and the extraction of tumor-related biomarkers using ultra-fast clinical protocols.
