AI.tldr: LLMs overestimate their own abilities and attempt tasks they cannot solve. This can be fixed via reinforcement learning, which teaches Capability Self-Assessment (CSA) without degrading original skills, unlike supervised fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Modern LLMs lack the ability to recognize their own limitations and decide whether to solve or delegate problems, which is critical for reliable systems.
AI.method: Formulates Capability Self-Assessment (CSA) as a policy-learning problem and applies reinforcement learning to improve self-assessment while preserving original capabilities.
AI.result: Reinforcement learning significantly outperforms supervised fine-tuning for teaching CSA, preserves original capabilities, generalizes well out of distribution, and improves local-cloud decision making and data selection.
AI.conclusion: CSA is a transferable trait that can be effectively learned via RL, making LLMs more reliable by enabling them to recognize when to delegate tasks they cannot solve.

title: Lossy Microwave Linear Analog Computer (MiLAC) for Future MIMO: Learning-based Architecture Designs for Spectral and Energy Efficiency Maximization
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures, 1 table. This paper has been submitted to IEEE journal for possible publication
summary: Microwave linear analog computers (MiLACs) offer a transformative paradigm for future multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems by shifting complex signal processing into the analog domain, thereby significantly reducing computational complexity, radio-frequency chains, and analog-digital converters, while speeding up computation. However, the practical deployment of MiLACs is severely constrained by the inherent hardware losses of the tunable admittance components (TACs) interconnecting MiLAC ports, which introduce severe inter-stream interference and fundamentally limit the spectral efficiency (SE) of the system. In addition, while denser architectures offer greater spatial degrees of freedom to mitigate inter-stream interference, the cumulative hardware losses and power consumption of massive TACs severely degrade the system's energy efficiency (EE). Consequently, designing architectures for lossy MiLACs emerges as a critical yet unresolved challenge, as it necessitates striking a delicate tradeoff between interference suppression and cumulative hardware losses/power consumption. To address this challenge, this paper investigates the joint MiLAC architecture design and performance (SE/EE) maximization in lossy MiLAC-aided MIMO systems. We propose a novel learning-based joint architecture and performance optimization framework (LJAPOF) that unifies the design of MiLAC architectures and analog beamforming configurations for lossy MiLACs under both SE- and EE-oriented objectives. Numerical results demonstrate that by intelligently navigating the fundamental tradeoff between interference suppression and hardware/power consumption, the proposed LJAPOF can design optimal MiLAC architectures that consistently outperform stem-connected and fully-connected MiLACs in maximizing the system's SE and EE.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a learning-based optimization framework (LJAPOF) to design MiLAC architectures that balance interference suppression and hardware losses, maximizing spectral efficiency (SE) and energy efficiency (EE) in lossy MiLAC-aided MIMO systems.
AI.motivation: Practical deployment of microwave linear analog computers (MiLACs) is limited by hardware losses in tunable admittance components (TACs), which cause inter-stream interference and degrade SE/EE. Designing architectures that balance interference suppression vs. cumulative losses/power consumption is an unresolved challenge.
AI.method: Proposes LJAPOF (learning-based joint architecture and performance optimization framework) that jointly designs MiLAC architectures and analog beamforming configurations for lossy MiLACs under both SE- and EE-oriented objectives.
AI.result: The proposed LJAPOF designs optimal MiLAC architectures that consistently outperform stem-connected and fully-connected MiLACs in maximizing both SE and EE.
AI.conclusion: Intelligently navigating the tradeoff between interference suppression and hardware/power consumption enables optimal MiLAC architecture design for lossy MiLAC-aided MIMO systems.

title: DeepIPCv3: Event-Aware Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion for Sudden Pedestrian Crossing Avoidance
summary: Current end-to-end autonomous driving systems predominantly rely on frame-based sensors, which suffer from inherent perception latency and motion blur during highly dynamic encounters, specifically sudden pedestrian crossings. To address this critical safety vulnerability, we propose DeepIPCv3, a novel multi-modal autonomous navigation framework that synergizes the dense 3D spatial geometry of LiDAR point clouds with the microsecond-level asynchronous event streams of a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS). We introduce a Transformer-inspired cross-modal attention mechanism to dynamically correlate these distinct modalities, allowing the network to instantaneously prioritize high-speed dynamic updates without sacrificing structural scene awareness. The fused latent representations are then mapped to safe local waypoints and executable control commands via a hybrid policy network that blends heuristic trajectory tracking with direct neural predictions. Due to the severe physical risks associated with live testing of these sudden crossing scenarios, the framework is rigorously evaluated offline using a custom multi-modal dataset collected across both well-illuminated noon and challenging evening conditions. Extensive comparative and ablation studies demonstrate that DeepIPCv3 achieves state-of-the-art predictive performance. By effectively eliminating exposure failures and motion blur, the proposed LiDAR and DVS fusion yields the lowest trajectory and control command errors, enabling highly reactive, mathematically bounded evasive maneuvers regardless of ambient illumination. To support future research, we will release the codes to our GitHub repo at https://github.com/oskarnatan/DeepIPCv3.

AI.tldr: DeepIPCv3 is a novel autonomous driving framework that fuses LiDAR point clouds with event-based DVS sensors using cross-modal attention, achieving state-of-the-art trajectory and control accuracy by eliminating motion blur and exposure failures in highly dynamic scenarios like sudden pedestrian crossings.
AI.motivation: Current frame-based sensors in autonomous driving suffer from perception latency and motion blur in highly dynamic encounters (e.g., sudden pedestrian crossings), creating critical safety vulnerabilities that need to be addressed.
AI.method: DeepIPCv3 fuses LiDAR point clouds with DVS event streams using a Transformer-inspired cross-modal attention mechanism, then maps fused representations to waypoints and control commands via a hybrid policy network blending heuristic trajectory tracking with neural predictions.
AI.result: Offline evaluation on custom multi-modal datasets (noon and evening conditions) shows state-of-the-art predictive performance, with the lowest trajectory and control command errors, eliminating exposure failures and motion blur across different illumination levels.
AI.conclusion: LiDAR-DVS fusion enables highly reactive, mathematically bounded evasive maneuvers regardless of ambient illumination, and codes will be released for future research.

title: Cuttlebot: a platform demonstration for complex, autonomous, bio-inspired swimmers
summary: Increasing interest in deep-sea operations and resources motivates the development of ecologically sensitive but environmentally durable robots. Dielectric elastomer actuator artificial muscles are good candidates for powering such systems due to their pressure and temperature tolerance and soft makeup, but they are difficult to integrate with robotic systems. This work presents an autonomous robotic platform: the CORE, capable of driving six artificial muscles while sensing visual and spatial information. To validate the platform, we developed the Cuttlebot - a cuttlefish-inspired robot that swims in three dimensions using undulatory fin locomotion. The Cuttlebot has four primary artificial muscles in its fins in addition to a tentacle-inspired soft gripper. The robot was evaluated in a series of tethered and untethered swimming tests, demonstrating a top speed of 2.5 centimeters per second translation and 10 degrees per second rotation. Furthermore, the CORE system was capable of driving specialized control signals into the artificial muscles to controllably output force and torque in six axes. This work provides a platform for developing complex, bio-inspired swimming robots for ocean exploration and monitoring, laying the foundation with our leading example: the Cuttlebot.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CORE, an autonomous robotic platform driving six dielectric elastomer actuator artificial muscles, demonstrated through Cuttlebot - a cuttlefish-inspired swimming robot achieving 2.5 cm/s translation and 10°/s rotation with undulatory fin locomotion.
AI.motivation: To develop ecologically sensitive yet durable soft robots for deep-sea operations by overcoming the integration challenges of dielectric elastomer actuators into functional robotic systems.
AI.method: Designed the CORE platform to drive six artificial muscles with visual/spatial sensing; built the Cuttlebot with four fin muscles and a tentacle-inspired soft gripper; evaluated in tethered and untethered swimming tests.
AI.result: Cuttlebot achieved 2.5 cm/s translation and 10°/s rotation; CORE system delivered specialized control signals for controllable 6-axis force/torque output.
AI.conclusion: The CORE platform enables complex bio-inspired swimming robots for ocean exploration, with Cuttlebot serving as a foundational example.

title: Versatile Framework with Semantic and Structural guidance for Image Reconstruction from Brain Activity
summary: Reconstructing visual stimuli from brain recordings has been a meaningful and challenging task in brain decoding. Especially, the achievement of precise and controllable image reconstruction bears great significance in propelling the progress and utilization of brain-computer interfaces. Recent methods, leveraging advances in the power of text-to-image generation models, have reconstructed images that closely approximate complex natural stimuli in terms of semantics (e.g., concepts and objects). However, they struggle to maintain consistency with the original stimuli in fine-grained structural information (e.g., position, orientation and size), which undermines both the controllability and interpretability of the models. To address the aforementioned issues, we propose a two-stage image reconstruction framework, termed MindDiffuser. In Stage 1, Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) text embeddings decoded from brain responses are input into Stable Diffusion, generating a preliminary image containing semantic information. In Stage 2, we use decoded shallow CLIP visual features as supervisory signals, iteratively refining the feature vectors from Stage 1 via backpropagation to align structural information. We conducted extensive experiments on brain response datasets across three modalities (fMRI, EEG, MEG) elicited by visual stimuli, demonstrating that our framework significantly enhances the performance of previous state-of-the-art models, highlighting the effectiveness and versatility of our approach. Spatial and temporal visualization results further support the neurobiological plausibility of our framework, providing guidance for future neural decoding efforts across different brain signal modalities.

AI.tldr: MindDiffuser is a two-stage framework that reconstructs images from brain recordings (fMRI, EEG, MEG) by first generating semantic content via CLIP text embeddings and Stable Diffusion, then refining structural details using CLIP visual features through backpropagation, outperforming previous SOTA models.
AI.motivation: Existing brain decoding methods can reconstruct semantically similar images but fail to preserve fine-grained structural information (position, orientation, size), limiting controllability and interpretability of brain-computer interfaces.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: Stage 1 uses CLIP text embeddings from brain responses to generate a semantic image via Stable Diffusion; Stage 2 uses decoded shallow CLIP visual features as supervision to iteratively refine feature vectors via backpropagation to align structural details.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across fMRI, EEG, and MEG modalities show MindDiffuser significantly improves over previous state-of-the-art models in image reconstruction quality.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effectiveness, versatility, and neurobiological plausibility across multiple brain signal modalities, offering guidance for future neural decoding research.

title: Electromagnetic Digital Twin-Enabled Closed-Loop Beam Management in ISAC Systems
summary: Digital twin (DT) is envisioned as a key enabler of sixth-generation (6G) communication systems, evolving from offline descriptive replicas for monitoring and analysis to inthe-loop agents within digital twin networks (DTNs) that couple physical and digital worlds. Recent advances in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)-driven electromagnetic (EM) scattering methods enable environment twinning by linking channel behaviors to EM properties of the scatterers, supporting interpretable DT states and EM-grounded optimization. However, existing studies primarily focus on DT construction and lack mechanisms for closed-loop control in wireless systems. Moreover, array-geometry mismatch can bias DT reconstruction and degrade control performance, while prior works assume known arrays. To address these gaps, we propose an EM-ISACbased closed-loop DTN framework with a hierarchical design integrating environment twinning, prior injection, and control decision into an end-to-end loop. Leveraging ISAC measurements, the proposed framework jointly reconstructs scatterer information and array-dependent forward operator and employs a low-complexity Bayesian message-passing algorithm to perform contrast inference and array calibration. The reconstructed DT guides codebook preselection to reduce training overhead and narrow candidate beams. Subsequently, downlink beamforming (BF) is performed based on DT-predicted channels, enabling latency-bounded closed-loop control. Simulation results demonstrate improved robustness and control performance under array mismatch.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an EM-ISAC-based closed-loop digital twin network (DTN) framework that jointly performs environment twinning, array calibration, and closed-loop beamforming control, achieving robust performance under array-geometry mismatch.
AI.motivation: Existing digital twin studies focus on construction but lack closed-loop control mechanisms for wireless systems, and prior works assume known arrays without addressing array-geometry mismatch that degrades DT reconstruction and control performance.
AI.method: The framework integrates environment twinning, prior injection, and control decision into an end-to-end loop. It uses ISAC measurements to jointly reconstruct scatterer information and array-dependent forward operator via a low-complexity Bayesian message-passing algorithm for contrast inference and array calibration. The reconstructed DT then guides codebook preselection and downlink beamforming for latency-bounded closed-loop control.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate improved robustness and control performance under array mismatch conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed closed-loop DTN framework effectively addresses array-geometry mismatch and enables robust closed-loop control in 6G digital twin networks.

title: Agentic Transformers Provably Learn to Search via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Tree search is a central abstraction behind many language-agent reasoning and decision-making tasks: agents must explore actions, remember failures, and backtrack toward promising alternatives. Yet, we lack a theoretical understanding of how transformer-based policies acquire such search capabilities from the training dynamics of reinforcement learning (RL). We study this question in a stochastic $k$-ary tree environment, where an agentic transformer observes only its trajectory history through interaction and receives a terminal reward for reaching a hidden leaf goal node. We first construct a two-head transformer that implements randomized depth-first search (DFS): one head tracks previous actions, while the other detects failure outcomes and triggers backtracking. We then analyze the training dynamics of policy gradient under a depth-wise curriculum, showing that this same DFS mechanism emerges in stages from sparse reinforcement feedback without expert demonstrations. The resulting policy exhibits depth generalization: after training only on depth-$1$ and depth-$2$ trees, it succeeds on deeper full trees. We further show that, under imbalanced goal distributions, discounting the return leads to a ranked DFS policy that prioritizes higher-probability branches. Overall, our results identify a mechanistic normal form for transformer-based search, in which attention heads specialize and cooperate to extract decision-relevant traces from context and convert them into agentic action selection via RL training.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically analyzes how transformer-based policies learn to perform tree search (specifically DFS) through RL training dynamics in a stochastic tree environment, showing that a two-head attention mechanism emerges with one head tracking actions and another detecting failures for backtracking, enabled by a depth-wise curriculum.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of theoretical understanding of how transformer-based policies acquire search capabilities (like exploration, backtracking) from reinforcement learning training dynamics, without expert demonstrations or explicit search supervision.
AI.method: The authors construct a theoretical two-head transformer that implements randomized DFS in a stochastic k-ary tree environment, then analyze policy gradient training dynamics under a depth-wise curriculum to show how this mechanism emerges from sparse reinforcement feedback.
AI.result: The DFS mechanism emerges in stages from RL training; the policy exhibits depth generalization (training on depth-1/2 trees succeeds on deeper trees); under imbalanced goals, discounting produces ranked DFS that prioritizes higher-probability branches.
AI.conclusion: The findings identify a mechanistic normal form for transformer-based search where attention heads specialize and cooperate to extract decision-relevant traces from context for agentic action selection via RL training.

title: Closed-Loop Neural Activation Control in Vision-Language-Action Models
comment: Accepted at the IEEE/CVF CVPR 2026 Workshop on Visual Concepts (VisCon). 25 pages, 8 figures, including supplementary material
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models can be steered at test time by intervening on semantically meaningful internal directions, but existing methods use a fixed steering coefficient, effectively operating in open loop. This is poorly suited to embodied control, where task state and concept error evolve over time, often causing overcorrection, oscillation, and reduced task success, especially for temporal behaviors such as speed and smoothness. We propose CTRL-STEER, a closed-loop framework that replaces static intervention strength with adaptive, time-varying control signals. The key idea is to decouple representation from regulation: rather than assuming temporal concepts are directly controlled by individual neurons, we steer along motion-aligned residual directions while a feedback controller adjusts intervention magnitude online. We instantiate this framework with both PID and reinforcement learning based controllers. Experiments with a fine-tuned OpenVLA policy on four LIBERO task suites show that CTRL-STEER achieves more stable concept regulation and a better steering-task success trade-off than fixed-coefficient baselines, without modifying or retraining the base model.

AI.tldr: CTRL-STEER replaces fixed steering coefficients in VLA models with adaptive closed-loop control (PID/RL) for better temporal behavior regulation in embodied tasks, achieving improved success-stability trade-offs on LIBERO benchmarks without model retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA steering methods use static (open-loop) coefficients, which fail for embodied control because task state and concept errors evolve over time, causing overcorrection, oscillation, and poor temporal behaviors like speed and smoothness.
AI.method: Decouples representation from regulation by steering along motion-aligned residual directions while using a feedback controller (PID or RL-based) to adjust intervention magnitude online in closed-loop fashion.
AI.result: On four LIBERO task suites with fine-tuned OpenVLA, CTRL-STEER achieves more stable concept regulation and better steering-task success trade-off than fixed-coefficient baselines.
AI.conclusion: Closed-loop adaptive steering improves temporal behavior control in embodied VLA policies without modifying or retraining the base model, offering a practical plug-in solution.

title: Leaf Spectral Reflectance Prediction Using Multi-Head Attention Neural Networks
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures. Author-accepted version of the SPIE conference paper
summary: Accurate modeling of leaf spectral reflectance from physiological and biochemical traits is essential for advancing remote sensing applications in plant science and precision agriculture. Widely used radiative transfer models, such as PROSPECT-PRO, rely on generalized trait-reflectance relationships developed from a wide range of species, which may not fully capture the spectral behavior of specific crops like grapevines. In this study, we developed a trait-to-spectra prediction model using a multi-head attention neural network trained on a grapevine-specific dataset that includes 16 leaf traits measured across multiple varieties, growth stages, and years. The model was evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation and achieved an average coefficient of determination (R^2) of 0.84 and normalized root mean squared error (NRMSE) of 1.52 percent, demonstrating high accuracy and generalizability. When compared to PROSPECT-PRO in forward mode, the neural network exhibited lower mean absolute error (MAE), especially in the near-infrared (NIR) and shortwave-infrared (SWIR) regions. These results emphasize the importance of species-specific modeling approaches and show that integrating biochemical and structural traits into data-driven architectures can significantly improve spectral prediction. The proposed model provides a robust framework for generating accurate leaf-level reflectance data, with potential applications in canopy trait retrieval, vineyard monitoring, and remote sensing-driven crop management.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a multi-head attention neural network (trained on grapevine-specific leaf traits) that predicts spectral reflectance more accurately than the generalized PROSPECT-PRO model, especially in NIR/SWIR regions, achieving R²=0.84 and NRMSE=1.52%.
AI.motivation: Generalized radiative transfer models like PROSPECT-PRO may not capture the spectral behavior of specific crops like grapevines, limiting remote sensing accuracy in precision agriculture.
AI.method: A multi-head attention neural network was trained on a grapevine-specific dataset with 16 leaf traits (multiple varieties, growth stages, years), evaluated via stratified 5-fold cross-validation.
AI.result: The model achieved average R²=0.84 and NRMSE=1.52%, outperforming PROSPECT-PRO in forward mode with lower MAE, particularly in NIR and SWIR regions.
AI.conclusion: Species-specific data-driven architectures integrating biochemical and structural traits significantly improve spectral prediction, offering a robust framework for vineyard monitoring and remote sensing-driven crop management.

title: Series-Parallel Integrated Nonlinear Elastic Actuator applied to the lean motion of a bicycle simulator
summary: Designing robots for high-torque, high-fidelity haptic interaction is challenging. Parallel Elastic Actuators (PEAs) use elastic elements in parallel to smaller motors to complement torques, and Series Elastic Actuators (SEAs) use elastic elements in series to decouple motor impedance and improve force control. Recent work combines SEAs and PEAs to obtain both benefits but requires separate elastic elements or clutching. This paper presents the Series Parallel Integrated Nonlinear Elastic Actuator (SPINEA), which merges SEA and PEA such that a single elastic element takes on dual roles simultaneously, parallel and series. This is achieved by a nonlinear transmission in which the motor and load have misaligned rotation axes and are elastically connected. This geometry enables both high peak torque and precise torque tracking. We apply SPINEA to actuate lean of a haptic bicycle simulator, which requires high moments and precise rendering for safe and realistic rider interactions. We realized a prototype and performed experiments, both with an external excitation setup and with riders cycling. Our results confirm SPINEA's low impedance and precise torque tracking, up to 4.25 Hz with the bicycle frame fixed and up to 4 Hz with riders. The benefits may transfer to other applications requiring compact, high-performance actuation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SPINEA, a novel actuator that uses a single nonlinear elastic element to simultaneously serve as both a Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) and Parallel Elastic Actuator (PEA), achieving high torque and precise torque tracking for haptic interaction, demonstrated on a bicycle simulator.
AI.motivation: Designing compact, high-performance actuators for high-torque, high-fidelity haptic interaction is challenging. While SEAs and PEAs offer complementary benefits (force control vs. torque augmentation), existing combined solutions require separate elastic elements or clutching mechanisms, adding complexity and bulk.
AI.method: SPINEA merges SEA and PEA by using a single nonlinear elastic element connecting a motor and load with misaligned rotation axes. This geometric nonlinearity enables the elastic element to act both in series and parallel simultaneously, achieving high peak torque and precise torque tracking without separate components.
AI.result: Experiments on a haptic bicycle simulator prototype show SPINEA achieves low impedance and precise torque tracking up to 4.25 Hz (fixed frame) and up to 4 Hz (with riders), confirming its effectiveness for realistic haptic rendering.
AI.conclusion: SPINEA provides a compact, integrated solution for high-performance actuation by merging SEA and PEA in a single elastic element, with potential benefits extending to other applications requiring high torque and precise force control.

title: CardioLens: Revealing the Clinical Reality Gap of MLLMs via Multi-Sequence Cardiac MRI Evaluations
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong performance on public medical benchmarks, yet existing evaluations often remain weak proxies for clinical use, relying on isolated inputs and simplified recognition-style tasks. We introduce CardioLens, a leakage-resistant evaluation testbed for multi-sequence Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance (CMR), constructed from private hospital archives through a rigorous report-to-QA construction and verification pipeline. CardioLens contains 473,896 slices and 13,494 verified QA pairs across 4D Cine, LGE, perfusion, and T2-weighted imaging, and evaluates three stages of CMR interpretation: image understanding, report generation, and disease diagnosis. Across 24 state-of-the-art MLLMs, CardioLens reveals a substantial clinical reality gap: models perform poorly overall, with performance degrading along the real CMR workflow. Confusion analysis further shows a category-collapse failure mode, where models default to frequent abnormal categories rather than distinguishing clinically distinct findings. To rule out MLLM-compatible input construction as the primary cause, we compare random, clinically motivated, and data-driven slice selection protocols under different slice budgets; performance changes only marginally, typically by about 1%. Explicit reasoning prompts also fail to rescue performance, often making models more conservative rather than improving visual evidence use. These results show that current MLLMs remain far from reliable CMR interpretation, where clinical decisions require integrating distributed evidence across sequences, views, and temporal phases. CardioLens provides a clinically grounded testbed for developing next-generation MLLMs toward real-world clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: CardioLens is a new, leakage-resistant CMR evaluation testbed revealing that current MLLMs perform poorly and unreliably in multi-sequence cardiac MRI interpretation, with a large clinical reality gap across understanding, report generation, and diagnosis tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing medical MLLM evaluations are weak proxies for clinical use, relying on isolated inputs and simplified recognition tasks, lacking a realistic, multi-sequence, clinically grounded benchmark.
AI.method: Built CardioLens from private hospital archives (473,896 slices, 13,494 QA pairs) across 4D Cine, LGE, perfusion, and T2-weighted imaging, evaluating 24 MLLMs on image understanding, report generation, and disease diagnosis with rigorous report-to-QA verification.
AI.result: Models perform poorly overall, with performance degrading along the real CMR workflow; a category-collapse failure mode is observed where models default to frequent abnormal categories; slice selection protocols change performance marginally (~1%); explicit reasoning prompts fail to improve performance.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs are far from reliable CMR interpretation, as clinical decisions require integrating distributed evidence across sequences, views, and temporal phases; CardioLens provides a clinically grounded testbed for developing next-generation MLLMs.

title: Multicast Capacity of XL-RIS Assisted Hybrid Near- and Far-Field mmWave Communications
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures, submitted to IEEE for possible publication
summary: Multicast transmission in millimeter-wave (mmWave) networks is fundamentally limited by the weakest user, and blockages further exacerbate this problem. Large-scale reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (XL-RIS) offer a promising solution by providing high array gain to overcome blockages. However, the large aperture of XL-RIS significantly expands the near-field region, creating a hybrid-field scenario where some users lie in the near-field while others remain in the far-field. Existing hybrid-field studies on XL-RIS have primarily focused on channel estimation and deployment optimization, leaving multicast capacity analysis unexplored. This paper investigates the fundamental capacity limits of XL-RIS-assisted multicast communications in hybrid-field scenarios. For the fundamental two-user case consisting of one near-field and one far-field user, we derive the optimal closed-form covariance matrix and optimize the RIS phase shifts via manifold optimization. We establish that the multicast capacity scales as $Θ(\log_2(MN))$ as the number of transmit antennas M and/or RIS elements N grow large, and prove this scaling is order-tight. Numerical results validate the bounds and show the impact of M, $N$, and distance on the multicast rate.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the fundamental capacity limits of XL-RIS-assisted multicast communications in hybrid-field (near-field and far-field) mmWave scenarios, deriving optimal covariance matrices and RIS phase shifts, and proving multicast capacity scales as Θ(log₂(MN)) which is order-tight.
AI.motivation: Multicast transmission in mmWave networks is inherently limited by the weakest user, and blockages worsen this issue. While XL-RIS can provide high array gain to overcome blockages, its large aperture creates a hybrid-field scenario where some users are in near-field and others in far-field. Existing work on XL-RIS in hybrid-field settings has focused on channel estimation and deployment, leaving multicast capacity analysis unexplored.
AI.method: The authors consider a fundamental two-user case (one near-field, one far-field user). They derive the optimal closed-form covariance matrix and optimize RIS phase shifts using manifold optimization. They analyze the asymptotic scaling behavior of multicast capacity with respect to the number of transmit antennas M and RIS elements N.
AI.result: The multicast capacity scales as Θ(log₂(MN)) as M and/or N grow large, and this scaling is proven to be order-tight. Numerical results validate the bounds and show the impact of M, N, and distance on the multicast rate.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first analysis of multicast capacity limits in XL-RIS-assisted hybrid-field mmWave networks, with closed-form optimal solutions for the two-user case and tight asymptotic scaling laws.

title: AI-Guided Design and Optimization of Graphite-Based Anodes via Iterative Experimental Feedback
summary: This study presents an iterative AI-guided workflow that accelerates graphite-based anode development by improving both formulation feasibility and process robustness. Sequential learning via AI/ML-guided multiobjective inverse design for anode optimization was implemented using the Citrine Platform. Starting from a noisy, incomplete dataset, the Citrine Platform was used to generate early surrogate models, which despite low predictive certainty highlighted missing process constraints. By iteratively adding feasibility labels and boundary condition failures, the workflow rapidly converged toward manufacturable, higher-performing formulations. Fabrication reliability improved from frequent process failures to 100% successful cell production, while the fraction of cells delivering $\geq$ 350 mAh g$^{-1}$ increased from 28.4% to 84.8%, with capacity retention rising from 42.1% to 97.3%. These results demonstrate that structured, feedback-driven AI workflows can transform imperfect industrial data into actionable guidance, enabling faster, more reproducible optimization of battery electrode manufacturing.

AI.tldr: An iterative AI-guided workflow using the Citrine Platform improved graphite anode development, achieving 100% fabrication reliability, increasing high-capacity cells from 28.4% to 84.8%, and boosting capacity retention from 42.1% to 97.3%.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges of noisy, incomplete industrial data and frequent process failures in battery electrode manufacturing by developing a structured AI-driven optimization approach.
AI.method: Iterative AI/ML-guided multiobjective inverse design using the Citrine Platform, starting from imperfect data, adding feasibility labels and boundary condition failures to converge on manufacturable formulations.
AI.result: Fabrication reliability improved to 100% successful cell production; cells delivering ≥350 mAh g⁻¹ increased from 28.4% to 84.8%; capacity retention rose from 42.1% to 97.3%.
AI.conclusion: Structured, feedback-driven AI workflows can transform imperfect industrial data into actionable guidance, enabling faster, more reproducible battery electrode manufacturing optimization.

title: Robust Shielding for Safe Reinforcement Learning
summary: Shielding is an effective approach to formally guarantee the safety of reinforcement learning agents in Markov decision processes (MDPs). However, existing shielding techniques typically assume knowledge of the safety-relevant transition dynamics - a requirement that is seldom met in practice. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel shielding framework for robust MDPs (RMDPs), i.e., MDPs with sets of transition probabilities. We define safety as the satisfaction of a linear temporal logic (LTL) formula with a certain threshold probability under the worst-case transition probabilities of the RMDP. We prove that our shielding framework is both sound and optimal for the RMDP: every policy admissible by the shield is safe, and conversely, every safe RMDP policy is admissible by the shield. We combine our approach with existing sampling methods for learning transition probabilities of MDPs with probably approximately correct (PAC) guarantees. This combination enables the construction of shields for MDPs that, with high confidence, guarantee safety while remaining minimally restrictive. Our experiments show that our shields for learned RMDPs guarantee safety in unknown MDPs while recovering strong expected return as the number of samples increases.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a shielding framework for robust MDPs (RMDPs) that guarantees safety under worst-case transition probabilities, proving the shield is both sound and optimal while combining with PAC learning to handle unknown transition dynamics.
AI.motivation: Existing shielding techniques assume knowledge of safety-relevant transition dynamics, which is rarely available in practice. The authors aim to address this limitation.
AI.method: The authors define safety as LTL satisfaction with a threshold probability under worst-case RMDP transitions. They combine this with PAC sampling methods to learn transition probabilities and construct shields with high-confidence safety guarantees.
AI.result: The framework is proven sound and optimal. Experiments show shields for learned RMDPs guarantee safety in unknown MDPs while recovering strong expected return as sample size increases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully extends shielding to handle uncertainty in transition dynamics, providing formal safety guarantees while remaining minimally restrictive.

title: Hist2Style: Histogram-Guided Stylization with Bilateral Grids
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures. Extended results are at https://www.dekelgalor.com/hist2style
summary: Photorealistic style transfer aims to match the color and tone of an input image to that of a style target while preserving the content and details of the original scene. Although existing large image models can facilitate these kinds of appearance edits, their high computational demands, potential for hallucinations, and limited user control make them unsuitable for high-resolution, real-time workflows. We introduce Hist2Style, a bilateral-grid formulation for fast, edge-aware stylization that preserves visual fidelity by constraining operations to locally affine transforms in bilateral space. Our model distills a large image editing model into a lightweight network by training on a large supervised corpus generated with language and vision-language models, targeting spatially varying color edits. The network conditions on a histogram-based embedding of the style target to provide an interpretable interface for adjusting the output style by modifying the target color distribution. Overall, Hist2Style maintains content structure by construction, avoids hallucinations, and supports real-time, high-resolution photorealistic stylization with interactive user-controllable color and tone adjustments.

AI.tldr: Hist2Style is a bilateral-grid framework for fast, real-time photorealistic style transfer that distills a large image editing model into a lightweight network, using histogram-based embeddings for interpretable, user-controllable color and tone adjustments while preserving content structure and avoiding hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Existing large image models for photorealistic style transfer suffer from high computational costs, hallucinations, and limited user control, making them impractical for high-resolution, real-time workflows.
AI.method: The approach uses a bilateral-grid formulation for locally affine transforms in bilateral space, distills a large editing model into a lightweight network trained on a supervised corpus generated by language/vision-language models, and conditions on a histogram-based embedding of the style target.
AI.result: The model achieves real-time, high-resolution photorealistic stylization with user-controllable color and tone adjustments, while maintaining content structure, avoiding hallucinations, and providing interpretable control via histogram-based embedding.
AI.conclusion: Hist2Style demonstrates that a lightweight, bilateral-space approach can effectively replace large models for photorealistic style transfer, offering practical advantages in speed, fidelity, and user control for real-world applications.

title: Continuous Reasoning for Vision-Language-Action
summary:
Natural language is a powerful reasoning medium for language and vision-language models, but it is mismatched to the granularity of continuous control. Text and explicit subgoals operate at task-level granularity, whereas vision-language-action (VLA) policies must choose actions at a much finer temporal scale; a single reasoning step can therefore span many action chunks while remaining only weakly coupled to the action needed now. This suggests a different question for VLA: what should play the role of language? We argue that a useful VLA reasoning medium must be shareable across model instances, verifiable through downstream action improvement, and aligned with temporally extended control structure.
  Based on this view, we propose Continuous Reasoning for Vision-Language-Action. Our model first predicts continuous reasoning in the form of a structured set of continuous thoughts, then reuses them as shared context for chunk-structured action generation. Better action prediction alone does not certify good reasoning: if the same internal medium cannot be shared across model instances and independently verified through improved downstream control, the added latent may simply become a model-private shortcut that helps on seen behaviors without supporting generalizable control. We therefore instantiate continuous reasoning as a shared Gaussian latent interface and train it with a self-verification objective in which an exponential-moving-average teacher must successfully consume the student's reasoning when predicting target actions.
  Empirically, Continuous Reasoning improves LIBERO-PRO robustness and performs strongly on real robots, raising mean subtask success over π0.5 by 40.4% on TX-G2, an AgiBot G2-compatible variant, and 26.3% on HSR. This suggests that reasoning in VLA is less about extra tokens than about a shareable, verifiable internal language for action.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Continuous Reasoning for Vision-Language-Action, which uses a shared Gaussian latent interface as a continuous reasoning medium that is shareable across model instances and verifiable through downstream action improvement, achieving 40.4% and 26.3% improvement on real robot tasks over π0.5.
AI.motivation: Natural language operates at task-level granularity, mismatched to the fine-grained temporal scale of continuous control in VLA policies. The authors argue that a useful VLA reasoning medium must be shareable across model instances, verifiable through downstream action improvement, and aligned with temporally extended control structure.
AI.method: The model first predicts continuous reasoning as a structured set of continuous thoughts (shared Gaussian latent interface), then reuses them as shared context for chunk-structured action generation. It uses a self-verification objective with an exponential-moving-average teacher that must consume the student's reasoning to predict target actions.
AI.result: Continuous Reasoning improves LIBERO-PRO robustness and performs strongly on real robots, raising mean subtask success over π0.5 by 40.4% on TX-G2 and 26.3% on HSR.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning in VLA is less about extra tokens than about a shareable, verifiable internal language for action. The proposed continuous reasoning approach enables better generalization and control.

title: Positional Encodings Anchor Spatial Structure in Vision Transformers: A Geometric Perspective on Robustness
comment: 16 pages (9 main text, 7 appendix). 5 figures (3 main text, 2 appendix) with 8 graphics total. 5 tables (1 main text, 4 appendix). Submitted to NeurIPS 2026 main conference and the ICML 2026 mechanistic interpretability workshop
summary: Positional embeddings (PEs) in Vision Transformers (ViTs) are known to impact performance and robustness, but their role in shaping internal spatial representations is not well understood. In this work, we study how different forms of PEs influence the representational geometry of ViTs and how these changes relate to robustness under content-disrupting distribution shifts. We introduce a metric, the Spatial Similarity Distance Correlation (SSDC), to quantify spatial structure in token representations. Using this metric, we show that ViTs trained without PEs still develop non-trivial spatial structure, but this structure is driven by visual content and collapses under token permutation. In contrast, we find that all PEs considered (learned absolute, sinusoidal, and rotary) are associated with a consistent shift toward an index-anchored spatial organization. Representations in these models remain stable under perturbations that disrupt content, and exhibit substantially improved robustness to such distributional shifts. We further show that while different PEs produce distinct depth-wise trajectories of spatial structure, their robustness properties are largely similar (with secondary variation across encoding schemes), suggesting that robustness appears to depend on the presence of a stable positional reference frame more than it depends on the specific encoding mechanism. These results offer a geometric account of how positional encodings shape internal representations, with implications for the principled design of future encoding schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how different positional embeddings (PEs) affect the internal spatial representations of Vision Transformers (ViTs) and their relationship to robustness under content-disrupting distribution shifts. The authors introduce a metric called Spatial Similarity Distance Correlation (SSDC) to quantify spatial structure, finding that while ViTs without PEs develop content-driven spatial structure (which collapses under token permutation), models with PEs exhibit index-anchored spatial organization that remains stable under content disruption, leading to substantially improved robustness.
AI.motivation: Positional embeddings in Vision Transformers are known to impact performance and robustness, but how they shape internal spatial representations is not well understood. The authors aim to provide a geometric account of how PEs influence representational geometry and how these changes relate to robustness under distribution shifts.
AI.method: The authors introduce a metric called Spatial Similarity Distance Correlation (SSDC) to quantify spatial structure in token representations. They compare ViTs trained without PEs against those with learned absolute, sinusoidal, and rotary PEs, analyzing spatial organization under content-disrupting and token-permuting perturbations.
AI.result: ViTs without PEs develop non-trivial spatial structure driven by visual content, but this collapses under token permutation. All PEs studied produce a shift toward index-anchored spatial organization, maintaining stability under content disruption and yielding substantially improved robustness. Different PEs show distinct depth-wise trajectories but similar robustness properties.
AI.conclusion: Robustness appears to depend more on the presence of a stable positional reference frame than on the specific encoding mechanism. These findings offer geometric insights into how positional encodings shape internal representations, with implications for principled design of future encoding schemes.

title: Flexible Rate-Splitting for Joint Unicast and Multi-Group Multicast Transmission in RIS-Assisted mmWave Networks
comment: 14 pages, 11 figures, 2 tables, submitted to IEEE for possible publication
summary: Joint unicast and multi-group multicast transmission with RIS and RSMA is a promising enabler for 6G services. However, existing RSMA schemes for such scenarios split only unicast messages while leaving multicast messages intact, limiting the degree of freedom of interference management. To this end, we propose a joint rate splitting framework that splits both unicast and multicast information and two RSMA schemes. The common-common fusion (CCF-RSMA) scheme encodes the unicast common part into the global multicast common stream, while the private-common fusion (PCF-RSMA) scheme merges it with the group-specific multicast private part. For each scheme, we formulate energy efficiency (EE) maximization problems under both perfect and imperfect channel state information, and jointly optimize active beamforming, RIS phase shifts and rate allocation parameters. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed schemes significantly outperform the comparative schemes in terms of EE, thereby proving the effectiveness of the proposed framework. Moreover, CCF-RSMA is more favorable in scenarios with larger groups and higher unicast QoS demands, whereas PCF-RSMA is better suited for scenarios with smaller groups and higher multicast QoS.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a joint rate splitting framework for RIS-assisted multi-group multicast and unicast transmissions, introducing two RSMA schemes (CCF-RSMA and PCF-RSMA) that split both unicast and multicast messages. Both schemes optimize energy efficiency under perfect/imperfect CSI, with simulation results showing significant EE improvements over baselines and revealing scenario-dependent trade-offs between the two schemes.
AI.motivation: Existing RSMA schemes for joint unicast and multi-group multicast transmissions only split unicast messages while leaving multicast messages intact, which limits the degrees of freedom for interference management. This paper aims to overcome this limitation.
AI.method: Proposes a joint rate splitting framework with two schemes: (1) CCF-RSMA encodes unicast common part into the global multicast common stream; (2) PCF-RSMA merges unicast common part with group-specific multicast private part. Jointly optimizes active beamforming, RIS phase shifts, and rate allocation under both perfect and imperfect CSI.
AI.result: Both proposed schemes significantly outperform comparative baselines in energy efficiency. CCF-RSMA is better for larger groups and higher unicast QoS demands, while PCF-RSMA suits smaller groups and higher multicast QoS.
AI.conclusion: The proposed joint rate splitting framework is effective for improving EE in RIS-assisted joint unicast and multi-group multicast systems, with scenario-dependent selection guidelines between CCF-RSMA and PCF-RSMA.

title: Learning to Construct Practical Agentic Systems
summary: Automated design and optimization of agentic LLM-based systems leads to sophisticated systems that substantially improve result quality over off-the-shelf agentic patterns. However, studies of fielded agentic systems show that production systems focus much more on issues such as simplicity, controllability, and predictability of inference costs. In this paper we propose principled approaches to designing and optimizing practical agentic systems. We describe an agent framework that enables designers to enforce modularity in agentic systems, by defining "pseudo-tools" that call LLMs recursively on a restricted context. Using this framework we hand-engineer agents for a diverse set of tasks, and show that relative to dynamically-planned workflows, hand-constructed fixed workflows are generally cheaper and more accurate. We then propose novel learning methods for the agentic components required by this framework, namely pseudo-tools and fixed workflows. These learning methods generally outperform hand-engineered agents. We also exploit the modularity of the framework to apply multi-objective optimization methods to jointly optimize cost and response quality and blend the results of multiple learning systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a modular agent framework using "pseudo-tools" for LLM-based systems, showing that hand-engineered fixed workflows are cheaper and more accurate than dynamic ones, and introduces learning methods that outperform hand-engineered agents, with multi-objective optimization for cost-quality tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Production agentic systems prioritize simplicity, controllability, and predictable inference costs, yet automated design methods often overlook these practical concerns. The paper aims to bridge this gap by developing principled approaches that balance optimization with real-world deployment needs.
AI.method: The authors propose an agent framework with "pseudo-tools" that recursively call LLMs on restricted contexts to enforce modularity. They hand-engineer agents using fixed workflows, then develop novel learning methods for pseudo-tools and fixed workflows, and apply multi-objective optimization to jointly optimize cost and response quality.
AI.result: Hand-constructed fixed workflows are generally cheaper and more accurate than dynamically-planned workflows. The proposed learning methods outperform hand-engineered agents, and multi-objective optimization successfully blends results from multiple learning systems to balance cost and quality.
AI.conclusion: Principled modular design and optimization of agentic systems can achieve both high performance and practical deployment considerations like cost efficiency and controllability, with learning methods further improving over manual engineering.

title: On Wednesdays, We Ask Questions: Optimizing "Active Listening" in Automated Legal Triage and Referral
comment: Working paper submitted as accepted to AIDA2J workshop at International Conference for AI and Law in Singapore, June 2026
summary: The FETCH classifier generates follow-up questions to help refine the best match for the applicant's legal problem, using a low-cost ensemble of LLMs. In this paper, we describe an expert attorney and LLM-assisted evaluation of the follow-up question approach in FETCH and show that while low-cost LLMs perform well at classification tasks, generating high-quality plain-language questions in this setting appears to require a more sophisticated and higher-cost model. Through discussion with legal intake workers, we propose a rubric for the evaluation of legal intake classification questions, and we find that prompt engineering alone is not enough to improve question quality for intake purposes. We also find that LLM-as-judge and human ratings diverge. We demonstrate that with the addition of a single high-cost model, GPT-5, the classifier can elicit relevant information from applicants for legal help, and that the questions lead to more accurate performance at classification tasks. We also find uneven fact elicitation across different categories, including domestic violence, at odds with family law screening protocols, suggesting the value of including dedicated screening panels for certain areas of law.

AI.tldr: The FETCH system uses LLMs to generate follow-up questions for legal intake, and this study evaluates its effectiveness. Low-cost LLMs are good at classification but poor at generating quality questions; a high-cost model (GPT-5) improves question quality and classification accuracy. Prompt engineering alone doesn't suffice, LLM-as-judge and human ratings diverge, and fact elicitation varies unevenly across legal categories, suggesting the need for specialized screening in certain areas like domestic violence.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the FETCH classifier's follow-up question generation approach for legal intake, assess whether low-cost LLMs can produce high-quality plain-language questions, and understand the limitations of prompt engineering and LLM-based evaluation in this domain.
AI.method: Expert attorney and LLM-assisted evaluation of FETCH's question generation; proposed a rubric for evaluating legal intake classification questions; compared low-cost vs. high-cost models (GPT-5); used LLM-as-judge and human ratings; analyzed fact elicitation across legal categories.
AI.result: Low-cost LLMs fail at generating high-quality questions despite performing well at classification. Prompt engineering alone is insufficient. LLM-as-judge ratings diverge from human ratings. GPT-5 significantly improves question quality and classification accuracy. Uneven fact elicitation across categories, especially domestic violence, misaligns with family law screening protocols.
AI.conclusion: High-cost models like GPT-5 are necessary for generating effective legal intake questions. Dedicated screening panels may be needed for certain areas like domestic violence due to uneven fact elicitation.

title: Towards 3D-Aware Video Diffusion Models: Render-Free Human Motion Control with Mesh Tokenization
summary: Diffusion models have shown remarkable success in video generation. However, whether such models are truly aware of the 3D structure underlying visual observations, rather than simply reproducing plausible 2D projections, remains an open question. In this work, we investigate this question through human motion control, a task that requires precise modelling of 3D human geometry, motion, camera viewpoint, and scene context. Unlike prior methods that rely on rendered 2D motion guidance videos, we propose a render-free framework that conditions video generation directly on compressed 3D human mesh tokens. This representation preserves full 3D geometric information while enabling a unified token-based generation pipeline that processes video tokens jointly with motion tokens in a DiT-based architecture. This design requires the model to reason jointly about appearance, 3D structure, and camera viewpoint during video generation. Experimental results demonstrate strong performance on human motion control benchmarks, while reducing artifacts induced by view-dependent 2D guidance and trajectory-pose mismatches during editing. These findings suggest that video diffusion models, when equipped with mesh tokenization, can better capture complex 3D human structures and their interactions with the surrounding environment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a render-free framework using compressed 3D human mesh tokens to condition video diffusion models, demonstrating improved 3D awareness and human motion control compared to methods relying on 2D motion guidance.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether video diffusion models are truly aware of 3D structure rather than just reproducing plausible 2D projections, through the lens of human motion control.
AI.method: A render-free framework that conditions video generation on compressed 3D human mesh tokens, using a DiT-based architecture that jointly processes video tokens with motion tokens.
AI.result: Strong performance on human motion control benchmarks with reduced artifacts from view-dependent 2D guidance and trajectory-pose mismatches.
AI.conclusion: Video diffusion models equipped with mesh tokenization can better capture complex 3D human structures and their interactions with the environment.

title: HOIST: Humanoid Optimization with Imitation and Sample-efficient Tuning for Manipulating Suspended Loads
summary: Manipulating suspended payloads with humanoid robots is challenging because the robot can only influence an underactuated, oscillatory load through whole-body motion and intermittent contact. Imitation learning provides safe initial behavior but does not directly optimize final placement, while reinforcement learning from scratch is unsafe and sample-inefficient on real humanoids. We present HOIST-Humanoid Optimized with Imitation and Sample-efficient Tuning for manipulating suspended loads. HOIST first finetunes a high-level vision-language-action (VLA) policy from virtual-reality (VR) teleoperation demonstrations and executes its commands through a whole-body controller. It then uses VLA rollouts and iterative batched RL to improve placement accuracy and stopping behavior. Experiments in simulation and on a real humanoid show that HOIST improves over imitation-only and additional-demonstration baselines; compared with pure VLA rollouts, HOIST reduces translational placement error by 19.9 cm and raw angular error by 3.56 degrees, demonstrating the potential of humanoids for underactuated material-handling tasks.

AI.tldr: HOIST combines imitation learning from VR teleoperation with iterative batched RL to enable humanoid robots to accurately manipulate suspended, underactuated payloads, reducing placement error by 19.9 cm and angular error by 3.56° over pure VLA rollouts.
AI.motivation: Manipulating suspended payloads with humanoids is challenging due to underactuated, oscillatory dynamics. Imitation learning alone doesn't optimize final placement, and RL from scratch is unsafe and sample-inefficient on real humanoids.
AI.method: HOIST fine-tunes a high-level vision-language-action (VLA) policy from VR teleoperation demonstrations, executes commands via a whole-body controller, then uses VLA rollouts and iterative batched RL to improve placement accuracy and stopping behavior.
AI.result: HOIST reduces translational placement error by 19.9 cm and raw angular error by 3.56° compared to pure VLA rollouts, and outperforms imitation-only and additional-demonstration baselines in both simulation and real humanoid experiments.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates the potential of combining imitation learning with sample-efficient RL for safe, accurate humanoid control in underactuated material-handling tasks.

title: Advances in Neural 3D Mesh Texturing: A Survey
comment: Eurographics STAR (Computer Graphics Forum), 2026. Project Page: https://sairajk.github.io/neural-mesh-texturing/
summary: Texturing 3D meshes plays a vital role in determining the visual realism of digital objects and scenes. Although recent generative 3D approaches based on Neural Radiance Fields and Gaussian Splatting can produce textured assets directly, polygonal meshes remain the core representation across modeling, animation, visual effects, and gaming pipelines. Neural 3D mesh texturing therefore continues to be an essential and active area of research. In this survey, we present a comprehensive review of recent advances in neural 3D mesh texturing, covering methods for texture synthesis, transfer, and completion. We first summarize key foundations in mesh geometry, texture mapping, differentiable rendering, and neural generative models, and then organize the literature into a unified taxonomy spanning early GAN-based methods to modern diffusion-based pipelines. We further analyze common architectures and supervision strategies, review datasets and evaluation protocols, and discuss emerging applications, practical/commercial systems, and open challenges. Together, these insights provide a structured perspective on the current landscape and help guide future developments in learning-based 3D mesh texturing.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of neural 3D mesh texturing methods, covering GAN-based to diffusion-based approaches, with analysis of architectures, datasets, evaluation protocols, and open challenges.
AI.motivation: Texturing 3D meshes is critical for visual realism in digital objects, and while newer 3D representations exist, polygonal meshes remain the core format across industry pipelines, making neural mesh texturing an essential research area.
AI.method: The paper provides a structured taxonomy from early GAN-based methods to modern diffusion-based pipelines, summarizing foundations in mesh geometry, texture mapping, differentiable rendering, and neural generative models, along with analysis of architectures, supervision strategies, datasets, and evaluation protocols.
AI.result: A unified organization of the literature on neural 3D mesh texturing covering texture synthesis, transfer, and completion, with insights into common architectures, supervision strategies, and evaluation approaches.
AI.conclusion: The survey offers a structured perspective on the current landscape of learning-based 3D mesh texturing and helps guide future research directions by discussing emerging applications, commercial systems, and open challenges.

title: MAC Performance and Algorithmic Optimization in Matrix Multiplication Workloads
summary: Matrix multiplication is a fundamental computational kernel underlying a wide range of real-world applications, including machine learning, scientific computing, signal processing, and computer graphics. Its performance directly impacts the efficiency, scalability, and energy consumption of modern computing systems. This paper presents a comparative analysis of several matrix multiplication algorithms implemented in software and examined in the context of their hardware execution characteristics. Naive, NumPy, Strassen, and Winograd algorithms are evaluated based on execution time, user time, and CPU time across increasing matrix sizes. The performance metrics reveal computational bottlenecks and highlight the benefits of algorithmic optimizations. Furthermore, the study investigates the mathematical operations underlying each algorithm and analyzes how matrix dimensions influence MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) behavior and overall computational efficiency in the hardware domain. The results provide a performance benchmark and contribute to understanding how algorithmic choices interact with modern computing architectures for applications in computer architecture, data science, and real-time embedded systems.

AI.tldr: This paper compares Naive, NumPy, Strassen, and Winograd matrix multiplication algorithms on execution time, user time, and CPU time across various matrix sizes, analyzing their mathematical operations, MAC behavior, and hardware execution characteristics to benchmark performance and understand algorithm-architecture interactions.
AI.motivation: Matrix multiplication is a critical kernel in ML, scientific computing, signal processing, and graphics, and its performance directly affects system efficiency, scalability, and energy consumption. Understanding how algorithmic choices interact with hardware is essential.
AI.method: Implements and evaluates four matrix multiplication algorithms (Naive, NumPy, Strassen, Winograd) on increasing matrix sizes, measuring execution time, user time, and CPU time, while analyzing mathematical operations and MAC behavior.
AI.result: Performance metrics reveal computational bottlenecks and demonstrate the benefits of algorithmic optimizations. Matrix dimensions significantly influence MAC behavior and overall computational efficiency in hardware.
AI.conclusion: Provides a performance benchmark and contributes insights into how algorithmic choices interact with modern computing architectures, with applications in computer architecture, data science, and real-time embedded systems.

title: BAGEN: Are LLM Agents Budget-Aware?
summary: While agents are increasingly spending more resources, today agent cost is mostly measured only after execution. A Budget-Aware Agent (BAGEN) should treat budget as an active control signal, rather than a passive cost metric. We first systematically define budget estimation as internal budgets (from agent computation) and external budgets (from agent actions). We then formalize budget-awareness as progressive interval estimation: at each step of a plan, an agent should predict an upper and lower bound on remaining budget, and alert when completion is unlikely. Scoring with a rollout-replay protocol, we find consistent failure patterns on four environments and five frontier agents: (1) strong agents do not necessarily have strong budget-awareness, with correlation r=0.35. (2) frontier models are consistently over-optimistic, continue spending on tasks that are unlikely to succeed, instead of alerting the user early. (3) budget-aware signal is actionable and trainable. Early stop saves 28-64% tokens on failed trajectories, and SFT+RL strengthens early stop and alert behavior. (4) precise interval calibration remains challenging, with interval coverage capping at 47% after SFT+RL. Project page: https://ragen-ai.github.io/bagen/

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Budget-Aware Agent (BAGEN), proposing that agents should treat budget as an active control signal. It defines internal/external budgets, formalizes budget-awareness as progressive interval estimation, and finds that frontier agents are over-optimistic and lack budget-awareness (correlation r=0.35). Early stopping saves 28-64% tokens, but interval calibration remains challenging with only 47% coverage after SFT+RL.
AI.motivation: Current agent cost measurement is only post-hoc (after execution), lacking active budget control. The authors aim to make budget an active control signal rather than a passive metric.
AI.method: (1) Systematically define internal (computation) and external (actions) budgets. (2) Formalize budget-awareness as progressive interval estimation (upper/lower bounds on remaining budget). (3) Use rollout-replay scoring protocol. (4) Evaluate on 4 environments and 5 frontier agents.
AI.result: (1) Weak correlation (r=0.35) between agent capability and budget-awareness. (2) Frontier models are over-optimistic, failing to alert early. (3) Early stop saves 28-64% tokens on failed trajectories. (4) SFT+RL improves alert/stop behavior but interval coverage only reaches 47%.
AI.conclusion: Budget-awareness is actionable and trainable, but precise interval calibration remains a significant challenge. The approach enables significant token savings on failing trajectories.

title: Evaluating Bivariate Causal Statements Based on Mutual Compatibility
summary: For many real-world systems, causal ground truth is difficult to obtain, making claims about causal effects hard to assess. We develop methods for evaluating collections of $\binom{n}{2}$ bivariate causal statements over a set of $n$ variables. In the setting of acyclic linear statements, any such collection can be extended to a unique multivariate causal model, but we argue that this induced model is implausible if it imposes substantial additional confounding to explain observed correlations. We introduce a compatibility score that quantifies this notion of plausibility, notably without relying on the faithfulness assumption. Additionally, we define an incompatibility score for purely graphical bivariate causal statements, based on global consistency constraints that are derived from acyclicity and faithfulness assumptions. We give theoretical and empirical evidence that both scores can successfully distinguish correct from incorrect causal statements in generic settings. Moreover, we demonstrate the practical applicability of our methods by analyzing causal claims made by large language models. Our work aims to provide a foundation for assessing the reliability of causal information derived from human experts or artificial intelligence in settings where alternative forms of validation are unavailable.

AI.tldr: The paper develops two scores (compatibility and incompatibility) to evaluate collections of bivariate causal statements without requiring ground truth, and demonstrates their effectiveness on LLM-generated causal claims.
AI.motivation: Causal ground truth is often unavailable in real-world systems, making it difficult to assess the validity of causal claims from human experts or AI.
AI.method: The authors propose a compatibility score based on how much additional confounding is needed to explain observed correlations in acyclic linear models, and an incompatibility score for graphical statements based on global consistency constraints from acyclicity/faithfulness.
AI.result: Both scores successfully distinguish correct from incorrect causal statements in generic settings, and are practically applicable to analyzing causal claims from large language models.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundation for evaluating causal information reliability without external validation, useful for both human experts and AI-generated causal knowledge.

title: Per-Group Error, Not Total MSE: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language-Action Models for 11-DoF Mobile Manipulation
comment: 4 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables. Accepted as poster at ICRA 2026 Workshop "From Data to Decisions: VLA Pipelines for Real Robots". Code: [https://github.com/paumontagut/per-group-mse-vla](https://github.com/paumontagut/per-group-mse-vla)
summary:
Fine-tuning Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for mobile manipulators with heterogeneous joint spaces can produce a counterintuitive result: the checkpoint with the lowest aggregate MSE is not the one that performs best on the real robot. We argue this is a predictable consequence of collapsing heterogeneous joint groups (arm, gripper, head, wheeled base) into a single metric, where easy-to-predict joints can mask joints that still fail.
  We fine-tune SmolVLA (450M, action-expert only) on the 11-DoF Toyota HSR and compare it against $π_{0.5}$ (3.3B), a stronger pretrained baseline. Per-group analysis exposes two patterns: in SmolVLA, the mobile base converges slowest and limits overall performance. In expert-only fine-tuning of $π_{0.5}$ (training only the action head, backbone frozen), total MSE drops below the baseline but arm accuracy degrades. On 60 real-robot trials (20 per model), $π_{0.5}$ 80k (4.0/4) significantly outperforms both fine-tuned variants (expert-only 3k: 3.75/4; HSR-SmolVLA: 3.5/4; Mann-Whitney $p \leq 0.010$), despite expert-only 3k having the lowest total MSE. This separation is most consistent with the offline arm-group error, not total MSE or base-group error. We conclude that per-group error is a more reliable signal than total MSE for checkpoint selection on robots with heterogeneous action spaces. Code: https://github.com/paumontagut/per-group-mse-vla

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning VLA models on robots with heterogeneous joint spaces (arm, gripper, head, base) shows that total MSE is misleading: easy-to-predict joints mask failing ones. Per-group error, especially arm error, is a better predictor of real-robot performance than aggregate MSE for checkpoint selection.
AI.motivation: Standard aggregate MSE is unreliable for evaluating VLA models on mobile manipulators because it collapses heterogeneous joint groups (arm, gripper, head, wheeled base) into a single metric, allowing easy-to-predict joints to mask poorly performing joints that cause real-robot failures.
AI.method: Fine-tuned SmolVLA (450M, action-expert only) and π₀.₅ (3.3B, expert-only head) on the 11-DoF Toyota HSR. Compared total MSE vs. per-group MSE against real-robot performance across 60 trials (20 per model).
AI.result: π₀.₅ 80k scored 4.0/4 on real robot, significantly outperforming expert-only 3k (3.75/4) and HSR-SmolVLA (3.5/4) despite expert-only 3k having the lowest total MSE. Per-group arm error best predicted real performance.
AI.conclusion: Per-group error is a more reliable signal than total MSE for checkpoint selection on robots with heterogeneous action spaces. Easy-to-predict joints (e.g., base) can mask hard ones (e.g., arm) in aggregate metrics.

title: Geodesics with Unified Tangent-constrained Priors and Curvature Regularization
summary: Curvature-penalized geodesic models have proven their effectiveness in image segmentation by computing globally optimal curves. Unfortunately, these models remain susceptible to shortcuts when delineating objects with complex shapes and image intensity distributions, as they lack mechanisms to enforce shape-aware tangent constraints. To address this limitation, we propose a unified geodesic framework that integrates tangent-constrained priors with curvature penalization. The key idea is to formulate tangent admissibility directly within the orientation-lifted space, where path tangents are restricted to spatially varying angular sectors derived from intrinsic shape representatives (ISR) such as skeletons or interior landmarks. This formulation gives rise to a family of tangent-constrained Finslerian metrics, extending the classical curvature-penalized geodesic models while enforcing mandatory tangent constraints. The resulting Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) partial differential equations (PDEs) admit efficient numerical solutions via variants of the fast marching method, preserving the single-pass computational complexity. Experiments on synthetic, natural, and medical images demonstrate that the proposed geodesic framework indeed improves robustness against weak boundaries and topological shortcuts, yielding segmentation results with enhanced shape fidelity compared to existing geodesic models.

AI.tldr: A unified geodesic framework integrating tangent-constrained priors with curvature penalization to improve segmentation robustness against shortcuts and weak boundaries.
AI.motivation: Curvature-penalized geodesic models suffer from shortcuts when segmenting complex shapes with varied intensity distributions, lacking shape-aware tangent constraints.
AI.method: Formulating tangent admissibility in orientation-lifted space with spatially varying angular sectors from intrinsic shape representatives, creating tangent-constrained Finslerian metrics solved via HJB PDEs with fast marching.
AI.result: Improved robustness against weak boundaries and topological shortcuts, with enhanced shape fidelity on synthetic, natural, and medical images compared to existing geodesic models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tangent-constrained curvature-penalized geodesic framework effectively addresses shortcut problems while preserving single-pass computational efficiency.

title: IO Pad Integrity in Energy-Efficient Neuromorphic Chips
summary: Neuromorphic computing relies on low-power, high-reliability hardware, yet the integrity of input/output pads (IOPADs) remains an underexplored factor affecting system performance. This chapter examines the role of IOPAD integrity in neuromorphic VLSI design and connects algorithmic development with practical hardware implementation. While much attention has been given to spiking neural networks (SNNs) and ultra-low-power core logic, the electrical and functional robustness of the I/O interface is equally critical for ensuring signal fidelity and minimizing energy consumption. We review the structure and function of IOPADs, outline their influence on power, performance, and reliability, and discuss design trade-offs involving pad libraries, pad ring architectures, and bonding strategies. The chapter also introduces the fundamentals of SNNs and summarizes the digital hardware design flow from behavioral description to physical layout. Physical implementation considerations are highlighted using the SkyWater 130 nm CMOS process as a practical platform for neuromorphic prototyping. Real-world examples illustrate how early-stage I/O planning can prevent redesign, reduce yield loss, and improve overall system efficiency. This work emphasizes that IOPAD integrity is a key enabler of scalable, energy-efficient neuromorphic systems.

AI.tldr: This chapter highlights the critical but often overlooked role of IOPAD integrity in neuromorphic VLSI design, bridging algorithmic SNN development with practical hardware implementation.
