We present LastAct, a trajectory-centric framework for streaming smart-home HAR that targets the most recent activity under mixed windows while explicitly modeling spatial structure. LastAct projects sensor events onto the home floorplan to form a layout-aligned trajectory image sequence that preserves spatial continuity. A lightweight gate identifies contaminated windows, and a boundary localizer estimates the last transition to enable boundary-guided masking that emphasizes post-boundary evidence and suppresses stale context. For efficiency, we reuse a precomputed layout-aligned template cache to avoid repeated rendering. Empirically, across four public smart-home datasets under near-realistic mixed-activity protocols, LastAct achieves competitive or superior performance on pure windows and yields substantial Macro-F1 gains on cross/mixed windows, demonstrating improved robustness under near-realistic sliding-window regimes.

AI.tldr: LastAct is a trajectory-centric framework for streaming smart-home HAR that uses floorplan-aligned trajectory images, a gate to detect contaminated windows, and boundary-guided masking to handle mixed-activity windows caused by sliding-window inference.
AI.motivation: Existing HAR models assume pre-segmented instances, but in real streaming settings, sliding windows produce boundary contamination (mixed activities). Spatial context from sensor IDs is also underutilized.
AI.method: 1) Projects sensor events onto a floorplan to create layout-aligned trajectory image sequences. 2) Uses a lightweight gate to detect contaminated windows. 3) Applies a boundary localizer to estimate the last activity transition. 4) Employs boundary-guided masking to emphasize post-boundary evidence. 5) Reuses a precomputed template cache for efficiency.
AI.result: On four public smart-home datasets under mixed-activity protocols, LastAct achieves competitive/ superior performance on pure windows and substantial Macro-F1 gains on cross/mixed windows.
AI.conclusion: LastAct demonstrates improved robustness for streaming HAR under near-realistic sliding-window regimes by explicitly handling boundary contamination and leveraging spatial structure.

title: Beyond the RF Paradigm: Rydberg Atomic Receivers for Next-Generation IoT
summary: Next-generation Internet-of-Things (IoT) is evolving toward a ubiquitous, ultra-low-power, and multi-band heterogeneous networking paradigm that seamlessly integrates terrestrial, non-terrestrial, and ambient devices. This vision places unprecedented demands on conventional radio frequency (RF) receivers, whose fundamental bottlenecks in sensitivity, power consumption, coverage, and multi-band operation are rooted in the RF antenna. To tackle these issues, we show that the quantum properties of Rydberg atomic quantum receivers (RAQRs), including ultra-high sensitivity, broad frequency agility, and diverse reception modalities, provide a physically distinct receiver-side path that replaces the conventional antenna-and-low-noise-amplifier chain. Using LoRa, narrowband IoT, and ambient IoT as case studies, this article shows that RAQRs deliver significant gains in weak-uplink, low-power, and battery-free regimes. A stochastic-geometry analysis in cellular and cell-free architectures then maps these device-level gains onto network coverage, where the RAQR retains roughly a 4 dB half-coverage advantage over the RF receiver in sparse deployments at \(λ\sim 10^{-5}~{\mathrm m}^{-2}\), with the gain eroded as device density grows. The open challenges are presented to stand between current RAQR prototypes and deployable IoT infrastructure.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Rydberg atomic quantum receivers (RAQRs) as a replacement for conventional RF antenna-and-LNA chains in next-generation IoT networks. RAQRs offer ultra-high sensitivity, broad frequency agility, and low-power operation, showing significant gains in weak-uplink, battery-free regimes, and up to ~4 dB half-coverage advantage in sparse deployments, though challenges remain for practical infrastructure deployment.
AI.motivation: Next-generation IoT requires ubiquitous, ultra-low-power, multi-band heterogeneous networking (terrestrial, non-terrestrial, ambient). Conventional RF receivers face fundamental bottlenecks in sensitivity, power consumption, coverage, and multi-band operation rooted in the RF antenna. This paper seeks to address these issues by leveraging quantum properties of Rydberg atomic quantum receivers.
AI.method: The paper analyzes RAQRs as a physically distinct receiver-side path that replaces conventional antenna-and-LNA chains. It uses LoRa, narrowband IoT, and ambient IoT as case studies, and employs stochastic-geometry analysis in both cellular and cell-free architectures to map device-level gains to network coverage.
AI.result: RAQRs deliver significant gains in weak-uplink, low-power, and battery-free regimes. In sparse deployments (λ~10⁻⁵ m⁻²), RAQR retains roughly a 4 dB half-coverage advantage over RF receivers. This gain diminishes as device density increases.
AI.conclusion: RAQRs provide a promising quantum-based alternative to conventional RF receivers for next-generation IoT, with notable advantages in sensitivity, power efficiency, and coverage for sparse deployments. However, open challenges persist between current prototypes and deployable IoT infrastructure.

title: When Softmax Fails at the Top: Extreme Value Corrections for InfoNCE
summary: InfoNCE is the standard contrastive learning objective, but its softmax form is not only a computational convenience: it also encodes a statistical assumption about how the top-scoring example is selected. Using extreme value theory, we show that this assumption is often misaligned with the normalized embedding setting used in modern contrastive learning. Motivated by this mismatch, we propose \textsc{WEINCE}, a simple modification of InfoNCE that uses anchor-wise online batch statistics to blend the usual softmax logits with an endpoint shortfall correction, adding no trainable parameters. Across five vision benchmarks, \textsc{WEINCE} yields consistent improvements in frozen-feature evaluation. These results show that a more faithful statistical treatment of hard negatives can improve contrastive objectives.

AI.tldr: InfoNCE has a statistical mismatch with normalized embeddings in contrastive learning, so the authors propose WEINCE—a simple modification using anchor-wise online batch statistics and endpoint shortfall correction—that improves frozen-feature evaluation across five vision benchmarks without adding parameters.
AI.motivation: InfoNCE's softmax form encodes a statistical assumption about top-scoring example selection that is misaligned with normalized embedding settings in modern contrastive learning.
AI.method: WEINCE modifies InfoNCE by blending standard softmax logits with an endpoint shortfall correction using anchor-wise online batch statistics, requiring no additional trainable parameters.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in frozen-feature evaluation across five vision benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A more faithful statistical treatment of hard negatives, grounded in extreme value theory, can improve contrastive learning objectives.

title: Weak Critics Make Strong Learners: On-Policy Critique Distillation for Scalable Oversight
summary: As large language models become stronger, weak supervisors may fail to provide reliable labels, preferences, or final judgments for complex outputs, limiting both weak-to-strong generalization and scalable oversight. We study a more tractable form of weak supervision: using a weak model as a critic rather than as a labeler or judge. Instead of solving the task or selecting the correct answer, the weak critic only needs to provide a non-misleading revision direction that helps the strong model better use its own knowledge. We call this setting *weak-critic strong oversight*. We first show that weak critiques can improve frozen strong models at inference time, and that critique quality is key to this improvement. We then propose progressive on-policy critique distillation (**OPCD**), which filters high-quality critiques and distills critic-guided behavior into the strong model through adaptive self-teacher signals. Experiments on reasoning and alignment benchmarks show that our method improves strong models over training epochs, suggesting an effective path for scalable oversight with weak supervision.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using weak models as critics (not labelers) to provide revision directions for strong models, introducing "weak-critic strong oversight" and a method called progressive on-policy critique distillation (OPCD) that improves strong models through filtered critiques and adaptive self-teacher signals.
AI.motivation: Weak supervisors (e.g., smaller or less capable models) fail to provide reliable labels or judgments for complex outputs from stronger models, limiting weak-to-strong generalization and scalable oversight. The authors aim to find a more tractable form of weak supervision.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) using a weak model as a critic to provide non-misleading revision directions rather than solving tasks, and (2) progressive on-policy critique distillation (OPCD), which filters high-quality critiques and distills critic-guided behavior into the strong model via adaptive self-teacher signals.
AI.result: Weak critiques improve frozen strong models at inference time, with critique quality being key. OPCD further improves strong models over training epochs on reasoning and alignment benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Weak-critic strong oversight offers an effective and scalable path for supervising strong models under weak supervision, suggesting practical directions for scalable oversight.

title: SoFiE: Soft Finger Exoskeleton for Intelligent Grasping
summary: Soft wearable robotic systems have emerged as a promising solution for assisting individuals with reduced hand function. This paper presents SoFiE, a modular soft finger exoskeleton designed to assist index-finger flexion during grasping tasks. The proposed system is primarily fabricated using 3D-printed flexible materials, enabling a lightweight, low-profile, and modular design. Actuation is achieved through a tendon-driven mechanism powered by a compact DC motor, while passive extension is provided by a compliant conductive spring. This element, termed StretchSense, also functions as a proprioceptive sensor by exhibiting resistance changes under deformation. Furthermore, a novel tactile sensing approach, MagSense, is introduced, using a magnet and magnetometer pair embedded in a soft fingertip structure to estimate contact force and object compliance. The system is fully untethered and controlled by an embedded microcontroller. In addition, actuator-level sensing through motor encoder feedback enables estimation of the system state, providing a foundation for safe and adaptive control strategies. Experimental validation demonstrates the capability of the system to provide reliable pose estimation, distinguish between materials with different stiffness, and generate distinct sensor signatures across different grasping tasks. This paper details the design, fabrication, and sensing concepts of the proposed exoskeleton as a proof of concept toward modular, soft, and assistive wearable robotics.

AI.tldr: SoFiE is a modular soft finger exoskeleton using tendon-driven actuation, a conductive spring for proprioception (StretchSense), and magnetic tactile sensing (MagSense) for contact estimation, validated for pose estimation and material discrimination.
AI.motivation: To assist individuals with reduced hand function using a lightweight, untethered, and modular soft wearable robotic system.
AI.method: 3D-printed flexible materials, tendon-driven DC motor actuation, StretchSense (conductive spring for proprioception), MagSense (magnet-magnetometer for tactile sensing), and motor encoder feedback for state estimation.
AI.result: Reliable pose estimation, ability to distinguish materials with different stiffness, and distinct sensor signatures across various grasping tasks.
AI.conclusion: SoFiE serves as a proof of concept for modular, soft, and assistive wearable robotics, laying groundwork for safe and adaptive control strategies.

title: The Harsh Truth: Segment-Level Analysis of Harsh Driving Events in Milan Using Large-Scale Telematics, Street Networks, and Google Street View
summary: Police-reported crash statistics remain the standard input for urban road-safety assessment, but their incompleteness and reporting lag limit their usefulness for timely, fine-grained intervention design. Harsh acceleration and braking events are widely used as surrogate safety indicators, but have so far been studied only in comparatively small urban samples. This study analyses harsh events across the urban road network of Milan, combining high-resolution telematics from more than 4.2 million vehicles equipped with On-Board Units, segment-level traffic metrics from TomTom, street-network and infrastructure attributes from OpenStreetMap, and visual streetscape features extracted from Google Street View via semantic segmentation using a OneFormer model. We employ an analytical framework combining non-parametric Mann--Whitney U tests of segment-feature distributions between high- and low-harshness groups with supervised machine-learning regressors. We find that, once exposure is controlled for, wider carriageways, crossings and transit stops, and more open visual fields (higher sky- and road-pixel proportions) are associated with higher harsh-event intensity, while denser built frontage is associated with lower intensity. Finally, the cycling-infrastructure case study identifies a gradient in harsh-event intensity across facility types: markings-only cycle lanes are associated with a 19.5% higher harshness score, and mixed-traffic configurations with an 11.5% higher score, relative to physically separated cycle paths, conditional on the included controls. These results support context-specific rather than uniform urban-safety interventions and illustrate how large-scale telematics combined with open geospatial and visual data can inform Vision Zero decision-making at the metropolitan scale.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes harsh driving events across Milan's urban road network using telematics from 4.2M+ vehicles, finding that wider roads, crossings, transit stops, and open visual fields are linked to higher harsh-event intensity, while dense built frontage reduces it. Physically separated cycle paths are safer than markings-only or mixed-traffic facilities.
AI.motivation: Police crash data is incomplete and lagging, limiting timely safety interventions. Harsh acceleration/braking events are promising surrogate safety indicators but have only been studied in small urban samples so far.
AI.method: Combined high-resolution telematics (4.2M+ vehicles), TomTraffic metrics, OpenStreetMap infrastructure data, and Google Street View visual features (via OneFormer semantic segmentation). Used Mann-Whitney U tests and supervised ML regressors to analyze relationships between road features and harsh-event intensity.
AI.result: Wider carriageways, crossings, transit stops, and open visual fields (sky/road) increase harsh-event intensity; dense built frontage reduces it. Cycle lanes (markings-only) associated with 19.5% higher harshness, mixed-traffic with 11.5% higher, compared to physically separated cycle paths.
AI.conclusion: Urban safety interventions should be context-specific rather than uniform. Large-scale telematics combined with open geospatial and visual data can effectively inform Vision Zero decision-making at metropolitan scale.

title: A Communication-Centric 6G-LLM Architecture for Scalable Tactical Autonomous Defense Vehicle Networks
comment: 10 pages, accepted in IEEE Network Magazine
summary: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and emerging 6G networks introduces new opportunities for scalable coordination in tactical autonomous vehicle systems. This paper proposes a communication-centric hierarchical architecture for Tactical Autonomous Defense Vehicle Networks (TADVNs) that models the integration of edge-assisted Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning with 6G-enabled connectivity and semantic communication. The framework is designed to improve coordination efficiency, reduce communication overhead, and enhance latency resilience under increasing fleet-scale operation. Unlike conventional task-specific AI pipelines that rely on structured feature processing and rule-based coordination, the proposed approach incorporates semantic abstraction and context-aware decision support within a layered edge-cloud communication architecture. We evaluate communication and coordination performance via Monte Carlo simulations across fleet sizes of 5-30 vehicles under contested network conditions. Results indicate that at a 30-vehicle scale, the 6G-LLM configuration achieves 75.2% latency reduction (29.1 ms vs. 117.5 ms), a 68.7 percentage point increase in mission success rate (82.9% vs. 14.2%), and an 88.6% reduction in communication overhead compared to a 5G-based conventional AI baseline. These findings demonstrate measurable benefits in coordination and communication when semantic reasoning is combined with low-latency 6G connectivity.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a 6G-enabled hierarchical architecture integrating LLM reasoning with semantic communication for Tactical Autonomous Defense Vehicle Networks (TADVNs), achieving significant latency reduction (75.2%), mission success improvement (68.7 percentage points), and communication overhead reduction (88.6%) over conventional 5G-based AI approaches in Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.motivation: To overcome scalability, coordination efficiency, and communication overhead limitations of conventional task-specific AI pipelines and rule-based coordination in tactical autonomous vehicle systems, especially under contested network conditions.
AI.method: A communication-centric hierarchical architecture combining edge-assisted LLM reasoning with 6G connectivity and semantic communication, evaluated via Monte Carlo simulations across fleet sizes of 5-30 vehicles.
AI.result: At 30-vehicle scale: 75.2% latency reduction (29.1 ms vs. 117.5 ms), 68.7 percentage point increase in mission success rate (82.9% vs. 14.2%), and 88.6% reduction in communication overhead compared to 5G-based conventional AI baseline.
AI.conclusion: Semantic reasoning combined with low-latency 6G connectivity yields measurable benefits in coordination efficiency and communication performance for tactical autonomous vehicle networks.

title: Inner Product Aware Quantization: Provably Fast, Accurate, and Adaptive Algorithms
summary:
Quantization is a fundamental tool used to compress datasets, neural network weights, and memory usage in a range of computational tasks. Many downstream applications of vector quantization perform inner products with arbitrary inputs. This motivates the study of inner product aware quantization schemes that approximately preserve inner products with unseen vectors -- in contrast to simply minimizing the mean-squared error.
  In this work, we formulate objectives that capture natural desiderata and develop adaptive and unbiased quantization methods that approximately preserve inner products with worst-case and average-case inputs. An analysis of these objectives shows a tight connection with the well-studied notion of Adaptive Stochastic Quantization (ASQ).
  We develop provably fast exact and approximate algorithms for our objectives. Our theoretical results inspire efficient practical algorithms that perform well across a variety of workload distributions. They also lead to practical algorithms for standard ASQ which are 2-10$\times$ faster than prior state-of-the-art methods while maintaining quality. These theoretical and empirical results contribute towards making adaptive quantization techniques more efficient and tractable in practical settings.

AI.tldr: This paper develops adaptive quantization methods that preserve inner products with unseen vectors, showing connections to Adaptive Stochastic Quantization (ASQ). The proposed algorithms achieve 2-10× speedup over prior ASQ methods while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Many downstream applications of vector quantization perform inner products with arbitrary inputs, but existing quantization schemes focus on minimizing mean-squared error rather than preserving inner products with unseen vectors.
AI.method: The authors formulate objectives capturing inner product preservation desiderata, establish a tight connection with Adaptive Stochastic Quantization (ASQ), and develop provably fast exact and approximate algorithms for these objectives.
AI.result: The proposed practical algorithms achieve 2-10× speedup over prior state-of-the-art ASQ methods while maintaining quality across various workload distributions.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical and empirical results make adaptive quantization techniques more efficient and tractable in practical settings, particularly for inner product-aware applications.

title: SDR: Set-Distance Rewards for Radiology Report Generation
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has rapidly advanced reasoning in vision--language models. However, for chest X-ray report generation, the standard rewards (i.e. exact-match accuracy and step-level processes) are incompatible because the reports consist of unordered and orthogonal findings, rather than a causal reasoning chain. We address this gap with a set-based view: each report is split into sentences and embedded by a frozen sentence transformer, yielding unordered embedding sets. We propose the use of set-to-set distances between generated and reference embeddings as continuous, permutation-invariant rewards. Across two datasets and three vision--language models (Qwen3-VL-2B/4B, Gemma3-4B), post-training with set-to-set distance based rewards via GRPO consistently outperforms supervised fine-tuning and exact-match GRPO on all headline metrics (BERTScore, RadGraph F1 and CheXbert F1 by average \%6.80, \%7.82 and \%4.45 relative improvements respectively). The same set distances also enable test-time best-of-$N$ selection: scoring candidates by their distance to training-report embeddings outperforms random selection on our trained models as well as three closed-source LLMs (Mistral-Small, Gemini-2.5 Flash-Lite, GPT-4o-mini) with on average \%16.4 relative improvement on BERTScore. Used as a streaming signal, they support a more efficient form of test-time scaling: pruning low-scoring candidates mid-generation reduces generated tokens by over 50\% while preserving the Findings quality of full best-of-$N$ selection. Together these results establish set-distance rewards as a unified signal for both post-training and test-time scaling in chest X-ray report generation. Our code is publicly \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Set-Distance-Rewards-CXR-BFDA}{available}.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using set-to-set distance rewards (instead of exact-match rewards) for reinforcement learning in chest X-ray report generation, achieving consistent improvements across multiple models and enabling efficient test-time scaling through pruning.
AI.motivation: Standard reinforcement learning rewards (exact-match accuracy, step-level processes) are incompatible with chest X-ray report generation because reports consist of unordered and orthogonal findings rather than a causal reasoning chain.
AI.method: Each report is split into sentences and embedded via a frozen sentence transformer to create unordered embedding sets. Set-to-set distances between generated and reference embeddings are used as continuous, permutation-invariant rewards during GRPO post-training. The same distances are also used for test-time best-of-N selection and mid-generation pruning.
AI.result: Post-training with set-to-set distance rewards consistently outperforms supervised fine-tuning and exact-match GRPO on all headline metrics (BERTScore +6.80%, RadGraph F1 +7.82%, CheXbert F1 +4.45% relative). Test-time selection improves BERTScore by 16.4% relative. Mid-generation pruning reduces tokens by over 50% while preserving quality.
AI.conclusion: Set-distance rewards serve as a unified signal for both post-training and test-time scaling in chest X-ray report generation, addressing the incompatibility of standard RL rewards with this domain.

title: Literary Emotions in Motion: A Soft Robotics Installation for Tactile Storytelling
summary: Soft robotics is increasingly explored in artistic contexts, where tactile interaction provides audiences with embodied engagement beyond visual or auditory signals. This work presents an interactive installation that maps semantic emotion analysis of narrative text into variable stiffness of soft pneumatic modules. A natural language model identifies two dominant emotions from a predefined set of six, driving the inflation of seven hexagonally arranged soft actuators. The central actuator represents the primary emotion, while the surrounding ones express the secondary. We develop and mechanically characterize silicone actuators, called soft modules, featuring a thin membrane layer, demonstrating how this morphological control expands the achievable stiffness range while preserving simplicity and low-cost fabrication. A user study with ten participants further evaluates how multisensory coupling of stiffness and LEDs intensity influences emotional perception. The results suggest that stiffness modulation accompanied by color change can support emotionally meaningful and engaging tactile interaction in soft robotic installations.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an interactive installation where soft pneumatic modules with variable stiffness, driven by semantic emotion analysis of narrative text, create emotionally meaningful tactile interactions through hexagonally arranged actuators.
AI.motivation: To explore tactile interaction in artistic contexts by mapping emotional analysis of text to variable stiffness in soft robotics, enabling embodied engagement beyond visual/auditory signals.
AI.method: A natural language model identifies two dominant emotions (from six predefined categories) from narrative text, driving inflation of seven hexagonally arranged silicone soft actuators with thin membrane layers for variable stiffness control.
AI.result: User study (N=10) shows that stiffness modulation combined with LED color changes can support emotionally meaningful and engaging tactile interaction.
AI.conclusion: Multisensory coupling of stiffness and color can effectively convey emotions in soft robotic art installations, with the proposed morphological design expanding stiffness range while maintaining simple, low-cost fabrication.

title: StressDream: Steering Video World Models for Robust Policy Evaluation and Improvement
summary: Video world models (WMs) have shown promise for policy evaluation and improvement by imagining realistic future observations conditioned on ego-robot actions. While WMs can model distributions over futures, policy evaluation and improvement typically rely on nominal imaginations, which can miss high-impact outcomes of robot actions unless prohibitively many samples are drawn. To enable robust policy evaluation and improvement over WM imaginations, we propose StressDream, which steers imaginations toward high-impact yet plausible outcomes specified at inference time by optimizing the initial noise of diffusion-based WMs. However, optimizing high-dimensional noise is challenging: the optimization must reason about nuanced, scene-dependent target events in generated videos while avoiding out-of-distribution (OOD) noise that yields implausible imaginations. We address this with two complementary objectives: a semantic objective with a Vision-Language Model that provides informative gradients by reasoning about the generated video, and a plausibility objective that prevents the optimized noise from drifting OOD. With state-of-the-art video world models for autonomous driving and robotic manipulation, we show that StressDream effectively steers imaginations toward high-impact yet plausible outcomes specified by text at inference time, such as task failures, enabling robust policy evaluation and improvement by identifying actions whose plausible futures include undesirable outcomes. Video results are available at https://junwon.me/StressDream/.

AI.tldr: StressDream steers diffusion-based video world model imaginations toward high-impact, plausible outcomes (e.g., task failures) by optimizing initial noise with semantic (VLM-guided) and plausibility objectives, enabling robust policy evaluation and improvement.
AI.motivation: Video world models can imagine future outcomes but typically rely on nominal (average-case) sampling, which misses rare high-impact events (e.g., task failures) unless prohibitive numbers of samples are drawn. This limits robust policy evaluation and improvement.
AI.method: StressDream optimizes the initial noise of diffusion-based world models at inference time using two complementary objectives: (1) a semantic objective with a Vision-Language Model that provides informative gradients by reasoning about the generated video, and (2) a plausibility objective that prevents the optimized noise from drifting out-of-distribution, ensuring imaginations remain realistic.
AI.result: Applied to state-of-the-art video world models for autonomous driving and robotic manipulation, StressDream effectively steers imaginations toward high-impact yet plausible outcomes specified by text at inference time, such as task failures.
AI.conclusion: By enabling robust policy evaluation and improvement through identifying actions whose plausible futures include undesirable outcomes, StressDream demonstrates the value of steering world model imaginations beyond nominal samples for safety-critical decision-making.

title: Adaptive UAV Communications for URLLC: From Preplanned Designs to Real-Time Intelligence
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are emerging as a key enabler of next-generation wireless networks, particularly for applications that require ultra-reliable and low-latency communication (URLLC), such as emergency response, industrial automation, and autonomous systems. In these scenarios, maintaining reliable connectivity under strict transmission time constraints is challenging due to dynamic environments, mobility, and limited onboard energy. In particular, communication performance and energy are closely coupled with UAV movement, making trajectory design a critical component of system operation. Most existing approaches rely on offline joint communication and trajectory optimization, where the UAV trajectory and communication parameters are optimized prior to execution based on assumed system information. Although effective under ideal assumptions, such designs cannot adapt to real-time variations in user demand, channel conditions, or environmental disturbances, which are particularly critical in URLLC settings. To address these challenges, this article investigates model predictive control (MPC) as an adaptive framework for UAV-enabled communications. Using a receding-horizon strategy, MPC enables the UAV to continuously update its trajectory based on real-time information, improving reliability and robustness in dynamic environments. Representative application scenarios are discussed to highlight the role of MPC in UAV-enabled URLLC systems. Furthermore, a case study is presented to illustrate key design trade-offs and performance insights under finite blocklength-based URLLC transmission, followed by a discussion on open challenges and future research directions for practical and scalable MPC-enabled UAV communication systems.

AI.tldr: This paper explores Model Predictive Control (MPC) as an adaptive framework for UAV-enabled URLLC communications, addressing limitations of offline optimization approaches by enabling real-time trajectory updates for improved reliability in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Existing offline joint communication and trajectory optimization methods cannot adapt to real-time variations in user demand, channel conditions, or environmental disturbances, which are critical in URLLC settings.
AI.method: The paper investigates Model Predictive Control (MPC) using a receding-horizon strategy to continuously update UAV trajectories based on real-time information.
AI.result: A case study illustrating key design trade-offs and performance insights under finite blocklength-based URLLC transmission is presented.
AI.conclusion: MPC provides an effective adaptive framework for UAV-enabled URLLC communications, with open challenges remaining for practical and scalable implementation.

title: Accurate Large-sample Uncertainty Quantification using Stochastic Gradient Markov Chain Monte Carlo
summary: Tuning algorithms such as stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD) for approximate sampling and uncertainty quantification remains challenging, particularly in the practically relevant settings when the batch size is large or the model is misspecified. Existing theory that provides tuning guidance relies on continuous-time limits or strong statistical assumptions, which can become quantitatively inaccurate in these regimes. We address these shortcomings by proposing new discrete-time approximations to SG(L)D with and without momentum, which enables accurate predictions of the stationary covariance, iterate average covariance, and integrated autocorrelation time. Moreover, we prove quantitative, non-asymptotic error bounds showing that these estimates are sufficiently accurate for practical tuning and uncertainty quantification. Numerical experiments demonstrate that our theory yields improved tuning guidance across a range of models and data-generating distributions where existing approaches fail, including when using the $β$-divergence rather than log-loss to obtain statistically robust inferences.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes new discrete-time approximations for stochastic gradient algorithms (SGD/SGLD) that provide accurate predictions of key statistical quantities (stationary covariance, iterate average covariance, integrated autocorrelation time) with proven non-asymptotic error bounds, enabling better tuning guidance in challenging regimes like large batch sizes or model misspecification.
AI.motivation: Existing tuning theory for SGD/SGLD relies on continuous-time limits or strong statistical assumptions that become inaccurate when batch size is large or the model is misspecified, making practical tuning and uncertainty quantification difficult.
AI.method: Proposes new discrete-time approximations to SG(L)D with and without momentum, along with quantitative non-asymptotic error bounds showing the estimates are sufficiently accurate for practical tuning.
AI.result: The discrete-time approximations enable accurate predictions of stationary covariance, iterate average covariance, and integrated autocorrelation time. Numerical experiments show improved tuning guidance across diverse models and data-generating distributions where existing approaches fail, including when using β-divergence for robust inference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed discrete-time framework provides practically useful tuning guidance for stochastic gradient algorithms in regimes where continuous-time theory breaks down, with provable error guarantees.

title: Doing What They Say, Not What They Reason: Locating the Faithfulness Gap in LLM Agents
comment: submitted to COLM social simulation with LLM workshop
summary: Do LLM agents act on the reasoning they state? This question of process fidelity is central to using LLMs in social simulation, yet it is hard to measure where no reference for correct behavior exists. We study it in acontrolled setting, a Texas Poker simulator with a verifiable reference action for every decision by decomposing the faithfulness gap into two steps: reasoning-conclusion and conclusion-action. The two steps behave oppositely.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether LLM agents actually follow the reasoning they state, focusing on process fidelity in social simulations. Using Texas Poker as a controlled setting with verifiable reference actions, the authors decompose the faithfulness gap into reasoning-conclusion and conclusion-action steps, finding they behave oppositely.
AI.motivation: To understand whether LLM agents act on the reasoning they state (process fidelity), which is crucial for using LLMs in social simulation but difficult to measure without reference behavior.
AI.method: The study uses a Texas Poker simulator with verifiable reference actions, decomposing the faithfulness gap into two components: reasoning-conclusion and conclusion-action steps.
AI.result: The two steps (reasoning-conclusion and conclusion-action) behave oppositely, revealing a nuanced faithfulness gap in LLM agents.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents do not always act on the reasoning they state, and the faithfulness gap can be decomposed into opposing behavioral patterns between reasoning-to-conclusion and conclusion-to-action steps.

title: ROG-Grasp: Root-Oriented Geometry for Robotic Grasping and Placement
summary: Orientation-aware manipulation is essential in post-harvest agricultural processing, where produce must be grasped and placed in consistent configurations. This paper presents ROG-Grasp, a geometry-based robotic grasping and placement framework that estimates the produce orientation from root surface geometry using RGB-D perception. A YOLO-based root detector and point cloud plane fitting are used to infer the root normal, enabling stable grasp pose generation and orientation-constrained Cartesian motion planning. Experiments on tomatoes and onions demonstrate high success rates and stable execution time in both isolated and cluttered scenarios. Compared with vision-language-action (VLA) policies, the proposed method achieves more reliable and accurate grasp completion with faster execution. These results highlight the effectiveness of geometry-driven perception for practical orientation-controlled manipulation tasks. A video of our paper is available online https://youtu.be/Ir2UtGODdMo.

AI.tldr: ROG-Grasp is a geometry-based robotic grasping framework that uses RGB-D perception to estimate produce orientation from root surface geometry, enabling stable grasp and placement for post-harvest agricultural processing.
AI.motivation: Orientation-aware manipulation is essential in post-harvest agricultural processing where produce must be grasped and placed in consistent configurations, motivating the need for a reliable method to estimate and control produce orientation during robotic handling.
AI.method: The framework uses YOLO-based root detection, point cloud plane fitting to infer root normal, grasp pose generation, and orientation-constrained Cartesian motion planning with RGB-D perception.
AI.result: Experiments on tomatoes and onions show high success rates and stable execution time in both isolated and cluttered scenarios, outperforming VLA policies with more reliable grasp completion and faster execution.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-driven perception is effective for practical orientation-controlled manipulation tasks in agriculture, offering reliable performance without the complexity of vision-language-action models.

title: Hyperbolic and Evidence-Prioritized Experts for Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance on multimodal tasks through scaled architectures and extensive training. Recent studies introduce Mixture of Experts (MoE) into LVLMs for improved computational efficiency. However, existing MoE approaches treat visual and linguistic modalities with symmetric architectures, overlooking the inherent asymmetry in how these two modalities are processed. This asymmetry causes two critical issues. First, text and vision form hierarchical rather than parallel relationships, as text queries typically describe partial aspects of complete visual scenes. Euclidean expert space struggles to encode such containment structures. Second, language experts in deeper layers progressively shift from evidence-based processing to parametric memory dependence, losing grounding in the provided visual and linguistic information. To address these issues, we propose AsyMoE, a novel architecture that explicitly models this asymmetry through three specialized expert groups. Intra-modality experts handle modality-specific processing. Hyperbolic inter-modality experts capture hierarchical cross-modal relationships through negative curvature geometry. Evidence-priority language experts suppress parametric memory activation and maintain contextual grounding throughout network depth. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AsyMoE achieves consistent improvements over baseline methods, with average gains of 1.5\% over MoE variants and up to 3.8\% on hallucination-sensitive tasks. AsyMoE activates 25.45\% fewer parameters compared to dense models.

AI.tldr: AsyMoE introduces asymmetric expert grouping (intra-modality, hyperbolic inter-modality, evidence-priority language experts) to model the inherent asymmetry between vision and language in LVLMs, achieving up to 3.8% improvement on hallucination-sensitive tasks with 25.45% fewer activated parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing MoE approaches treat visual and linguistic modalities symmetrically, overlooking inherent asymmetry: (1) text hierarchically describes partial aspects of visual scenes (Euclidean space fails to capture these containment structures), and (2) language experts in deeper layers lose grounding by shifting from evidence-based processing to parametric memory dependence.
AI.method: AsyMoE introduces three specialized expert groups: (1) Intra-modality experts for modality-specific processing, (2) Hyperbolic inter-modality experts that use negative curvature geometry to capture hierarchical cross-modal relationships, and (3) Evidence-priority language experts that suppress parametric memory activation to maintain contextual grounding throughout network depth.
AI.result: AsyMoE achieves average gains of 1.5% over MoE variants and up to 3.8% on hallucination-sensitive tasks, while activating 25.45% fewer parameters compared to dense models.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the asymmetric relationship between vision and language with specialized expert groups improves both computational efficiency and performance, particularly for hallucination-prone scenarios.

title: Power consumption and spectral efficiency analysis for uplink analog radio-over-fiber
summary: Radio-over-fiber centralizes radio access networks by using a low-loss optical fiber link between the remote radio head and the central unit. Analog radio-over-fiber (A-RoF) transmits RF signals modulated directly onto an optical carrier, avoiding digitization and digital signal processing at the remote radio head. In this way, A-RoF shifts power-hungry processing from the antenna to the baseband unit. This paper outlines a mathematical framework to analyze the effect of fiber nonlinearity in an uplink wireless system supported by A-RoF. We model an input/output relationship that incorporates the wireless channel, thermal noise, and impairments encountered in the optical fiber channel: chromatic dispersion, electrical-to-optical conversion loss, amplification noise, and fiber nonlinear interference. We compare A-RoF with DSP-assisted A-RoF and digital radio receivers. Our results show that A-RoF achieves higher energy efficiency as compared to digital receivers with 8- and 16-bit analog-to-digital converters and DSP-assisted A-RoF. We further characterize the trade-offs among transmit power, nonlinear interference, and spectral efficiency, demonstrating that nonlinear effects fundamentally limit achievable rates. These results identify the linear operating regions where A-RoF is most effective for uplink wireless communication.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a mathematical framework analyzing how fiber nonlinearity affects analog radio-over-fiber (A-RoF) systems in uplink wireless communication, comparing A-RoF with DSP-assisted A-RoF and digital receivers, finding A-RoF achieves higher energy efficiency than 8-/16-bit digital receivers and DSP-assisted A-RoF, while nonlinear effects fundamentally limit achievable rates.
AI.motivation: To understand and model the impact of fiber nonlinearity on A-RoF systems, which shifts power-hungry processing from the antenna to the baseband unit, and to characterize the trade-offs among transmit power, nonlinear interference, and spectral efficiency.
AI.method: Develops a mathematical framework modeling the input/output relationship incorporating wireless channel, thermal noise, chromatic dispersion, electrical-to-optical conversion loss, amplification noise, and fiber nonlinear interference. Compares A-RoF against DSP-assisted A-RoF and digital radio receivers.
AI.result: A-RoF achieves higher energy efficiency compared to digital receivers with 8- and 16-bit ADCs and DSP-assisted A-RoF. Nonlinear effects fundamentally limit achievable rates, and the paper identifies linear operating regions where A-RoF is most effective.
AI.conclusion: A-RoF is energy-efficient for uplink wireless communication but fundamentally limited by fiber nonlinearity; the linear operating regions define its optimal application scope.

title: Adaptive Order Policies for Masked Diffusion
summary: Masked diffusion models have seen great success in capturing data distributions over discrete sequences in domains such as text and proteins. These models generate data by iteratively unmasking tokens starting from a fully masked sequence, with the unmasking order typically chosen at random or using a heuristic based on denoiser probabilities. In this work, we propose a scheme for learning the unmasking order using an additional lightweight policy network on top of a diffusion model. Our proposed loss reweights terms in the masked diffusion loss according to policy probabilities, and results in a policy that prefers positions where the denoiser is more likely to be correct. We study this loss in two settings: (i) training solely the policy while using a frozen pre-trained denoiser, and (ii) training the policy and denoiser jointly with the weighted loss to allow for mutual adaptation. We demonstrate that our approach outperforms common heuristics on problems that are sensitive to token ordering, such as combinatorial tasks and proteins.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes learning the unmasking order in masked diffusion models using a lightweight policy network, which outperforms random or heuristic-based orderings on combinatorial tasks and protein generation.
AI.motivation: Existing masked diffusion models use random or heuristic-based unmasking orders, which may be suboptimal for tasks sensitive to token ordering. The authors aim to learn a better ordering policy.
AI.method: A lightweight policy network is added to the diffusion model. The loss reweights masked diffusion loss terms by policy probabilities. Two settings are explored: (i) training policy with frozen denoiser, (ii) joint training of policy and denoiser for mutual adaptation.
AI.result: The learned policy outperforms common heuristics on combinatorial tasks and protein generation, favoring positions where the denoiser is more likely to be correct.
AI.conclusion: Learning the unmasking order via a policy network improves performance over fixed heuristics, especially for ordering-sensitive problems.

title: TAPS: Target-Aware Prefix Tree Selection for Diffusion-Drafted Speculative Decoding
summary: Using a diffusion model for parallel drafting is a promising approach for speculative decoding. By predicting tokens at multiple future positions in a single forward pass, diffusion drafters substantially reduce drafting latency. However, this shifts the bottleneck to verification: verifying a single sequence limits acceptance length, while verifying large draft trees incurs excessive target-model latency. We identify a key mismatch in existing draft-tree methods: existing diffusion-tree methods rank nodes by the marginal probability, ignoring that verification is prefix-conditioned. As a result, they may verify unreachable descendants of rejected prefixes, increasing latency with limited acceptance gains. To address this, we propose TAPS, a target-aware prefix selection method that turns diffusion marginals into path-conditioned acceptance estimates. TAPS then selects a compact prefix-closed subtree under a fixed verification budget, improving the acceptance-cost tradeoff rather than simply expanding the draft tree. Experiments across diverse datasets and model families demonstrate that TAPS achieves up to 7.9x lossless end-to-end speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding, outperforming state-of-the-art DFlash and DDTree by 1.36x and 1.74x respectively. Our work is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TAPS-EMNLP2026-53DD

AI.tldr: TAPS improves speculative decoding with diffusion drafters by selecting compact, prefix-closed subtrees for verification, achieving up to 7.9x speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-tree methods rank nodes by marginal probability, ignoring that verification is prefix-conditioned, leading to verification of unreachable descendants of rejected prefixes, increasing latency with limited acceptance gains.
AI.method: TAPS (Target-Aware Prefix Selection) turns diffusion marginals into path-conditioned acceptance estimates and selects a compact prefix-closed subtree under a fixed verification budget to improve the acceptance-cost tradeoff.
AI.result: Achieves up to 7.9x lossless end-to-end speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding; outperforms DFlash by 1.36x and DDTree by 1.74x across diverse datasets and model families.
AI.conclusion: TAPS effectively addresses the mismatched node-ranking problem in diffusion-tree methods by using target-aware prefix selection, leading to significant speed improvements in speculative decoding.

title: Adaptive PD Gains for Energy-Conscious Control in Physical Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Compliant force or torque control are approaches often investigated to achieve safe physical human-robot interaction (pHRI). However, these approaches have limitations. Force control requires a robot to be equipped with external force sensors to track the amplitude and direction of applied forces. Torque control requires torque sensing or estimation in each joint. As this is not available on every robot, energy-based approaches offer a promising alternative. Such approaches aim to achieve safe pHRI by limiting the mechanical energy of the robot. Current schemes leveraging an energy-based approach tend to have a complex implementation, and some may require further stability verification. We hence propose an adaptive proportional-derivative (PD) controller that can limit a robot's energy under any given limit to achieve safe pHRI. The proposed controller can limit both the kinetic and potential energy of a robot, and the behaviour of the controller gains can be shaped using various parameters, defining precisely the cutoff limit and sharpness. We construct a stability proof for the controller and define a condition to ensure the controller's stability. The proposed controller's behaviour and compliance are tested on the TALOS robot from PAL Robotics both in simulation and on hardware, verifying the expected compliant and energy-limiting behaviour of the controller.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an adaptive PD controller that limits a robot's kinetic and potential energy to achieve safe physical human-robot interaction, with stability proof and validation on the TALOS robot.
AI.motivation: Existing force/torque control methods for safe pHRI require external sensors or joint torque estimation, which are not universally available. Energy-based approaches offer an alternative but often involve complex implementation or lack stability verification.
AI.method: An adaptive PD controller is designed to limit both kinetic and potential energy under a given threshold. Controller gains are shaped via parameters that define cutoff limits and sharpness. A stability condition is derived and proven.
AI.result: The controller was tested on the TALOS robot (PAL Robotics) in both simulation and hardware, demonstrating compliant and energy-limiting behavior as expected.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive PD controller offers a simpler, stable, and sensor-efficient alternative for safe pHRI by directly limiting mechanical energy.

title: Real2SAM2Real: Generative 3D Caches as Complementary Context for Video Diffusion
summary: While Video Diffusion Models (VDMs) excel at synthesizing high-fidelity videos, enabling precise camera and scene control remains challenging. Existing methods predominantly rely on implicit diffusion priors to generate unobserved regions, inevitably leading to structural collapse during high-dynamic movements or complex occlusions. To address this challenge, we propose Real2SAM2Real, a framework that leverages 3D lifting models (e.g., SAM3D) to extract an explicitly editable 3D cache, serving as a robust geometric scaffold for the VDM. By capturing the entire 3D volume of foreground entities rather than just their visible shells, this cache injects holistic spatial priors into the VDM, providing dependable 3D-aware guidance for complex scene dynamics. To effectively leverage this 3D guidance while preserving pre-trained priors, we design a Soft Spatial-Aligned Injection mechanism alongside a minimally invasive fine-tuning strategy tailored for VDMs. Furthermore, we employ masked normal maps as a cross-modal bridge to construct a 3D-free data curation and perturbation pipeline. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Real2SAM2Real enables precise, decoupled control over both camera trajectories and multi-entity motions. By utilizing the complementary context from generative 3D caches, our framework overcomes typical breakdowns caused by over-reliance on diffusion priors, maintaining exceptional spatiotemporal consistency under large camera shifts and severe occlusions. Crucially, by decoupling geometry from appearance, our VDM-tailored 3D cache eradicates perspective ambiguities caused by structural holes and erroneous facades, as well as misleading cues from reflections and refractions. Project website is available at https://jiayi-wu-leo.github.io/real2sam2real

AI.tldr: Real2SAM2Real is a framework that extracts explicit 3D caches from video data using 3D lifting models (SAM3D) to provide geometric scaffolding for Video Diffusion Models, enabling precise and decoupled control over camera trajectories and multi-entity motions while preventing structural collapse during high-dynamic movements and occlusions.
AI.motivation: Existing Video Diffusion Models struggle with precise camera and scene control, especially under high-dynamic movements or complex occlusions, because they rely on implicit diffusion priors that can lead to structural collapse and perspective ambiguities.
AI.method: The framework uses 3D lifting models (SAM3D) to extract an explicit editable 3D cache capturing the entire 3D volume of foreground entities, then employs a Soft Spatial-Aligned Injection mechanism with a minimally invasive fine-tuning strategy to inject 3D guidance into VDMs. Masked normal maps serve as a cross-modal bridge for data curation and perturbation.
AI.result: The framework enables precise, decoupled control over camera trajectories and multi-entity motions, maintains exceptional spatiotemporal consistency under large camera shifts and severe occlusions, and eliminates perspective ambiguities caused by structural holes, erroneous facades, reflections, and refractions.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling geometry from appearance and providing explicit 3D caches, Real2SAM2Real overcomes the limitations of diffusion-prior-only approaches, offering robust 3D-aware guidance for complex scene dynamics in video generation.

title: Spatially Distributed Task-Oriented Compression for Multi-Emitter Localization and Characterization with Spectral Overlap
summary: Radio frequency spectrum awareness requires the ability to detect, localize, and characterize emitters in dense and contested wireless environments. In this work, we propose a task-oriented distributed compression framework for joint multi-emitter localization and characterization using spatially distributed receivers. Each receiver observes a short window of complex IQ samples, converts the observation to a time--frequency representation, and encodes it into a compact latent vector. A central fusion decoder combines the receiver latents to estimate an unordered set of active emitters, including their locations, center-frequency offsets, occupied bandwidths, and waveform families. A permutation-invariant training objective is used to handle the arbitrary ordering of emitters and predictions. Experiments on synthetic multi-emitter scenes with spectral overlap show that even extremely compact receiver-side representations can preserve useful information for emitter counting and waveform-family estimation. However, accurate localization and spectral-parameter regression require larger latent dimensions. Increasing the receiver latent dimension from $d_{\mathrm{rx}}=1$ to $d_{\mathrm{rx}}=16$ provides the largest improvement, while further increasing to $d_{\mathrm{rx}}=64$ gives smaller gains. These results demonstrate the potential of learned task-oriented compression for communication-efficient distributed spectrum awareness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a task-oriented distributed compression framework for joint multi-emitter localization and characterization using spatially distributed receivers, demonstrating that compact receiver-side representations can preserve useful information while larger latent dimensions improve accuracy.
AI.motivation: Radio frequency spectrum awareness in dense and contested wireless environments requires detecting, localizing, and characterizing multiple emitters efficiently, motivating a communication-efficient distributed compression approach.
AI.method: Each receiver captures IQ samples, converts to time-frequency representation, and encodes into a compact latent vector. A central fusion decoder estimates an unordered set of emitters (locations, center-frequency offsets, bandwidths, waveform families) using permutation-invariant training.
AI.result: Even extremely compact representations preserve information for emitter counting and waveform estimation. Latent dimension d_rx=16 provides largest improvement; d_rx=64 yields smaller gains. Accurate localization requires larger latents.
AI.conclusion: Learned task-oriented compression shows potential for communication-efficient distributed spectrum awareness, with trade-offs between compression rate and task performance.

title: FLaG: Fine-Grained Latent Grouping for Hallucination Detection
summary: Hallucinations in large language models (LLMs) arise from heterogeneous failure mechanisms, making reliable detection difficult for any single global uncertainty score. In this work, we formulate hallucination detection as a mechanism-aware evidence aggregation problem, where diverse representation- and token-level signals must be interpreted under multiple latent explanations. We propose FLaG, a lightweight hallucination detection framework that models correctness through a set of latent evidence groups. Each instance is softly associated with multiple groups via an energy-based routing mechanism, and group-conditional reliability signals are combined through a principled log-marginal aggregation. This design enables FLaG to capture heterogeneous hallucination patterns while remaining invariant to decision thresholds and evaluation metrics. The framework operates as a frozen-model head, requires no modification to the underlying language model, and incurs minimal computational overhead. We further provide a theoretical perspective that connects FLaG to optimal evidence aggregation under heterogeneous error mechanisms, showing that the Bayes-optimal test statistic necessarily admits a log-marginal form and that FLaG constitutes a tractable approximation with a controllable error bound. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and LLM backbones demonstrate that FLaG consistently achieves SOTA performance, while exhibiting robust transfer across datasets and models, and remaining effective under limited supervision.

AI.tldr: FLaG is a lightweight hallucination detection framework that uses latent evidence groups and energy-based routing to capture heterogeneous hallucination patterns, achieving SOTA performance without modifying the underlying LLM.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in LLMs arise from diverse failure mechanisms, making detection difficult with any single global uncertainty score. Existing methods fail to capture heterogeneous patterns.
AI.method: FLaG models correctness through latent evidence groups, uses energy-based routing for soft association, and combines group-conditional reliability signals via log-marginal aggregation. It operates as a frozen-model head with no LLM modification.
AI.result: Consistently achieves SOTA performance across multiple benchmarks and LLM backbones, exhibits robust transfer across datasets and models, and remains effective under limited supervision.
AI.conclusion: The Bayes-optimal test statistic for heterogeneous error mechanisms necessarily admits a log-marginal form, and FLaG provides a tractable approximation with a controllable error bound.

title: EnergyMamba: An Uncertainty-Aware Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model for Energy Consumption Prediction
summary: Energy consumption prediction is essential for efficient grid management, demand-side optimization, and sustainable energy planning. Although advanced machine learning methods have been employed for better prediction performance, existing works have two key limitations: (1) they usually formulate this task as a purely time-series prediction problem without explicitly modeling the spatial dependencies among different regions, and (2) they fail to provide reliable predictions with uncertainty estimates under abnormal situations such as extreme weather events. To advance existing research, we propose EnergyMamba, an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal learning framework for accurate and reliable energy consumption prediction, which comprises two key components: (i) a novel Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model (GE-Mamba) that injects spatial context learned from the grid topology into the temporal dynamics, enabling coupled spatiotemporal modeling, and (ii) an Adaptive Sequential Conformalized Quantile Regression (AS-CQR) module, which includes locally adaptive normalization and an online feedback mechanism to dynamically calibrate prediction intervals under potential distribution shifts. We evaluate EnergyMamba on four large-scale real-world datasets from Florida, New York, and California. Results show EnergyMamba achieves around 5% improvement in prediction accuracy and 6% improvement in uncertainty quantification over 15 state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: EnergyMamba is an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal learning framework for energy prediction that uses graph-enhanced state space modeling for spatial-temporal coupling and adaptive conformalized quantile regression for dynamic uncertainty calibration, achieving ~5% accuracy and ~6% uncertainty improvement over 15 baselines on real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing energy consumption prediction methods fail to model spatial dependencies among different regions and cannot provide reliable uncertainty estimates under abnormal situations (e.g., extreme weather events), limiting their practical utility for grid management.
AI.method: EnergyMamba consists of (1) GE-Mamba, a Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model that injects spatial graph topology into temporal dynamics, and (2) AS-CQR, an Adaptive Sequential Conformalized Quantile Regression module with locally adaptive normalization and online feedback for dynamic prediction interval calibration under distribution shifts.
AI.result: Evaluated on four large-scale real-world datasets from Florida, New York, and California, EnergyMamba achieves approximately 5% improvement in prediction accuracy and 6% improvement in uncertainty quantification over 15 state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses both spatial dependency modeling and uncertainty quantification limitations in energy prediction, demonstrating superior accuracy and reliability, especially under abnormal conditions.

title: A passive universal grasping mechanism based on an everting shell
summary: A passive monolithic compliant grasping mechanism that works based on the eversion of an elastically deformable bistable shell is conceptualized. It comprises grasping arms made of beam segments that work in conjunction with the everting shell. The grasper is capable of picking up a stiff object of any shape up to a maximum size and weight. The bistable shell everts upon contact with the object to enable the grasping arms envelop the object forming an enclosure. The mechanism then stays in that configuration until it is actuated again to turn the shell back to its original configuration and thereby opening the enclosure to release the object. The stiffness of the arms decides the payload of the mechanism. The size of the arms decides the largest object that can be grasped and held. The arms have distributed compliance so that they can conform to the shape of the object without applying undue force on it.

AI.tldr: A passive monolithic compliant grasping mechanism using a bistable shell that everts on contact to envelop objects, holding them until actuated again to release.
AI.motivation: To develop a simple, passive grasping mechanism that can pick up stiff objects of any shape without requiring continuous actuation or complex control systems.
AI.method: A monolithic compliant mechanism combining a bistable everting shell with beam-segment grasping arms that conform to object shapes through distributed compliance.
AI.result: The mechanism can grasp objects of any shape up to a certain size and weight; payload is determined by arm stiffness, maximum object size by arm dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The bistable shell-based grasper offers a passive, shape-adaptive grasping solution that maintains its configuration without energy input until actively released.

title: Where to Refine, When to Stop: Rethinking Redundancy via Latent Discrepancy for Efficient Visual Autoregressive Generation
summary: Visual Autoregressive (VAR) models deliver high-quality image generation but suffer from significant inference latency at high resolutions. Recent acceleration approaches most rely on heuristic measures with layer features to prune tokens. Such heuristics are sensitive to complex contextual semantics, leading to inaccurate identification of redundant computation and poor adaptability across prompts. We rethink redundancy in VAR from the perspective of its impact on pixel-space generation and introduce Latent Discrepancy. This unified metric quantifies a token's contribution by measuring the change in model states during generation. Our analysis shows that redundancy is more accurately identified when guided by image latent or pixel-space signals. We further observed that in classifier-free guidance (CFG), the convergence trend of the discrepancy between conditional and unconditional branches exhibits high dynamics with different prompts. Based on these findings, we propose LD-Pruning (Latent Discrepancy Pruning), a training-free framework that removes redundancy via latent discrepancy by integrating decoding-free region selection and adaptive unconditional-branch skipping. Extensive experiments show that LD-Pruning substantially reduces inference latency while maintaining high generation quality, achieving up to 2.35x speedup on Infinity-8B.

AI.tldr: VAR models are slow at high resolutions. LD-Pruning speeds them up by 2.35x using Latent Discrepancy (a metric from pixel-space impact) plus CFG adaptive skipping, without training.
AI.motivation: VAR models suffer high inference latency at high resolutions; existing heuristic pruning methods fail due to sensitivity to complex semantics and poor cross-prompt adaptability.
AI.method: Proposes Latent Discrepancy, which quantifies token contribution via pixel-space state changes. Integrates decoding-free region selection and adaptive unconditional-branch skipping in CFG. Training-free framework called LD-Pruning.
AI.result: Up to 2.35x speedup on Infinity-8B while maintaining generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Latent Discrepancy-based pruning effectively identifies redundancy in VAR models, enabling substantial inference acceleration without sacrificing quality.

title: FlexLink: Decoupling Control and Data Beams for Next-Generation Wideband Networks
summary: The next generation of 6G networks aims to utilize ultra-wideband spectrum and massive antenna arrays to serve multiple users with both control and data channels at low latency and high efficiency. However, phased arrays at mmWave and mid-bands are fundamentally constrained to a single beam or suffer sharp beamforming loss when split across directions, limiting simultaneous control-data support. In FlexLink, we introduce and prototype a novel delay-phased array architecture that overcomes this limitation by redistributing energy jointly across frequency and space, enabling multiple narrow beams without sacrificing per-beam gain or requiring additional power. We design and prototype FlexLink on a custom 4-7 GHz hardware testbed, demonstrating for the first time that control and data beams can be decoupled in practice, achieving nearly double spectral efficiency compared to conventional phased arrays.

AI.tldr: FlexLink introduces a delay-phased array architecture that redistributes energy across frequency and space, enabling simultaneous multiple narrow beams for 6G networks without sacrificing per-beam gain or additional power, achieving nearly double spectral efficiency on a 4-7 GHz testbed.
AI.motivation: Conventional phased arrays at mmWave/mid-bands are limited to a single beam or suffer significant beamforming loss when splitting across directions, making it difficult to simultaneously support control and data channels for multiple users in 6G ultra-wideband networks.
AI.method: A novel delay-phased array architecture that redistributes energy jointly across frequency and space, enabling multiple narrow beams without sacrificing per-beam gain or requiring additional power.
AI.result: Implemented on a custom 4-7 GHz hardware testbed; demonstrated that control and data beams can be decoupled in practice, achieving nearly double spectral efficiency compared to conventional phased arrays.
AI.conclusion: FlexLink effectively overcomes the fundamental single-beam constraint of phased arrays, offering a practical solution for simultaneous multi-user control-data support in future 6G networks.

title: Modeling Spectral Energy Shifts in Spatio-Temporal Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection methods aim to distinguish anomalous nodes. While prior methods characterize anomalies through increased variation in the spectral energy distributions, they overlook those that result in decreased variation, i.e., camouflaged anomalies that appear normal. We show that this type of anomaly persists across multiple datasets and remains undetectable by existing spectral approaches. To address this limitation, we propose a node-level spectral energy formulation that is fully compatible with message passing and enables the detection of camouflaged anomalies. Building on this formulation, we introduce an energy-aware graph learning framework that models spectral shifts through energy-driven message passing in both static and time-series graphs. Besides, our unified architecture extends to temporal settings without introducing specialized sequence modules, enabling efficient learning under long sliding windows. Extensive experiments on large-scale benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and scalability of our approach.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an energy-aware graph learning framework for detecting camouflaged anomalies that appear normal, using node-level spectral energy formulation compatible with message passing in both static and time-series graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing spectral anomaly detection methods only capture anomalies with increased spectral energy variation, failing to detect camouflaged anomalies that exhibit decreased variation and appear normal.
AI.method: Proposes a node-level spectral energy formulation compatible with message passing, and an energy-aware graph learning framework with energy-driven message passing for both static and time-series graphs, extending to temporal settings without specialized sequence modules.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on large-scale benchmarks demonstrate effectiveness and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully detects camouflaged anomalies that prior spectral methods miss, and works efficiently across static and temporal graph settings.

title: Threshold-Based Exclusive Batching for LLM Inference
summary: Mixed batching (MB)--interleaving prefill and decode in a single batch--has become the standard scheduling strategy for large language model (LLM) inference due to its efficiency in maximizing compute and memory utilization. However, through controlled experiments, we find that prefill-decode interference inflates MB's per-step marginal cost above that of pure decode. On the high-bandwidth H200 (4.8 TB/s), this occurs only when decode tokens exceed 80% of the batch; however, on the bandwidth-constrained RTX PRO 6000 (1.792 TB/s), this threshold plummets to just 20%. Consequently, the optimal choice between MB and exclusive batching (EB) fundamentally depends on GPU memory bandwidth, model size, and workload composition. We derive a closed-form condition for this EB-MB performance crossover, along with asymptotically optimal phase-switching thresholds and memory-safe batch sizing for EB. Optimized EB achieves up to 41.9% higher throughput on bandwidth-constrained GPUs, while MB retains its advantage on high-bandwidth hardware with larger models. Our hybrid scheduler EB+ applies this condition online to dynamically switch between EB and MB without manual intervention. Under non-stationary traffic with distribution or concurrency shifts, EB+ attains the highest or near-highest throughput in every setting, outperforming MB by up to 36.4%.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the performance trade-off between mixed batching (interleaving prefill and decode) and exclusive batching for LLM inference, finding that prefill-decode interference causes mixed batching to be suboptimal on bandwidth-constrained GPUs. The authors derive a closed-form condition for switching between the two strategies and propose EB+, a hybrid scheduler that dynamically switches between them, achieving up to 41.9% throughput improvement on bandwidth-limited hardware.
AI.motivation: While mixed batching is widely used in LLM inference for its high utilization, the paper identifies and quantifies prefill-decode interference that inflates per-step marginal cost. The key motivation is to understand when mixed batching is actually optimal versus exclusive batching, and to provide a practical scheduling strategy that adapts to hardware and workload conditions.
AI.method: Controlled experiments on H200 (high bandwidth) and RTX PRO 6000 (bandwidth-constrained) GPUs to measure prefill-decode interference. Derivation of a closed-form condition for the EB-MB performance crossover and asymptotically optimal phase-switching thresholds. Implementation of EB+, a hybrid scheduler that dynamically switches between exclusive and mixed batching online without manual tuning.
AI.result: On H200, MB is degraded only when decode tokens exceed 80% of the batch; on RTX PRO 6000, the threshold drops to 20%. Optimized exclusive batching achieves up to 41.9% higher throughput on bandwidth-constrained GPUs. EB+ outperforms MB by up to 36.4% under non-stationary traffic.
AI.conclusion: The optimal batching strategy depends on memory bandwidth, model size, and workload composition. The hybrid scheduler EB+ dynamically adapts to achieve near-optimal throughput across diverse hardware and traffic conditions without manual intervention.

title: PaCo-VLA: Passivity-Shielded Compliance Prior for Contact-Rich Vision-Language-Action Manipulation
comment: Under review, code will be available soon
summary: Contact-rich manipulation demands both high-level semantic reasoning and the safe regulation of high-frequency contact dynamics. While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models provide unprecedented semantic generalization, their low-rate outputs lack the reliability required for direct plant authority in force-sensitive tasks. To bridge this semantic-to-control gap, we introduce PaCo-VLA, a passivity-shielded compliance prior that recasts the VLA interface. Rather than trusting VLAs with direct motor commands, PaCo-VLA treats network outputs as task-level compliance proposals: semantic bindings, task stages, and admittance schedules. A high-frequency, proposal-independent passivity shield governs these proposals through energy-tank accounting and boundary checks, preventing invalid, stale, or unverified model predictions from bypassing low-level contact physics. This decoupled architecture also enables causal evaluation, isolating semantic contributions from geometric shortcuts. Extensive simulated and real-world connector-insertion experiments demonstrate that PaCo-VLA achieves superior precision over unshielded VLA baselines, sustaining zero passivity violations even under adversarial compliance shifts. This framework establishes a provably sampled-passive runtime contract at the admittance port and provides a runtime interface for deploying foundation models in contact-rich domains.

AI.tldr: PaCo-VLA introduces a passivity-shielded compliance prior that treats VLA outputs as task-level compliance proposals rather than direct motor commands, using energy-tank accounting and boundary checks to enforce zero passivity violations in contact-rich manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between high-level semantic reasoning from Vision-Language-Action models and the safe, reliable low-level control required for force-sensitive contact-rich manipulation tasks.
AI.method: Decoupled architecture where VLA outputs are treated as compliance proposals (semantic bindings, task stages, admittance schedules), governed by a high-frequency passivity shield using energy-tank accounting and boundary checks to reject invalid/stale predictions.
AI.result: Superior precision over unshielded VLA baselines in connector-insertion experiments, sustaining zero passivity violations even under adversarial compliance shifts.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a provably sampled-passive runtime contract at the admittance port and provides a safe interface for deploying foundation models in contact-rich domains.

title: Training-Free Object-Agnostic Jam Detection in Fulfillment Centers
