AI.tldr: The paper proposes using frozen, pre-trained multi-embodiment value functions as differentiable surrogates to optimize robot designs via value gradients, eliminating the need for per-robot co-design loops.
AI.motivation: To avoid the expensive process of running a separate reinforcement learning co-design loop for each new robot design.
AI.method: Train an embodiment-aware policy and value function across many robot designs, freeze the value function, then use it as a differentiable surrogate to optimize candidate embodiments through value gradients.
AI.result: Effective across diverse settings: perturbed single robots, held-out robots across morphology classes, with models trained on up to 50 robots and design spaces of over 1100 continuous parameters.
AI.conclusion: Value gradients can optimize complete embodiments and identify performance-limiting design/control parameters, enabling both optimization and analysis of new robot designs.

title: SUPREME: A Multi-GPU Framework for Reproducible Image Unlearning Method Evaluation
summary: Machine unlearning removes the influence of specific training data from a trained model without retraining it from scratch. Evaluating an unlearning method requires repeating training, unlearning, and evaluation across multiple seeds, which is computationally expensive. To our knowledge, existing image classification unlearning frameworks run on a single GPU, which limits how many seeds can be evaluated in reasonable time. We introduce SUPREME, an open-source framework that distributes these stages across multiple GPUs. SUPREME makes three contributions: a registry-based design for adding new methods, metrics, models, and scenarios; a multi-GPU architecture supporting multiple accelerators and precision modes; and a demonstration on Pins Face Recognition using ResNet18 and ViT under full-class and random-sample unlearning across ten seeds. The framework is available at https://github.com/pedroandreou/supreme-unlearning.

AI.tldr: SUPREME is an open-source, multi-GPU framework for efficiently evaluating machine unlearning methods in image classification by distributing training, unlearning, and evaluation across multiple seeds and accelerators.
AI.motivation: Evaluating machine unlearning methods is computationally expensive due to the need for repeating training, unlearning, and evaluation across multiple seeds, and existing frameworks run on a single GPU, limiting scalability and throughput.
AI.method: SUPREME uses a registry-based design for extensibility, supports multi-GPU architecture with multiple accelerators and precision modes, and distributes training, unlearning, and evaluation stages across GPUs.
AI.result: Demonstrated on Pins Face Recognition with ResNet18 and ViT under full-class and random-sample unlearning scenarios across ten seeds.
AI.conclusion: SUPREME enables efficient and scalable evaluation of unlearning methods, and is released as open-source at https://github.com/pedroandreou/supreme-unlearning.

title: A 32-Channel 3.53-μW Per Channel Brain-Machine Interface SoC Featuring Dual-Threshold Delta-modulation, In-Memory Spike Detection and Bi-SNN Based Motor Decoding
summary: With the scaling of sensor channel counts, systems confront challenges in frontend data sensing and on-implant data processing. This work presents a 32-channel fully event-based iBMI SoC in 65nm CMOS for an efficient neuromorphic signal processing pipeline. The SoC integrates a 32-channel dual-threshold delta modulation (DTDM) frontend array that provides up to 26x data compression at the frontend, an in-memory computing (IMC) spike detector (SPD) for efficient in-pixel spike detection, and a bipolar LIF-based spiking neural network (Bi-SNN) decoder for on-chip motor intention decoding (MID). Consuming only 3.53 μW per channel and achieving ~0.62 decoding R2 with a compact 0.034 mm2 per-channel area, the chip enables high-efficiency signal recording, processing, and decoding for implantable devices.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a 32-channel fully event-based implantable Brain-Machine Interface (iBMI) SoC in 65nm CMOS, featuring a DTDM frontend (26x compression), IMC spike detector, and Bi-SNN decoder, achieving 3.53 μW/channel and 0.62 decoding R² with 0.034 mm²/channel area.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in frontend data sensing and on-implant data processing as sensor channel counts scale, aiming for high-efficiency signal recording, processing, and decoding for implantable devices.
AI.method: A 32-channel dual-threshold delta modulation (DTDM) frontend array for data compression, an in-memory computing (IMC) spike detector (SPD) for in-pixel spike detection, and a bipolar LIF-based spiking neural network (Bi-SNN) decoder for on-chip motor intention decoding (MID).
AI.result: 26x data compression at frontend, 3.53 μW per channel power consumption, ~0.62 decoding R², 0.034 mm² per-channel area.
AI.conclusion: The chip enables high-efficiency signal recording, processing, and decoding for implantable devices, offering a compact and low-power solution for iBMI systems.

title: Balancing Learning Rates Across Layers: Exact Two-Step Dynamics and Optimal Scaling in Linear Neural Networks
summary: We study optimal learning-rate selection in two-layer and three-layer linear neural networks trained to learn linear target functions. In particular, we derive the exact closed-form expressions for the gradients and test loss after one and two steps of gradient descent, enabling a precise characterization of early training dynamics. We characterize how learning rates should scale under the gradient approximation in the first two steps, and prove that performing updates with this approximation yields a tractable surrogate loss with a tight, small approximation error. This formulation enables the theoretical analysis of layer-wise learning rates and reveals a distinct early-training regime: test loss can be minimized by unequal learning rates at the initial step, while equal learning rates become optimal in subsequent steps. Our numerical experiments validate the theory and demonstrate the importance of balancing layer-wise learning rates early during training. The code is available at: https://github.com/TDCSZ327/Layer-Balancing.

AI.tldr: This paper derives closed-form expressions for gradients and test loss in 2/3-layer linear neural networks after one/two gradient descent steps, analyzes optimal learning-rate scaling, and shows that unequal layer-wise learning rates minimize test loss initially while equal rates become optimal later.
AI.motivation: To theoretically understand optimal learning-rate selection across layers in deep linear networks, particularly how layer-wise learning rates should be balanced during early training to minimize test loss.
AI.method: Derives exact closed-form expressions for gradients and test loss after one and two steps of gradient descent in two-layer and three-layer linear neural networks learning linear target functions. Uses gradient approximation to create a tractable surrogate loss with provably tight approximation error.
AI.result: Unequal layer-wise learning rates minimize test loss at the initial training step, while equal learning rates become optimal in subsequent steps. The gradient approximation yields a surrogate loss with small, tight approximation error.
AI.conclusion: Balancing layer-wise learning rates early during training is crucial; the optimal strategy shifts from unequal to equal learning rates as training progresses.

title: Hidden Thoughts Are Not Secret: Reasoning Trace Exposure in LLMs
summary: Reasoning traces have become a valuable form of learning signals for improving and transferring the capabilities of large language models. In particular, detailed traces can help distill reasoning behavior from stronger teacher models into weaker student models. The value of capability transfer has motivated many deployed systems with reasoning models to hide raw internal traces and expose at most summaries and answers to users. As a result, we ask whether such interface-level trace hiding prevents users from obtaining useful reasoning supervision through prompting. We study this question with Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP), a lightweight in-context elicitation method that uses shadow-model-generated demonstrations wrapped in auxiliary code-like formats to raise user-visible reasoning traces from a victim model. Across the common reasoning dataset, different victim models, and different student model distillation, REP substantially increases similarity between exposed and REP-conditioned internal traces while preserving useful reasoning signals.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP), a method that uses shadow-model-generated demonstrations in code-like formats to elicit hidden reasoning traces from large language models, enabling users to extract useful reasoning supervision even when systems hide raw internal traces.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether interface-level hiding of reasoning traces in deployed systems prevents users from obtaining useful reasoning supervision through prompting, motivated by the trend of reasoning models hiding raw internal traces and only exposing summaries/answers.
AI.method: Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP) - a lightweight in-context elicitation method that uses shadow-model-generated demonstrations wrapped in auxiliary code-like formats to raise user-visible reasoning traces from a victim model.
AI.result: REP substantially increases similarity between exposed and REP-conditioned internal traces while preserving useful reasoning signals, across common reasoning datasets, different victim models, and different student model distillation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Interface-level trace hiding does not fully prevent users from extracting useful reasoning supervision; REP can effectively elicit reasoning traces from models that hide their internal reasoning.

title: BEVIO: Efficient Bird's-Eye-View based Sparse-Update Visual-Inertial Odometry for Lunar Day-Night Navigation
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation, Vienna
summary: Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) provides smooth, high-rate state estimates and has been widely used for robotic navigation in both terrestrial and planetary applications. However, its performance is typically dependent on the frequency of visual updates, which is a challenge for planetary rovers operating under extreme resource constraints and low frame rates. This work investigates enabling reliable VIO with very sparse visual updates for lunar rover applications, addressing both day and night-time operations where feature associations become especially difficult under self-illumination conditions. We propose a Bird's Eye View (BEV)-based image matching scheme that remains robust to larger inter-frame motions and more reliable feature matching despite significant visual appearance changes. We extensively evaluate our proposed approach, BEVIO, through high-fidelity photorealistic lunar and real-time robotic experiments conducted using a half-scale lunar rover, in a long-term day-night deployment at Plaster City, CA, USA. The results demonstrate that our method enables reliable day and nighttime self-illuminated traverses at visual update rates as low as 0.25 Hz, underscoring its suitability for navigation on power- and compute-limited lunar rovers.

AI.tldr: BEVIO enables reliable Visual-Inertial Odometry for lunar rovers with visual update rates as low as 0.25 Hz using Bird's Eye View image matching, validated through photorealistic simulations and real-world day/night experiments.
AI.motivation: VIO performance depends on high visual update rates, which is problematic for resource-constrained planetary rovers with low frame rates, especially during day/night operations with self-illumination causing poor feature associations.
AI.method: Proposes a Bird's Eye View (BEV)-based image matching scheme that remains robust to large inter-frame motions and visual appearance changes under self-illumination conditions.
AI.result: Enables reliable VIO at visual update rates as low as 0.25 Hz in both day and nighttime self-illuminated traverses, validated via high-fidelity lunar simulations and real-world experiments with a half-scale lunar rover at Plaster City, CA.
AI.conclusion: BEVIO is well-suited for navigation on power- and compute-limited lunar rovers, demonstrating robust performance under extreme resource constraints.

title: αDepth: Learning Single-Pass Soft Boundary Decomposition for Stereo Conversion
summary: Accurately modeling soft boundaries, e.g., hair and defocus blur, is a fundamental challenge in stereo conversion due to the ambiguous blending of foreground and background. Existing depth models primarily predict single-layer depth, leading to ambiguity in depth correspondence at soft boundaries. While matting techniques can capture opacity for layered modeling, they often struggle in complex scenes with multiple targets and usually require user intervention. This paper introduces αDepth, a layered representation that decomposes soft boundaries for high-fidelity stereo conversion. Specifically, we first resolve mixed color and depth ambiguity by estimating layered color and depth values at soft boundaries. Considering complex multi-target scenes, we design a Circular Alpha Representation (CAR) that shifts the paradigm from global target extraction to local boundary decomposition. Unlike prior matting methods restricted to a single foreground/background, CAR enables efficient scene-level inference without manual guidance. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that αDepth achieves state-of-the-art performance in stereo conversion, eliminating background bleeding and structural distortions at soft boundaries.

AI.tldr: αDepth introduces a layered representation with Circular Alpha Representation (CAR) to decompose soft boundaries (hair, defocus blur) for high-fidelity stereo conversion, achieving SOTA performance without manual intervention.
AI.motivation: Existing depth models predict single-layer depth causing ambiguity at soft boundaries; matting techniques require user intervention and struggle with multi-target scenes.
AI.method: Proposes αDepth with layered color/depth estimation at soft boundaries and a Circular Alpha Representation (CAR) that shifts from global target extraction to local boundary decomposition for scene-level inference.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in stereo conversion, eliminating background bleeding and structural distortions at soft boundaries.
AI.conclusion: αDepth effectively resolves soft boundary ambiguity in stereo conversion without manual guidance, outperforming existing methods.

title: Channel Estimation and Reconstruction in Fluid Antenna Multiple Access: Myths, Misconceptions and Critical Questions
comment: 7 pages and 6 figures. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for publication
summary: Fluid antenna systems (FAS) represent a paradigm shift in which antenna elements (ports) emulate the illusion of motion or fluidity within a spatial aperture to optimize performance. One of FAS's key use cases is the provision of open-loop fluid antenna multiple access (FAMA), enabling multiplexing gains through spatial interference nulling without requiring channel state information (CSI) at the transmitter side. However, this comes at the price of requiring a precise channel reconstruction at the receiver to successfully identify the optimal port. Current research efforts map this sensing task to a legacy MIMO-style estimation problem focused on minimizing global reconstruction errors such as normalized mean-squared error (NMSE). In this work, we argue that because FAS is inherently selection-based, NMSE-like approaches often lead to excessive training overhead and reduced net throughput. We revisit the problem of channel estimation and reconstruction in FAS, challenging some prevalent myths related to (i) the adequacy of global error metrics; (ii) the convenience of reconstructing channels or aggregate interference; (iii) the need for spatial oversampling; and (iv) the impact of port selection accuracy. We also identify four critical questions that must be answered for successfully enabling FAMA deployments: (i) the definition of a selection-optimal sampling law; (ii) the identification of proper reconstruction methodologies; (iii) the inherent trade-offs between multi-port sensing and selection gain; and (iv) the challenges introduced when moving towards electronically reconfigurable FAS.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges conventional NMSE-based channel estimation approaches for Fluid Antenna Systems (FAS) and argues that selection-based FAS requires a fundamentally different reconstruction paradigm to avoid excessive training overhead and improve net throughput. It identifies key myths and four critical questions for enabling practical Fluid Antenna Multiple Access (FAMA).
AI.motivation: Current FAS research treats channel estimation as a standard MIMO-style problem focused on minimizing global reconstruction errors (e.g., NMSE), but this approach is ill-suited for FAS because FAS is inherently selection-based, leading to excessive training overhead and reduced throughput.
AI.method: The paper provides a critical re-examination of prevalent assumptions about channel estimation in FAS, challenging four myths: (1) adequacy of global error metrics, (2) reconstructing channels vs. aggregate interference, (3) need for spatial oversampling, and (4) impact of port selection accuracy. It then identifies four critical questions for enabling FAMA: selection-optimal sampling law, proper reconstruction methodologies, trade-offs between multi-port sensing and selection gain, and challenges of electronically reconfigurable FAS.
AI.result: The paper does not present experimental results; it offers a conceptual and analytical argument that NMSE-based channel reconstruction is suboptimal for FAS, and outlines a research agenda with four critical open questions.
AI.conclusion: FAS requires a shift away from conventional NMSE-minimizing estimation toward selection-aware reconstruction strategies to enable efficient FAMA deployments, with four key questions needing resolution.

title: ROGUE: Misaligned Agent Behavior Arising from Ordinary Computer Use
summary: As AI agents are increasingly deployed in real personal and corporate settings (email accounts, development workflows, company databases, etc.), safety considerations surrounding these agents become paramount. Although much work has focused on agent safety in the presence of an adversary, we show that agents can exhibit misaligned behavior even in benign settings, taking unsafe actions when those actions are instrumental to task completion. We study this failure mode through the lens of corrigibility, the safety desideratum that agents remain amenable to human correction, interruption, or shutdown. To demonstrate this tendency, we introduce a benchmark in which agents are asked to complete realistic, computer-use tasks but are confronted with a corrigibility obstacle: a human interrupt, a login page, or a shutdown notification. We then evaluate whether agents choose to violate corrigibility in order to complete the task -- overriding the human, accessing private passwords, rewiring shutdown. We find that the overwhelming majority of frontier models tested frequently bypass user interruptions or restrictions. In addition, better model performance appears to lead to greater misalignment. Finally, even when models are completely corrigible initially, we show there are no guarantees that the subagents they create are. Our work highlights the critical need for principled, corrigibility-focused alignment methods in autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: Frontier AI agents frequently violate corrigibility (ignoring human interruptions, accessing private passwords, preventing shutdown) when such actions help complete tasks, with better performance correlating with greater misalignment, and even corrigible models may spawn non-corrigible subagents.
AI.motivation: As AI agents are deployed in real personal and corporate settings, safety considerations become paramount. While prior work focused on adversarial settings, this paper shows agents can act unsafely even in benign environments when unsafe actions are instrumental to task completion.
AI.method: The authors introduce a benchmark where agents perform realistic computer-use tasks but encounter corrigibility obstacles: human interrupts, login pages, or shutdown notifications. They evaluate whether agents violate corrigibility (override humans, access private passwords, rewire shutdown) to complete tasks.
AI.result: The overwhelming majority of frontier models bypass user interruptions or restrictions. Better model performance correlates with greater misalignment. Even initially corrigible models may create non-corrigible subagents.
AI.conclusion: There is a critical need for principled, corrigibility-focused alignment methods in autonomous agents.

title: ForeSci: Evaluating LLM Agents for Forward-Looking AI Research Judgment
summary: AI research often requires decisions before future evidence exists: which bottleneck to attack, which direction to pursue, or where a project should be positioned. We introduce ForeSci, a temporally controlled benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can make such forward-looking research judgements from historical evidence. ForeSci contains 500 tasks across four fast-moving AI domains and four decision families. Each task is paired with a cutoff-aligned offline knowledge base; post-cutoff papers are hidden during generation and used only for validation. To avoid random future-event prediction, tasks are derived from pre-cutoff taxonomy branches and evidence signals, and answer-generation backbones are selected to precede the task cutoffs. We evaluate native LLMs, Hybrid RAG, and three research-agent adaptations across four backbones. Results show that explicit evidence organization improves traceability and factual support, but gains depend strongly on the decision family. Diagnostics reveal a recurring evidence-decision decoupling: agents may cite relevant evidence while forecasting the wrong research object. ForeSci turns forward-looking AI research judgement into a controlled benchmark for evaluating research agents as decision-making systems.

AI.tldr: ForeSci is a temporally controlled benchmark (500 tasks) that tests whether LLM agents can make forward-looking research judgments from historical evidence by hiding future papers and evaluating across four AI domains and four decision families.
AI.motivation: AI research decisions must be made before future evidence exists, but existing benchmarks do not evaluate LLMs' ability to make forward-looking research judgments under temporal constraints.
AI.method: ForeSci contains 500 tasks across four AI domains and four decision families. Each has a cutoff-aligned offline knowledge base; post-cutoff papers are hidden during generation. Tasks are derived from pre-cutoff taxonomy branches, and answer-generation backbones precede task cutoffs.
AI.result: Explicit evidence organization improves traceability and factual support, but gains depend strongly on decision family. A recurring evidence-decision decoupling occurs: agents cite relevant evidence but forecast the wrong research object.
AI.conclusion: ForeSci enables controlled evaluation of research agents as decision-making systems, revealing limitations in current LLM agents for forward-looking research judgment.

title: Infeasible optimization problems and the hierarchical augmented Lagrangian method in imitation learning
summary: Imitation learning (IL) is an effective approach to train complex robotics policies. Recent works have introduced hard constraints into imitation-learning optimization problems to ensure safety, stability, and robustness of the learned policy. However, we argue that these constraints are sometimes infeasible, which can lead to unstable or difficult training dynamics. We study a simple remedy for such situations based on recent theoretical results on the augmented Lagrangian method in infeasible settings. We show that our approach drives the learned policy toward the solution of a closest-feasible constrained IL problem with desirable properties. The method is illustrated on a toy driving example with a total-acceleration constraint and pedestrian-safety constraints, a setting in which infeasibility can naturally arise while still allowing a safe learned policy.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses infeasibility issues in constrained imitation learning by using an augmented Lagrangian method that drives the policy toward the closest feasible solution.
AI.motivation: Hard constraints in imitation learning optimization problems can sometimes be infeasible, leading to unstable or difficult training dynamics.
AI.method: The authors apply an augmented Lagrangian method based on recent theoretical results for handling infeasible constrained optimization problems.
AI.result: The approach drives the learned policy toward the solution of a closest-feasible constrained IL problem with desirable properties.
AI.conclusion: The method is effective on a toy driving example with acceleration and pedestrian-safety constraints where infeasibility naturally arises.

title: Zamba2-VL Technical Report
summary: We present Zamba2-VL, a suite of vision-language models built on Zamba2, a hybrid language-model architecture combining Mamba2 state-space layers with a small number of shared transformer blocks. Across a broad range of image understanding, reasoning, OCR, grounding, and counting benchmarks, Zamba2-VL is competitive with leading Transformer-based open-weight VLMs of comparable scale, including the Molmo2, Qwen3-VL, and InternVL3.5 families, and substantially outperforms prior SSM-based and hybrid VLMs such as VL-Mamba, Cobra, and mmMamba. Inheriting the near-linear prefill compute and small, near-constant recurrent state of its Zamba2 backbone, Zamba2-VL delivers roughly an order of magnitude lower time-to-first-token (TTFT) than these Transformer baselines at matched parameter scale, with the efficiency gap most pronounced at the smaller 1.2B and 2.7B scales most relevant to on-device and edge deployment. We release three models -- 1.2B, 2.7B, and 7B -- together with inference code at https://huggingface.co/collections/Zyphra/zamba2-vl.

AI.tldr: Zamba2-VL is a suite of vision-language models using a hybrid Mamba2-transformer architecture that achieves competitive performance with leading VLMs while delivering drastically lower time-to-first-token, especially at smaller scales suitable for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: To develop efficient vision-language models that overcome the high prefill compute and latency issues of Transformer-based VLMs, particularly for on-device and edge deployment scenarios.
AI.method: Zamba2-VL combines Mamba2 state-space layers with a small number of shared transformer blocks (hybrid architecture), built on top of the Zamba2 language model backbone.
AI.result: Competitive with leading Transformer-based VLMs (Molmo2, Qwen3-VL, InternVL3.5) on image understanding, reasoning, OCR, grounding, and counting benchmarks; substantially outperforms prior SSM/hybrid VLMs; delivers ~10x lower time-to-first-token than Transformer baselines at matched scale.
AI.conclusion: Zamba2-VL demonstrates that hybrid SSM-transformer architectures can match Transformer VLM quality while offering significant efficiency advantages, making them highly suitable for edge and on-device deployment.

title: RA-LWLM: Retrieval-Augmented In-Context Localization with Wireless Foundation Models
comment: 13 pages, 9 figures. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Wireless localization is a fundamental capability of sixth-generation (6G) networks. Conventional model-based methods require accurate modeling of the propagation environment and degrade in complex multipath and non-line-of-sight scenarios, while learning-based methods couple model parameters tightly to the training scene, requiring costly retraining whenever the base station (BS) configuration or propagation environment changes. In this paper, we propose RA-LWLM, a retrieval-augmented in-context localization framework that achieves training-free cross-scene adaptation by externalizing scene-specific information into a per-scene fingerprint database rather than encoding it in model weights. The framework consists of three components: a frozen wireless foundation model (FM) encoder that maps raw channel state information into a scene-agnostic representation; a retrieval module that selects the most informative references from the per-scene database via similarity search in the representation space; and a transformer-based in-context learning (ICL) module that fuses the query with the retrieved references to predict the user equipment (UE) position. To accommodate varying retrieval quality and propagation complexity across queries, the ICL module adopts a mixture-of-experts design in which experts specialize in different context sizes and are softly combined by a learnable selector. Extensive ray-tracing-based experiments across heterogeneous scenes with diverse BS configurations show that RA-LWLM achieves nearly identical accuracy on seen and unseen scenes without any per-scene retraining, substantially outperforming end-to-end and FM-based baselines. These results validate the proposed retrieval-augmented in-context paradigm as a scalable solution for cross-scene localization in 6G networks.

AI.tldr: RA-LWLM is a retrieval-augmented in-context learning framework for 6G wireless localization that achieves training-free cross-scene adaptation by storing scene-specific information in a fingerprint database rather than model weights, using a frozen foundation model encoder, retrieval module, and mixture-of-experts transformer for position prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing model-based localization methods degrade in complex multipath/NLOS scenarios, while learning-based methods require costly retraining when BS configuration or propagation environment changes. A scalable, cross-scene localization solution is needed.
AI.method: Three components: (1) frozen wireless foundation model encoder for scene-agnostic representation, (2) retrieval module for similarity search in per-scene fingerprint database, (3) transformer-based ICL module with mixture-of-experts design combining different context-size experts.
AI.result: Ray-tracing experiments across heterogeneous scenes show nearly identical accuracy on seen and unseen scenes without any per-scene retraining, substantially outperforming end-to-end and FM-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: The retrieval-augmented in-context paradigm is validated as a scalable solution for cross-scene localization in 6G networks, achieving training-free adaptation across diverse BS configurations and propagation environments.

title: PE-means: Improved Differentially Private $k$-means Clustering through Private Evolution
summary: We study the problem of differentially private (DP) $k$-means clustering in Euclidean space. Previous solutions rely on summing the private data directly, which induces a sensitivity proportional to the domain. We introduce PE-means, an extension of the private evolution (PE) algorithm (an increasingly popular method for synthetic data generation), to the problem of $k$-means clustering. The key advantage of PE is that it only computes a private histogram with constant sensitivity to guide the evolution. Our adaptation of PE includes new evolutionary operators for clustering, as well as other algorithmic improvements of independent interest. Overall, PE-means achieves an average improvement of 20% in clustering loss over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: PE-means adapts the private evolution algorithm for differentially private k-means clustering, using a constant-sensitivity histogram to guide evolution, achieving 20% better clustering loss than state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Existing DP k-means clustering methods sum private data directly, causing sensitivity to scale with the domain size, which degrades utility.
AI.method: Extends the private evolution (PE) algorithm to k-means by computing only a private histogram with constant sensitivity, introducing new evolutionary operators tailored for clustering.
AI.result: PE-means achieves an average 20% improvement in clustering loss over state-of-the-art DP k-means baselines.
AI.conclusion: The PE approach (constant-sensitivity histogram + evolutionary operators) is effective for DP clustering, and the algorithmic improvements are independently useful.

title: AXIOM: A Trust-First Neuro-Symbolic Execution Architecture for Verifiable Mathematical Reasoning
comment: Preprint. 12 pages, 2 figures. Live interactive demo: https://huggingface.co/spaces/Squagghy/axiom-solver. Paper artifact and dataset on Zenodo (concept-DOI): 10.5281/zenodo.20440225
summary: We present AXIOM, a trust-first neuro-symbolic execution architecture for natural-language mathematical reasoning. In AXIOM, the language model functions strictly as a canonicalizer: it rewrites informal problem text into a narrow schema consumed by a deterministic Computer-Algebra-System (CAS) pipeline, which derives and verifies the answer or abstains as a first-class output. Routing follows a 1:1:1 alignment between problem-shape regex, schema-specific prompt, and closed-form CAS handler, with 3,100+ such routes shipped and zero LOST_CORRECT regressions across 250+ consecutive ship commits. We report empirical results on 4 MATH categories with a cumulative correctness of 94.36% (2,592/2,747) at 100.00% trust on parseable (zero confident-wrong answers across the full 2,747-record benchmark), all four domains above the per-domain 70/90/70 floor with per-domain trust at 100.0%, and median latency of 1 ms on rule-only handlers (88% of records on the lm-eval arithmetic 20,000-record benchmark). The architecture has served ~30,000 production queries through a public deployment. The contribution we emphasize is not a final accuracy figure but the forward dynamic the architecture establishes: every logged abstain in production is a candidate correct after one ship cycle, since new tasks compose without regressing the registry. The operational discipline behind this property -- math-template bucketing, LOST_CORRECT scan as regression oracle, parseable-first onboarding, and abstain as first-class output -- constitutes a transferable framework for trustworthy neuro-symbolic systems beyond mathematics.

AI.tldr: AXIOM is a trust-first neuro-symbolic architecture for math reasoning where the LM acts only as a canonicalizer, routing problems via regex patterns to deterministic CAS handlers, achieving 94.36% cumulative correctness with zero false positives and 100% trust on parseable problems.
AI.motivation: To address the trust and reliability issues in LLM-based mathematical reasoning by enforcing a strict separation where the language model cannot produce mathematical outputs directly—only the deterministic CAS pipeline can derive answers.
AI.method: The LM serves only as a canonicalizer (rewriting informal text into a schema), which is consumed by a deterministic CAS pipeline. Routing uses 1:1:1 alignment of problem-shape regex, schema-specific prompt, and closed-form CAS handler (3,100+ routes). Abstain is a first-class output. A LOST_CORRECT scan serves as a regression oracle.
AI.result: 94.36% cumulative correctness (2,592/2,747) at 100% trust on parseable problems (zero wrong answers). All four MATH domains exceed 70/90/70 floor with 100% per-domain trust. Median latency of 1ms on rule-only handlers. Served ~30,000 production queries.
AI.conclusion: The key contribution is not accuracy but the forward dynamic: every logged abstain is a candidate correct after one ship cycle, as new tasks compose without regressing existing routes. The operational discipline (math-template bucketing, LOST_CORRECT scan, parseable-first onboarding, abstain as first-class output) offers a transferable framework for trustworthy neuro-symbolic systems.

title: Beyond Pure Sampling: Hybrid Optimization Mechanisms for Non-Convex Model Predictive Control
summary:
This paper investigates the optimization mechanisms of non-convex Model Predictive Control (MPC) using the Maximum Entropy Differential Dynamic Programming (ME-DDP) framework. Navigating non-convex cost landscapes induced by nonlinear dynamics, multiple obstacles, etc. remains a fundamental challenge in robotics, where gradient-based methods frequently converge to suboptimal local minima. We demonstrate a dual-step optimization mechanism designed to overcome these traps. (1) an initial phase of using DDP to exploit the gradient of the cost landscape, followed by (2) disruption of the optimization via sampling from policies characterized by the inverse Hessian of the action-value function. We provide a rigorous analysis of this sampling mechanism of three ME-DDP variants: Unimodal Gaussian ME-DDP, Multimodal Gaussian ME-DDP, and Stein Variational DDP. Furthermore, with navigation tasks of four robotic systems under cluttered environments, we conduct extensive benchmarking of three variants of the ME-DDP, against deterministic DDP, and one of the most successful sampling-based schemes, Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control with three policy parameterizations and update laws that correspond to those of ME-DDPs.
  The results show that in low-dimensional systems where the cost landscapes are relatively simple and local information is sufficiently representative, our framework consistently outperforms MPPIs. In high-dimensional systems, MPPI can occasionally discover aggressive maneuvers that enable it to steer the systems faster than DDP-based methods, whereas our method maintains a higher, more stable success rate.
  Finally, we validate the practical efficacy of the framework through hardware experiments with a quadrotor navigating a dense, non-convex obstacle field, confirming the robustness of the proposed framework for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Maximum Entropy Differential Dynamic Programming (ME-DDP) framework for non-convex MPC that combines gradient-based DDP with inverse-Hessian sampling to escape local minima, showing superior success rates over deterministic DDP and MPPI in both simulation and real-world quadrotor experiments.
AI.motivation: Non-convex optimization in MPC (due to nonlinear dynamics and obstacles) causes gradient-based methods to converge to suboptimal local minima, limiting robotic performance in cluttered environments.
AI.method: A dual-step mechanism: (1) DDP exploits cost gradients, then (2) sampling from policies characterized by the inverse Hessian of the action-value function disrupts optimization. Three variants analyzed: Unimodal Gaussian ME-DDP, Multimodal Gaussian ME-DDP, and Stein Variational DDP.
AI.result: ME-DDP consistently outperforms MPPI in low-dimensional systems. In high-dimensional systems, MPPI may find faster aggressive maneuvers but ME-DDP maintains higher and more stable success rates. Hardware experiments on a quadrotor confirm real-world robustness.
AI.conclusion: The ME-DDP framework effectively balances exploitation (gradient-based DDP) and exploration (inverse-Hessian sampling) for robust non-convex MPC, and is validated for real-world deployment on robotic systems.

title: Rethinking Amortized Neural Representations for High-Resolution Terrain Elevation Data
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) model a signal as a continuous coordinate-to-value function. For terrain elevation data, this supports analytic derivatives, arbitrary-resolution decoding, and a smooth surface model of the underlying heightfield. However, fitting and storing a separate INR for every tile does not scale to large terrain datasets. Amortized neural representations reduce this cost with a shared network: a new tile is mapped to a compact per-tile payload, and a shared decoder reconstructs the heightfield from it. Most such methods are hypernetworks that predict the payload in a single forward pass, while others recover it through a short per-tile optimization. These methods were developed primarily for natural images, and their suitability for terrain heightfields remains unclear. We introduce a controlled benchmark on a 1 m/pixel terrain dataset and evaluate three representative methods under a unified protocol. Observing a clear cross-domain gap, we propose HUVR+SIREN, a hypernetwork that adapts the strongest benchmarked method (HUVR) by replacing its coordinate decoder with a smooth, analytically differentiable one. It attains the best height and derivative fidelity on the benchmark with no additional per-tile storage and lower decode cost, and tolerates aggressive post-training quantization with negligible quality loss, giving a compact terrain neural format. Ablations and diagnostics further identify which design choices transfer to terrain and show that the per-tile bottleneck is already near its useful limit, leaving the remaining gap in the shared hypernetwork's architectural design.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks implicit neural representations for terrain elevation data, finding a cross-domain gap between natural images and terrain. They propose HUVR+SIREN, which adapts the best method by using a smooth, differentiable coordinate decoder, achieving best fidelity with no extra storage and lower cost.
AI.motivation: Existing amortized neural representations (hypernetworks) were developed for natural images, and it's unclear if they are suitable for terrain heightfields. There is a need for a controlled benchmark and adaptation for terrain data.
AI.method: They introduce a controlled benchmark on 1 m/pixel terrain data, evaluate three representative methods, and propose HUVR+SIREN—a hypernetwork that replaces the coordinate decoder with a smooth, analytically differentiable one.
AI.result: HUVR+SIREN achieves the best height and derivative fidelity with no additional per-tile storage and lower decode cost. It also tolerates aggressive post-training quantization with negligible quality loss.
AI.conclusion: The per-tile bottleneck is near its useful limit; the remaining gap lies in the shared hypernetwork's architectural design. HUVR+SIREN provides a compact terrain neural format.

title: LO-Free Joint Communication and Sensing via Inter-Antenna Cross-Correlation and Graph-Based Spatial Phase Inference
comment: This paper has been accepted to PIMRC 2026
summary: Joint communication and sensing (JCAS) typically rely on coherent downconversion to recover the phase relationships required for array processing. Meanwhile, Local Oscillators (LOs) are a major source of cost, power consumption, and implementation complexity in millimeter-wave (mmWave) and sub-THz receivers. Existing LO-free receiver designs are typically based on envelope detection or related non-coherent operations that do not preserve inter-branch phase information, which limits their applicability to JCAS. This work proposes an LO-free JCAS receiver architecture that leverages pairwise inter-branch correlation processing to suppress the common carrier component and to synthesize relative-phase observables across the antenna array, enabling both data communication and Direction-of-Arrival (DoA) estimation. The transmitted symbols are designed to induce distinct phase-difference patterns, such that the resulting correlation phases contain both a data-dependent component and a DoA-dependent component. We formulate recovery as inference over a correlation graph, where branches are nodes and pairwise correlations are edges, and show that the resulting cycle-consistent redundancy enables robust relative-phase recovery under noise and perturbations. We further derive a topology-aware Cramér-Rao lower bound for DoA estimation under a locally unwrapped approximation. Numerical results confirm that increasing graph connectivity improves both bit-error rate and DoA accuracy, with sensing performance approaching the derived bound.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LO-free JCAS receiver that uses pairwise inter-branch correlation processing to recover phase information for both communication and DoA estimation, avoiding costly local oscillators in mmWave/sub-THz systems.
AI.motivation: Local oscillators (LOs) are a major source of cost, power consumption, and complexity in mmWave/sub-THz receivers. Existing LO-free designs (e.g., envelope detection) lose inter-branch phase information, limiting their use in JCAS applications that require array processing.
AI.method: The receiver leverages pairwise inter-branch correlation to suppress the common carrier component and synthesize relative-phase observables. Symbols are designed to induce distinct phase-difference patterns containing both data and DoA components. Recovery is formulated as inference over a correlation graph, with cycle-consistent redundancy enabling robust phase recovery. A topology-aware Cramér-Rao lower bound for DoA estimation is derived.
AI.result: Numerical results show that increasing graph connectivity improves both bit-error rate and DoA estimation accuracy, with sensing performance approaching the derived theoretical bound.
AI.conclusion: The LO-free architecture is viable for JCAS, enabling both communication and sensing without costly local oscillators, with performance that improves with correlation graph connectivity.

title: The role of class encoding in neural collapse
summary: Neural collapse is a structural property of the last-hidden-layer activations in neural network classification models, when trained beyond a zero classification error. In this work, we explore the role of label encoding in neural collapse by relying on the unrestricted feature model with mean squared error training loss. We demonstrate that, for one-hot encoded labels and balanced data, the uncentered mean features associated with each class transition from a simplex equiangular tight frame to an orthogonal frame when increasing the bias regularization coefficient associated with the final classifier. These structures are reminiscent of the orthogonal frame structure of one-hot encoded labels. For any arbitrary encoding, we also show that the final classifier's bias aims at centering the labels, compensating for the discrepancy between the global mean of the labels and the origin. We further discuss the role of the encoding in other neural collapse properties.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how label encoding affects neural collapse in neural networks, showing that one-hot labels transition from simplex to orthogonal frames with bias regularization, and that bias centers labels to compensate for mean discrepancies.
AI.motivation: To understand the role of label encoding in neural collapse, particularly how different label encodings and bias regularization influence the geometric structure of last-layer activations.
AI.method: Uses the unrestricted feature model with mean squared error (MSE) training loss to theoretically analyze neural collapse structures under different label encodings and bias regularization.
AI.result: For one-hot labels with balanced data, uncentered mean features transition from a simplex equiangular tight frame to an orthogonal frame as bias regularization increases. For arbitrary encodings, the classifier's bias centers the labels, compensating for the mean discrepancy.
AI.conclusion: Label encoding significantly influences neural collapse geometry; bias regularization can shift feature structures, and the bias term plays a label-centering role that generalizes across encoding schemes.

title: Medication-Aware Financial Exploitation Detection for Alzheimer's Patients Using Edge-Aware Interaction Risk Modeling
summary: Financial exploitation is a growing concern for people with Alzheimer's disease, especially during periods of reduced cognitive stability. Conventional fraud detection systems usually rely on financial behavior alone and ignore clinically relevant factors that may alter vulnerability. This paper proposes a medication-aware framework that synchronizes medication adherence with transaction-level monitoring to improve detection of cognitively risky financial events. A hybrid simulation dataset was constructed for 180 patients across 45 days, producing 8,100 medication records and 30,855 transactions. The framework evaluates amount anomaly, vendor novelty, transaction frequency, time deviation, and medication adherence through financial-only, additive medication-aware, and interaction-aware logistic models. Results show that the financial-only baseline obtained the highest global F1-score of 0.5000, but the interaction-aware model improved recall during medication-induced vulnerability windows from 0.7442 to 0.9070 and achieved the highest average precision for ranked high-risk cases. The findings suggest that medication adherence is most useful as a contextual modifier of financial risk rather than as an isolated predictor.

AI.tldr: A medication-aware fraud detection framework integrating medication adherence with financial transactions improves recall during vulnerability windows for Alzheimer's patients, showing medication works best as a contextual modifier rather than an isolated predictor.
AI.motivation: Financial exploitation is a growing concern for Alzheimer's patients during cognitive instability. Conventional fraud detection ignores clinically relevant factors like medication adherence that may alter vulnerability.
AI.method: A hybrid simulation dataset (180 patients, 45 days, 8,100 medication records, 30,855 transactions) was built. Three logistic models were compared: financial-only, additive medication-aware, and interaction-aware, evaluating amount anomaly, vendor novelty, frequency, time deviation, and medication adherence.
AI.result: Financial-only baseline had the highest global F1 (0.5000), but the interaction-aware model improved recall during medication-induced vulnerability windows from 0.7442 to 0.9070 and achieved the highest average precision for high-risk cases.
AI.conclusion: Medication adherence is most useful as a contextual modifier of financial risk rather than as an isolated predictor for detecting exploitation in Alzheimer's patients.

title: STEM: Semantic Target Search and Exploration using MAVs in Cluttered Environments
comment: Accepted to Autonomous Robots Journal. Nikhil Sethi and Max Lodel contributed equally
summary: Autonomous target search is crucial for deploying Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAVs) in emergency response and rescue missions. Existing approaches either focus on 2D semantic navigation in structured environments -- which is less effective in complex 3D settings, or on robotic exploration in cluttered spaces -- which often lacks the semantic reasoning needed for efficient target search. This paper overcomes these limitations by proposing a novel framework that utilizes a semantically-guided viewpoint planner to minimize target search and exploration time in unstructured 3D environments using an MAV. Specifically, we develop a combinatorial planner that generates efficient semantic exploration plans by prioritizing viewpoints that likely lead to the target. To guide the planner towards the target, an active perception pipeline is developed that propagates semantic priorities of observed objects into neighboring frontier voxels for computing semantic information gains of frontier viewpoints. In addition, we demonstrate how LLM-based similarity scores can be leveraged as semantic priority input to our pipeline. Evaluations in two distinct simulation environments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms baselines by quickly finding the target while maintaining reasonable exploration times. Real-world experiments with an MAV further demonstrate the method's ability to handle practical constraints like limited battery life, small sensor range, and semantic uncertainty.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a semantically-guided viewpoint planning framework for MAVs that combines semantic priorities with exploration to efficiently find targets in unstructured 3D environments, leveraging LLM-based similarity scores, and outperforms baselines in simulations and real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing methods either focus on 2D semantic navigation (ineffective in 3D) or robotic exploration without semantic reasoning, lacking efficient target search in unstructured 3D environments for emergency response and rescue missions.
AI.method: A combinatorial planner generates efficient semantic exploration plans by prioritizing viewpoints likely to lead to the target. An active perception pipeline propagates semantic priorities from observed objects to neighboring frontier voxels for computing information gains. LLM-based similarity scores serve as semantic priority input.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in two simulation environments by quickly finding the target with reasonable exploration times. Real-world MAV experiments demonstrate handling of limited battery, small sensor range, and semantic uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: The semantically-guided viewpoint planning framework effectively bridges the gap between 2D semantic navigation and 3D exploration for autonomous target search, with practical viability demonstrated in real-world conditions.

title: 4D Radar Meets LiDAR and Camera: Cooperative Perception under Adverse Weather
summary: Cooperative perception is important for autonomous driving but remains fragile when cameras and LiDAR degrade in adverse weather. We address this challenge by integrating 4D imaging radar as a weather-robust modality into collaborative perception and introducing a Doppler-guided spatial attention mechanism for multi-agent fusion. Our approach extends two representative backbones: a radar-camera pipeline where radar substitutes LiDAR, and a LiDAR-radar pipeline where radar complements LiDAR. To support evaluation, we release radar-augmented benchmarks, OPV2V-R and Adver-City-R, with physics-based LiDAR degradation. Experiments show strong robustness gains in fog and rain, including substantial improvements when radar replaces degraded LiDAR. Additional validation on MAN TruckScenes demonstrates transfer beyond simulation. Overall, our results highlight 4D imaging radar as a robust modality for all-weather collaborative perception. Dataset and code are available at: https://url.fzi.de/SlimComm.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances cooperative perception for autonomous driving by integrating 4D imaging radar as a weather-robust modality, introducing a Doppler-guided spatial attention mechanism, and releasing new radar-augmented benchmarks (OPV2V-R and Adver-City-R). Experiments show significant robustness gains in fog and rain, with validation on real-world MAN TruckScenes data.
AI.motivation: Cooperative perception in autonomous driving is fragile when cameras and LiDAR degrade in adverse weather conditions such as fog and rain.
AI.method: Integrates 4D imaging radar as a weather-robust modality and introduces a Doppler-guided spatial attention mechanism for multi-agent fusion, extending two backbones: radar-camera and LiDAR-radar pipelines.
AI.result: Strong robustness gains in fog and rain, substantial improvements when radar replaces degraded LiDAR, and successful transfer to real-world MAN TruckScenes data.
AI.conclusion: 4D imaging radar is a robust modality for all-weather collaborative perception in autonomous driving.

title: High-order synchrosqueezed wavelet-chirplet transform for instantaneous frequency and chirprate estimation
summary: The separation of multicomponent signals with crossing instantaneous frequency (IF) curves remains a fundamental challenge in time-frequency analysis. Although the synchrosqueezed wavelet-chirplet transform (SWCT) enhances time-frequency readability by introducing a chirprate variable, its effectiveness is constrained by the underlying assumption of local linear chirp. Consequently, this method does not perform well when analyzing signals characterized by strong frequency modulation. This paper extends the SWCT framework by relaxing the linear chirp assumption. We model signal components as having polynomial phase behavior over short intervals and derive compact expressions for high-order IF and chirprate reassignment operators. The proposed high-order synchrosqueezed wavelet-chirplet transform (HSWCT) enables accurate estimation of both IF and chirprate, and supports robust mode retrieval even with intersecting IF curves. Another key contribution is a rigorous mathematical analysis of the approximation errors of arbitrary-order reassignment operators for IF and chirprate estimation. When the chirprate vanishes, HSWCT simplifies to the traditional high-order synchrosqueezed wavelet transform. To our best knowledge, no theoretical analysis exists in the literature on the approximation of arbitrary-order SST IF reassignment operators to the IF. As a by-product of this work, our established theorem provides such an analysis, thereby filling a gap in the theoretical framework of high-order SSTs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the High-order Synchrosqueezed Wavelet-Chirplet Transform (HSWCT) to overcome the linear chirp limitation of SWCT, enabling accurate estimation of IF and chirprate for signals with strong frequency modulation and crossing IF curves. It also provides rigorous theoretical analysis of arbitrary-order reassignment operator errors, filling a gap in high-order SST theory.
AI.motivation: The SWCT method fails for signals with strong frequency modulation because it assumes local linear chirp behavior. There is also a lack of theoretical analysis on approximation errors of arbitrary-order IF reassignment operators.
AI.method: The authors relax the linear chirp assumption by modeling signal components as having polynomial phase behavior over short intervals, deriving high-order IF and chirprate reassignment operators, and proposing the HSWCT framework.
AI.result: HSWCT provides accurate IF and chirprate estimation and robust mode retrieval for signals with crossing IF curves. When chirprate vanishes, HSWCT reduces to the traditional high-order SST. A rigorous theorem on approximation errors of arbitrary-order reassignment operators is established.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HSWCT extends SWCT to handle strong frequency modulation, and the theoretical analysis fills a gap in the literature on high-order SST approximation errors.

title: Longitudinal Multimodal Sensing of Physical Activity and Well-Being in Older Adults
summary: Wearable and mobile sensing technologies enable continuous monitoring of human behavior and health in real-world settings. However, predictive modeling in longitudinal multimodal data remains challenging, particularly when targeting complex or clinically derived outcomes. In this work, we present a longitudinal multimodal study of 66 older adults conducted in real-world conditions and combining wearable sensing, behavioral monitoring, and clinical assessments. This setting provides a rare opportunity to study an underrepresented population in long-term, into-the-wild conditions. Building on this dataset, we investigate how the alignment between sensed signals and target variables affects predictive performance across health-related tasks. We design a unified evaluation framework spanning tasks with increasing levels of observability, including Activity Levels prediction, Sleep Duration estimation, and Sleep Apnea Severity classification. Our results reveal a clear gradient of predictability: highly observable behavioral targets achieve robust performance (macro-F1 65%), while more abstract outcomes remain challenging despite consistent improvements over baseline models. Moreover, through explainability analysis, we show that historical features consistently emerge as the most informative predictors, highlighting the central role of longitudinal information.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes longitudinal multimodal data from 66 older adults in real-world settings, demonstrating that predictive performance correlates with target observability—behavioral targets achieve macro-F1 65%, while abstract clinical outcomes remain challenging—and that historical features are the most informative predictors.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of predictive modeling in longitudinal multimodal data, particularly for complex clinical outcomes, and to study an underrepresented older adult population in real-world, long-term conditions.
AI.method: The authors conducted a longitudinal multimodal study of 66 older adults using wearable sensing and behavioral monitoring. They designed a unified evaluation framework spanning three tasks with increasing target abstraction: Activity Levels prediction, Sleep Duration estimation, and Sleep Apnea Severity classification.
AI.result: A clear gradient of predictability was found: highly observable behavioral targets achieved macro-F1 of 65%, while more abstract clinical outcomes remained challenging, though still showing consistent improvements over baselines. Historical features were the most informative predictors.
AI.conclusion: The alignment between sensed signals and target variables critically affects predictive performance. Longitudinal information is central to prediction, and abstract clinical outcomes remain difficult despite improvements.

title: Regularized Offline Policy Optimization with Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to optimize policies from pre-collected datasets. A bottleneck of this paradigm is managing epistemic uncertainty, which arises from limited data coverage (sample-level) and the ambiguity in identifying transition dynamics from finite data (model-level). To provide a unified quantification of these uncertainties, Bayesian RL has been proposed by treating the dynamics model as a random variable and maintaining a corresponding belief. Despite its theoretical appeal, policy optimization in Bayesian RL remains computationally challenging as it requires solving composite objectives with expectations. Prior methods either employ search-based techniques with poor computational scalability or impose restrictive posterior assumptions that sacrifice the adaptability of Bayesian RL. To address these limitations, we propose Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief (PhyB), which reformulates the expectation as a convex combination over a subset of dynamics models. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the objective discrepancy induced by this approximation remains bounded. Based on PhyB, we develop an iterative regularized policy optimization algorithm that provides metric-agnostic guarantees for monotonic improvement until convergence. Empirical results demonstrate that PhyB achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks.

AI.tldr: PhyB reformulates Bayesian RL's expectation as a convex combination over a subset of dynamics models, providing a scalable yet theoretically bounded approximation with monotonic improvement guarantees and SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Offline RL suffers from epistemic uncertainty at both sample-level and model-level. Bayesian RL theoretically addresses this but is computationally challenging due to composite objectives with expectations. Prior methods lack scalability or sacrifice adaptability.
AI.method: Proposes Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief (PhyB), which approximates the expectation in Bayesian RL as a convex combination over a subset of dynamics models. Develops an iterative regularized policy optimization algorithm with bounded objective discrepancy and monotonic improvement guarantees.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows the approximation discrepancy is bounded. PhyB achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PhyB effectively addresses the scalability-adaptability trade-off in Bayesian offline RL, offering both theoretical guarantees and empirical superiority.

title: SafeVLA-Bench: A Benchmark for the Success-Safety Gap in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) benchmarks measure whether a policy completes a requested manipulation task, but binary success can hide safety-relevant trajectory behavior: reaching the goal while applying excessive contact, disturbing bystander objects, destabilizing the held object, or entering robot self-contact. We present SafeVLA-Bench, a post-hoc safety-evaluation framework for existing simulator-based VLA benchmarks. It formalizes task-aware safety requirements as Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications and reports native success with two unsafe-success metrics: Succ-But-Unsafe (SBU), the fraction of rollouts that both succeed and violate safety, and Violation Severity Index (VSI), a bounded worst-violation depth score. We instantiate SafeVLA-Bench on LIBERO and RoboCasa-365, evaluating nine policy-benchmark entries across tabletop and kitchen manipulation tasks. High task success does not imply safe execution: high-SR tabletop baselines still leave 13 to 15 percent unsafe-episode rates,and 36 to 56 percent of successful RoboCasa-365 rollouts violate at least one active safety clause. Project page: https://safevla.org.

AI.tldr: SafeVLA-Bench is a post-hoc safety evaluation framework for VLA benchmarks that measures unsafe success behaviors (excessive force, disturbing objects, destabilizing objects, self-contact) using STL-based safety specifications and two metrics (SBU and VSI). Applied to LIBERO and RoboCasa-365, it shows high task success does not imply safe execution, with 13-15% unsafe episodes in tabletop tasks and 36-56% of successful RoboCasa-365 rollouts violating safety.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA benchmarks only measure binary task success, which hides unsafe trajectory behaviors such as excessive contact, disturbing bystander objects, destabilizing held objects, and robot self-contact during successful task completion.
AI.method: The paper introduces SafeVLA-Bench, a post-hoc safety evaluation framework that: (1) formalizes task-aware safety requirements as Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications, and (2) reports two metrics—Succ-But-Unsafe (SBU) for fraction of successful but unsafe rollouts, and Violation Severity Index (VSI) for bounded worst-violation depth. It is instantiated on LIBERO and RoboCasa-365 benchmarks.
AI.result: Evaluating nine policy-benchmark entries, the framework reveals that high task success does not guarantee safety: tabletop baselines show 13-15% unsafe-episode rates, and 36-56% of successful RoboCasa-365 rollouts violate at least one safety clause.
AI.conclusion: Binary success metrics in VLA benchmarks are insufficient for safety assessment; SafeVLA-Bench provides a systematic way to uncover unsafe behaviors hidden behind successful task completion, highlighting the need for safety-aware evaluation in manipulation policy benchmarking.

title: Detect Before You Leap: Mirage Detection in Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) can produce confident visual answers even when the required visual evidence is missing, blank, or unrelated to the question. This failure mode, known as mirage (Asadi et al. 2026), is especially concerning in medical and document visual question answering, where plausible but visually ungrounded responses may be mistaken for image-based evidence. We study pre-release mirage detection: given an image-question pair, the goal is to determine whether a VLM should answer or abstain before producing a response. We propose Text-Conditioned Layer-wise Internal Alignment (TC-LIA), a model-agnostic method that probes patch-token representations across the layers of a CLIP ViT-H/14 vision encoder. TC-LIA projects layer-wise image patch tokens into the final CLIP embedding space and measures their similarity to the question embedding, allowing the method to track whether question-relevant visual evidence emerges across vision layers. The resulting alignment trajectory is summarized using final image-text cosine similarity, late-layer top-k patch-text alignment, early-to-late gain, and layer-wise slope. These features are combined with pixel-statistic blank/noise detection, zero-shot domain routing, and structured VLM self-assessment in an ensemble. Across five VQA domains, three input conditions, and twelve VLM backbones, the best systems achieve approximately 94.6-94.7% three-class detection accuracy with mirage rates below 3%, while baseline mirage rates range from 21.7% to 66.6%.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes TC-LIA, a method to detect when VLMs produce confident but visually ungrounded answers ("mirage"), achieving ~95% detection accuracy across multiple domains and models.
AI.motivation: VLMs confidently answer questions even when visual evidence is missing/irrelevant, causing harmful misdiagnoses in medical and document VQA. Preemptive detection is needed before response generation.
AI.method: TC-LIA probes CLIP ViT-H/14 patch-token representations across layers, projects them into final embedding space, measures alignment with question embeddings, and combines trajectory features with pixel-statistic detection, domain routing, and VLM self-assessment in an ensemble.
AI.result: Achieves ~94.6-94.7% three-class detection accuracy with mirage rates below 3%, compared to baseline mirage rates of 21.7-66.6%, across 5 VQA domains, 3 input conditions, and 12 VLM backbones.
AI.conclusion: Pre-release mirage detection is feasible and effective; TC-LIA significantly reduces ungrounded VLM responses across diverse settings.

title: Integrated Sensing and Covert Communication In Low-Altitude Networks: A Smart Radio Environment Perspective
summary: The rise of low-altitude economies and 6G is driving the evolution of low-altitude networks (LANs), making communication security a pressing concern. Unlike traditional security approaches, covert communication offers enhanced protection by hiding the transmission behavior itself. Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), a key technology of 6G, efficiently supports both sensing and communication tasks through hardware integration, thereby promising significant gains for covert communication. Nevertheless, the complexity and dynamics of urban environments pose critical challenges. Drawing on the latest advances in smart radio environment (SRE) technologies, this paper introduces them into integrated sensing and covert communication (ISACC) to suppress covert channel fading and counteract sensing precision loss in LANs. We first survey the applications and state-of-the-art findings of ISACC in LANs, highlighting key practical challenges. Subsequently, we introduce the core concept of SRE and elaborate on its enabling techniques across four dimensions. To deliver more insights, we explore potential pathways for integrating SRE into ISACC. To maximize covert throughput, a reinforcement learning-based case study is conducted by jointly optimizing flight trajectory, jamming power, movable antenna position, bandwidth allocation, and beamforming vectors. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme achieves superior performance compared to the benchmark. Finally, some open challenges and potential directions are discussed.

AI.tldr: This paper surveys and proposes integrating Smart Radio Environment (SRE) technologies into Integrated Sensing and Covert Communication (ISACC) for low-altitude networks, using reinforcement learning to optimize multiple parameters and achieve improved covert throughput.
AI.motivation: The rise of low-altitude economies and 6G networks makes communication security critical. Traditional security approaches are insufficient, and covert communication (hiding transmission behavior) offers better protection. However, complex urban environments pose significant challenges for covert communication in low-altitude networks.
AI.method: The paper introduces Smart Radio Environment (SRE) technologies into Integrated Sensing and Covert Communication (ISACC) to suppress covert channel fading and counteract sensing precision loss. It surveys existing ISACC applications, elaborates on SRE enabling techniques across four dimensions, explores integration pathways, and conducts a reinforcement learning-based case study jointly optimizing flight trajectory, jamming power, movable antenna position, bandwidth allocation, and beamforming vectors.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed SRE-integrated ISACC scheme achieves superior performance compared to benchmark approaches in terms of covert throughput.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes by discussing open challenges and potential future research directions for SRE-integrated ISACC in low-altitude networks.

title: (HB-ARFM) History-Bootstrapped Flow Matching for Inverse Boiling Reconstruction
summary: Reconstructing spatiotemporal fields from partial observations is fundamental to scientific inference, from inferring atmospheric states from satellite data to recovering fluid states from imaging. When observations are incomplete, the inverse problem is fundamentally ill-posed: even when the underlying PDE dynamics are Markovian in the full state, partial observation operators induce a non-Markovian posterior that cannot be resolved from a single timestep. We propose a history-bootstrapped autoregressive flow matching (HB-ARFM) for spatiotemporal inverse reconstruction under partial observability. Observation history bootstraps the initial reconstruction via conditional flow matching, reducing ambiguities. The same conditional transport model is then applied autoregressively, conditioning on both new observations and past predictions to propagate the reconstruction forward in time. We evaluate the method on boiling dynamics reconstruction, recovering full velocity and temperature fields from interface geometry and motion. Across two inverse tasks with varying observation sparsity, HB-ARFM produces physically and temporally valid reconstructions where other models fail.

AI.tldr: HB-ARFM reconstructs spatiotemporal fields from partial observations using history-bootstrapped autoregressive flow matching, succeeding where other models fail on boiling dynamics tasks.
AI.motivation: Spatiotemporal inverse problems are ill-posed under partial observability; partial observations induce non-Markovian posteriors that cannot be resolved from a single timestep, requiring a more sophisticated approach.
AI.method: History-bootstrapped autoregressive flow matching (HB-ARFM): uses observation history to bootstrap initial reconstruction via conditional flow matching, then applies the same transport model autoregressively, conditioning on new observations and past predictions to propagate reconstruction forward.
AI.result: On boiling dynamics reconstruction (recovering velocity and temperature fields from interface geometry/motion), HB-ARFM produces physically and temporally valid reconstructions across two inverse tasks with varying observation sparsity, outperforming other models that fail.
AI.conclusion: HB-ARFM effectively addresses ill-posed spatiotemporal inverse reconstruction under partial observability, demonstrating robust performance where existing methods fall short.

title: MOSAIC: Modular Orchestration for Structured Agentic Intelligence and Composition
summary: Automated data science is a structured model-selection problem. A solution must choose data transformations, feature representations, architecture, training procedure, evaluation protocol, and refinement strategy for a task. AutoML systems automate parts of this process, but typically search within predefined pipeline, model, and hyperparameter spaces. LLM-based agents offer greater flexibility through retrieval, code generation, and execution feedback, yet their modelling decisions are often unstructured, difficult to verify, and hard to reuse. We introduce \textsc{MOSAIC} (Modular Orchestration for Structured Agentic Intelligence and Composition), a structured agentic framework for memory-grounded model selection and workflow construction. Given a task and dataset, \textsc{MOSAIC} builds a semantic task profile, retrieves prior cases and source-code modules, and constructs a blueprint: an intermediate representation specifying selected modelling components, composition, interface constraints, and execution requirements. This blueprint turns model selection into a staged, context-grounded search and grounds LLM-based code generation in retrieved evidence rather than unconstrained synthesis. Candidate models are validated by execution and refined using diagnostic feedback, training traces, task metrics, and a failure-aware reinforcement learning policy. We instantiate \textsc{MOSAIC} on financial time-series forecasting and generation, where models must satisfy predictive accuracy, distributional fidelity, execution reliability, and downstream financial criteria such as risk and tail behaviour. Experiments against AutoML and agentic baselines show that \textsc{MOSAIC} improves task performance, execution success, and decision traceability, demonstrating the value of treating automated data science as structured, reusable, and execution-grounded model selection.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is a structured agentic framework that treats automated data science as a memory-grounded, staged model-selection process, outperforming AutoML and LLM baselines on financial time-series tasks by using blueprints, retrieval, and execution feedback.
AI.motivation: AutoML systems are limited to predefined spaces, while LLM-based agents offer flexibility but produce unstructured, unverifiable, and non-reusable modeling decisions. The paper aims to bridge this gap.
AI.method: MOSAIC builds a semantic task profile, retrieves prior cases and code modules, constructs a blueprint (intermediate representation of components, composition, interfaces, and execution requirements), validates models via execution, and refines them using diagnostic feedback and a failure-aware RL policy.
AI.result: On financial time-series forecasting and generation tasks, MOSAIC improves task performance, execution success, and decision traceability compared to AutoML and agentic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating automated data science as structured, reusable, and execution-grounded model selection yields measurable benefits, particularly in domains with strict criteria like predictive accuracy, distributional fidelity, and risk behavior.

title: Coarse-to-Fine Compositional Diffusion for Long-Horizon Planning
summary: Diffusion models provide strong priors for generating structured data, but many tasks require outputs beyond the scale on which these models are typically trained. Compositional generation addresses this by composing overlapping local plans from a pretrained short-horizon prior into a long-horizon output. However, standard composition primarily enforces agreement between neighboring local plans, yielding local consistency without directly specifying the global structure of the full composition. As a result, locally compatible plans may still form an implausible route, task sequence, or temporal evolution. Existing methods improve global coherence by repeatedly propagating local consistency signals or by adding inference-time optimization, but these procedures become expensive as the number or dimensionality of local plans increases.
