We propose Coarse-to-Fine Compositional Diffusion (CoFi), an inference-time sampler that separates global structure formation from local detail refinement. CoFi first aligns local denoised estimates around a shared coarse structure, producing a global scaffold that captures the long-range task-level arrangement. It then diffuses this scaffold to an intermediate noise level and denoises it with the same pretrained local prior, restoring local fine structure while preserving the scaffold-induced global coherence. Across long-horizon robotic planning, panoramic image generation, and long video generation, CoFi not only improves both global coherence and local sample quality over prior compositional baselines, but also requires 2-8x fewer denoiser evaluations.

AI.tldr: CoFi is an inference-time sampler that separates global structure formation from local detail refinement in compositional diffusion models, improving long-horizon generation across robotics, panoramas, and video tasks while requiring 2-8x fewer denoiser evaluations.
AI.motivation: Standard compositional diffusion enforces local consistency between neighboring plans but fails to capture global structure, leading to implausible long-range outputs. Existing global coherence methods are computationally expensive.
AI.method: CoFi first aligns local denoised estimates around a shared coarse structure to form a global scaffold, then diffuses this scaffold to an intermediate noise level and denoises it with the pretrained local prior, preserving global coherence while restoring fine details.
AI.result: CoFi improves both global coherence and local sample quality over prior compositional baselines across long-horizon robotic planning, panoramic image generation, and long video generation, while requiring 2-8x fewer denoiser evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Separating global structure formation from local refinement is an effective and efficient strategy for compositional generation with diffusion models, outperforming prior methods with significantly lower computational cost.

title: Physical Object Understanding with a Physically Controllable World Model
summary: A central challenge in visual intelligence is learning the physical structure of scenes from raw videos: how regions form objects and the laws that govern their interactions. Solving these tasks requires world models capable of inferring distributional states of the world from partial observations - capabilities that current architectures do not provide. We introduce a new class of probabilistic world models that support estimation of the probability of any visual variable, such as appearance and dynamics, conditioned on any other variables. Here, we identify that these models can be trained efficiently with autoregressive sequence modeling, yielding world models from which rich object understanding emerges. First, we demonstrate that our model captures the physical laws governing how objects move by generating multiple plausible future states of the world through sequential inference. Then, by analyzing motion correlations across these futures, we extract objects and articulated object subparts. Having discovered these objects, we show that our world model can manipulate them in 3D. Finally, we demonstrate how physical relationships between objects can be computed from the world model, enabling applications such as Visual Jenga.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces probabilistic world models trained with autoregressive sequence modeling that can learn physical structure, objects, and their interactions from raw videos, enabling object discovery, 3D manipulation, and physical relationship reasoning.
AI.motivation: A central challenge in visual intelligence is learning the physical structure of scenes from raw videos, requiring world models capable of inferring distributional states from partial observations—capabilities current architectures lack.
AI.method: The authors introduce a class of probabilistic world models that support estimation of any visual variable conditioned on others, trained efficiently with autoregressive sequence modeling.
AI.result: The model captures physical laws of object motion, extracts objects and articulated subparts via motion correlation analysis, enables 3D manipulation of discovered objects, and computes physical relationships between objects (e.g., Visual Jenga).
AI.conclusion: Autoregressive sequence modeling can yield probabilistic world models from which rich object understanding emerges, supporting future state generation, object discovery, manipulation, and physical reasoning.

title: Spectrum Anomaly Detection in OFDMA Systems: Simulation Framework and Benchmark Dataset
comment: 12 pages, 9 figures, submitted for publication to IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
summary: Wireless connectivity underpins modern society and industry, enabling critical applications such as 5G ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) for industrial automation. However, the openness of the wireless medium exposes it to spectrum anomalies, including unintentional interference and malicious jamming, which threaten communication and sensing functionalities in 5G and emerging 6G networks. Despite its importance, spectrum anomaly detection research is hindered by a lack of publicly available datasets reflecting real-world scenarios. To address this, we present a benchmark dataset for spectrum anomaly detection in orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing access (OFDMA) systems, a core technology for 5G and beyond. The dataset includes spectrograms generated across a distributed network of sensing units, covering five distinct jammer types, from simple noise to advanced pilot-aware attacks. These anomalies are simulated in an industrial factory environment using a versatile open-source framework developed and published as part of this work, enabling extensibility to new scenarios and interference types. We provide baseline evaluations for supervised and unsupervised learning methods, demonstrating the challenges posed by different jammers and highlighting areas for further research. The dataset and framework support reproducible studies and serve as a foundation for advancing spectrum anomaly detection, with applications extending to network digital twins. By bridging the gap in open dataset availability, this work empowers the research community to validate and compare advanced detection methods for resilient next-generation wireless systems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a benchmark dataset for spectrum anomaly detection in OFDMA systems (5G/6G), featuring five jammer types in an industrial factory environment, along with baseline evaluations for supervised and unsupervised learning methods.
AI.motivation: Spectrum anomaly detection research is hindered by a lack of publicly available datasets reflecting real-world scenarios, despite the critical need for resilient wireless communication in 5G/6G networks.
AI.method: The authors generated spectrograms across a distributed network of sensing units covering five jammer types (from simple noise to pilot-aware attacks), simulated in an industrial factory environment using a versatile open-source framework.
AI.result: Baseline evaluations for supervised and unsupervised learning methods demonstrate the challenges posed by different jammers and highlight areas for further research.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and framework support reproducible studies, bridge the gap in open dataset availability, and serve as a foundation for advancing spectrum anomaly detection with applications extending to network digital twins.

title: Drift Q-Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning requires improving a policy from fixed data while avoiding out-of-distribution actions with unreliable value estimates. Diffusion and flow policies handle this trade-off by modeling the behavior distribution to regularize the RL objective, but they require iterative denoising, solver integrations, and in more efficient variants, distillation or other approximations at inference. We propose DriftQL, which combines a drift-based behavioral regularizer with critic-driven policy improvement. The value signal biases the policy toward high-value regions of the data support, while attraction and repulsion together keep generated actions near the data and prevent collapse onto a single mode. DriftQL is implemented as a single network with a unified training objective and generates actions in a single forward pass. On D4RL and OGBench, DriftQL consistently outperforms diffusion and flow methods, advancing the state of the art. Under degraded data quality, where the baselines visibly struggle, DriftQL remains close to its clean-data performance, positioning it as a promising alternative to diffusion and flow-based methods while maintaining the simplicity and efficiency of deterministic approaches. Project page: https://driftql.github.io/

AI.tldr: DriftQL proposes a drift-based behavioral regularizer combined with critic-driven policy improvement for offline RL, achieving state-of-the-art results on D4RL and OGBench with a simple, single-pass architecture that outperforms diffusion and flow methods.
AI.motivation: Offline RL suffers from out-of-distribution actions and unreliable value estimates. Existing diffusion/flow policies handle this but require iterative denoising, solver integrations, distillation, or other approximations, making them computationally inefficient and complex.
AI.method: DriftQL combines a drift-based behavioral regularizer with critic-driven policy improvement. Attraction and repulsion forces keep generated actions near the data support while preventing mode collapse, all implemented as a single network with unified training objective that generates actions in one forward pass.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms diffusion and flow methods on D4RL and OGBench benchmarks. Under degraded data quality, DriftQL remains close to its clean-data performance while baselines visibly struggle.
AI.conclusion: DriftQL is a promising alternative to diffusion/flow-based methods, maintaining simplicity and efficiency of deterministic approaches while advancing state-of-the-art performance.

title: LLM-Driven Co-Evolutionary Automated Heuristic Design for Bi-Component Coupled Combinatorial Optimization
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently shown promise in Automated Heuristic Design (AHD), existing methods typically generate and evolve heuristics as a single operator or search strategy, limiting their ability to model strong coupling among multiple decision substructures in problems such as the Traveling Thief Problem (TTP) and the Traveling Purchaser Problem (TPP). In this work, we propose CoEvo-AHD, an LLM-driven dual-population co-evolutionary framework for automated heuristic design in coupled combinatorial optimization. Unlike prior methods that evolve individual heuristics in isolation, CoEvo-AHD leverages LLMs to co-evolve two closely related operator populations. A cooperative evaluation mechanism explicitly captures interactions between route and selection operators, while pairwise scoring and synergistic joint crossover help discover complementary operator logic for joint improvement across coupled decision subspaces. We further design a tool-invocation environment library that encapsulates frequently used core operations, such as local-search delta computation, into callable functions, enabling LLM-generated operators to use standardized interfaces instead of reimplementing inefficient and error-prone problem-specific loops. Experiments on TTP and TPP show that CoEvo-AHD automatically discovers cooperative heuristic combinations and achieves competitive solution quality against traditional heuristics.

AI.tldr: CoEvo-AHD is an LLM-driven framework that uses dual-population co-evolution to automatically design cooperative heuristics for coupled combinatorial optimization problems like TTP and TPP, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based AHD methods evolve heuristics as single operators, failing to capture strong couplings between multiple decision substructures in problems like TTP and TPP.
AI.method: CoEvo-AHD uses LLMs to co-evolve two operator populations (route and selection), with a cooperative evaluation mechanism, pairwise scoring, synergistic joint crossover, and a tool-invocation environment library for standardized interfaces.
AI.result: Experiments on TTP and TPP show CoEvo-AHD automatically discovers cooperative heuristic combinations and achieves competitive solution quality against traditional heuristics.
AI.conclusion: Co-evolution of coupled heuristics via LLMs is an effective approach for automated heuristic design in coupled combinatorial optimization problems.

title: From Cues to Horizons: Dynamic Risk Horizon Profiling for Trajectory Prediction
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems (T-ITS)
summary: Accurate and reliable vehicle trajectory prediction is essential for safe autonomous driving. Recent studies have incorporated safety risk into trajectory prediction to quantify dangers posed by surrounding agents. However, most risk-aware approaches use past risk information as a secondary signal to help guide decisions, overlooking its future evolution and uncertainty. In this paper, we propose a risk horizon profiling (RHP) module that incorporates a continuous, learnable potential field model for risk-aware trajectory prediction. The RHP module calculates the spatial-temporal proximity of surrounding objects to profile risk distributions across future horizons, which supports better trajectory prediction by adaptively identifying what human drivers perceive as critical moments. We evaluate our method on two datasets from different driving settings, highD for highway corridors and SHRP2 for urban streets, which cover diverse risk scenarios including safe, near-crash, and crash events. Compared to the baseline methods, our framework achieves a 25.0\% reduction in 5s RMSE on the highD dataset and a 29.1\% reduction in 5s minFDE on SHRP2. These results indicate strong performance for both short and long horizon prediction and robust generalization across highway and urban scenarios. The proposed method enables more realistic AV path planning and strategic selection, thereby supporting safer autonomous driving and more advanced driver-assistance systems. The source code for this work is available at: https://github.com/bilab-nyu/RHP

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Risk Horizon Profiling (RHP) module for trajectory prediction that incorporates a continuous, learnable potential field to model future risk distributions, achieving significant error reductions (25-29%) across highway and urban driving datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing risk-aware trajectory prediction methods rely on past risk information as a secondary signal, overlooking the future evolution and uncertainty of risk, which limits their effectiveness for safe autonomous driving.
AI.method: The authors introduce a Risk Horizon Profiling (RHP) module with a continuous, learnable potential field model that calculates spatial-temporal proximity of surrounding objects to profile risk distributions across future horizons, enabling adaptive identification of critical moments.
AI.result: 25.0% reduction in 5s RMSE on highD dataset and 29.1% reduction in 5s minFDE on SHRP2 dataset compared to baseline methods, with strong performance across both short and long horizon predictions in highway and urban scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RHP method enables more realistic autonomous vehicle path planning and strategic selection by incorporating future risk profiling, supporting safer autonomous driving and advanced driver-assistance systems.

title: Real-Time Physics Simulation with Dynamic Mesh-Gaussian Reconstructions
summary: Integrating dynamic 3D reconstructions into physics simulation requires fixed mesh topology for efficient collision detection, but state-of-the-art methods like DG-Mesh produce varying topology optimized for geometric quality. We investigate whether topology conversion can enable physics integration while preserving reconstruction fidelity. We propose a dual-representation framework combining fixed-topology meshes for physics with Gaussian splatting for rendering, achieving 4.65$\times$ speedup over varying-topology baselines through runtime vertex buffer updates. We evaluate two conversion strategies, temporal correspondence tracking and template-based projection, against native fixed-topology methods (MaGS) on the DG-Mesh dataset. Our evaluation reveals that both conversion approaches incur 65-80% geometric degradation, producing results inferior to MaGS despite DG-Mesh's superior initial quality. This demonstrates that high-quality reconstruction and physics-compatible topology represent fundamentally distinct objectives that cannot be reconciled through post-processing. Our findings inform future development of physics-aware reconstruction methods and our framework enables real-time simulation with any fixed-topology approach.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that converting variable-topology 3D meshes (DG-Mesh) to fixed-topology meshes for physics simulation causes 65-80% geometric degradation, and that reconstruction quality and physics-compatible topology are fundamentally conflicting goals that post-processing cannot reconcile.
AI.motivation: Integrating dynamic 3D reconstructions into physics simulation requires fixed mesh topology for efficient collision detection, but state-of-the-art methods like DG-Mesh produce varying topology optimized for geometric quality.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-representation framework combining fixed-topology meshes for physics with Gaussian splatting for rendering, achieving 4.65× speedup. Evaluates two conversion strategies (temporal correspondence tracking and template-based projection) against native fixed-topology methods (MaGS) on the DG-Mesh dataset.
AI.result: Both conversion approaches incur 65-80% geometric degradation, producing results inferior to MaGS despite DG-Mesh's superior initial quality. Achieves 4.65× speedup over varying-topology baselines.
AI.conclusion: High-quality reconstruction and physics-compatible topology represent fundamentally distinct objectives that cannot be reconciled through post-processing. The framework enables real-time simulation with any fixed-topology approach.

title: Scattering Environment Aware Joint Multi-BS Channel Estimation and Localization with Clock Asynchronism
summary: Clock asynchronism between base stations (BSs) and users significantly degrades scatterer localization accuracy. To address this issue, this paper proposes a multi-BS joint channel estimation and localization scheme that exploits shared scatterer information among multiple BSs. First, channel modeling in the location domain is performed by leveraging the joint sparsity of multi-BS channels. Subsequently, a multi-BS scatterer association algorithm is developed based solely on Angle of Arrival (AoA) estimates. By utilizing the shared scatterers and the geometric relationships among the scatterers, BSs, and the user equipment (UE), coarse estimates of the UE location and timing offsets are obtained. Based on these coarse estimates of scatterer locations, UE location, and timing offsets, an expectation-maximization (EM) framework is employed. Specifically, the UE location and timing offsets are iteratively refined while jointly enabling high-precision estimation of scatterer locations and channel coefficients. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves significant improvements in both channel estimation and localization accuracy compared with baseline methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-BS joint channel estimation and localization scheme that uses shared scatterer information to mitigate clock asynchronism, employing AoA-based scatterer association and EM-based iterative refinement to improve both channel estimation and localization accuracy.
AI.motivation: Clock asynchronism between BSs and users significantly degrades scatterer localization accuracy.
AI.method: 1) Channel modeling using joint sparsity of multi-BS channels. 2) Multi-BS scatterer association based on AoA estimates. 3) Coarse estimation of UE location and timing offsets using shared scatterer geometry. 4) EM-based iterative refinement for fine estimation of scatterer locations, channel coefficients, UE location, and timing offsets.
AI.result: The proposed scheme achieves significant improvements in both channel estimation and localization accuracy compared with baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging shared scatterer information across multiple BSs effectively mitigates clock asynchronism, enabling joint high-precision channel estimation and localization.

title: GLENS: Global Search via Learning from Solver Iterates with Diffusion Models
summary: We consider the problem of generating a large collection of initial guesses for local minima of multimodal non-convex continuous optimization problems. The goal is for these initial guesses to be high-quality (i.e., a numerical solver converges quickly) and diverse (i.e., represent many different local minima). Identifying multiple locally optimal solutions enables flexible downstream decision-making, but typically requires expensive global search. Existing data-driven methods predict initial guesses using only the final converged optima from offline solver runs, which discards information about the local neighborhoods of solutions and limits the available training data. We propose GLENS (Global Search via Learning from Solver Iterates), a data-efficient global search method that leverages intermediate solver iterates as free data augmentation. GLENS consists of two components: a neighborhood structure model that uses diffusion models to learn the local geometry around optima conditioned on problem parameters, and a solver behavior model that learns refinement directions to further guide samples towards nearby optima during diffusion sampling. Experiments on modified non-convex benchmark problems and a two-robot obstacle-avoidance navigation problem show that GLENS generates high-quality initial guesses while preserving the multimodal distribution of diverse local optima. The resulting initial guesses lead to faster solver convergence across different problem settings and solvers. We also analyze how key hyperparameter choices affect the performance.

AI.tldr: GLENS leverages intermediate solver iterates as free data augmentation to generate high-quality and diverse initial guesses for local minima in multimodal non-convex optimization, using diffusion models to learn local neighborhood geometry and refinement directions.
AI.motivation: Existing data-driven global search methods only use final converged optima from offline solver runs, discarding information about local neighborhoods and limiting training data. This makes it expensive to identify multiple diverse local minima for downstream decision-making.
AI.method: GLENS has two components: (1) a neighborhood structure model using diffusion models to learn local geometry around optima conditioned on problem parameters, and (2) a solver behavior model that learns refinement directions to guide samples toward nearby optima during diffusion sampling.
AI.result: Experiments on modified non-convex benchmarks and a two-robot obstacle-avoidance navigation problem show GLENS generates high-quality initial guesses that preserve multimodal diversity and lead to faster solver convergence across different problem settings and solvers.
AI.conclusion: GLENS is a data-efficient global search method that effectively leverages intermediate solver iterates as data augmentation, outperforming existing approaches in generating diverse, high-quality initial guesses for multimodal non-convex optimization.

title: Latent Reward Steering: An Adaptive Inference-Time Framework that Implicitly Promotes Cognitive Behaviors in Reasoning LLMs
summary: Strong reasoning depends not only on model knowledge but also on how effectively cognitive behaviors are deployed during generation. Existing methods often rely on explicit behavior-level control, making them insufficiently adaptive when failures and required corrections vary across reasoning states, tasks, and models. To this end, we propose Latent Reward Steering (LRS), an adaptive inference-time framework that promotes cognitive behaviors by optimizing the sparse-autoencoder (SAE) latent states that implicitly carry them. Rather than relying on predefined cognitive behaviors or steering directions derived from them, LRS trains a latent reward model on reasoning traces by final answer correctness to estimate the quality of intermediate latent states. During inference, reward gradients provide state-specific correction directions for fragile latent states, while a reward and confidence gate restricts intervention to states the reward signal flags as fragile. Experiments on multiple reasoning LLM backbones and benchmarks show that \ours consistently improves performance over various baselines, and post-hoc analyses further indicate that \ours implicitly promotes good cognitive behaviors that fix the original reasoning errors. Code is available at: https://github.com/jiakanglee/Latent-Reward-Steering.

AI.tldr: LRS is an inference-time framework that uses latent reward signals from sparse autoencoders to implicitly steer cognitive behaviors during reasoning, outperforming explicit behavior-control methods across multiple LLMs and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on explicit behavior-level control, which lacks adaptability when reasoning failures and required corrections vary across different reasoning states, tasks, and models.
AI.method: LRS trains a latent reward model on reasoning traces using final answer correctness to estimate intermediate latent state quality, then uses reward gradients to steer fragile latent states during inference, with a reward-and-confidence gate restricting interventions to states flagged as fragile.
AI.result: Consistent performance improvements over various baselines across multiple reasoning LLM backbones and benchmarks; post-hoc analyses show LRS implicitly promotes beneficial cognitive behaviors that fix original reasoning errors.
AI.conclusion: Latent-level reward steering is an effective and adaptive alternative to explicit behavior control for improving reasoning performance at inference time.

title: Generative Multi-Robot Motion Planning via Diffusion Modeling with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Guidance
comment: 11 pages, 6 figures, 1 table. This paper has been accepted for publication in the proceedings of ASME IDETC-CIE 2026
summary: Coordinating multiple robots in shared environments requires generating feasible trajectories for each agent while accounting for interactions among agents. Centralized planning approaches become difficult to scale as the number of robots increases, while decentralized approaches that allow each agent to plan independently do not inherently account for inter-agent interactions. This paper presents a framework for coordinated multi-robot motion planning that combines decentralized generative trajectory planning with multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL)-based coordination. Each robot independently generates candidate trajectories using a diffusion model trained on single-agent motion data, leveraging the generative model's ability to produce feasible and diverse trajectories. To reduce conflicts between agents, a centralized value function trained via MARL guides the reverse diffusion process through gradient-based steering, enabling interaction-aware trajectory generation without centralized joint planning or retraining of the generative model. This guidance follows an exponential tilting formulation, in which the value function biases the denoising distribution toward trajectories with higher expected multi-agent return. The framework is evaluated in a simulated maze environment with four mobile robots. Experimental results show that the proposed value-guided diffusion planning reduces the inter-agent interference rate from 55.4% to 41.8%, demonstrating that coordination can be effectively achieved while preserving the scalability of decentralized trajectory generation. These results suggest that MARL-based value guidance can effectively introduce coordination into decentralized generative planners without requiring a fully joint multi-robot model.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework that combines decentralized diffusion-based trajectory generation with MARL-based value guidance to coordinate multiple robots, reducing inter-agent interference from 55.4% to 41.8% in a simulated maze environment.
AI.motivation: Centralized multi-robot planning doesn't scale well, while decentralized approaches fail to account for inter-agent interactions. The authors aim to achieve coordination without sacrificing scalability.
AI.method: Each robot independently generates trajectories using a diffusion model trained on single-agent data. A centralized MARL-trained value function then guides the reverse diffusion process via gradient-based steering (exponential tilting), biasing toward high-return multi-agent trajectories.
AI.result: In a simulated maze with four robots, the proposed method reduced inter-agent interference from 55.4% to 41.8%.
AI.conclusion: MARL-based value guidance can effectively introduce coordination into decentralized generative planners without requiring a fully joint multi-robot model, preserving scalability.

title: DarkVesselNet: Multi-Modal Remote Sensing and Trajectory Reasoning for Dark Vessel Detection
summary: Dark vessel detection requires fusing what vessels report through AIS with what satellites observe through radar and optical sensors. DarkVesselNet is a multi-modal remote sensing stack that combines Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 optical imagery, geospatial foundation model backbones, AIS trajectory reasoning, TGARD-style gap detection, and a Pi-DPM-inspired anomaly head. The repository exposes the system as a tested Python package and a public Hugging Face Space. The paper presents the sensor stack, backbone abstraction, fusion path, anomaly head, and current validation. The evidence currently available is software-grounded: tests for SAR speckle filtering, optical band ratios, Haversine distance, TGARD gap emission, sensor coregistration, backbone token shapes, and differentiable anomaly scoring.

AI.tldr: DarkVesselNet is a multi-modal remote sensing system that fuses AIS data with Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 optical imagery using geospatial foundation models, TGARD gap detection, and Pi-DPM-inspired anomaly scoring to detect dark vessels, validated through software tests.
AI.motivation: To detect dark vessels (ships that disable AIS transmissions to evade detection) by fusing complementary AIS and satellite data modalities.
AI.method: Multi-modal stack combining Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 optical imagery, geospatial foundation models, AIS trajectory reasoning, TGARD-style gap detection, and Pi-DPM-inspired anomaly head, exposed as a Python package and Hugging Face Space.
AI.result: Software-grounded evidence with tests for SAR speckle filtering, optical band ratios, Haversine distance, TGARD gap emission, sensor coregistration, backbone token shapes, and differentiable anomaly scoring.
AI.conclusion: The system is validated through software tests but lacks real-world operational validation against actual dark vessel incidents.

title: Distributed MoE-based Uplink Detection for Cell-Free Communication Systems
summary: Cell-free Massive multiple input and multiple output (MIMO) is recognized as a key technology for beyond-5G networks, where distributed access points (APs) jointly serve user equipments (UEs) to address the inherent inter-cell interference issue inherent in cellular systems. While conventional distributed signal detection methods offer a practical balance between performance and fronthaul load, they are fundamentally limited by linear processing constraints. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning based uplink detection framework by introducing the distributed mixture of experts detection network (DMoE-DetNet). In this architecture, each AP acts as a local expert employing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for non-linear feature extraction, and transmits the local minimum mean square error (MMSE) detection results and statistical channel information to the central processing unit (CPU). In the CPU, an attention-based encoder module captures complex spatio-temporal dependencies among users for global feature fusion, with a gating network at the central processor dynamically weighting the contributions from different APs. At last, a linear detector outputs the symbol probability. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DMoE-DetNet significantly outperforms conventional linear processing based cell-free signal detection methods in terms of symbol error rate, showcasing the potential of artificial intelligence-enabled communication systems.

AI.tldr: DMoE-DetNet is a deep learning framework for cell-free massive MIMO uplink detection, where distributed APs use CNNs as local experts and a central CPU employs an attention-based gating network to fuse features, outperforming conventional linear methods.
AI.motivation: Cell-free massive MIMO addresses inter-cell interference in beyond-5G, but conventional distributed signal detection methods are fundamentally limited by linear processing constraints, motivating a non-linear deep learning approach.
AI.method: Each AP acts as a local expert using CNNs for non-linear feature extraction, transmitting local MMSE detection results and statistical channel info to CPU. The CPU uses an attention-based encoder for spatio-temporal feature fusion and a gating network to dynamically weight AP contributions, followed by a linear detector for symbol probability output.
AI.result: DMoE-DetNet significantly outperforms conventional linear processing based cell-free signal detection methods in terms of symbol error rate.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning approach showcases the potential of AI-enabled communication systems for cell-free massive MIMO detection.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Pairwise Preferences in Long-Term Decision Problems
summary: Reinforcement learning problems typically define the goal as maximizing the expected value of a scalar reward function. But, pairwise preferences are often easier to specify than scalar rewards, and they express certain goals that scalar rewards cannot. Methods for reinforcement learning with pairwise preferences have thus received growing interest. Unfortunately, these methods are inefficient in problems with long time horizons, and they lack guarantees on the performance of Markov policies relative to history-dependent policies, which bridge the theory and practice of reinforcement learning. We therefore propose the \textit{Markov decision contest} as a new problem model for reinforcement learning with pairwise preferences. We prove that stationary Markov policies are optimal among all history-dependent policies, that solving a Markov decision contest exactly is in P, and that a simple iterative algorithm converges to an optimal policy at a sublinear rate. Lastly, in a set of high-dimensional decision problems with long time horizons, we show that our approximate algorithm is significantly more learning-efficient than prior work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Markov decision contests, a new framework for reinforcement learning with pairwise preferences that guarantees optimality of Markov policies, has polynomial-time exact solutions, and demonstrably outperforms prior methods in high-dimensional, long-horizon problems.
AI.motivation: Pairwise preferences are easier to specify than scalar rewards and can express goals that scalar rewards cannot, but existing RL methods for preferences are inefficient in long-horizon problems and lack guarantees on Markov policy optimality relative to history-dependent policies.
AI.method: The authors propose the Markov decision contest (MDC) as a new problem model, prove stationary Markov policies are optimal among all history-dependent policies, show exact MDC solving is in P, and develop a simple iterative algorithm with sublinear convergence.
AI.result: The exact algorithm runs in polynomial time, the iterative algorithm converges sublinearly to optimal, and the approximate algorithm significantly outperforms prior work in high-dimensional, long-horizon decision problems in terms of learning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Markov decision contests provide a theoretically grounded and practically efficient framework for preference-based RL, bridging the theory-practice gap with optimality guarantees and strong empirical performance.

title: AI Sovereignty as National Learning Capacity: A Human-Centered Learning Mechanics Viewpoint on France, the United States, and China
summary: Artificial Intelligence is often discussed in France in terms of investment, compute capacity, regulation, employment, sovereignty, and education. These dimensions are usually treated separately. This viewpoint paper proposes a unified interpretation: France should be understood as a \emph{national AI learning system}. Building on Human-Centered Learning Mechanics (HCLM), recently formulated as a dynamical framework for entropy-regulated representation learning, we interpret national AI development as a controlled balance between information injection and entropy dissipation. Information injection corresponds to compute, data, talent, research, capital, industrial deployment, and institutional experimentation. Entropy dissipation corresponds to organizational complexity, coordination frictions, energy constraints, regulatory uncertainty, talent mobility pressures, and opportunities to strengthen industrial absorption. The central claim is that AI sovereignty does not emerge from scale alone but from a country's capacity to regulate its own information dynamics. This paper connects HCLM with neural scaling laws, endogenous growth theory, creative destruction, and game theory. It argues that the French AI debate should move beyond the binary opposition between techno-optimism and regulation-first caution. A competitive and human-centered AI strategy requires a controlled regime in which information injection grows faster than institutional dissipation, while avoiding unstable, unequal, or energy-intensive expansion. We provide a mathematical model, measurable policy indicators, game-theoretic propositions, illustrative simulations of national AI regimes, and concrete policy implications for France. The proposed viewpoint reframes AI policy as the governance of an open, strategic, non-equilibrium learning system.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes viewing France as a "national AI learning system" using Human-Centered Learning Mechanics (HCLM), arguing that AI sovereignty depends on a country's ability to balance information injection (compute, data, talent, capital) with entropy dissipation (coordination frictions, regulatory uncertainty, energy constraints). It provides a mathematical model, simulations, and policy implications to move beyond the binary opposition between techno-optimism and regulation-first approaches.
AI.motivation: Current French AI discourse treats investment, compute, regulation, employment, sovereignty, and education as separate dimensions. The authors aim to provide a unified framework that connects these aspects through the lens of national AI learning systems, addressing the need for a more integrated understanding of AI development dynamics.
AI.method: The paper applies Human-Centered Learning Mechanics (HCLM), an entropy-regulated representation learning framework, to national AI development. It builds mathematical models connecting HCLM with neural scaling laws, endogenous growth theory, creative destruction, and game theory. It provides measurable policy indicators, game-theoretic propositions, and illustrative simulations of national AI regimes.
AI.result: The paper finds that AI sovereignty emerges not from scale alone but from a country's capacity to regulate its own information dynamics. A competitive and human-centered AI strategy requires a controlled regime where information injection grows faster than institutional dissipation, while avoiding unstable, unequal, or energy-intensive expansion.
AI.conclusion: The French AI debate should move beyond the binary opposition between techno-optimism and regulation-first caution. AI policy should be reframed as the governance of an open, strategic, non-equilibrium learning system, with concrete policy implications for France.

title: Threading Optimization for Vision-Language-Action Model Inference in Low-Cost Smart Agricultural Manipulation
summary: Vision-Language Action (VLA) models continue to face challenges such as slow inference speed and difficulty performing fine-grained motion adjustments, limiting their widespread adoption in industry. While the Real-Time Action Chunking (RTAC) algorithm has been proposed to address these bottlenecks, bridging the gap between the algorithm provided in pseudocode to a stable, real-world deployment on a low-cost robotic arm remains a challenge. In this work, we present a complete system-level implementation of RTAC tailored for a low-cost robotic manipulation system. We advance beyond the original high-level pseudocode by optimizing the threading implementation for the policy inference and control pipeline, reducing end-to-end latency and improving responsiveness without modifying the underlying policy. We evaluate this system on tasks involving the manipulation of agricultural produce, specifically garlic bulbs and walnuts. Experimental results demonstrate that our custom threading implementation significantly improves control stability and speed compared to the base implementation of RTAC.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a complete system-level implementation of RTAC on low-cost robotic arms, optimizing threading for policy inference and control to improve speed and stability, demonstrated on agricultural produce manipulation.
AI.motivation: VLA models face slow inference and fine-grained motion adjustment challenges, limiting industrial adoption. RTAC algorithm exists but bridging pseudocode to real-world low-cost robotic deployment is difficult.
AI.method: Optimized threading implementation for policy inference and control pipeline, advancing beyond original high-level pseudocode to reduce end-to-end latency without modifying the underlying policy.
AI.result: Custom threading implementation significantly improves control stability and speed compared to the base RTAC implementation on tasks manipulating garlic bulbs and walnuts.
AI.conclusion: System-level optimization of RTAC threading enables stable, real-world deployment on low-cost robotic arms for fine-grained manipulation tasks.

title: GeoSAM-3D: Geodesic Prompt Propagation for Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Segmentation from Monocular Video
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D scene segmentation usually assumes RGB-D video, calibrated multi-view imagery, or a reconstructed mesh. GeoSAM-3D studies a lighter setting: a user uploads a short monocular video, clicks or names an object in one frame, and receives a propagated 3D mask over a Gaussian scene. The implementation combines frozen image and video foundation models with a monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction and a differentiable graph-geodesic propagation kernel over Gaussian centroids. The central design choice is to propagate prompts by heat-kernel distance on the reconstructed scene graph, rather than by Euclidean nearest neighbors in 3D. This preserves continuity around curved surfaces and reduces leakage across nearby but disconnected objects. This paper describes the repository state, the mathematical kernel implemented in geosam3d.propagate, the feature head trained from Segment Anything masks, and the validation already present in the codebase. The evaluation protocol separates implementation validation, graph propagation quality, leakage control, and interactive latency.

AI.tldr: GeoSAM-3D performs open-vocabulary 3D segmentation from a single monocular video using frozen foundation models and heat-kernel geodesic propagation on a Gaussian scene graph, requiring only one user click or text prompt in one frame.
AI.motivation: To enable 3D scene segmentation from a lighter input setting (short monocular video with a single user prompt) rather than requiring RGB-D video, calibrated multi-view imagery, or reconstructed meshes.
AI.method: Combines frozen image/video foundation models, monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting reconstruction, and a differentiable graph-geodesic propagation kernel over Gaussian centroids using heat-kernel distance instead of Euclidean nearest neighbors.
AI.result: The propagation via heat-kernel distance preserves continuity around curved surfaces and reduces leakage across nearby but disconnected objects. The evaluation covers implementation validation, graph propagation quality, leakage control, and interactive latency.
AI.conclusion: Heat-kernel geodesic propagation on scene graphs is more effective than Euclidean distance for prompt propagation in monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes, enabling practical single-prompt 3D segmentation.

title: How Much Orthogonalization Does Muon Need?
summary: Muon optimizers improve neural-network training by replacing ill-conditioned momentum updates with approximately semi-orthogonal updates. This motivates a practical question: how much orthogonalization does Muon actually require? We study this question using a relaxed cubic Newton--Schulz schedule derived directly for Muon's low precision singular value band. The resulting five-step cubic construction uses ten dominant matrix multiplications, compared with fifteen for five quintic Newton--Schulz iterations. The cubic schedule is not intended as a more accurate polar solver; instead, it is a principled low-cost variant that lets us probe the relation between polar accuracy, spectral shaping, and training quality. Across synthetic diagnostics, NanoGPT ablations, and training experiments on hybrid MoE/Mamba models, we find that training quality is not governed monotonically by polar-decomposition accuracy: truncated Polar Express, Muon-Jordan, cubic Newton--Schulz, and an explicit FP32 SVD polar factor can reach nearly indistinguishable final loss on GPT-2 Small, and cubic5 matches the Muon-Jordan quintic update within about $10^{-3}$ validation loss on hybrid MoE/Mamba models with one billion to four billion parameters. These results support cubic5 as a practical low-cost Muon orthogonalization variant, with empirical evidence of training-quality parity in the settings tested.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how much orthogonalization is needed for Muon optimizers, proposing a cheaper 5-step cubic Newton-Schulz schedule that uses 10 matrix multiplications instead of 15, and shows it achieves comparable training quality to more expensive methods across GPT-2 and MoE/Mamba models up to 4B parameters.
AI.motivation: Muon optimizers improve training by using semi-orthogonal updates instead of ill-conditioned momentum updates, but the practical question of how much orthogonalization is actually needed remains unanswered.
AI.method: Derives a relaxed 5-step cubic Newton-Schulz schedule from Muon's low-precision singular value band, using 10 dominant matrix multiplications (vs 15 for quintic). Compared across synthetic diagnostics, NanoGPT ablations, and hybrid MoE/Mamba model training.
AI.result: Training quality is not monotonically governed by polar-decomposition accuracy. Truncated Polar Express, Muon-Jordan, cubic Newton-Schulz, and explicit FP32 SVD polar factor achieve nearly indistinguishable final loss on GPT-2 Small. Cubic5 matches Muon-Jordan quintic within ~10^-3 validation loss on 1B-4B MoE/Mamba models.
AI.conclusion: Cubic5 is a practical low-cost Muon orthogonalization variant that achieves training-quality parity with more expensive methods, suggesting excessive orthogonalization accuracy is unnecessary for training.

title: SHARP: Sleep-based Hierarchical Accelerated Replay for Long Range Non-Stationary Temporal Pattern Recognition
summary: Learning long-range non-stationary temporal patterns remains a core challenge for modern sequence models, particularly in strict streaming settings. In these settings, data arrive sequentially and must be processed in a single pass without simultaneously revisiting past observations. Standard architectures, including recurrent neural networks and transformers, are constrained by either truncated backpropagation through time horizon or explicit input window length for long range credit assignment. To address these limitations, we propose SHARP (Sleep-based Hierarchical Accelerated Replay), a framework that decomposes temporal learning into two complementary components: a memory module that accumulates a structured history of past inputs, and a pattern-recognition module that operates over this memory. This separation enables resource- and compute-efficient adaptation to non-stationary dynamics by eliminating the need for backpropagation through time across many steps for long-range credit assignment. Inspired by the accelerated replay observed in rodents during slow-wave sleep, SHARP incorporates offline (sleep) phases in which temporally structured memory traces are replayed in an accelerated form and integrated into higher-level memory representations, improving long-range context retention. Through controlled simulations and ablation studies, we characterize the key properties of the proposed framework. In benchmark datasets such as text8 and PG-19, we demonstrate that SHARP improves over recurrent baselines by retaining next-token predictive performance on previously seen data while continuing to learn from the current stream and generalizing to future unseen data. These gains are enabled by its hierarchical structure, which yields an exponentially increasing effective temporal context with only linear-time computational cost.

AI.tldr: SHARP is a framework that decomposes temporal learning into memory and pattern-recognition modules, using sleep-phase accelerated replay to achieve long-range credit assignment in streaming settings without backpropagation through time.
AI.motivation: Long-range non-stationary temporal patterns in strict streaming settings cannot be effectively learned by standard RNNs or Transformers due to truncation limits or fixed input windows, making long-range credit assignment infeasible in a single-pass setting.
AI.method: SHARP separates temporal learning into a memory module (structured history) and a pattern-recognition module, with offline "sleep" phases that replay temporally structured memory traces in accelerated form to integrate into higher-level representations, inspired by rodent slow-wave sleep.
AI.result: SHARP improves over recurrent baselines on text8 and PG-19 by retaining predictive performance on seen data while learning from current streams and generalizing to unseen data, with exponentially increasing effective temporal context at only linear computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical decomposition with sleep-based accelerated replay enables efficient long-range context retention and adaptation to non-stationary dynamics in strict streaming settings, eliminating the need for backpropagation through time.

title: Make Your VLA More Robust Without More Data By Interleaving Motion Planning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown remarkable progress for mobile manipulation, but their performance on long-horizon tasks remains poor. These tasks are especially challenging because (1) progress toward high-level goals must be maintained across extended sequences of spatially distributed subtasks, and (2) early execution errors compound rapidly over the task horizon. These challenges persist despite finetuning on large human teleoperated mobile manipulation data, indicating that more data alone may not resolve the problem. To address these challenges, we propose MPVI: Motion Planner / VLA Interleaving, a framework that integrates model-based motion planning with VLAs to improve robustness without further training. The proposed integration enables localization and navigation to distant or occluded target objects through cluttered scenes using open-vocabulary object detection, frontier exploration and motion planning. However, such integration is non-trivial, requiring reliable switching between modules; we show one way forward via VLM-based completion checking with proprioceptive triggers. We evaluate our approach on the BEHAVIOR-1K benchmark and demonstrate 113% improvement in task progress over a top end-to-end VLA baseline. Additional details are available at the project page: https://mpvi.netlify.app/.

AI.tldr: MPVI is a framework that interleaves model-based motion planning with Vision-Language-Action models to improve long-horizon mobile manipulation, achieving 113% improvement on BEHAVIOR-1K without additional training.
AI.motivation: VLA models perform poorly on long-horizon tasks due to compounding errors and difficulty maintaining progress across extended subtask sequences, and more data alone may not resolve these issues.
AI.method: MPVI integrates model-based motion planning with VLAs via VLM-based completion checking and proprioceptive triggers to enable reliable switching between modules, supporting localization, navigation, open-vocabulary detection, frontier exploration, and motion planning.
AI.result: Achieved 113% improvement in task progress over a top end-to-end VLA baseline on the BEHAVIOR-1K benchmark, without requiring additional training.
AI.conclusion: Interleaving structured motion planning with VLAs is a promising direction for robust long-horizon mobile manipulation, addressing limitations of pure end-to-end approaches.

title: Optimizing 3D Gaussian Splatting via Point Cloud Upsampling
comment: Accepted in Journal of Computational Vision and Imaging Systems (JCVIS)
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is a technique for creating and rendering 3D scenes, however its performance depends heavily on the quality of initial seed points. To improve 3DGS initialization, this study presents and evaluates several point cloud upsampling approaches: linear interpolation, triangular interpolation, spline-based surface reconstruction, moving least squares surface fitting, and Voronoi-based point generation. Additionally, this research introduces a depth-guided point lifting method that leverages depth maps to maintain geometric consistency with Structure-from-Motion (SfM) reconstructions. Through extensive experiments on the Mip-NeRF360 and Replica datasets, the proposed methods demonstrate improvements in reconstruction quality across diverse scene types. Results indicate that different upsampling strategies excel in different scenarios: surface reconstruction methods perform better with organic, detailed scenes, while simpler interpolation approaches are more suited for scenes dominated by piecewise-smooth geometries. In comparison, the depth-guided approach shows promise for adding geometry-aware points across the entire scene, importantly in texture-less regions. These findings, which provide preliminary practical guidelines for selecting appropriate upsampling methods based on scene characteristics and computational constraints, advances the understanding of how point cloud initialization affects 3DGS quality.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates multiple point cloud upsampling methods (linear/triangular interpolation, spline/MSL surface reconstruction, Voronoi-based generation) and introduces a depth-guided point lifting technique to improve 3D Gaussian Splatting initialization. Experiments on Mip-NeRF360 and Replica datasets show surface reconstruction works best for organic scenes, interpolation for piecewise-smooth geometries, and depth-guided methods help in texture-less regions.
AI.motivation: 3DGS performance heavily depends on the quality of initial seed points, and current initialization methods are suboptimal for diverse scene types, motivating the exploration of better point cloud upsampling strategies.
AI.method: Evaluates five upsampling approaches (linear interpolation, triangular interpolation, spline-based surface reconstruction, MLS surface fitting, Voronoi-based point generation) and introduces a novel depth-guided point lifting method leveraging depth maps for geometric consistency with SfM.
AI.result: Surface reconstruction methods excel in organic/detailed scenes; simpler interpolation works better for piecewise-smooth geometries; depth-guided approach shows promise for geometry-aware points, especially in texture-less regions.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical guidelines for selecting upsampling methods based on scene characteristics and computational constraints, advancing understanding of how point cloud initialization affects 3DGS quality.

title: Diffusion-Based Heart Sound Generation: Evaluation with Physiological Signal Metrics, Classifiers, and Expert Listening
summary: Publicly available phonocardiogram (PCG) datasets remain limited in size and pathological diversity, constraining both auscultation training and the generalisation of automated heart-sound classifiers. A class-conditional diffusion model for PCG generation is developed in the log-mel domain and synthetic fidelity is assessed using complementary (i) physiology-inspired plausibility metrics, (ii) downstream label-consistency evaluation, and (iii) expert listening. Experiments use the Phy-sioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2016 dataset (3240 recordings) with recording-level splits. After preprocessing and quality control, 16,749 non-overlapping 4 s clips are mapped to a normalised 1 x 128 x 128 log-mel representation to train a conditional 2D U-Net denoiser with classifier-free guidance. Signal-level plausibility is quantified on reconstructed waveforms using three lightweight metrics: an envelope-autocorrelation rhythm score, an amplitude-based explosion score, and the dominant cycle lag. Synthetic clips preserve similar dominant cycle durations but exhibit reduced envelope periodicity and increased transient burstiness relative to real clips. For downstream evaluation, a ResNet-50 classifier achieves 92.24% accuracy on the held-out real test set and 82.8% accuracy on class-balanced synthetic batches, indicating that generated signals retain discriminative structure relevant to normal/abnormal classification. In a pilot expert listening study (60 clips, two clinicians), most synthetic clips are judged as heart-sound-like, while abnormality sensitivity is low for both real and synthetic 4 s excerpts. Overall, the results provide a practical baseline for diffusion-based PCG generation while highlighting remaining challenges in retaining abnormal acoustic cues and reducing reconstruction-induced artefacts.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a class-conditional diffusion model to generate synthetic phonocardiogram (PCG) signals in the log-mel domain, evaluates synthetic fidelity using plausibility metrics, downstream classification, and expert listening, and establishes a baseline for diffusion-based PCG generation while noting challenges in preserving abnormal acoustic features.
AI.motivation: Publicly available PCG datasets are limited in size and pathological diversity, which constrains auscultation training and the generalization of automated heart-sound classifiers.
AI.method: A class-conditional diffusion model (2D U-Net denoiser with classifier-free guidance) is trained on 16,749 non-overlapping 4s PCG clips mapped to 1×128×128 log-mel representations from the PhysioNet/CinC Challenge 2016 dataset. Synthetic fidelity is assessed via plausibility metrics (envelope-autocorrelation rhythm score, amplitude-based explosion score, dominant cycle lag), downstream ResNet-50 classification, and expert listening.
AI.result: Synthetic clips preserved dominant cycle durations but showed reduced envelope periodicity and increased burstiness. ResNet-50 achieved 92.24% (real) vs 82.8% (synthetic) accuracy. Expert listening found most clips heart-sound-like but low abnormality sensitivity for both real and synthetic clips.
AI.conclusion: The results provide a practical baseline for diffusion-based PCG generation while highlighting ongoing challenges in retaining abnormal acoustic cues and reducing reconstruction-induced artifacts.

title: CRMA: A Spectrally-Bounded Backbone for Modular Continual Fine-Tuning of LLMs
comment: 38 pages, 10 figures. Patent-pending construction details deferred to companion technical report (in preparation)
summary: Sequential fine-tuning of large language models forces a choice: let the shared substrate keep learning and accept catastrophic forgetting, or freeze it after task one and foreclose cross-task refinement. Per-task adapter methods (LoRAHub, AdapterFusion, PackNet, Progressive Networks) take the second path. We introduce CRMA (Constrained Residual Mixing Adapter), a residual adapter whose internal mixing matrix M is doubly-stochastic at every forward pass via Sinkhorn normalization, so by Birkhoff's theorem ||M||_2 <= 1 holds by construction -- a structural bound, not a penalty. CRMA's spectrally bounded backbone provides a continuously trained shared substrate that earlier modular methods could not, while preserving their forgetting guarantees. On Mistral-7B across 5 sequential domains and 3 seeds, modular per-task LoRA on a CRMA backbone reduces loss-relative drift from +42.96% +/- 5.5 (naive sequential fine-tuning) to -0.17% +/- 0.17, with disjoint per-seed ranges, and improves prior-task holdout loss by 1.99% +/- 0.54 over a matched frozen-substrate baseline. Three independent experimental setups (Mistral-7B 4-domain controlled ablation, TinyLlama 3-domain contamination-controlled replication, Mistral-7B cross-domain probes at 7B) all show positive backward transfer -- without replay buffers, without growing per-task memory, and without distillation. An inference-time ablation on Gemma-2-9B confirms CRMA mediates access to sequentially trained knowledge: 98/100 vs. 38/100 on the same weights and same questions with only CRMA injection toggled. 867 logged training steps verify ||M||_2 = 1.0 within float32 precision (max deviation 1.2 x 10^-7). The forgetting-prevention effect holds across 1.1B-9.2B parameters and four architecture families.

AI.tldr: CRMA introduces a doubly-stochastic residual adapter matrix with spectral norm ≤1 by construction, enabling continuous training of a shared backbone across sequential tasks without catastrophic forgetting, achieving positive backward transfer (-0.17% drift vs +42.96%) across Mistral-7B, TinyLlama, and Gemma-2-9B up to 9.2B parameters.
AI.motivation: Sequential fine-tuning of LLMs suffers from catastrophic forgetting — either the shared backbone keeps learning (forgetting) or is frozen after task one (preventing cross-task refinement). Per-task adapters (LoRA, AdapterFusion, etc.) take the frozen route, sacrificing the benefits of a continuously trained shared substrate.
AI.method: CRMA (Constrained Residual Mixing Adapter) uses a residual adapter whose internal mixing matrix M is doubly-stochastic via Sinkhorn normalization, ensuring by Birkhoff's theorem that ||M||_2 ≤ 1 holds as a structural bound (not a penalty). This spectral bound allows the backbone to be continuously trained while preserving forgetting guarantees.
AI.result: On Mistral-7B across 5 domains, CRMA reduces loss drift from +42.96% (naive sequential fine-tuning) to -0.17%, improves prior-task holdout loss by 1.99% over frozen-substrate baselines, achieves positive backward transfer across three separate experimental setups without replay or distillation, and on Gemma-2-9B achieves 98/100 vs 38/100 on the same weights/questions with only CRMA injection toggled. ||M||_2 = 1.0 within float32 precision (1.2×10^-7 deviation) across 867 logged steps.
AI.conclusion: CRMA provides a principled structural solution to catastrophic forgetting that enables a continuously trained shared backbone — impossible with prior modular methods — while maintaining their forgetting guarantees, scaling across 1.1B-9.2B parameters and four architecture families, with positive backward transfer, no replay buffers, no memory growth, and no distillation.

title: CoMIC: Collaborative Memory and Insights Circulation for Long-Horizon LLM Agents in Cloud-Edge Systems
summary: Deploying lightweight Large Language Model (LLM) agents on edge servers can reduce latency and move agentic services closer to users, but resource-constrained edge models often struggle with long-horizon tasks that require persistent memory, subgoal tracking, and reflection. Fine-tuning edge models after deployment is costly and difficult to scale across heterogeneous nodes, while purely local memory leaves agents with isolated experience and growing prompt context. We propose \textsc{CoMIC}, a parameter-update-free cloud-edge framework for Collaborative Memory and Insights Circulation. \textsc{CoMIC} follows a \textit{Centralized Reflection, Decentralized Execution} design: edge agents execute locally using subgoal-oriented hierarchical memory and selective re-expansion of relevant histories, while a cloud-side LLM critic asynchronously evaluates completed trajectories, filters reusable experience, and aggregates cross-agent guidance keyed by semantic subgoal identifiers. Across five long-horizon agent tasks spanning symbolic planning and text interaction, \textsc{CoMIC} improves progress rate and action grounding for weak edge agents and yields task-dependent success-rate gains without updating model parameters.

AI.tldr: CoMIC is a cloud-edge framework that improves lightweight LLM agents on edge servers for long-horizon tasks by using centralized reflection (cloud critic) and decentralized execution (edge agents with hierarchical memory), without updating model parameters.
AI.motivation: Deploying lightweight LLM agents on edge servers reduces latency but edge models struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring memory and reflection; fine-tuning is costly and heterogeneous, while local memory leads to isolated experience and growing context.
AI.method: CoMIC uses a Centralized Reflection, Decentralized Execution design: edge agents execute with subgoal-oriented hierarchical memory and selective history re-expansion, while a cloud LLM critic asynchronously evaluates trajectories, filters reusable experience, and aggregates cross-agent guidance keyed by semantic subgoal identifiers.
AI.result: Across five long-horizon agent tasks (symbolic planning and text interaction), CoMIC improves progress rate and action grounding for weak edge agents, and yields task-dependent success-rate gains without parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: CoMIC effectively enhances edge agent performance on long-horizon tasks through collaborative memory and insight circulation without costly fine-tuning, offering a scalable cloud-edge paradigm for heterogeneous edge deployments.

title: OSCAR: Obstacle Survival Curves for Adaptive Robot Navigation
summary: A mobile robot following a graph of known routes can make costly navigation errors when a temporary obstacle blocks a critical edge: waiting too long behind a parked cart wastes time, but immediately rerouting around a person who would move in a few seconds is also inefficient. Standard reactive obstacle avoidance addresses local motion around obstacles, while fixed wait-or-reroute rules ignore how long different obstacle types tend to persist. We propose OSCAR: an adaptive survival-modeling framework for graph-based navigation with temporary blockages. Assuming obstacle class labels are available at encounter time, the robot learns class-conditioned residual clearance-time distributions from online experience, including right-censored observations when it reroutes before observing clearance. These survival models are integrated into a time-dependent graph planner that maintains obstacle memory and computes a patience threshold at each blocked edge: how long to wait before taking an alternate route. The method continuously updates its clearance estimates across episodes and uses them to balance waiting against rerouting. We evaluate the approach in simulation and on a real mobile robot in a university atrium with obstacles including people, chairs, bins, and tubes. In simulation, the learned policy's time-to-goal converges to within 1% of an oracle with access to ground-truth clearance distributions after fewer than 20 observations per obstacle class, outperforming all heuristic baselines. Real-world deployment confirms that the policy improves online, adapting its patience thresholds from experience across 50 navigation episodes.

AI.tldr: OSCAR is an adaptive framework enabling mobile robots to learn how long to wait versus reroute when encountering temporary obstacles, using survival models updated from online experience to optimize navigation efficiency.
AI.motivation: Standard navigation approaches use fixed wait-or-reroute rules that ignore how long different obstacle types (e.g., people vs. parked carts) tend to persist, leading to costly inefficiencies.
AI.method: OSCAR uses class-conditioned survival models with right-censored observations to learn residual clearance-time distributions per obstacle type, integrated into a time-dependent graph planner that computes patience thresholds for waiting vs. rerouting.
AI.result: In simulation, the learned policy converges to within 1% of an oracle with ground-truth clearance distributions after fewer than 20 observations per class. Real-world deployment shows improvement across 50 navigation episodes.
AI.conclusion: Online survival modeling of obstacle clearance times effectively balances waiting and rerouting, outperforming heuristic baselines and adapting to real-world environments.

title: Beyond Static Gaussians: An Empirical Investigation of Architectural Paradigms for Dynamic 3D Scene Reconstruction
comment: Accepted in Journal of Computational Vision and Imaging Systems (JCVIS)
summary: Dynamic scene reconstruction via 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a compelling approach for representing evolving environments, yet understanding trade-offs between methodologies remains crucial. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of dynamic 3DGS methods, categorizing them into two paradigms: structure-guided methods employing auxiliary representations (deformation fields, canonical spaces, grids) to model temporal changes, and gaussian-centric methods encoding dynamics directly into primitives via continuous functions or 4D representations. We evaluate representative methods from both paradigms on the D-NeRF benchmark. Our findings reveal that structure-guided methods achieve superior reconstruction fidelity and compact model sizes, while gaussian-centric approaches demonstrate significantly higher rendering speeds enabling real-time performance, though with greater quality variability and potentially substantial storage overhead. This analysis highlights a fundamental trade-off between reconstruction quality/compactness versus rendering speed, providing insights to guide future research and application development in dynamic scene reconstruction.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting methods, comparing structure-guided (deformation fields, canonical spaces) vs. gaussian-centric (continuous functions, 4D representations) paradigms on the D-NeRF benchmark, finding structure-guided methods offer better quality and compactness while gaussian-centric methods enable real-time rendering speeds.
AI.motivation: To systematically analyze and understand the trade-offs between different dynamic 3DGS methodologies, as dynamic scene reconstruction via 3DGS has emerged as a compelling approach but understanding trade-offs between methodologies remains crucial.
AI.method: Categorizes dynamic 3DGS methods into two paradigms: (1) structure-guided methods using auxiliary representations like deformation fields, canonical spaces, and grids, and (2) gaussian-centric methods encoding dynamics directly into primitives via continuous functions or 4D representations. Evaluates representative methods from both paradigms on the D-NeRF benchmark.
AI.result: Structure-guided methods achieve superior reconstruction fidelity and compact model sizes. Gaussian-centric approaches demonstrate significantly higher rendering speeds enabling real-time performance, but with greater quality variability and potentially substantial storage overhead.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental trade-off between reconstruction quality/compactness versus rendering speed in dynamic 3DGS methods, providing insights to guide future research and application development.

title: An explainable hierarchical self attention-based approach for tremor detection in the time domain
summary: Tremor is a common movement disorder associated with conditions like Parkinson's disease and Essential tremor, traditionally diagnosed through expert clinician assessment. Current automated detection methods rely on frequency-domain features informed by clinical expertise. In this work, we present an explainable, two-stage hierarchical framework for tremor detection in the time domain that learns tremor patterns directly from 3D kinematic marker time-series data across entire tremor-provoking trials. Our framework combined a deep convolutional and long short-term memory network to learn tremor representations from short, discrete, non-overlapping time segments of kinematic time series data from trials, which are then processed by a vision transformer that models their long-term temporal dynamics of time segment features for trial (session) level classification. Evaluated across nine body parts, the framework achieved F1-scores of 0.594 - 0.947 depending on body parts (average: 0.765), falling short of the frequency-domain state-of-the-art performance (0.909) while requiring minimal preprocessing. Attention weights and gradient-based class activation maps (Grad-CAM) identified time-domain features of tremor across body parts. This proof of concept demonstrated the feasibility of data-driven time-domain modeling for tremor detection across anatomically diverse body parts, while reducing reliance on expert-engineered spectral features and providing posthoc interpretability of temporal and anatomical patterns of tremor.

AI.tldr: A two-stage hierarchical deep learning framework (CNN-LSTM + Vision Transformer) detects tremor from raw 3D kinematic time-series data in the time domain, achieving average F1 of 0.765 across nine body parts, below frequency-domain SOTA (0.909), but offering interpretability via attention and Grad-CAM.
AI.motivation: Traditional tremor detection relies on expert-engineered frequency-domain features and manual clinical assessment. The authors aim to reduce reliance on domain expertise by learning tremor patterns directly from raw time-domain kinematic data.
AI.method: A two-stage hierarchical framework: (1) a CNN-LSTM extracts representations from short non-overlapping time segments, (2) a Vision Transformer models long-term temporal dynamics across segments for trial-level classification.
AI.result: F1-scores ranged 0.594–0.947 (average 0.765) across nine body parts, below frequency-domain SOTA (0.909). Attention and Grad-CAM provided posthoc interpretability of temporal and anatomical patterns.
AI.conclusion: Proof of concept that data-driven time-domain modeling can detect tremor across diverse body parts with minimal preprocessing, though performance lags behind frequency-domain methods. Offers interpretability benefits.

title: Detector-Evasive LLM Paraphrasing via Constrained Policy Optimization
summary: AI-text detectors are vulnerable to paraphrasing and detector-guided paraphrasing attacks, but existing detector-evasion methods often lack precise control over semantic preservation. In particular, optimizing directly for detector evasion can degrade fine-grained semantics, whereas scalarized reward designs provide only indirect, weight-sensitive control over the evasion-semantics trade-off. We address this limitation by formulating detector-evasive LLM paraphrasing as a Constrained Markov Decision Process, where detector evasion is the primary objective and semantic preservation is enforced as an explicit constraint. We propose Detector Evasion Policy Optimization (DEPO), a Lagrangian primal-dual reinforcement learning algorithm with a novel GRPO-style group-based policy update. DEPO adaptively balances semantic preservation and detector evasion during training, enabling the policy to improve attack success within a prescribed semantic-preservation region. Experiments on MAGE, M4, RAID, and peer-review datasets, evaluated against MAGE, RoBERTa, RADAR, Binoculars, and Fast-DetectGPT detectors, show that DEPO achieves strong detector evasion while precisely satisfying the semantic preservation constraint. DEPO also exhibits cross-domain, cross-detector, and prompt-level robustness.

AI.tldr: DEPO uses Constrained MDP and Lagrangian RL to create detector-evading paraphrases while explicitly maintaining semantic quality.
AI.motivation: Existing detector-evasion methods either degrade fine-grained semantics during optimization or use scalarized rewards that provide indirect, weight-sensitive control over the evasion-semantics trade-off.
AI.method: Formulates detector-evasive paraphrasing as a Constrained Markov Decision Process, and proposes DEPO (Detector Evasion Policy Optimization) - a Lagrangian primal-dual RL algorithm with GRPO-style group-based policy updates.
AI.result: DEPO achieves strong detector evasion while precisely satisfying semantic preservation constraints, tested across multiple datasets (MAGE, M4, RAID, peer-review) and detectors (MAGE, RoBERTa, RADAR, Binoculars, Fast-DetectGPT).
AI.conclusion: DEPO provides robust, cross-domain, cross-detector, and prompt-level generalization for adversarial paraphrasing with controllable semantic fidelity.

title: FALAT: Tracing Failures in LLM Agent Trajectories via Dependency-Guided Search
summary:
LLM-based agents increasingly solve complex tasks through long trajectories involving reasoning steps, tool calls, and inter-agent communication. However, when these agents fail, it is often unclear which agent caused the failure and which step introduced the decisive error. This attribution problem is challenging because mistakes can propagate across the trajectory: later actions may appear incorrect, but only because they depend on an earlier corrupted state. Therefore, failure attribution cannot be treated as independent step-level classification.
  We propose FALAT, a diagnostic framework for failure attribution in LLM agent trajectories. FALAT frames attribution as a dependency-guided search problem. It first constructs an expectation of how the task should be solved and uses this expectation to identify suspicious regions in the trajectory. It then traces dependencies among decisions, tool outputs, and agent messages to distinguish error-introducing steps from steps that merely inherit or propagate prior mistakes.
