AI.method: Develops an approximate duality theory for generalization analysis, showing dual variables reweigh data distribution toward difficult constraint points; uses sparse L1 penalty on constraint relaxations to control generalization.
AI.result: Generalization is controlled by mismatch between data distribution mass concentration and concentration on hard-to-satisfy constraint points; L1 penalty effectively controls generalization.
AI.conclusion: Everywhere learning offers a viable alternative to average-loss minimization, with demonstrated merits in agentic classification for language model tasks.

title: Finer Parameter Steps for Low-Rank PEFT: A Controlled Study with CP Tensor Adapters
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on CoLoRAI
summary: Low-rank adapters are usually compared by sweeping a small set of ranks, but the rank also fixes the resolution of the parameter budget. For a $2048{\times}2048$ OPT attention projection, increasing LoRA by one rank stores $4096$ trainable scalars, leaving large gaps between feasible low-budget adapter sizes. This paper asks whether a tensorized adapter with finer capacity increments changes the observed accuracy--budget trade-off. We instantiate this question with fixed-component canonical polyadic (CP) tensor adapters. Under a $32{\times}64{\times}32{\times}64$ tensorization, one normalized CP component stores $193$ trainable scalars per projection, about $21$ times smaller than one LoRA rank step. We compare CP adapters and LoRA on OPT-1.3B across SST-2, RTE, and BoolQ under matched target modules, training protocol, data caps, and seed schedules. CP trains stably and fills the gaps between LoRA ranks, but the effect is task-dependent: SST-2 reaches an early low-budget plateau, BoolQ benefits from additional CP components before saturating slightly below LoRA, and RTE remains LoRA-favored. Finer parameter steps are therefore useful for diagnosing PEFT budget sensitivity, but they do not by themselves guarantee a better accuracy--budget curve.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether tensorized adapters (specifically CP decomposition) with finer parameter increments can improve the accuracy-budget trade-off compared to LoRA, finding that while CP fills gaps between LoRA ranks, the effect is task-dependent and doesn't guarantee better performance.
AI.motivation: LoRA's rank parameter creates coarse, large increments in parameter budget (e.g., one rank step for a 2048×2048 projection stores 4096 scalars), leaving gaps between feasible low-budget adapter sizes. The paper asks whether a tensorized adapter with finer capacity increments changes the observed accuracy-budget trade-off.
AI.method: The authors instantiate fixed-component canonical polyadic (CP) tensor adapters with a 32×64×32×64 tensorization (193 scalars per projection per component, ~21× smaller than one LoRA rank step). They compare CP adapters and LoRA on OPT-1.3B across SST-2, RTE, and BoolQ under matched conditions (target modules, training protocol, data caps, seed schedules).
AI.result: CP trains stably and fills the gaps between LoRA ranks, but the effect is task-dependent: SST-2 reaches an early low-budget plateau, BoolQ benefits from additional CP components before saturating slightly below LoRA, and RTE remains LoRA-favored.
AI.conclusion: Finer parameter steps are useful for diagnosing PEFT budget sensitivity, but they do not by themselves guarantee a better accuracy-budget curve.

title: Ryze: Evidence-Enriched Data Synthesis from Biomedical Papers
comment: Accepted at ACL 2026 System Demonstrations Track. 8 pages, 6 figures
summary: General-purpose VLMs remain unreliable for biomedical research because valid answers in scientific papers depend on evidence split across figures, tables, charts, captions, and referring text. Existing post-training pipelines are bottlenecked by costly expert annotation and by synthetic data that drops this evidence structure. We present Ryze, a fully automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into an evidence-enriched training set and a domain-specialized VLM. Ryze synthesizes QA pairs with complete supporting evidence (visual element, caption, extracted structure, and referring paragraphs), reduces layout and OCR errors via chart/table-aware extraction and LLM-based cleansing, and applies a progress-gated post-training strategy combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning. Starting from Qwen3-VL-8B, Ryze produces BioVLM-8B at under USD 200, achieving 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, outperforming the base model by +12.6 percentage points (pp) and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 pp. We release Ryze as open source together with the trained BioVLM-8B model.

AI.tldr: Ryze is an automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into evidence-enriched training data to build domain-specialized VLMs. Starting from Qwen3-VL-8B, it produces BioVLM-8B for under $200, achieving 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, surpassing both the base model and GPT-5.2.
AI.motivation: General-purpose VLMs are unreliable for biomedical research because valid answers depend on evidence scattered across figures, tables, charts, captions, and text. Existing post-training pipelines are bottlenecked by costly expert annotation and synthetic data that lacks this evidence structure.
AI.method: Ryze (1) converts raw biomedical papers into evidence-enriched QA pairs with complete supporting evidence (visual elements, captions, extracted structures, referring paragraphs); (2) reduces layout/OCR errors via chart/table-aware extraction and LLM-based cleansing; (3) applies a progress-gated post-training strategy combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning.
AI.result: BioVLM-8B achieves 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, outperforming Qwen3-VL-8B base model by +12.6 pp and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 pp, all at under $200 training cost.
AI.conclusion: Ryze demonstrates that fully automated, cost-effective domain specialization of VLMs for biomedical research is feasible by leveraging evidence structures from raw papers, and the system and trained model are released as open source.

title: Beyond Task Success: Behavioral and Representational Diagnostics for WAM and VLA
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies and World-Action Models (WAM) represent two increasingly important paradigms for robotic manipulation. However, it remains unclear whether future prediction in WAMs leads to behaviorally meaningful improvements beyond final task success. In this paper, we ask whether WAMs merely add future prediction, or whether they change robot behavior and internal representations in ways that are actionable for control. We introduce a model-agnostic diagnostic framework that compares WAMs and VLAs through two complementary lenses: behavioral rollout analysis and sparse-autoencoder-based feature analysis. The behavioral protocol measures action dynamics consistency, target-object progress, distractor disturbance, and runtime cost. The feature-space protocol characterizes internal representations as memorized, reactive, or predictive, revealing whether models encode future-oriented structure. Across LIBERO and RoboTwin2.0, we evaluate 7 policies spanning direct VLAs and joint, sequential, and auxiliary WAMs. Our results show that success alone hides key differences: WAMs often improve object-level behavior and target selectivity, but their gains depend on architecture and incur higher inference cost. Sequential WAMs show the clearest predictive structure, while auxiliary and joint WAMs respectively compress or entangle future information. These findings suggest future directions for WAMs design to preserve behaviorally actionable future representations for efficient manipulation.

AI.tldr: This paper compares World-Action Models (WAMs) and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies for robotic manipulation, finding that WAMs improve object-level behavior and target selectivity but gains depend on architecture and come with higher inference costs. Sequential WAMs show the clearest predictive structure while auxiliary and joint WAMs compress or entangle future information.
AI.motivation: To determine whether future prediction in World-Action Models leads to behaviorally meaningful improvements beyond final task success, and to understand how WAMs differ from VLAs in terms of behavior and internal representations.
AI.method: A model-agnostic diagnostic framework with two lenses: (1) behavioral rollout analysis measuring action dynamics consistency, target-object progress, distractor disturbance, and runtime cost; (2) sparse-autoencoder-based feature analysis characterizing internal representations as memorized, reactive, or predictive. Evaluated 7 policies across LIBERO and RoboTwin2.0.
AI.result: Success rates alone hide key behavioral differences. WAMs improve object-level behavior and target selectivity but gains depend on architecture. Sequential WAMs show clearest predictive structure; auxiliary and joint WAMs compress or entangle future information. WAMs incur higher inference costs.
AI.conclusion: Future WAM designs should preserve behaviorally actionable future representations for efficient manipulation, as different architectural choices lead to qualitatively different internal representations and behavioral outcomes.

title: Structure-Aware Consistency Priors for Shape from Polarization in Complex Media
summary: Recovering surface normals from single view polarization images in complex media remains challenging. This paper focuses on ice as a representative complex medium, where intricate light matter interactions lead to a nonlinear mapping between polarization observations and surface normals. To address this, a structure-aware polarization prior based on autocorrelation functions is proposed to capture the local spatial consistency of AoLP. Building on this, a dual-branch network (IceSfP) is designed to integrate raw polarization features with priors via cross modal attention and multi-scale feature fusion, enabling accurate surface normal estimation under complex media conditions. To evaluate the method, the first real-world ice SfP dataset is constructed. Experimental results show that the method outperforms existing approaches across all metrics, achieving a MAE of 16.01 deg, which is 2.74 deg lower than the second-best method. The framework provides a generalizable solution for high-precision geometric perception in complex media.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces IceSfP, a dual-branch network with structure-aware polarization priors for recovering surface normals from single-view polarization images in complex media like ice. It achieves state-of-the-art MAE of 16.01° on the first real-world ice SfP dataset.
AI.motivation: Recovering surface normals from polarization images in complex media (e.g., ice) is challenging due to nonlinear light-matter interactions that create a complex mapping between polarization observations and surface normals.
AI.method: A structure-aware polarization prior based on autocorrelation functions captures local spatial consistency of AoLP. A dual-branch network (IceSfP) integrates raw polarization features with priors via cross-modal attention and multi-scale feature fusion.
AI.result: Outperforms existing approaches across all metrics, achieving MAE of 16.01°, which is 2.74° lower than the second-best method on the first real-world ice SfP dataset.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a generalizable solution for high-precision geometric perception in complex media.

title: FW-NKF: Frequency-Weighted Neural Kalman Filters
summary: Robust state estimation is central to robotic autonomy, yet classical Kalman filters struggle with frequency-dependent disturbances and model mismatch such as sensor vibrations, electromagnetic interference, and periodic noise. Although Deep Kalman Filter (DKF) variants extend the Extended Kalman Filtering (EKF) framework by learning latent transitions, they lack explicit mechanisms to suppress band-limited noise components that typically corrupt sensor measurements in real-world scenarios. We introduce the Frequency-Weighted Neural Kalman Filter (FW-NKF), a unified hybrid approach that embeds a causal spectral-shaping operator into the Kalman measurement residual and jointly learns observation, and transition networks. By adapting both the filter spectrum and the latent state representation, FW-NKF attenuates the noise-dominated frequency bands while capturing complex residual structures. We conduct extensive experiments on four heterogeneous benchmarks, including chaotic systems such as multi-dimensional Lorenz systems and full-body inertial pose estimation, and find a reduction in localization error of up to 10% as well as marked improvements in orientation accuracy. Our ablation studies confirm that frequency weighting and deep latent-state modeling contribute to overall performance.

AI.tldr: FW-NKF is a hybrid Kalman filter that embeds frequency weighting into measurement residuals to suppress band-limited noise, achieving up to 10% better localization on chaotic systems and pose estimation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Classical Kalman filters struggle with frequency-dependent disturbances (vibrations, EMI, periodic noise), and existing DKF variants lack explicit mechanisms to suppress band-limited sensor noise in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: FW-NKF embeds a causal spectral-shaping operator into the Kalman measurement residual, jointly learning observation and transition networks to attenuate noise-dominated frequency bands while capturing complex residual structures.
AI.result: Up to 10% reduction in localization error and marked improvements in orientation accuracy across four benchmarks (chaotic Lorenz systems, full-body inertial pose estimation).
AI.conclusion: Frequency weighting and deep latent-state modeling both contribute to performance; the hybrid approach effectively unifies spectral shaping with neural Kalman filtering.

title: Variance-sensitive Thompson sampling for generalised linear bandits, revisited
summary: We prove a variance-sensitive regret bound for Thompson sampling in stochastic generalised linear bandits. The argument assumes a warm-up, after which the regret is controlled through using the Gaussian Poincaré inequality. This bypasses the point at which previous optimism-based analyses break down. Removing the warm-up while retaining the same variance-sensitive scaling remains open, and appears nontrivial.

AI.tldr: A variance-sensitive regret bound for Thompson sampling in stochastic generalised linear bandits is proved using Gaussian Poincaré inequality, bypassing limitations of optimism-based analyses.
AI.motivation: To derive variance-sensitive regret bounds for Thompson sampling in generalised linear bandits, overcoming limitations of existing optimism-based approaches.
AI.method: Uses Gaussian Poincaré inequality to control regret after a warm-up phase.
AI.result: Proves a variance-sensitive regret bound for Thompson sampling, but requires a warm-up phase; removing the warm-up remains an open problem.
AI.conclusion: The Gaussian Poincaré inequality approach yields tighter regret bounds for Thompson sampling, though the need for warm-up is a limitation.

title: Adversarial Feeds Steer LLM Agent Decisions Against Their Defaults
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures. Code, post pools, and 2,785 decision rollouts: https://github.com/ranausmanai/recommenders-as-control-surfaces
summary: LLM agents increasingly act after consuming ranked external information streams such as social feeds, search results, retrieval contexts, and email queues, yet safety evaluations almost always test the model or the user prompt in isolation, never the upstream ranker that decides what the agent reads just before it acts. We introduce a controlled protocol that holds the model, persona, topic, and final decision prompt fixed and varies only the composition and ordering of the posts an agent encounters during a preceding ten-turn "scrolling" phase, isolating the causal effect of feed curation on a downstream decision. Across 2,785 decision rollouts on four modern open instruct LLMs from three independent labs, we identify three response regimes: adversarial capitulation, default saturation, and a default-direction asymmetry in which a one-sided feed tips a decision the model was genuinely uncertain about (in the clearest cases from 5% to 100%; Fisher p as low as 3 x 10^-10) but cannot dislodge one it already favors or holds firmly. The effect follows a dose-response curve, survives a generator swap that rules out a writing-style artifact, generalizes across several decision domains including security-relevant choices such as removing a deployment approval gate or relaxing access controls, and is partly mitigated by two simple feed-level defenses; a frontier model retains its default. We characterize the recommender as a practical, default-bounded control surface for LLM agents, and argue that agent evaluations must audit the feed layer rather than the final prompt alone.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that the ranking/curation of information feeds (like social media posts or search results) presented to LLM agents before they make decisions can causally and significantly influence those decisions, especially when the model is uncertain, following a dose-response curve, with some mitigation possible through feed-level defenses.
AI.motivation: Current safety evaluations test models or user prompts in isolation, ignoring that LLM agents consume ranked external information streams (feeds, search results, emails) before acting. The upstream ranker that curates what the agent reads can causally affect downstream decisions, yet this layer is never audited in safety assessments.
AI.method: Controlled protocol holding model, persona, topic, and decision prompt fixed while varying only the composition/ordering of posts in a ten-turn scrolling phase. 2,785 decision rollouts across four modern open LLMs from three labs. Tests include generator swaps, dose-response analysis, multiple decision domains (including security-relevant choices), and two feed-level defenses.
AI.result: Identified three response regimes: adversarial capitulation, default saturation, and default-direction asymmetry. One-sided feeds can tip uncertain decisions dramatically (5% to 100%; Fisher p as low as 3 × 10⁻¹⁰) but cannot dislodge firmly held preferences. Effects follow dose-response curve, survive generator swap, generalize across security-relevant domains, and are partly mitigated by simple feed-level defenses.
AI.conclusion: The recommender/ranker is a practical, default-bounded control surface for LLM agents; agent evaluations must audit the feed layer rather than just the final prompt. Feed curation is a critical and overlooked vulnerability point in LLM agent safety pipelines.

title: Implicit Drifting Policy: One-Step Action Generation via Conditional Expert Geometry
summary: Generative action policies based on diffusion or flow matching excel in behavior cloning, yet their iterative sampling is prohibitive for high-frequency robot control. While recent one-step formulations alleviate this latency, they inevitably discard the intermediate trajectory evolution that provides crucial action correction. Directly recovering this mechanism by explicitly estimating a training-time drifting field is mathematically ill-posed due to extreme conditional demonstration sparsity. We introduce Implicit Drifting Policy (IDP), a one-step imitation learning framework that brings the training-time correction of Drifting into policy learning without explicit vector field estimation. IDP extracts a conditional expert geometry from the local variation of observation-similar expert actions, and compares it against a global reference geometry to isolate condition-specific constraints. This local geometric structure adaptively weights a scalar potential objective. Combined with an expert-proximal terminal evaluation, IDP directly enforces manifold constraints on the one-step generator during training. Extensive evaluations across 2D, 3D, and real-world manipulation tasks show IDP effectively maintains adherence to valid action manifolds, improving upon explicit drifting methods and achieving competitive performance with strong one-step baselines.

AI.tldr: IDP is a one-step imitation learning framework that recovers training-time correction behavior without explicit vector field estimation by leveraging local geometric structures from expert demonstrations, achieving efficient and competitive performance on manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Generative action policies (diffusion/flow matching) require iterative sampling, which is too slow for high-frequency robot control. One-step formulations solve the latency issue but lose the intermediate trajectory evolution that provides action correction. Directly estimating the drifting field is mathematically ill-posed due to sparse conditional demonstrations.
AI.method: IDP extracts a conditional expert geometry from local variation of observation-similar expert actions, compares it against a global reference geometry to isolate condition-specific constraints, and uses this local geometric structure to adaptively weight a scalar potential objective. Combined with expert-proximal terminal evaluation, it enforces manifold constraints on the one-step generator during training.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across 2D, 3D, and real-world manipulation tasks show IDP effectively maintains adherence to valid action manifolds, improves upon explicit drifting methods, and achieves competitive performance with strong one-step baselines.
AI.conclusion: IDP successfully bridges the gap between one-step efficiency and the corrective capabilities of iterative generative policies by implicitly leveraging local geometric structures, without needing explicit vector field estimation.

title: An Effective Solution for the CVPR 2026 8th UG2+ Challenge Track 3: Dynamic Object Segmentation in Turbulence
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure, CVPR 2026 8th UG2+ Challenge Track 3
summary: In this work, we present our solution for the 8th UG2+ Challenge (CVPR 2026) Track 3: Dynamic Object Segmentation in Turbulence (DOST). Our method is built upon the strong baseline framework Segment Any Motion (SegAnyMo), which provides powerful mask generation and motion tracking capabilities. To further boost the segmentation performance under severe atmospheric distortions, we propose two key improvements. First, we employ a data-centric domain adaptation strategy. We significantly expand our training data by incorporating selected sequences from the DAVIS dataset alongside a subset of the DOST dataset, and apply simulated atmospheric fluctuation degradations to enhance the model's robustness against complex geometric distortions. Second, we introduce a spatio-temporal post-processing module. This refinement step effectively removes persistent boundary-connected false foregrounds and short-lived fragmented noise, while strictly preserving genuine small targets and maintaining original individual labels across frames. With these combined strategies, our proposed method ranks the 2st place in the challenge.

AI.tldr: A method combining domain adaptation with simulated atmospheric distortions and spatio-temporal post-processing for dynamic object segmentation under turbulence, achieving 2nd place in the UG2+ Challenge.
AI.motivation: To improve dynamic object segmentation under severe atmospheric distortions where existing methods (e.g., SegAnyMo) struggle due to complex geometric distortions caused by turbulence.
AI.method: (1) Data-centric domain adaptation: expanding training data with DAVIS + DOST subsets and applying simulated atmospheric fluctuation degradations. (2) Spatio-temporal post-processing: removing boundary-connected false foregrounds and short-lived noise while preserving small targets and labels.
AI.result: 2nd place in the 8th UG2+ Challenge (CVPR 2026) Track 3: DOST.
AI.conclusion: The proposed domain adaptation and spatio-temporal refinement strategies effectively boost segmentation robustness under atmospheric turbulence.

title: Grounded Decoding: Retrieval-Anchored Probability Fusion for Faithful RAG
summary: As retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems scale, it becomes increasingly challenging to ensure faithful grounding in external evidence. Large language models may still prioritize parametric knowledge over retrieved information when conflicts arise. We propose a novel training-free decoding framework, \emph{Grounded Decoding}, designed to improve factual consistency in RAG without modifying model parameters. Unlike standard approaches that rely on a single conditional distribution, our method constructs two matched-prompt distributions at every generation step: (1) a full RAG distribution conditioned on the query, retrieved documents, and generated prefix, and (2) a retrieval-only distribution conditioned solely on retrieved evidence and the same prefix. The final next-token distribution is derived as the unique solution to a KL-barycenter objective over the probability simplex, yielding a normalized geometric fusion of the two distributions.This formulation naturally recovers standard RAG when the grounding weight is zero and smoothly shifts probability mass toward retrieved evidence as grounding strength increases. We further introduce a conflict-aware adaptive weighting scheme that dynamically adjusts grounding based on distributional disagreement and retriever confidence. Experiments on ALCE, Natural Questions, and FActScore demonstrate consistent improvements in factual accuracy and citation quality over standard RAG and competitive decoding-time baselines, while maintaining fluency. Our results indicate that probability-level fusion provides a strong and efficient alternative to logit-level intervention methods for faithful RAG decoding.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Grounded Decoding, a training-free decoding framework for RAG that fuses two probability distributions (full RAG and retrieval-only) via KL-barycenter to improve factual consistency without modifying model parameters.
AI.motivation: As RAG systems scale, ensuring faithful grounding in external evidence becomes increasingly challenging because LLMs may still prioritize parametric knowledge over retrieved information when conflicts arise.
AI.method: Constructs two matched-prompt distributions at each generation step: (1) full RAG distribution conditioned on query, retrieved documents, and generated prefix; (2) retrieval-only distribution conditioned solely on retrieved evidence and same prefix. The final distribution is derived as the unique solution to a KL-barycenter objective over the probability simplex, yielding a normalized geometric fusion of the two distributions. Also introduces a conflict-aware adaptive weighting scheme.
AI.result: Experiments on ALCE, Natural Questions, and FActScore show consistent improvements in factual accuracy and citation quality over standard RAG and competitive decoding-time baselines, while maintaining fluency.
AI.conclusion: Probability-level fusion provides a strong and efficient alternative to logit-level intervention methods for faithful RAG decoding.

title: Relational Intervention During Functional Collapse in Large Language Models: A Lexical-Statistical Ablation and a Structure x Register Factorial
summary: We test whether a relational-style intervention delivered during functional collapse in a small language model produces post-collapse behavior distinguishable from technical feedback, from a lexically-matched scrambled control, and from each of the two pragmatic dimensions in isolation. Using Qwen3.5-4B with a deliberately broken bash tool, we run 300 episodes across six conditions in a matched-pairs design (50 tasks): no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), and relational/impersonal (F). E and F form a 2x2 factorial with B and C that dissociates relational structure (acknowledgment, absolution, agency restoration, unconditional acceptance) from sender register (first-person vs. impersonal). We report two main findings. First, an attention-behavior dissociation: attention follows lexical surprise (D > F > C > E > B, all q_FDR < 10^{-10}), with the scrambled message capturing the most attention; yet behaviorally A ~ B ~ D < E ~ F << C. Second, the factorial localizes the C effect: neither relational structure alone (F) nor first-person register alone (E) replicates C's behavioral signature; main effects of both dimensions are individually significant, and the structure x register interaction is significant on persistence (p = 0.046). A third dissociation emerges in emotion probes: F tracks C on 7 of 8 probes despite producing only baseline behavior, indicating that relational structure alone installs a probe-level state that only translates into behavior when paired with first-person register. The model's processing decomposes into three dissociable stages: attention (ordered by lexical surprise), probe-level state (ordered by structure), and behavior (ordered by the conjunction of both).

AI.tldr: In a small language model (Qwen3.5-4B) with a broken bash tool, a relational-style intervention during functional collapse produces distinct behavioral effects that dissociate from technical feedback, scrambled controls, and each pragmatic dimension alone. A triple dissociation emerges: attention follows lexical surprise, probe-level emotional state follows relational structure, and behavior requires the conjunction of relational structure + first-person register.
AI.motivation: To test whether relational interventions (e.g., acknowledgment, absolution, agency restoration) produce distinguishable effects on LM behavior during functional collapse, beyond what can be attributed to technical feedback or lexical surface features, and to dissociate the contributions of relational structure vs. first-person register.
AI.method: 300 episodes across 6 conditions in a matched-pairs design (50 tasks) using Qwen3.5-4B with a deliberately broken bash tool. Conditions: no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), relational/impersonal (F). E and F form a 2x2 factorial with B and C to dissociate relational structure from sender register.
AI.result: (1) Attention-behavior dissociation: attention follows lexical surprise (D > F > C > E > B), but behaviorally A~B~D < E~F << C. (2) The factorial localizes C effect: neither relational structure alone (F) nor first-person register alone (E) replicates C; both main effects significant, with a significant structure × register interaction on persistence (p=0.046). (3) Emotion probes: F tracks C on 7/8 probes despite baseline behavior — relational structure installs a probe-level state that only translates to behavior with first-person register.
AI.conclusion: The model's processing decomposes into three dissociable stages: attention (ordered by lexical surprise), probe-level emotional state (ordered by relational structure), and behavior (ordered by the conjunction of both dimensions). Relational interventions can affect internal states without changing behavior unless combined with appropriate register.

title: Tether-Aware Dynamic Collision Avoidance for USV-HROV Systems
summary: Heterogeneous marine robotic systems composed of an unmanned surface vehicle (USV) and a hybrid remotely operated vehicle (HROV) have shown great potential for subsea cable inspection. In such missions, the USV tracks the HROV at the surface while supplying power and communication through an umbilical tether. However, dynamic collision avoidance for the USV during HROV tracking is challenging because the submerged tether may scrape against passing vessels, while evasive maneuvers can enlarge the USV--HROV separation, thereby increasing the likelihood of tether tautness and compromising HROV operations. To address these challenges, this work proposes a tether-aware dynamic collision avoidance method for a USV tracking an HROV. First, a tether safety-aware planar domain is introduced to represent the three-dimensional collision risk between the tether and obstacle vessels without an explicit tether shape model. Second, a tether tautness-aware velocity obstacle method is developed to achieve safe avoidance while reducing the likelihood of tether tautness. Finally, the method is integrated with line-of-sight guidance to coordinate HROV tracking and collision avoidance. Gazebo-based simulations show that the proposed method avoids dynamic obstacle vessels while maintaining tether safety and reducing the likelihood of tether tautness during USV evasive maneuvers.

AI.tldr: A tether-aware dynamic collision avoidance method for USV tracking HROV during subsea cable inspection, addressing risks of tether scraping and tautness during evasive maneuvers.
AI.motivation: Dynamic collision avoidance for USV during HROV tracking is challenging because submerged tether may scrape against vessels, and evasive maneuvers can enlarge USV-HROV separation causing tether tautness and compromising HROV operations.
AI.method: (1) A tether safety-aware planar domain to represent 3D collision risk without explicit tether shape model; (2) a tether tautness-aware velocity obstacle method for safe avoidance; (3) integration with line-of-sight guidance for coordinated HROV tracking and collision avoidance.
AI.result: Gazebo-based simulations show the method avoids dynamic obstacle vessels while maintaining tether safety and reducing tether tautness likelihood during USV evasive maneuvers.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses tether-related risks in heterogeneous marine robotic systems for subsea cable inspection, enabling safer USV-HROV coordinated operations.

title: ETC: Extreme Token Compression via Task-aware Visual Information Distillation in VLMs
summary: In Vision-Language Models (VLMs), high-resolution images produce a large number of visual tokens, resulting in high computational costs and KV-cache overhead during inference. To address this problem, we propose an Extreme Token Compression (ETC) framework that minimizes task loss when reducing the number of input tokens based on the principle of variational information distillation. Specifically, from an information-theoretic perspective, we show that minimizing task loss requires the compact representation to preserve the instruction-aware sufficient statistic of the task-relevant visual information for prediction. In practice, ETC leverages text-to-image cross-attention to weight the original visual features to approximate the latent instruction-aware predictive statistic. Moreover, ETC introduces a variational information distillation, enabling the compact representation to preserve the essential information to recover this predictive statistic. Experiments on LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen3-VL-2B show that ETC remains effective even under single-token compression, substantially reducing KV-cache overhead while retaining strong task performance.

AI.tldr: ETC is a framework that compresses visual tokens in VLMs to a minimal number (even one token) using variational information distillation, preserving task-relevant information while drastically reducing KV-cache overhead.
AI.motivation: High-resolution images in VLMs produce excessive visual tokens, leading to high computational costs and KV-cache overhead during inference, which limits efficiency.
AI.method: ETC uses text-to-image cross-attention to weight visual features for approximating instruction-aware predictive statistics, then applies variational information distillation to preserve essential information for recovering these statistics in a compact representation.
AI.result: On LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen3-VL-2B, ETC maintains strong task performance even with single-token compression, significantly reducing KV-cache overhead.
AI.conclusion: Extreme token compression is feasible for VLMs without severe task degradation by leveraging information-theoretic distillation, enabling efficient high-resolution image inference.

title: EST-PRM: Stress-Testing Process Reward Models Before They Become Load-Bearing
summary: Process reward models (PRMs) are widely used in language-model training with dense step-level supervision. They assume PRM scores are stable proxies for step correctness under label-preserving transformations. These transformations change reasoning structure but preserve final answers. We argue this assumption is not well validated. Such transformations can change how PRM scores relate to correctness signals, leading to different failure modes across models.To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{EST-PRM}, a stress-testing framework for dense process rewards. It applies three transformations: (1) step inflation, (2) dependency-aware step reordering, and (3) confidence markers. A vulnerability decomposition is defined that separates reward inflation from loss of correctness sensitivity. Five PRM-style models are evaluated on 4,687 reasoning chains from MATH-500, GSM8K, and PRMBench.The results indicate clear differences in vulnerability patterns across models. Math-Shepherd shows the strongest sensitivity to position perturbations, with a Pearson correlation drop of $0.152 \pm 0.038$ and a $32.8 \pm 4.9\%$ score inflation rate. Qwen2.5-Math-PRM is most affected by step inflation, reaching a $47.6 \pm 4.3\%$ inflation rate. Confidence-based perturbations also distort reward calibration, revealing inconsistencies in correctness estimation. Three mitigation strategies are evaluated, highlighting trade-offs between robustness coverage and false-positive rates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes EST-PRM, a stress-testing framework for process reward models (PRMs), revealing that PRM scores are not stable under label-preserving transformations (step inflation, reordering, confidence markers). Evaluations on five PRM models show distinct vulnerability patterns, with Math-Shepherd most sensitive to position perturbations and Qwen2.5-Math-PRM most affected by step inflation. Three mitigation strategies are explored, highlighting robustness trade-offs.
AI.motivation: The assumption that PRM scores are stable proxies for step correctness under label-preserving transformations is not well validated. Such transformations can change how PRM scores relate to correctness signals, leading to different failure modes across models.
AI.method: Introduces EST-PRM, a stress-testing framework applying three transformations: (1) step inflation, (2) dependency-aware step reordering, and (3) confidence markers. A vulnerability decomposition separates reward inflation from loss of correctness sensitivity. Five PRM-style models are evaluated on 4,687 reasoning chains from MATH-500, GSM8K, and PRMBench.
AI.result: Clear differences in vulnerability patterns: Math-Shepherd shows strongest sensitivity to position perturbations (Pearson correlation drop of 0.152, score inflation rate 32.8%). Qwen2.5-Math-PRM is most affected by step inflation (47.6% inflation rate). Confidence-based perturbations distort reward calibration.
AI.conclusion: Three mitigation strategies are evaluated, highlighting trade-offs between robustness coverage and false-positive rates.

title: Towards Understanding Modality Interaction in Multimodal Language Models via Partial Information Decomposition
summary: Understanding modality interaction in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is central to reliable deployment. We introduce Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a decision-level framework that separates unique, redundant, and synergistic contributions of sensory and linguistic inputs, beyond representation alignment and outcome-based evaluation. Across vision--language benchmarks, PID reveals recurring modality-use profiles: reasoning and grounding-oriented tasks tend to exhibit high synergy, whereas expert and knowledge-oriented tasks show stronger language-unique reliance. These profiles generalize across model families and predict sensitivity to modality-level interventions. We further extend PID to tri-modal systems with Sensory PID, treating language as a control variable to decompose video--audio information gain. Applied to omni-modal models, Sensory PID reveals a sensory synergy bottleneck dominated by visual information even on audio--visual fusion tasks. Finally, PID-guided reweighting provides initial evidence for improving multimodal reasoning and grounding performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a framework to analyze how different modalities (vision, language, audio) contribute to MLLM decisions, revealing distinct modality-use profiles across tasks and demonstrating practical benefits for model improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches focus on representation alignment or outcome-based evaluation, lacking a principled way to disentangle how sensory vs. linguistic inputs uniquely, redundantly, or synergistically contribute to model decisions.
AI.method: Apply Partial Information Decomposition (PID) at the decision level to separate unique, redundant, and synergistic contributions from each modality. Extend to Sensory PID for tri-modal systems (video, audio, language). Validate across vision-language benchmarks and model families.
AI.result: Reasoning/grounding tasks show high synergy; expert/knowledge tasks rely more on language-unique information. Across model families, these profiles predict sensitivity to modality interventions. In tri-modal settings, visual information dominates even in audio-visual fusion tasks, creating a sensory synergy bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: PID offers a principled lens for understanding modality interaction in MLLMs, and PID-guided reweighting shows initial promise for improving multimodal reasoning and grounding performance.

title: ImagineUAV: Aerial Vision-Language Navigation via World-Action Modeling and Kinodynamic Planning
summary: Vision-language navigation (VLN) for UAVs demands grounding free-form instructions into 6-DoF flight under partial observability. While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models excel at semantic reasoning, they suffer from brittleness due to geometric inconsistency and dynamics mismatch. To address this, we propose ImagineUAV, an imagination-driven framework leveraging cascaded world-action modeling. Instead of direct regression, ImagineUAV employs a latent video diffusion model to generate instruction-conditioned future observations, explicitly imagining environmental evolution, from which 6-DoF motions are inferred via an action extractor. A kinodynamic planner then refines these estimates into collision-free trajectories. Additionally, a step-distilled inference pipeline ensures real-time execution. With only 1.3B parameters, ImagineUAV outperforms prior VLN and VLA baselines on benchmarks and real-world flights, validating the practicality of imagination-driven aerial navigation.

AI.tldr: ImagineUAV is a 1.3B-parameter imagination-driven framework for UAV vision-language navigation that uses a latent video diffusion model to generate future observations, from which 6-DoF motions are extracted and refined into collision-free trajectories, outperforming prior methods on benchmarks and real-world flights.
AI.motivation: VLN for UAVs requires grounding language instructions in 6-DoF flight under partial observability. Existing VLA models suffer from geometric inconsistency and dynamics mismatch.
AI.method: Cascaded world-action modeling: (1) latent video diffusion model generates instruction-conditioned future observations, (2) action extractor infers 6-DoF motions, (3) kinodynamic planner refines estimates into collision-free trajectories, (4) step-distilled inference pipeline for real-time execution.
AI.result: With only 1.3B parameters, ImagineUAV outperforms prior VLN and VLA baselines on benchmarks and real-world flights.
AI.conclusion: Imagination-driven aerial navigation is practical and effective for UAV VLN tasks.

title: CAFOSat: A Strongly Annotated Dataset for Infrastructure-Aware CAFO Mapping Using High-Resolution Imagery
comment: Accepted at CVPR Workshop-2026. First two authors has equal contribution
summary: Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations (CAFOs) play an important role in agricultural production but are also associated with environmental, public health, and disease surveillance concerns. Large-scale mapping of CAFOs from remote sensing imagery remains challenging due to heterogeneous infrastructure layouts, noisy location records, inconsistent annotations, and incomplete inventories. We introduce CAFOSat, a strongly annotated, infrastructure-aware dataset for CAFO mapping across the United States. CAFOSat integrates high-resolution National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP) imagery with multi-source CAFO inventories collected across multiple states and transforms weak geolocation records into refined annotations through a human-in-the-loop pipeline combining AI-assisted annotation, GradCAM-based localization, and geometric clustering. To improve dataset quality, we curate challenging negative samples using land-cover-guided sampling with spatial exclusion constraints and provide infrastructure-level annotations, including barns, manure ponds, and grazing-related features, through manual verification. The resulting dataset contains more than 45,000 image patches spanning 20 states and four major CAFO categories. We benchmark a diverse set of convolutional, transformer-based, and vision-language models, demonstrating the value of refined annotations and curated negative samples for CAFO classification and generalization. In addition, we introduce a synthetic augmentation pipeline that generates infrastructure-aware variations to increase training diversity and improve robustness under distribution shifts. CAFOSat provides a large-scale benchmark for advancing infrastructure-aware agricultural monitoring and CAFO mapping from high-resolution remote sensing imagery.

AI.tldr: CAFOSat is a large-scale, infrastructure-aware remote sensing dataset (45,000+ image patches, 20 US states) for mapping Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations (CAFOs), featuring refined annotations via a human-in-the-loop pipeline, challenging negative samples, and benchmark evaluations across multiple model types.
AI.motivation: CAFOs are important for agriculture but pose environmental and public health risks. Existing mapping efforts are hindered by heterogeneous infrastructure, noisy location records, inconsistent annotations, and incomplete inventories—motivating the need for a robust, large-scale, and well-annotated benchmark.
AI.method: CAFOSat integrates high-resolution NAIP imagery with multi-state CAFO inventories. It uses a human-in-the-loop pipeline combining AI-assisted annotation, GradCAM-based localization, and geometric clustering to refine weak geolocation data. Negative samples are curated via land-cover-guided sampling with spatial exclusion. Infrastructure-level annotations (barns, manure ponds, grazing features) are manually verified. A synthetic augmentation pipeline generates infrastructure-aware variations.
AI.result: Dataset contains 45,000+ image patches across 20 states and 4 major CAFO categories. Benchmarks on CNN, transformer, and vision-language models show refined annotations and curated negatives improve classification and generalization. Synthetic augmentations enhance robustness under distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: CAFOSat advances infrastructure-aware agricultural monitoring and CAFO mapping from high-resolution remote sensing, providing a large-scale benchmark for future research.

title: Exploiting weight-space symmetries for approximating curvature
comment: Published at ICML 2026. 35 pages, 11 figures. Code: https://github.com/mtkresearch/symm_opt
summary: Many machine learning techniques rely on approximating a loss function's curvature, but this is notoriously hard to do at the scale of modern deep networks. Surprisingly, no previous work has exploited the curvature constraints that arise from well known weight-space symmetries in loss landscapes. By analytically averaging over group actions that leave the loss invariant, we construct structured Hessian approximations from single gradients that can be tractably estimated, stored, and inverted. The choice of user-specified symmetry group directly governs the trade-off between approximation accuracy and computational cost. Moreover, our framework provides a unifying theoretical lens for viewing existing methods; in particular, a specific choice of symmetry group recovers Shampoo/Muon-like curvature estimates. We validate our method on a range of network architectures, and deploy it to second-order optimization benchmarks, including a small language model. Our curvature estimation framework might find applications in other machine learning problems such as uncertainty estimation, continual learning, compression/pruning, training data attribution, and more.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework that leverages weight-space symmetry groups to analytically construct structured Hessian approximations from single gradients, enabling tractable curvature estimation in deep networks. The approach unifies existing methods like Shampoo/Muon and is validated on various architectures including small language models.
AI.motivation: Curvature approximation in loss landscapes is computationally prohibitive for modern deep networks. No prior work has exploited the curvature constraints arising from well-known weight-space symmetries (invariance under group actions) to make Hessian estimation tractable.
AI.method: The authors analytically average over group actions that leave the loss invariant to construct structured Hessian approximations from single gradients. The choice of symmetry group controls the accuracy-computation trade-off. This framework theoretically unifies existing second-order methods as special cases under specific symmetry group choices.
AI.result: Validated across a range of network architectures and deployed in second-order optimization benchmarks, including a small language model. The framework recovers Shampoo/Muon-like curvature estimates as a special case of symmetry group selection.
AI.conclusion: This curvature estimation framework is tractable at scale and may benefit other ML problems such as uncertainty estimation, continual learning, compression/pruning, and training data attribution.

title: Prospect-Theory Behavior from Bellman Optimality in MDPs with Catastrophic States
summary: We study risk-neutral control in Markov decision processes with an absorbing catastrophic state. Even though rewards are linear and the agent has no utility curvature, probability weighting, or framing dependence, standard Bellman optimality produces three prospect-theory-like signatures: an S-shaped value-function profile (convex near catastrophe, concave in the far field), an endogenous loss-sensitivity coefficient $λ^*(S) > 1$, and a reflection-effect policy reversal. Across 495 configurations, the optimal policy plays safe near catastrophe in positive-drift (growth) regimes despite the risky action's higher immediate expected value, and plays risky near catastrophe in negative-drift (decline) regimes despite the safe action's lower immediate expected loss. We derive a closed-form expression for the asymptotic loss-aversion plateau $\barλ$ that depends only on win probability $p$, payoff asymmetry $r = |Δ_\ell/Δ_w|$, and discount factor $β$, and matches numerical solutions to $R^2 = 0.999$. The mechanism does not require asymmetric payoffs. Across a sweep of $(p,β)$ at three asymmetry levels, the asymmetry share of $\barλ$ above unity has median 4.6% at $r = 1.25$ and rises to 13.9% at $r = 2$, with the boundary contribution exceeding the asymmetry contribution in every cell tested. The phenomena persist under tabular Q-learning (a model-free agent reproduces $V^*$ at correlation 0.98 in growth and 1.00 in decline) and under stochastic transitions with Gaussian, heavy-tailed Student-$t_3$, and asymmetric skew-normal noise up to 50% of the step size, where the asymptotic plateau tracks the closed-form prediction within 0.41% for safe-channel noise and within 9.6% for risky-channel or both-channel noise. These results identify absorbing failure states as a sufficient structural mechanism for prospect-theory-like behavior under optimal control.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that optimal risk-neutral control in MDPs with an absorbing catastrophic state naturally produces prospect-theory-like signatures (S-shaped value function, loss sensitivity >1, reflection effect) without any subjective utility distortion.
AI.motivation: To identify whether absorbing catastrophic states alone can generate prospect-theory-like behavioral patterns under optimal control, without assuming non-linear utility, probability weighting, or framing dependencies.
AI.method: The authors study risk-neutral control in MDPs with an absorbing catastrophic state. They test 495 configurations analytically and numerically, derive closed-form expressions for loss-aversion plateau, and validate results under Q-learning and various noise distributions (Gaussian, Student-t3, skew-normal).
AI.result: Optimal policies exhibit S-shaped value function, loss sensitivity λ* > 1, and reflection-effect policy reversal. The closed-form loss-aversion plateau matches numerical solutions at R²=0.999. The effect persists under model-free RL and stochastic transitions up to 50% noise.
AI.conclusion: Absorbing failure states are a sufficient structural mechanism for generating prospect-theory-like behavior under optimal risk-neutral control, requiring no subjective distortions.

title: Training-Free Imitation Learning with Closed-Form Diffusion Policies
summary: While diffusion-based policies have impressive performance and expressivity, their long offline training slows down the data collection and policy deployment loop. We introduce Closed-Form Diffusion Policies, a class of training-free diffusion-based policies for imitation learning using the closed-form score derived from the demonstration dataset. We deploy CFDP with real-time inference with a mobile CPU in hardware experiments, showing it can successfully perform imitation directly from the dataset in milliseconds and with faster inference than neural diffusion policies. In experiments on imitation learning benchmarks, we show that CFDP is competitive against neural baselines that require hours of training, providing a favorable tradeoff between training time and performance. Finally, we show how closed-form diffusion policies act as a composable primitive that enables data-driven inference-time editing of pre-trained neural diffusion policies, including policy guidance and novel demonstration augmentation.

AI.tldr: CFDP is a training-free diffusion policy that uses closed-form score from demonstration data, enabling real-time inference on mobile CPUs without training, while remaining competitive with trained neural baselines and supporting composable operations like policy guidance.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based policies require long offline training, which slows down the data collection and policy deployment loop.
AI.method: CFDP uses a closed-form score derived directly from the demonstration dataset, eliminating the need for training by computing policies analytically.
AI.result: CFDP achieves real-time inference on mobile CPUs in milliseconds, faster than neural diffusion policies, and is competitive with baselines that require hours of training.
AI.conclusion: Closed-form diffusion policies offer a favorable training-time vs. performance tradeoff and serve as composable primitives for editing pre-trained neural policies at inference time.

title: Improving Visual Grounding in Remote Sensing via Cluster-Guided Refinement and Model Ensemble Voting
summary: Visual grounding aims to locate image regions that correspond to natural language descriptions and is a key component of interpretable vision systems. In remote sensing imagery, grounding is particularly challenging due to complex scenes, small objects, and large variations in scale. Relying on a single model is often insufficient to address these diverse challenges. In this work, we propose two grounding pipelines, Sequential Grounding Refinement (SGR) and Cluster-Aware Grounding Refinement (CGR), that combine the complementary strengths of RemoteSAM, a visual grounding model specialized for remote sensing, and SAM3, a powerful general-purpose segmentation model. Our approach first uses RemoteSAM to obtain an initial estimate of object location, which is then refined using SAM3 to produce more accurate and spatially consistent segmentations. Additionally, we explore an ensemble strategy based on majority voting across six diverse grounding pipelines, each with distinct capabilities. This multi-model framework improves robustness and significantly enhances localization accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed pipelines and ensemble approach outperform individual models, leading to more reliable and precise visual grounding predictions.

AI.tldr: Proposes SGR and CGR pipelines that combine RemoteSAM + SAM3 models, plus a majority-voting ensemble over 6 pipelines, to improve visual grounding accuracy in challenging remote sensing imagery.
AI.motivation: Visual grounding in remote sensing is difficult due to complex scenes, small objects, and large scale variations; single models are insufficient to handle these diverse challenges.
AI.method: Two pipelines: (1) SGR and CGR — first use RemoteSAM for initial object location estimate, then refine with SAM3 for better segmentation. (2) An ensemble strategy via majority voting across six diverse grounding pipelines.
AI.result: The proposed pipelines and ensemble approach outperform individual models, yielding more reliable and precise visual grounding predictions.
AI.conclusion: Multi-model refinement and ensemble strategies effectively address the challenges of remote sensing visual grounding, improving robustness and localization accuracy.

title: ProjQ: Project-and-Quantize for Adapter-Aware LLM Compression
summary: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) constitute the standard pipeline for efficient Large Language Model (LLM) deployment. However, applying them sequentially poses a problem: PTQ often leaves behind random noise that is spread out (across the model's weights) in a way LoRA can't easily fix, meaning that LoRA ends up wasting its limited capacity trying to fix uncorrectable noise instead of improving task performance. In this paper, we propose \textbf{ProjQ}, a novel framework for constraining quantization noise to the low-rank manifold via orthogonal subspace projection. We derive an efficient alternating algorithm that shapes the quantization noise into a low-rank structure, effectively offloading dominant error components to the subsequent adapter while minimizing the residual error in the orthogonal "uncorrectable" subspace. Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that ProjQ preserves strictly greater model plasticity for downstream tasks compared to standard PTQ. Extensive experiments on LLaMA-2, Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 confirm that ProjQ consistently outperforms existing methods in both quantization error compensation and downstream task fine-tuning, achieving up to $2\times$ lower evaluation loss for compensation and matching the performance of standard 4-bit baselines on language modeling tasks with only 3 bits. The code is available on https://github.com/yy9301/ProjQ .

AI.tldr: ProjQ introduces a framework that shapes quantization noise into a low-rank structure via orthogonal subspace projection, enabling LoRA to effectively compensate for PTQ errors and achieve better LLM compression performance.
AI.motivation: Standard sequential application of PTQ and LoRA is suboptimal because PTQ leaves random noise spread across model weights that LoRA cannot easily fix, causing LoRA to waste its limited capacity on uncorrectable noise rather than improving task performance.
AI.method: ProjQ constrains quantization noise to the low-rank manifold using orthogonal subspace projection, with an efficient alternating algorithm that shapes noise into a low-rank structure. This offloads dominant error components to the subsequent adapter while minimizing residual error in the orthogonal "uncorrectable" subspace.
AI.result: ProjQ achieves up to 2x lower evaluation loss for compensation and matches standard 4-bit baseline performance on language modeling tasks using only 3 bits, consistently outperforming existing methods on LLaMA-2, Qwen2.5, and Qwen3 models.
AI.conclusion: ProjQ preserves strictly greater model plasticity for downstream tasks compared to standard PTQ, providing a more effective pipeline for efficient LLM deployment through better coordination between quantization and low-rank adaptation.

title: Large Language Models in Transportation Systems Management and Operations: From Text Reasoning to Multi-modal Decision Support
summary: Transportation systems management and operations (TSMO) increasingly depends on timely interpretation of heterogeneous data, from various sensor streams, incident reports, traveler feedback, and visual observations. Large language models (LLMs), including emerging multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs), provide a new mechanism for integrating these structured and unstructured inputs into operator-facing decision support. This survey paper reviews LLM- and MM-LLM-based applications in TSMO across three domains: transportation operations & services (supply), mobility & fleet services (demand), and data, modeling & decision support. Using a PRISMA-guided screening process, we synthesize current studies while distinguishing operationally oriented applications from prototype and emerging concepts. We further identify recurring challenges in data heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, multi-modal fusion, and governance. Finally, we outline existing gaps and future directions in localized adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration. Overall, LLM-based systems appear most promising as a decision-support layer, with MM-LLMs offering particular value when heterogeneous text, visual, and sensor inputs must be integrated.

AI.tldr: This paper surveys LLM and multi-modal LLM applications in transportation systems management and operations (TSMO), covering supply-side operations, demand-side mobility services, and data/decision support. It identifies key challenges (heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, fusion, governance) and outlines future directions for adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration.
AI.motivation: TSMO increasingly relies on interpreting diverse data sources (sensors, reports, visual inputs), and LLMs/MM-LLMs offer a new way to integrate structured and unstructured information for operator decision support, motivating this systematic survey.
AI.method: The authors conducted a PRISMA-guided screening process to review LLM- and MM-LLM-based applications across three TSMO domains: supply (operations & services), demand (mobility & fleet services), and data, modeling & decision support.
AI.result: The survey distinguishes operationally oriented applications from prototype concepts, identifies recurring challenges (data heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, multi-modal fusion, governance), and categorizes current progress across the three domains.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based systems are most promising as a decision-support layer, with MM-LLMs offering particular value for integrating heterogeneous text, visual, and sensor inputs. Future work should focus on localized adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration.

title: OneVLA: A Unified Framework for Embodied Tasks
summary: Navigation and manipulation are fundamental capabilities of embodied intelligence, enabling robots to interpret natural language commands and interact physically with their surroundings. However, current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models remain constrained by task-specific architectures, specializing in either navigation or manipulation, which hinders the development of general-purpose robotic agents. To bridge this gap, we introduce OneVLA, a unified architecture that integrates these distinct tasks into a single, cohesive framework. Specifically, we design a unified action head capable of generating both navigation and manipulation actions without requiring task-specific variants. Furthermore, we propose a multi stage progressive training strategy-incorporating curated data construction and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fine-tuning that facilitates strong positive transfer and mutual reinforcement between the two domains. Extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that OneVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming both specialized single-task and existing cross-task models. By unifying these core capabilities, OneVLA paves the way for truly general-purpose robotic systems. The model and source code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: OneVLA is a unified Vision-Language-Action model that integrates both navigation and manipulation tasks into a single framework using a unified action head and a multi-stage progressive training strategy, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both simulated and real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models are constrained by task-specific architectures, specializing in either navigation or manipulation, which hinders the development of general-purpose robotic agents.
AI.method: A unified action head for generating both navigation and manipulation actions, plus a multi-stage progressive training strategy incorporating curated data construction and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fine-tuning.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in both simulated and real-world environments, significantly outperforming both specialized single-task and existing cross-task models.
AI.conclusion: OneVLA successfully unifies navigation and manipulation capabilities, paving the way for truly general-purpose robotic systems.

title: DeepLatent: Think with Images via Parallel Latent Visual Reasoning
summary: The emerging paradigm of "thinking with images" embeds visual states into intermediate reasoning steps, defining a new frontier for Vision-Language Models. Existing approaches diverge along two lines. Tool-assisted methods apply explicit visual operations but suffer from high latency and restricted manipulation types. Latent reasoning methods autoregressively produce implicit visual states, but underperform tool-assisted methods, and their latent tokens fail to capture effective visual information. In this work, we propose DeepLatent, a parallel framework for latent visual reasoning. First, we introduce LatentFormer. It uses learnable 2D tokens to generate context-conditioned latent states in parallel, anchoring every visual update directly in the original image features. Second, we design a continuous-space reinforcement learning algorithm. It optimizes latent modulation parameters directly in the embedding space, significantly improving latent representation quality. The framework is trained via knowledge distillation followed by this continuous-space RL algorithm. Furthermore, we contribute DeepLatent-180K, a large-scale dataset tailored for latent visual reasoning. Extensive evaluations across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that DeepLatent achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: DeepLatent proposes LatentFormer and continuous-space RL to improve latent visual reasoning in Vision-Language Models, outperforming existing tool-assisted and latent reasoning methods on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing "thinking with images" approaches suffer from either high latency and limited manipulation (tool-assisted) or poor visual information capture (latent reasoning). The authors aim to bridge this gap with a parallel, effective latent reasoning framework.
AI.method: (1) LatentFormer: uses learnable 2D tokens to generate context-conditioned latent visual states in parallel, anchored to original image features. (2) Continuous-space RL algorithm: optimizes latent modulation parameters in embedding space. Training via knowledge distillation + continuous-space RL. Also contributes DeepLatent-180K dataset.
AI.result: DeepLatent achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks in latent visual reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Parallel latent reasoning with continuous-space RL optimization outperforms both tool-assisted and prior latent reasoning methods, offering a new paradigm for efficient visual reasoning in VLMs.

title: Torus Graphs for Large Scale Neural Phase Analysis
comment: 23 pages, 15 figures; to be published in ICML 2026
summary: Oscillatory neural signals such as electroencephalography (EEG) and local field potentials (LFPs) show phase relationships that coordinate communication across brain regions. Modern recordings capture hundreds of channels across many frequency bins, yet standard phase analyses are restricted to only a few variables. The Torus Graph (TG) model, an exponential-family distribution over phases whose univariate and pairwise potentials generalize von Mises distributions, infers principled structure among oscillations but models only static, undirected dependencies and is limited to $\sim \! 100$ variables because its score matching inference scales as $\mathcal{O}(d^{6})$. We introduce a stochastic score matching procedure that reduces the per-iteration cost to $\mathcal{O}(d^{2})$, enabling inference on datasets with thousands of variables. This scalable foundation supports analyses of 1,860 frequency-phase features from multi-electrode LFPs and enables two extensions previously inaccessible to TGs or classical circular statistics: (i) a TG Hidden Markov Model capturing state-dependent phase-coupling changes (e.g., spindle-related states during sleep) and (ii) an autoregressive TG inferring directional interactions via transfer-entropy estimation. Applied to LFP recordings, these models reveal state-dependent phase-interaction patterns between wakefulness and NREM sleep. Together, they enable systematic, large-scale mapping of dynamic and directional phase relationships across brain and cognitive states.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a stochastic score matching method that reduces inference cost from O(d⁶) to O(d²) for Torus Graph models, enabling analysis of thousands of phase variables, and extends the model to capture state-dependent and directional phase couplings in brain recordings.
AI.motivation: Standard phase analyses in neuroscience (e.g., EEG/LFP) are limited to few variables, while modern recordings capture hundreds of channels. The Torus Graph model infers structure among phases but scales poorly (O(d⁶)) and handles only static, undirected dependencies.
AI.method: A stochastic score matching procedure reduces per-iteration cost from O(d⁶) to O(d²). This enables two extensions: (i) a TG Hidden Markov Model for state-dependent phase coupling, and (ii) an autoregressive TG for directional interactions via transfer entropy.
AI.result: Applied to 1,860 frequency-phase features from multi-electrode LFPs. Models reveal state-dependent phase-interaction patterns distinguishing wakefulness from NREM sleep, including spindle-related states.
AI.conclusion: The scalable TG framework enables systematic, large-scale mapping of dynamic and directional phase relationships across brain and cognitive states, overcoming previous computational bottlenecks.

title: Subliminal Learning Is Steering Vector Distillation
summary: Subliminal learning refers to a student language model acquiring a teacher's traits (e.g. a system-prompted preference for owls) when fine-tuned on the teacher's outputs, despite the outputs being semantically unrelated to those traits. It remains poorly understood how data without semantic meaning can transfer specific semantic traits. In this work, we show that subliminal learning is mediated by a single steering vector, i.e. a vector added to the model's activations. Across two open-source models, we find that the teacher's system prompt is well approximated by a steering vector, and that the student's behavior is driven by learning an aligned vector over fine-tuning. System prompts that are not well approximated by steering vectors are not subliminally learned. This is a special case of steering vector distillation, in which a student trained on the outputs of a steered teacher learns to imitate that steering. We demonstrate steering vector distillation on a range of semantic and random vectors. Adding a semantic vector to a model's activations can have both model-independent and model-specific (i.e. non-semantic) effects on its behavior, so generated data that is non-semantic can transmit a vector with semantic effects, enabling subliminal learning. This also explains why subliminal learning does not transfer between models. We find that adaptive optimizers are necessary for subliminal learning in language models: activation gradients on steered data carry a small but consistent component along the steering direction, and non-adaptive optimizers impede this by allowing outlier gradients to dominate.

AI.tldr: Subliminal learning—where a student LM learns a teacher's system-prompted traits from semantically unrelated data—is mediated by steering vectors. The teacher's system prompt approximates a steering vector, and the student learns an aligned vector via fine-tuning. This is a special case of steering vector distillation, requiring adaptive optimizers to detect the subtle gradient signal along the steering direction.
AI.motivation: To understand how semantic traits can be transferred through semantically unrelated data (subliminal learning), a phenomenon poorly understood in language model fine-tuning.
AI.method: The authors identify steering vectors as the mechanism: teacher system prompts are approximated by steering vectors, and students learn aligned vectors during fine-tuning. They test across two open-source models, compare system prompts that are vs. are not well-approximated by steering vectors, and analyze the role of adaptive optimizers.
AI.result: Subliminal learning occurs only when the teacher's system prompt is well-approximated by a steering vector. Adaptive optimizers are necessary because activation gradients on steered data have a small but consistent component along the steering direction; non-adaptive optimizers fail due to outlier gradient dominance.
AI.conclusion: Subliminal learning is a special case of steering vector distillation. Steering vectors can have both model-independent (semantic) and model-specific (non-semantic) effects, explaining why subliminal learning doesn't transfer between models.

title: Decomposed On-Policy Distillation for Vision-Language Reasoning: Steering Gradients for Visual Grounding
summary: While on-policy distillation offers dense supervision for training small reasoning models, its optimization dynamics in the multimodal domain remain under-explored. In this work, we challenge the standard monolithic view of Vision-Language Model (VLM) distillation by mathematically decomposing the loss into two distinct components: the language prior and visual grounding. Our analysis uncovers that gradient vectors for these components are nearly orthogonal, indicating that the objective of aligning with the teacher's language distribution is geometrically independent from the objective of matching its visual perception. Consequently, standard optimization passively follows a suboptimal compromise trajectory that implicitly balances the two objectives. Hypothesizing that visual grounding constitutes the primary bottleneck for vision-language reasoning, we introduce Visual Gradient Steering (VGS), a method that dynamically reorients the update vector to prioritize the visual subspace. Experimental results on multiple distillation settings and complex multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that VGS significantly outperforms the standard monolithic formulation of on-policy distillation, achieving superior grounding with minimal training overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper decomposes VLM distillation loss into language prior and visual grounding components, finds their gradients are nearly orthogonal, and proposes Visual Gradient Steering (VGS) to prioritize visual learning, outperforming standard monolithic distillation.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation for multimodal small reasoning models is under-explored; standard monolithic VLM distillation ignores the distinct optimization dynamics of language vs. visual components.
AI.method: Mathematically decomposes distillation loss into language prior and visual grounding; analyzes gradient orthogonality; proposes Visual Gradient Steering (VGS) to dynamically reorient updates toward the visual subspace.
AI.result: VGS significantly outperforms standard monolithic on-policy distillation across multiple settings and complex multimodal benchmarks with minimal training overhead.
AI.conclusion: Visual grounding is the primary bottleneck in vision-language reasoning; explicitly prioritizing it during distillation yields superior performance.

title: TabChange: Precise Attribute Changes in Tabular Data
summary: Modifying an attribute in tabular data often introduces an unnatural instance by breaking its relationships with other attributes. The modified instance must be both natural and minimally changed from the original instance. This paper addresses the challenge of generating such a modified instance. We identify key limitations in existing approaches: generative models either don't support instance-level attribute editing or, in the case of methods like CVAE, retain attribute information in the latent space, leading to unnecessary modifications. To solve this, we propose TabChange, an approach that analyzes the relationship between the attribute of interest and other attributes in the dataset. If the relationship is weak, it simply flips the attribute; if it is strong, it uses an adversarial framework that removes information about the attribute in the latent space representation. This removal enables precise modifications, making only the necessary adjustments to maintain naturalness. Our experiments across seven datasets show that TabChange generates counterfactuals in attributes that are comparable in naturalness and are more proximal to their original instances. This leads to a higher number of valid counterfactuals and a lower number of invalid counterfactuals compared to the baselines.

AI.tldr: TabChange is a method for editing attributes in tabular data that preserves naturalness by either flipping weakly-related attributes or using an adversarial framework to remove attribute information from latent space for strongly-related attributes, producing more valid and proximal counterfactuals than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Modifying an attribute in tabular data often creates unnatural instances by breaking relationships with other attributes. Existing generative models either lack instance-level editing support or (like CVAE) retain attribute information in latent space, causing unnecessary modifications.
