AI.tldr: A framework using adaptive prescribed-time control barrier functions that allows multi-robot systems to temporarily disconnect and reconnect within adjustable time limits, improving task efficiency while ensuring timely information sharing.
AI.motivation: Maintaining persistent connectivity in multi-robot systems is overly restrictive for large environments with limited communication ranges; temporary disconnections are more desirable for efficient task execution.
AI.method: Proposes an adaptive prescribed-time control barrier function (PT-CBF) framework with a reconnection triggering mechanism that jointly considers task execution and reconnection urgency.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate improved task efficiency and satisfying reconnections within prescribed finite time.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive PT-CBF framework enables flexible, temporary disconnections with guaranteed timely reconnection, offering a principled alternative to persistent connectivity constraints.

title: MM-Snowball: Evaluating and Mitigating Hallucination Snowballing in Multimodal Multi-Turn Dialogue
comment: Accepted by The International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) demonstrate remarkable visual understanding, yet their reliability in interactive settings is severely undermined by hallucination snowballing: a phenomenon where initial errors amplify across conversational turns, leading to a collapse in coherence. This failure reveals a fundamental vulnerability where models progressively neglect visual grounding in favor of over-relying on polluted textual history. Existing benchmarks are predominantly confined to single-turn VQA, which fail to capture the complex dynamics of error propagation in long-horizon interactions. To address this, we introduce MM-Snowball, the first benchmark for fine-grained diagnosis of hallucination snowballing within dialogues. Extensive evaluation shows that our benchmark poses a significant challenge even to advanced MLLMs and reveals the inefficacy of existing mitigation methods designed for single-turn VQA. To counteract this degradation, we propose Conflict-Aware Visual Rectification (CAVR). This training-free method mitigates snowballing through a synergistic dual-mechanism that refreshes visual grounding at the representation level and rectifies output distributions at the logit level, effectively re-anchoring the model to visual facts. Experiments demonstrate that CAVR achieves state-of-the-art performance, offering a promising path toward more reliable interactive AI. Data and code are available at: https://frenkie-chiang.github.io/MM-Snowball

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MM-Snowball, a benchmark for diagnosing hallucination snowballing (error propagation across conversational turns) in MLLMs, and proposes CAVR, a training-free method that refreshes visual grounding and rectifies output distributions to mitigate this issue.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs suffer from hallucination snowballing in multi-turn dialogues, where initial errors amplify across turns causing coherence collapse. Existing benchmarks are limited to single-turn VQA and fail to capture this dynamic.
AI.method: The authors propose Conflict-Aware Visual Rectification (CAVR), a training-free dual-mechanism approach that (1) refreshes visual grounding at the representation level and (2) rectifies output distributions at the logit level to re-anchor the model to visual facts.
AI.result: MM-Snowball benchmark poses significant challenges to advanced MLLMs, existing mitigation methods for single-turn VQA are ineffective for this problem, and CAVR achieves state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: CAVR offers a promising path toward more reliable interactive AI by effectively counteracting hallucination snowballing in multi-turn dialogues.

title: The Assistant as a Privileged Persona: A canonical reference in cross-persona self-recognition
summary:
Post-trained language models can recognize their own outputs from a sentence or two out of context. In a companion paper \citep{jack2026twomodes} we showed they can also recognize when they are currently acting on-policy, through the sharp entropy drop of assistant-mode generation. Both signals are tied to the Assistant persona that post-training mainly shapes.
  This paper widens the frame to cross-persona authorship judgement on Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct. We measure a matrix of authorship claim rates over a panel of evaluator and generator personas spanning librarian to dragon to Shakespeare, and make two claims. \emph{First}, on the Assistant's own row of the matrix, the Assistant's claim rate, the persona-vector distance from the Assistant in activation space, and the entropy gap between the Assistant's surprise on a persona's text and the persona's surprise on its own text are all tightly coupled. This extends the entropy signature of \emph{acting} from the companion paper to a retrospective signature of \emph{having acted}. \emph{Second}, this coupling fails off the Assistant's row: the natural symmetric extension of the entropy gap does not predict authorship for distinctive evaluators (pirate, dragon, Shakespeare); what does is asymmetric -- the evaluator's surprise compared to the Assistant's surprise on the same text, not to the generator's. We rule out the alternative that any persona could play this reference role by trying many candidate substitutes; none does. We interpret the asymmetry as the model performing an implicit Bayesian likelihood-ratio test against the Assistant as the canonical alternative hypothesis, with the persona-vector geometry of \citet{chen2025persona} (every persona a delta off the Assistant) ensuring that the Assistant is the only persona universally accessible to that test.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how post-trained LLMs (Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct) perform cross-persona authorship judgment, finding that the Assistant persona serves as a universal reference point for detecting other personas' outputs, and that the entropy-based authorship signature from the companion paper generalizes to retrospective detection but only when the Assistant is the evaluator.
AI.motivation: To understand how language models recognize their own outputs and the outputs of different personas they can adopt, and to investigate whether the entropy-based self-recognition signal extends to cross-persona settings.
AI.method: Measured authorship claim rates across a matrix of evaluator and generator personas (librarian, dragon, Shakespeare, etc.) on Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct. Analyzed coupling between claim rates, persona-vector distances in activation space, and entropy gaps. Tested alternative reference personas to rule out universal applicability.
AI.result: Found tight coupling between authorship claim rate, persona-vector distance, and entropy gap on the Assistant's row. This coupling fails off the Assistant's row; the asymmetric entropy gap (evaluator vs. Assistant surprise) predicts authorship, not the symmetric one. No other persona can substitute the Assistant as the reference.
AI.conclusion: The model performs implicit Bayesian likelihood-ratio testing against the Assistant as the canonical alternative hypothesis. The Assistant's unique status arises because every persona is geometrically a delta off the Assistant in activation space, making it the only universally accessible reference.

title: Before the Model Learns the Bug:Fuzzing RLVR Verifiers
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) replaces human preference labels with executable reward functions such as math answer checkers, JSON tool-call validators, and code unit-test harnesses. That makes the reward partly a software artifact: if the verifier is wrong, optimization can learn the bug. We study this failure mode with a lightweight verifier-fuzzing framework that generates adversarial completions, compares buggy and stricter reference verifiers, logs paired decisions, and reports false-positive, false-negative, disagreement, exploit, and uncertainty metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper studies failures in RLVR when reward verifiers have bugs, proposing a fuzzing framework to detect adversarial completions that exploit verifier errors.
AI.motivation: RLVR relies on verifiable reward functions (e.g., answer checkers, code test harnesses), but if the verifier contains bugs, optimization can learn to exploit those bugs instead of the intended behavior. The paper aims to study and detect this failure mode.
AI.method: The authors propose a lightweight verifier-fuzzing framework that generates adversarial completions, compares buggy vs. stricter reference verifiers, logs paired decisions, and reports metrics like false positives, false negatives, disagreements, exploits, and uncertainty.
AI.result: The framework produces metrics (false-positive, false-negative, disagreement, exploit, uncertainty) that help identify when a verifier is buggy and being exploited by the RL optimization process.
AI.conclusion: RLVR systems are vulnerable to verifier bugs, and the proposed fuzzing framework can help detect such failures by comparing buggy and reference verifiers.

title: Hierarchical Object Representation for Spatial Robot Perception: Points, Meshes, and Superquadrics
summary: Hierarchical 3D Scene Graphs (3DSG) have emerged as an actionable and scalable representation for long-term autonomy incorporating metric, semantic, and topological information in the scene. However, the question of geometric representation of objects in 3DSG has been overlooked as most methods use simplified geometric models such as partial point clouds or 3D bounding boxes. In this work, we introduce a hierarchical object representation that can be leveraged for high-fidelity object-level reconstruction, object-based robust re-localization or map alignment, and efficient and analytical collision checking for safe robot navigation planning in dense and cluttered environments. The representation is structurally organized into four distinct layers, progressively abstracting the scene from raw sensor data to dense 3D meshes to analytical primitives such as superquadrics, which provide a sparse and analytical representation for object geometry. We develop a pipeline that builds the hierarchical object representation from RGB-D image stream captured by a robot, and demonstrate its working in real-world open-set object scenes in both indoor and outdoor environments. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets including HOPE, ReplicaCAD, Kimera-Multi, and NUS Campus Dataset collected using Unitree B2 Robot validate our pipeline in both indoor and outdoor environments. We show that our superquadric-based map alignment method outperforms the current state-of-the-art object based map alignment method ROMAN. Our code can be found at https://github.com/perceptica-robotics/Hickory.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hickory, a hierarchical object representation for 3D Scene Graphs with four layers (raw data, dense meshes, superquadrics) that enables high-fidelity reconstruction, robust re-localization, and collision checking for robots in indoor/outdoor environments.
AI.motivation: Current 3D Scene Graph methods overlook geometric object representation, relying on simplified models like partial point clouds or 3D bounding boxes, which limits high-fidelity reconstruction, re-localization, and safe navigation planning.
AI.method: A four-layer hierarchical representation is built from RGB-D streams: raw sensor data → dense 3D meshes → superquadrics (analytical primitives). The pipeline handles open-set object scenes in both indoor and outdoor environments.
AI.result: Validated on HOPE, ReplicaCAD, Kimera-Multi, and a self-collected NUS Campus Dataset using Unitree B2 robot. Superquadric-based map alignment outperforms the SOTA method ROMAN.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical object representation with superquadrics as sparse analytical primitives effectively supports object-level reconstruction, re-localization, and collision checking for long-term robot autonomy.

title: A Systematic Benchmark of Intraoperative Ultrasound-to-MR Synthesis for Brain Tumour Surgery
summary: Intraoperative ultrasound (ioUS) is a versatile, cost-effective modality in brain tumour surgery, but its interpretation is difficult: acquisition planes are non-standard, artefacts are modality-specific, and its appearance differs markedly from the preoperative MRI on which surgical-planning tools, segmentation models and the surgeon's experience rely. Synthesising MRI-like images from ioUS could let this MRI-based infrastructure be reused intraoperatively without an extra scan. Most prior work evaluates a single architecture in isolation; to our knowledge, no benchmark has spanned architectural paradigms, inference regimes and downstream-task endpoints under a common protocol. We address this gap on the public ReMIND data set (76 patients; 153 paired ioUS/T2w and 104 paired ioUS/FLAIR studies; 60/16 patient-level train/held-out split). Six generators (four GAN baselines: Pix2Pix, SwinPix2Pix, CycleGAN, CUT; the transformer-augmented ResViT; and the few-step diffusion model SynDiff) were each trained under four inference regimes (2D, 2.5D, 2D + 3D-refinement, full-3D) and two targets (T2w only; T2w + FLAIR multi-task), yielding 48 experiments. Image-fidelity metrics (SSIM, PSNR, MAE, LPIPS) were complemented by an nnU-Net v2 downstream segmentation evaluation (tumour and resection cavity) and by subgroup analyses by histological grade and reoperation. No architecture dominated every axis, and, critically, perceptual quality tracked downstream utility most closely (LPIPS, r=-0.66, p<0.001), whereas higher SSIM was associated with worse utility (r=-0.64, p<0.001); SynDiff-2.5D best preserved downstream segmentation (U_Dice=0.55). Perceptual and downstream-task metrics should therefore be reported alongside or in preference to global SSIM, and architecture choice conditioned on surgical phase, patient history and clinical objective.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark of 6 MRI-from-ioUS synthesis architectures across 48 experiments on the ReMIND dataset, finding that no single model dominates all metrics, perceptual quality (LPIPS) best correlates with downstream segmentation utility, and SSIM inversely correlates with it—with SynDiff-2.5D achieving best downstream performance (U_Dice=0.55).
AI.motivation: Intraoperative ultrasound is difficult to interpret due to non-standard acquisition planes, modality-specific artefacts, and appearance differences from preoperative MRI, limiting reuse of MRI-based surgical planning tools, segmentation models, and surgeon expertise during surgery.
AI.method: Six generators (Pix2Pix, SwinPix2Pix, CycleGAN, CUT, ResViT, SynDiff) trained under four inference regimes (2D, 2.5D, 2D+3D-refinement, full-3D) and two targets (T2w only; T2w+FLAIR multi-task) on 76 patients from ReMIND dataset, evaluated with image-fidelity metrics (SSIM, PSNR, MAE, LPIPS) and nnU-Net v2 downstream segmentation.
AI.result: No architecture dominated all axes; LPIPS correlated best with downstream utility (r=-0.66, p<0.001) while SSIM inversely correlated (r=-0.64, p<0.001); SynDiff-2.5D achieved best downstream segmentation (U_Dice=0.55).
AI.conclusion: Perceptual and downstream-task metrics should be reported alongside or in preference to global SSIM, and architecture choice should be conditioned on surgical phase, patient history, and clinical objective.

title: Normalized Relevance Measure as a Unifying Framework to Explain Neural Network Latent Structures
summary: To understand how a neural network (NN) functions and makes predictions, it has become increasingly clear that analyzing only the input domain is insufficient -- one must also examine its internal inference mechanisms to capture the complete picture. To explain the internal inference mechanisms of such models, it is essential to analyze the importance of latent representations for a given task. In this paper, we propose the \emph{normalized relevance measure} (NRM) framework -- a novel general explanation procedure that attributes relevance to \emph{arbitrary sets of neurons across layers of arbitrary architectures}. In the NRM framework, relevance of selected neurons is explicitly defined as a normalized signed measure, constructed using simple operations -- marginalization and conditioning based on additive and multiplicative laws -- in analogy to the probability measures. The normalization property further guarantees comparability across layers. The NRM framework subsumes existing propagation-based explanation algorithms by explicitly identifying the underlying quantity being computed. We demonstrate the utility of the framework in computer vision applications, where joint relevance analysis across multiple layers reveals key information flows in VGG16 networks. Overall, the NRM framework provides a general, mathematically grounded approach to understanding how modern NNs propagate information, offering a versatile and broadly applicable foundation for explainable artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, a general method for explaining neural network predictions by attributing normalized signed relevance scores to arbitrary sets of neurons across layers, using operations analogous to probability theory.
AI.motivation: Understanding neural network predictions requires analyzing internal inference mechanisms beyond just input-level analysis, but existing methods lack a general framework for attributing relevance to arbitrary neuron subsets across layers.
AI.method: The NRM framework defines relevance as a normalized signed measure, constructed via marginalization and conditioning (analogous to additive/multiplicative probability laws), enabling attribution to arbitrary neuron sets across any architecture.
AI.result: The framework subsumes existing propagation-based explanation methods by revealing the underlying computed quantities, and demonstrates utility in computer vision by analyzing joint relevance across multiple layers to uncover information flows in VGG16.
AI.conclusion: NRM provides a general, mathematically grounded foundation for explainable AI, offering versatility and comparability across layers for understanding information propagation in modern neural networks.

title: CAREAgent: Clinical Agent with Structured Reasoning and Tool-Integrated for Order Generation
summary: Clinical order generation serves as a critical bridge between clinical decision-making and real-world practice, translating medical decisions into concrete and executable orders. Existing agents mainly focus on coarse-grained decisions and overlook the fine-grained, executable information required for clinical orders. To address this gap, we propose CAREAgent, an agent for clinical order generation. To support its training, we introduce a two-stage agentic reasoning data construction method. First, we design an agent framework that constructs verifiable reasoning trajectories aligned with realistic clinical tool usage. Second, we filter reasoning trajectories by format compliance, order validity, and clinical plausibility. Building on the constructed data, the model is first trained via supervised fine-tuning to acquire fundamental reasoning formats and medical knowledge, and is subsequently optimized through reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional reward functions to enhance complex clinical reasoning capabilities. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of CAREAgent. On ClinicalBench (unseen during training), CAREAgent improves the F1 score by 5.05%, 2.09%, and 0.86% over the single-agent, multi-agent, and agentic reasoning methods, respectively.

AI.tldr: CAREAgent is a novel agent for generating clinical orders (e.g., medications, lab tests) from medical decisions. It uses a two-stage data construction method (verifiable reasoning trajectories followed by filtering) and is trained via supervised fine-tuning + reinforcement learning. It outperforms existing methods on ClinicalBench by up to 5.05% F1.
AI.motivation: Existing clinical agents focus on coarse-grained decisions (e.g., diagnoses) but neglect fine-grained executable orders (e.g., specific drug dosages, lab tests). This gap between decision-making and actionable practice limits real-world utility.
AI.method: (1) Two-stage agentic reasoning data construction: first build verifiable reasoning trajectories with realistic tool usage, then filter by format/validity/plausibility. (2) Training: supervised fine-tuning for reasoning format & medical knowledge, then reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional rewards for complex reasoning.
AI.result: On unseen ClinicalBench, CAREAgent improves F1 by 5.05% over single-agent, 2.09% over multi-agent, and 0.86% over agentic reasoning baselines.
AI.conclusion: A specialized agent for clinical order generation can effectively bridge clinical decisions and executable practice, with the two-stage training pipeline (SFT + RL) proving effective for complex medical reasoning tasks.

title: Hierarchical Semantic-Augmented Navigation: Optimal Transport and Graph-Driven Reasoning for Vision-Language Navigation
comment: Published in NeurIPS 2025, address some typos
summary: Vision-Language Navigation in Continuous Environments (VLN-CE) poses a formidable challenge for autonomous agents, requiring seamless integration of natural language instructions and visual observations to navigate complex 3D indoor spaces. Existing approaches often falter in long-horizon tasks due to limited scene understanding, inefficient planning, and lack of robust decision-making frameworks. We introduce the \textbf{Hierarchical Semantic-Augmented Navigation (HSAN)} framework, a groundbreaking approach that redefines VLN-CE through three synergistic innovations. First, HSAN constructs a dynamic hierarchical semantic scene graph, leveraging vision-language models to capture multi-level environmental representations, from objects to regions to zones, enabling nuanced spatial reasoning. Second, it employs an optimal transport-based topological planner, grounded in Kantorovich's duality, to select long-term goals by balancing semantic relevance and spatial accessibility with theoretical guarantees of optimality. Third, a graph-aware reinforcement learning policy ensures precise low-level control, navigating subgoals while robustly avoiding obstacles. By integrating spectral graph theory, optimal transport, and advanced multi-modal learning, HSAN addresses the shortcomings of static maps and heuristic planners prevalent in prior work. Extensive experiments on multiple challenging VLN-CE datasets demonstrate that HSAN achieves state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements in navigation success and generalization to unseen environments.

AI.tldr: HSAN introduces a hierarchical framework combining semantic scene graphs, optimal transport-based planning, and graph-aware reinforcement learning to achieve state-of-the-art performance in Vision-Language Navigation in Continuous Environments.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN-CE approaches struggle in long-horizon tasks due to limited scene understanding, inefficient planning, and lack of robust decision-making frameworks, especially relying on static maps and heuristic planners.
AI.method: HSAN comprises three key components: (1) a dynamic hierarchical semantic scene graph using vision-language models for multi-level environmental representation; (2) an optimal transport-based topological planner using Kantorovich's duality for long-term goal selection; (3) a graph-aware reinforcement learning policy for precise low-level control and obstacle avoidance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple challenging VLN-CE datasets show state-of-the-art performance with significant improvements in navigation success and generalization to unseen environments.
AI.conclusion: HSAN effectively addresses shortcomings of prior VLN-CE methods by integrating spectral graph theory, optimal transport, and multi-modal learning, providing theoretical guarantees of optimality and robust real-world navigation.

title: An Attribute-Based Measure of Video Complexity
summary: A new framework for the estimation of the complexity posed by video-question pairs to video-LLMs, Video Attribute-Based Complexity (VideoABC), is proposed. Video complexity is defined as the probability of failure of a video-LLM for a given video-question pair. VideoABC is a non-parametric complexity measure, using a reference video dataset and a pre-defined vocabulary of video attributes informative of complexity, \eg the scene complexity or the speed of the video event informative of the question. In a training phase, reference videos are projected into the space of these attributes, which is then quantized. The expected ABC of each quantization cell is then computed. Given a new video and its projection into the attribute space, complexity is estimated by the expected ABC of the associated quantization cell. To enable the use of VideoABC with small reference video datasets, two quantizers are combined: a k-means quantizer that enables accurate complexity estimates for samples in the distribution of the reference dataset and a universal lattice quantizer that guarantees generalization to out-of-distribution samples. A synthetic video generation procedure, inspired by target-distractor manipulations of psychophysics studies, is proposed to populate the cells of the lattice quantizer during training, enabling the computation of their expected ABCs. Experimental results show that VideoABCis effective even with very low-dimensional attribute representations, substantially outperforming approaches like `video-LLM as judge' with much less complexity. Finally, the explainable nature of the VideoABC score, in terms of well-defined attributes, is shown to provide insights on how the attribute composition of benchmarks affects their complexity.

AI.tldr: VideoABC is a new, explainable complexity measure for video-question pairs that estimates how likely a video-LLM is to fail, using attribute-based quantization with k-means and lattice quantizers, outperforming "video-LLM as judge" approaches.
AI.motivation: To create a lightweight, non-parametric, explainable method for estimating video-question complexity for video-LLMs, replacing expensive and opaque "LLM-as-judge" approaches.
AI.method: Defines complexity as failure probability. Projects videos into a pre-defined attribute space (e.g., scene complexity, speed). Trains a k-means quantizer (in-distribution) and a universal lattice quantizer (out-of-distribution generalization) with synthetic psychophysics-inspired video generation. Complexity = expected failure in a given quantization cell.
AI.result: VideoABC works even with very low-dimensional attribute representations and substantially outperforms "video-LLM as judge" methods with much less computational complexity.
AI.conclusion: VideoABC provides an effective, explainable complexity metric that also reveals how attribute composition of benchmarks affects their difficulty.

title: Semi-Supervised Noise Adaptation: Transferring Knowledge from Noise Domain
summary: Transfer learning aims to facilitate the learning of a target domain by transferring knowledge from a source domain. The source domain typically contains semantically meaningful samples (*e.g.*, images) to facilitate effective knowledge transfer. However, a recent study observes that the noise domain constructed from simple distributions (*e.g.*, Gaussian distributions) can serve as a surrogate source domain in the semi-supervised setting, where only a small proportion of target samples are labeled while most remain unlabeled. Based on this surprising observation, we formulate a novel problem termed *Semi-Supervised Noise Adaptation* (SSNA), which aims to leverage a synthetic noise domain to improve the generalization of the target domain. To address this problem, we first establish a generalization bound characterizing the effect of the noise domain on generalization, based on which we propose a Noise Adaptation Framework (NAF). Extensive experiments demonstrate that NAF effectively leverages the noise domain to tighten the generalization bound of the target domain, leading to improved performance. The codes are available at https://github.com/AIResearch-Group/SSNA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Semi-Supervised Noise Adaptation (SSNA), using synthetic noise (e.g., Gaussian) as a source domain in semi-supervised learning. A Noise Adaptation Framework (NAF) is proposed with a theoretical generalization bound, showing performance improvements.
AI.motivation: To explore whether a synthetic noise domain (Gaussian) can serve as a surrogate source domain in semi-supervised learning, challenging the conventional requirement for semantically meaningful source domains.
AI.method: Formulates the SSNA problem, establishes a generalization bound linking noise domain to target generalization, and proposes the Noise Adaptation Framework (NAF).
AI.result: NAF effectively leverages the noise domain to tighten the generalization bound, leading to improved target domain performance; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic noise domains can be useful surrogate source domains in semi-supervised settings, and NAF provides a principled way to exploit them.

title: Diagnosing LLM Arbitration Behavior over Pre-evidence Epistemic States in RAG-based Fact-Checking
summary: In RAG-based fact-checking, LLMs are increasingly used as verifiers to check given claims against retrieved evidence. Their parametric knowledge can induce pre-evidence tendencies that may conflict with the retrieved context, yet existing evaluation frameworks do not characterize such prior-context discrepancy or measure how verifiers arbitrate between parametric and contextual signals. We introduce \textsc{PAVE} (\emph{Prior-Aware Verifier Evaluation}), a diagnostic testbed that stratifies an LLM verifier into four epistemic states based on the correctness and confidence of its pre-evidence prior and evaluates its arbitration behavior on this new benchmark, i.e., whether it persists in correct prior under misleading evidence, and whether it corrects wrong prior when accurate evidence is provided. Experiments across seven LLMs reveal unreliable and highly model-dependent prior-context arbitration, highlighting the importance of verifier selection for real-world RAG-based fact-checking applications. Based on these findings, we propose a lightweight JSD-based test-time arbitration method that improves factual reliability without modifying the underlying model, achieving competitive performance across diverse LLM families.

AI.tldr: PAVE is a diagnostic testbed that evaluates how LLM verifiers in RAG-based fact-checking arbitrate between their parametric prior knowledge and retrieved evidence, revealing unreliable prior-context arbitration across models and proposing a lightweight JSD-based method to improve factual reliability.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation frameworks for RAG-based fact-checking do not characterize the discrepancy between LLMs' parametric prior knowledge (pre-evidence tendencies) and retrieved evidence, nor measure how verifiers arbitrate between these conflicting signals.
AI.method: PAVE stratifies LLM verifiers into four epistemic states based on correctness and confidence of pre-evidence prior, then evaluates arbitration behavior (persisting in correct prior under misleading evidence, correcting wrong prior with accurate evidence). Experiments across seven LLMs are conducted, followed by a lightweight JSD-based test-time arbitration method.
AI.result: Reveals unreliable and highly model-dependent prior-context arbitration across seven LLMs; the proposed JSD-based method achieves competitive performance without model modification.
AI.conclusion: Verifier selection is critical for real-world RAG fact-checking; the JSD-based arbitration method offers a practical lightweight solution to improve factual reliability.

title: Physics-Informed Modeling and Control of Emergent Behaviors in Robot Swarms
summary: Robot swarms can exhibit coherent collective behaviors through local perception, limited communication and decentralized decision-making, yet modeling and controlling such emergence remains challenging when behaviors unfold over multiple phases. Here we introduce PhySwarm, a physics-informed micro--macro framework that represents multi-stage swarm emergence as physically constrained density-field evolution coupled to executable robot motion. At the macroscopic level, a multi-phase advection--diffusion--reaction model (Macro-ADR) describes phase-dependent swarm-density evolution through directed transport, diffusion-based spatial regulation and behavioral phase transitions. At the microscopic level, an equivalent deterministic motion model (Micro-EDM) realizes these mechanisms through potential-field advection, density-gradient compensation and rate- or event-gated phase switching. A neural-physics controller (NPC) maps local observations and temporal memory to bounded physical parameters, and is trained with a reinforcement learning--PINN objective that combines task rewards with macro-scale density residuals and micro-scale motion-consistency constraints. In several proof-of-concept swarm missions -- including trail-guided foraging, formation-reconfigurable navigation and role-adaptive search and rescue -- we demonstrate that PhySwarm can generate distinct multi-stage emergent behaviors within a unified physics-informed modeling framework. The learned density fields and physical parameters provide interpretable evidence of how advection, diffusion and reaction jointly regulate multi-stage swarm organization. These results establish a physics-informed route for learning, interpreting and controlling emergent behaviors in robot swarms.

AI.tldr: PhySwarm is a physics-informed framework that models multi-stage robot swarm emergence by coupling density-field evolution (macro) with executable robot motion (micro), using a neural-physics controller trained with reinforcement learning and physics-informed neural network objectives to unify swarm behaviors across phases.
AI.motivation: Modeling and controlling multi-phase emergent behaviors in robot swarms remains challenging due to the complexity of decentralized decision-making and phase transitions; existing approaches lack a unified physics-informed framework that bridges macro-level density dynamics with micro-level robot actions.
AI.method: PhySwarm integrates three components: (1) Macro-ADR (advection-diffusion-reaction model) for phase-dependent swarm-density evolution, (2) Micro-EDM (equivalent deterministic motion model) for robot-level potential-field advection and density-gradient compensation, and (3) a neural-physics controller trained via a combined RL-PINN objective enforcing task rewards, macro density residuals and micro motion consistency.
AI.result: The framework successfully generates distinct multi-stage emergent behaviors in three proof-of-concept missions (trail-guided foraging, formation-reconfigurable navigation, role-adaptive search and rescue), with interpretable density fields and physical parameters revealing how advection, diffusion and reaction jointly regulate swarm organization.
AI.conclusion: PhySwarm establishes a physics-informed route for learning, interpreting and controlling emergent multi-stage behaviors in robot swarms, unifying macro and micro modeling within a single framework.

title: Collaborative Few-Step Distillation and Low-Bit Quantization for Wan2.2 Dual-Expert Video Diffusion Models
summary: Large video diffusion models achieve strong visual quality but remain expensive to deploy because each sample requires many denoising steps and a large resident parameter footprint. This paper studies a deployment-oriented compression pipeline for Wan2.2-T2V-A14B by combining few-step distribution-matching distillation with low-bit quantization. The pipeline follows the model's dual-expert denoising route, calibrates the high-noise and low-noise branches separately, protects sensitive entrance layers, and uses HiF4-style low-bit representation to improve dynamic-range coverage. Quantization is calibrated on the distilled few-step student rather than on the original long-step trajectory, reducing activation-distribution mismatch during inference. The proposed co-design keeps the quantized model close to the same-step full-precision model and surpasses the original full-precision baseline at 8 and 20 steps on average. The 20-step setting gives the best quality-efficiency trade-off in the tested configurations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a deployment-oriented compression pipeline for Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video diffusion models, combining few-step distribution-matching distillation with low-bit quantization. By calibrating quantization on the distilled student model and using HiF4-style representation, the method achieves competitive quality while reducing deployment costs.
AI.motivation: Large video diffusion models are expensive to deploy due to many denoising steps and large parameter footprint; the paper aims to reduce these costs while maintaining visual quality.
AI.method: Combines few-step distribution-matching distillation with low-bit quantization; follows dual-expert denoising route with separate calibration for high-noise and low-noise branches; protects sensitive entrance layers; uses HiF4-style low-bit representation; calibrates quantization on distilled few-step student rather than original long-step trajectory.
AI.result: Quantized model stays close to same-step full-precision model; surpasses original full-precision baseline at 8 and 20 steps on average; 20-step setting achieves best quality-efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Co-design of distillation and quantization effectively reduces deployment cost of Wan2.2-T2V-A14B while maintaining or improving quality, with the 20-step configuration offering optimal balance.

title: Richer Representations for Neural Algorithmic Reasoning via Auxiliary Reconstruction
summary: Neural algorithmic reasoning has emerged as a popular research direction. It aims to train neural networks to mimic the step-by-step behavior of classical rule-based algorithms. More specifically, the execution of such algorithms can be abstracted as a sequence of states, where each state represents the intermediate outcome after an execution step. The training objective is to generate state sequences that replicate the underlying algorithmic process. A common framework for this task adopts an encoder-processor-decoder architecture, where the encoder learns representations of states, the processor simulates algorithmic steps, and the decoder reconstructs output states. While prior work has focused on improving the processor, the role of the encoder in representation learning has received little attention. Most methods rely on simple MLP encoders, raising the question of whether such representations are sufficiently informative for supporting algorithmic reasoning. This paper investigates how to improve encoder representations for neural algorithmic reasoning. We propose a reconstruction module that aims to recover the input state from its encoded representation. This auxiliary reconstruction task encourages the encoder to retain critical information about the input. We demonstrate that incorporating this task during training improves the performance of existing neural architectures on standard benchmarks. Furthermore, we observe that current encoders often underutilize the correlations among features within a state. To address this, we draw inspiration from self-supervised learning and design an enhanced variant of the auxiliary task that encourages the encoder to capture intra-state feature dependencies. Experimental results show that our method enables the encoder to learn richer representations, thereby enhancing the performance of existing processors on algorithmic reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes improving encoder representations in neural algorithmic reasoning by adding a reconstruction module and an enhanced self-supervised variant that captures intra-state feature dependencies, leading to better performance on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: While prior work has focused on improving the processor in encoder-processor-decoder architectures, the role of the encoder in representation learning has received little attention. Most methods rely on simple MLP encoders, raising the question of whether such representations are sufficiently informative for supporting algorithmic reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose a reconstruction module that recovers the input state from its encoded representation as an auxiliary training task. Additionally, they design an enhanced variant inspired by self-supervised learning that encourages the encoder to capture intra-state feature dependencies.
AI.result: Incorporating the reconstruction task during training improves the performance of existing neural architectures on standard benchmarks. The enhanced variant enables the encoder to learn richer representations, enhancing the performance of existing processors.
AI.conclusion: Improving encoder representations through auxiliary reconstruction and self-supervised learning objectives is an effective way to boost neural algorithmic reasoning performance.

title: SkillRevise: Improving LLM-Authored Agent Skills via Trace-Conditioned Skill Revision
summary: Agent skills are procedural artifacts that enable LLM agents to execute workflows, verify constraints, and recover from failures. Existing self-evolving methods refine skills using accumulated trajectories. However, they struggle in cold-start settings, where only an initial, imperfect skill is available. Consequently, skill construction defaults to expert authoring or one-shot LLM generation. Expert-authored skills are costly and may not align with how LLM agents actually execute tasks, while one-shot generated skills can be syntactically well formed yet behaviorally weak. To bridge this gap, we propose SkillRevise, an execution-grounded framework designed to iteratively refine these initial skills. SkillRevise diagnoses skill defects from execution evidence, retrieves relevant repair principles from a general memory, and applies execution-anchored edits. By re-executing candidates and measuring empirical utility, it systematically retains the optimal skill version. Evaluated across three benchmarks and five LLMs, SkillRevise substantially outperforms one-shot baselines, improving the base agent's success rate on SkillsBench from 36.05% to 61.63%. Furthermore, the revised skills exhibit strong cross-model transferability, capturing generalized procedural knowledge over model-specific artifacts.

AI.tldr: SkillRevise is an execution-grounded framework that iteratively refines initial LLM agent skills by diagnosing defects from execution evidence, retrieving repair principles, and applying execution-anchored edits, improving success rates from 36.05% to 61.63% on SkillsBench.
AI.motivation: Cold-start settings for agent skills suffer from costly expert authoring or behaviorally weak one-shot LLM generation - there's no effective method to iteratively improve initial imperfect skills.
AI.method: SkillRevise iteratively diagnoses skill defects from execution evidence, retrieves repair principles from a general memory, applies execution-anchored edits, re-executes candidates, and retains the optimal version based on empirical utility.
AI.result: Outperforms one-shot baselines across three benchmarks and five LLMs, improving success rate from 36.05% to 61.63% on SkillsBench. Revised skills show strong cross-model transferability.
AI.conclusion: Execution-grounded iterative refinement effectively bridges the gap between costly expert-authored and weak one-shot generated skills, producing transferable procedural knowledge.

title: Embedding Semantic Risk into Distance Fields and CBFs for Online Monocular Safe Control
summary: We propose an online monocular perception-to-control framework that embeds semantic risk into the distance field used by Control Barrier Function (CBF)-based safe navigation and teleoperation. Many perception-based safety filters assign the same distance-based safety margin to all mapped obstacles or use semantics only as a downstream controller adjustment, rather than encoding semantic risk in the spatial representation. Our framework instead reasons online about obstacle geometry and class-dependent risk by embedding semantic information directly into the Euclidean Signed Distance Field (ESDF). This design encodes semantic risk before control optimization, so high-risk objects exert a larger spatial influence in the safety field while retaining efficient ESDF queries at runtime. Specifically, a foundation-model-based SLAM front end reconstructs dense 3-D geometry from monocular RGB video, while per-frame semantic segmentation provides pixel-level class labels that are fused into the reconstructed geometry. The resulting geometric-semantic representation is then converted into an ESDF, where semantic labels identify safety-relevant regions and impose class-dependent inflation before field computation. The semantic-aware ESDF provides the local distance values and spatial derivatives required by the CBF controller, while class-dependent gains further regulate the controller response. Extensive simulation and hardware experiments demonstrate online operation at 10--20 Hz and semantic-aware safe behavior in both teleoperation and autonomous navigation.

AI.tldr: Proposes an online monocular framework that embeds semantic risk into ESDF for CBF-based safe navigation, enabling class-dependent distance fields that influence control optimization directly, validated at 10-20 Hz in simulation and hardware.
AI.motivation: Existing perception-based safety filters assign uniform safety margins to all obstacles or only use semantics as downstream adjustments, failing to encode semantic risk directly in the spatial representation for control.
AI.method: Uses foundation-model-based SLAM front end for dense 3D reconstruction from monocular RGB, fuses per-frame semantic segmentation labels into geometry, converts to ESDF with class-dependent inflation, and feeds semantic-aware distance fields to CBF controller with class-dependent gains.
AI.result: Achieves online operation at 10-20 Hz; demonstrates semantic-aware safe behavior in both teleoperation and autonomous navigation in simulation and hardware experiments.
AI.conclusion: Encoding semantic risk directly into the ESDF before control optimization allows high-risk objects to exert larger spatial influence while maintaining efficient runtime queries, improving safety-aware navigation.

title: TAP-JEPA: Frozen Future-Latent Probing and Two-Stage Score Fusion for EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Action Anticipation
comment: The runner-up solution for the Action Anticipation Challenge, EPIC-KITCHENS-100 at the CVPR EgoVis Workshop 2026
summary: This report presents TAP-JEPA, our runner-up submission to the EPIC-KITCHENS-100 (EK-100) Action Anticipation Challenge at EgoVis 2026. The task is to anticipate the next verb, noun, and verb-noun action from an egocentric clip that ends before the target action begins. Instead of fine-tuning a large video backbone, TAP-JEPA builds a compact anticipation model on frozen V-JEPA 2.1 features: a ViT-G/384 encoder extracts visible pre-action tokens, the pre-trained latent predictor estimates near-future tokens from the observed context, and both token groups are fused by attentive probes with task-specific queries for verbs, nouns, and action pairs. For the final submission, we expand supervised training with the official training split and most of the validation split, reserving a small subset for sanity checks and qualitative inspection, and adopt a two-stage score fusion that first averages eight independently initialized probe replicas within each epoch and then merges candidates from epochs 12-20 with field-dependent weights. On the official open-testing leaderboard, our sunshinesky entry achieves 27.91 percent overall action Mean Top-5 Recall (MT5R), ranking second and only 0.04 percentage points behind the top score.

AI.tldr: TAP-JEPA is a runner-up action anticipation model that uses frozen V-JEPA 2.1 features with latent prediction and attentive probes, achieving 27.91% MT5R on EPIC-KITCHENS-100, just 0.04% behind the top score.
AI.motivation: To build a compact and effective action anticipation model without fine-tuning large video backbones, addressing the challenge of predicting future actions from egocentric video clips.
AI.method: Uses frozen V-JEPA 2.1 (ViT-G/384) encoder to extract pre-action tokens, a pre-trained latent predictor to estimate near-future tokens, fuses both token groups via attentive probes with task-specific queries, expands training with most of validation split, and applies two-stage score fusion across multiple probe replicas and epochs.
AI.result: 27.91% overall action Mean Top-5 Recall (MT5R) on the official EK-100 leaderboard, ranking second place (runner-up) with only 0.04 percentage points gap from the top score.
AI.conclusion: TAP-JEPA demonstrates that competitive action anticipation can be achieved with a compact model built on frozen features and effective fusion strategies, nearly matching the top performer.

title: Interpretable Policy Distillation for Power Grid Topology Control
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) offers a promising route to real-time power grid operation, yet large neural policies are costly to evaluate, hard to deploy on constrained hardware, and opaque to operators. We ask whether a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent for grid topology control can be compressed into compact tree-based surrogates without losing operational performance. A PPO teacher is trained on Grid2Op's standard 14-bus environment with a stability-oriented reward, using stress-focused data collection on critical, high-loading states. The policy is then distilled into a decision tree and a random forest. Across held-out validation episodes, both surrogates exceed the teacher in mean reward and survival length at a fraction of the inference cost. The decision tree shows high exact-action agreement with the PPO argmax and near-complete agreement within its top-ranked actions, while remaining small enough to be inspected directly. Feature-importance analysis reveals a representational shift: the PPO policy relies mainly on line-loading signals, while the distilled tree is driven primarily by bus-topology variables. These results suggest that stress-focused distillation can convert a black-box neural controller into a lightweight, auditable rule-like surrogate suited for real-time deployment, while also surfacing risks tied to deterministic actions and topology-specific generalization.

AI.tldr: A PPO-based deep RL agent for grid topology control is successfully distilled into compact decision tree and random forest surrogates that match or exceed the teacher's performance while being interpretable and computationally cheaper.
AI.motivation: Deep RL policies for power grid operation are costly to evaluate, hard to deploy on constrained hardware, and opaque to operators, motivating the need for compact, interpretable surrogates.
AI.method: A PPO teacher is trained on Grid2Op's 14-bus environment using stress-focused data collection on critical states. The policy is then distilled into a decision tree and a random forest surrogate.
AI.result: Both surrogates exceed the teacher in mean reward and survival length at lower inference cost. The decision tree shows high exact-action agreement with PPO argmax. Feature-importance analysis reveals the PPO relies on line-loading signals while the tree uses bus-topology variables.
AI.conclusion: Stress-focused distillation can convert black-box neural controllers into lightweight, auditable, rule-like surrogates suitable for real-time deployment, while surfacing risks tied to deterministic actions and topology-specific generalization.

title: Reasoning4Sciences: Bridging Reasoning Language Models to All Scientific Branches
summary: While Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) are rapidly emerging as powerful tools for scientific research, their impact is primarily concentrated in "hard science" fields. The slow -- or lack of -- adoption of RLMs in other branches of science is causing a widening gap in research productivity. In this survey, we provide the first comprehensive analysis of RLM adoption across 28 scientific disciplines following the classification used by the European Research Council (ERC), spanning the Social Sciences and Humanities, Physical Sciences and Engineering, and Life Sciences. We examine how RLMs are developed, evaluated, and applied across disciplines. Furthermore, we introduce a maturity-oriented assessment framework based on available domain-specific development and evaluation resources, revealing substantial disparities in RLM maturity that become even more pronounced when only publicly available resources are considered. Finally, we highlight current implementation paradigms that are gaining popularity across disciplines, current challenges, and future directions in enabling RLM adoption across science.

AI.tldr: This survey analyzes the adoption of Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) across 28 scientific disciplines, revealing a significant gap between "hard sciences" where RLMs are thriving and other fields. It introduces a maturity assessment framework showing disparities become more pronounced when considering only publicly available resources.
AI.motivation: RLMs are rapidly advancing but their adoption is concentrated in "hard science" fields, creating a widening productivity gap across scientific disciplines. The lack of comprehensive analysis on RLM adoption patterns across different fields motivates this survey.
AI.method: The authors conduct the first comprehensive survey of RLM adoption across 28 scientific disciplines classified by ERC, covering Social Sciences and Humanities, Physical Sciences and Engineering, and Life Sciences. They introduce a maturity-oriented assessment framework based on domain-specific development and evaluation resources.
AI.result: Substantial disparities in RLM maturity exist across disciplines, becoming even more pronounced when only publicly available resources are considered. The analysis reveals which disciplines have more mature RLM infrastructure and which lag behind.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights current implementation paradigms, challenges, and future directions for enabling broader RLM adoption across all scientific disciplines, aiming to bridge the gap between hard sciences and other fields.

title: FlipItRight: Stable Pose-Targeted Throw-Flip Across Diverse Objects
summary: We propose FlipItRight, a framework for stable planar pose-targeted throw-flip with a high-DoF manipulator. The task is decomposed into an object-level planner, which generates candidate release states satisfying the desired landing pose, and a robot-level planner, which evaluates executability and constructs a feasible swing motion. Treating the release state as an explicit intermediate representation enables principled candidate filtering, adaptive selection of release and pre-swing configurations, and structured near-release motion design -- in particular, approximately constant end-effector velocities during the final swing phase to improve robustness to release-timing uncertainty. We validate on a real platform across objects of varying shape, size, and mass, achieving a 90% success rate across 120 trials. Ablation studies confirm that each design choice contributes to throwing performance, and the framework requires no prior data or learned model, enabling direct deployment on new objects and targets without environment-specific calibration or data collection.

AI.tldr: FlipItRight is a framework for stable planar pose-targeted throw-flip with a high-DoF manipulator, achieving 90% success rate across 120 trials using explicit release-state representation without requiring prior data or learned models.
AI.motivation: To enable stable and precise planar throw-flip manipulation (tossing objects to land in a desired pose) using a high-DoF robot arm, without relying on data-driven models or environment-specific calibration.
AI.method: Decomposes the task into object-level planning (generating candidate release states for desired landing pose) and robot-level planning (evaluating executability and constructing feasible swing motions). Uses explicit release state as intermediate representation for candidate filtering, adaptive selection, and near-release motion design with constant end-effector velocities to handle release-timing uncertainty.
AI.result: 90% success rate across 120 trials on a real platform with objects of varying shape, size, and mass. Ablation studies confirm each design choice contributes to performance.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables direct deployment on new objects and targets without prior data, learned models, or environment-specific calibration/data collection, demonstrating robust and generalizable throw-flip manipulation.

title: T-CLIP: Enabling Thermal Perception for Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining
summary: Thermal imaging offers a powerful alternative to visible-spectrum vision under challenging conditions such as low illumination and adverse weather, yet foundational vision-language models like CLIP fail to align thermal images with textual descriptions due to a fundamental thermal perception gap. We identify three major challenges: the lack of captioned thermal datasets, the inability of standard LLMs to reason about thermal phenomena, and a key representational challenge in thermal imaging where global scene context and object-level heat signatures conflict when learned together in a single embedding space. To address these, we introduce IR-Cap, the first physics-aware thermal captioning pipeline and dataset providing complementary global and fine-grained thermal descriptions across three public benchmarks, and T-CLIP, a decoupled dual-LoRA framework that independently adapts CLIP for scene-level and object-level thermal understanding. T-CLIP achieves consistent improvements over all baselines across three thermal benchmarks in cross-modal retrieval, and we provide an exploratory demonstration of its applicability to text-conditioned thermal image generation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces IR-Cap (a physics-aware thermal captioning pipeline) and T-CLIP (a decoupled dual-LoRA framework) to bridge the thermal perception gap in vision-language models, achieving consistent improvements across thermal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language models like CLIP fail to align thermal images with text due to a fundamental thermal perception gap, compounded by lack of captioned thermal datasets, LLMs' inability to reason about thermal phenomena, and conflicting global scene vs. object-level heat signatures in a single embedding space.
AI.method: The authors propose IR-Cap, a physics-aware thermal captioning pipeline/dataset, and T-CLIP, a decoupled dual-LoRA framework that independently adapts CLIP for scene-level and object-level thermal understanding.
AI.result: T-CLIP achieves consistent improvements over all baselines across three thermal benchmarks in cross-modal retrieval, with exploratory demonstration in text-conditioned thermal image generation.
AI.conclusion: The decoupled dual-LoRA approach effectively resolves the representational conflict in thermal imaging, enabling better alignment between thermal images and text for downstream tasks.

title: A Practical Upper Bound on Selection Bias Effects in Medical Prediction Models
comment: 32 pages, 27 figures, will be published at ACM SIGKDD '26
summary: Selection bias is a common and often unavoidable aspect of real-world data that challenges the generalizability of machine learning models. When models trained on biased data are deployed in the broader target population, poor model generalization may lead to real harm, particularly in high-risk settings such as healthcare. This risk highlights the need for practitioners to reliably assess model generalizability prior to deployment. However, existing methods for predicting model performance rely on unrealistic access to the target distribution or knowledge of the selection mechanism causing bias. To address these limitations, we propose a novel upper bound on the worst-case model performance on the target population under the realistic setting where the selection mechanism and the target population data are only partially observed. We demonstrate the validity and practical utility of our method through experiments on fully synthetic data, semi-synthetic data derived from the All of Us Research Program, and real-world selection bias in MIMIC-IV. Our work offers a principled and practical tool to estimate the impact of selection bias in an otherwise intractable setting, thereby enabling practitioners to build safer and more generalizable models in healthcare and beyond.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to bound worst-case model performance under selection bias when target distribution and selection mechanism are only partially observed, validated on synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real-world healthcare data.
AI.motivation: Selection bias challenges model generalizability in real-world settings, especially in high-risk domains like healthcare. Existing methods require unrealistic access to target distribution or knowledge of selection mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel upper bound on worst-case model performance on the target population under a realistic setting where both the selection mechanism and target population data are only partially observed.
AI.result: The method is validated on fully synthetic data, semi-synthetic data from the All of Us Research Program, and real-world selection bias in MIMIC-IV, demonstrating validity and practical utility.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a principled and practical tool to estimate the impact of selection bias in otherwise intractable settings, enabling safer and more generalizable models in healthcare and beyond.

title: Expected Value Alignment for Generative Reward Modeling in Formal Mathematics Verification
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used with formal interactive theorem provers such as Lean 4. Scaling these systems with reinforcement learning or search methods requires process reward models (PRMs) that can evaluate intermediate reasoning steps. Existing reward-model designs expose a practical trade-off. Value-head models provide continuous scores but modify the generative model interface, while generative reward models preserve textual rationales but are poorly matched to continuous floating-point regression because numeric values are split across tokens. We introduce Expected Value Alignment (EVA), a reward-modeling procedure that keeps the surface output discrete while extracting continuous scores from the model's token distribution. The model emits integer scores in a structured JSON format, and EVA computes a continuous score as the expectation over the logits of the corresponding anchor tokens. Training combines the causal language modeling objective with an auxiliary mean squared error loss on these expected values. We instantiate EVA in \textit{Leibniz}, a reward model for Lean 4 formal verification, and evaluate it against zero-shot and reward-modeling baselines. The evaluation demonstrates that continuous logit-based scoring significantly reduces discretization artifacts while retaining the interpretability of generative critiques.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Expected Value Alignment (EVA), a reward-modeling procedure that extracts continuous scores from token distributions while keeping outputs discrete, and instantiates it in Leibniz for Lean 4 formal verification, demonstrating reduced discretization artifacts with maintained interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing reward-model designs for LLMs in formal theorem proving face a trade-off: value-head models provide continuous scores but modify the generative model interface, while generative reward models preserve textual rationales but struggle with continuous regression due to numeric token splitting.
AI.method: EVA emits integer scores in structured JSON format and computes continuous scores as expectations over logits of anchor tokens, combining causal language modeling loss with an auxiliary MSE loss on these expected values.
AI.result: Leibniz, the EVA-based reward model for Lean 4, outperforms zero-shot and reward-modeling baselines, with continuous logit-based scoring reducing discretization artifacts while preserving generative critique interpretability.
AI.conclusion: EVA effectively bridges the gap between discrete generative rewards and continuous scoring, offering a practical approach for process reward modeling in formal verification tasks.

title: Trans2Occ: Voxel Occupancy Estimation and Grasp for Transparent Objects from Simulation to Reality
summary: Transparent objects remain challenging for robotic perception due to unreliable depth sensing caused by refraction and reflection. While prior approaches rely on multi-view reconstruction or depth completion, they are often difficult to scale or deploy in real-world robotic systems. In this paper, we present a practical framework for transparent object perception and manipulation based on single-view RGB input. Our approach predicts voxel-space occupancy directly from a single image, providing a geometry-aware representation that supports downstream robotic grasping. To enable large-scale training, we construct a simulation pipeline that generates paired RGB images and voxel occupancy annotations under diverse materials and lighting conditions. We demonstrate that the predicted occupancy representation is robust to domain shifts and transfers effectively from simulation to real-world robotic setups without fine-tuning. A simple rule-based grasping strategy built on top of the occupancy further achieves reliable grasp performance on transparent objects. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments show that our framework provides accurate 3D understanding and enables practical manipulation of transparent objects. These results suggest that single-view occupancy prediction offers a scalable and effective solution for transparent object perception in robotics.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a single-view RGB-based framework for transparent object perception and manipulation, using voxel-space occupancy prediction trained on simulation data, which transfers to real-world without fine-tuning and enables reliable robotic grasping.
AI.motivation: Transparent objects are difficult for robotic perception due to unreliable depth sensing from refraction/reflection. Prior multi-view or depth completion methods are hard to scale or deploy in real-world robotic systems.
AI.method: The approach predicts voxel-space occupancy directly from a single RGB image, providing a geometry-aware representation for grasping. A simulation pipeline generates paired RGB images and voxel occupancy annotations under diverse materials/lighting conditions for large-scale training.
AI.result: The occupancy representation is robust to domain shifts and transfers from simulation to real-world without fine-tuning. A simple rule-based grasping strategy achieves reliable grasp performance on transparent objects in both simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: Single-view occupancy prediction offers a scalable and effective solution for transparent object perception and manipulation in robotics, enabling practical deployment without complex multi-view setups or depth sensors.

title: A Modelling and Evaluation Framework for EuroCrops-Driven Sentinel-2 Crop Segmentation
summary:
This work presents a configurable pipeline for generating semantic-segmentation-ready agricultural datasets from Sentinel-2 imagery and EuroCrops parcel-level annotations. The workflow transforms heterogeneous vector crop annotations into aligned multispectral image--mask pairs through label harmonization, Sentinel-2 product selection, spatial alignment, rasterization, patch extraction, quality filtering, and class-aware sample selection. The generated dataset contains 67,337 patches from five European countries and uses a reduced taxonomy of ten crop classes plus background.
  A four-level U-Net with Group Normalization was trained using 10 Sentinel-2 spectral bands and a composite loss combining class-weighted cross-entropy and Dice loss. On the internal EuroCrops-based test split, the model achieved a mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) of 0.7665, a pixel accuracy of 0.8693, and a mean class accuracy of 0.9072. Compared with spectral and spatial-context Random Forest baselines, the U-Net showed the importance of learned multi-scale spatial representations for crop segmentation.
  External evaluation was performed on unseen Belgian EuroCrops subsets, DACIA5, and PASTIS. The results show a clear performance gap under external and cross-dataset evaluation, especially for benchmarks with different taxonomies, annotation protocols, spatial coverage, or temporal organization. The model transfers more reliably to dominant and taxonomically aligned classes such as maize and wheat, while performance remains limited for several minority classes and for the adapted single-date PASTIS setting. These findings highlight both the potential and the limitations of using EuroCrops-derived supervision for Sentinel-2 crop segmentation under realistic domain shifts.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a configurable pipeline for generating agricultural semantic segmentation datasets from Sentinel-2 imagery and EuroCrops annotations, trains a U-Net achieving 0.7665 mIoU on internal tests, and evaluates cross-dataset performance showing reliable transfer for dominant classes but limitations for minority classes and different taxonomies.
AI.motivation: To address the need for large-scale, standardized agricultural datasets for semantic segmentation from satellite imagery, and to systematically evaluate the transferability of EuroCrops-derived supervision under realistic domain shifts.
AI.method: A configurable pipeline processes Sentinel-2 imagery and EuroCrops parcel annotations through label harmonization, product selection, spatial alignment, rasterization, patch extraction, quality filtering, and class-aware sample selection. A four-level U-Net with Group Normalization is trained on 10 spectral bands using combined class-weighted cross-entropy and Dice loss.
AI.result: Internal test: mIoU 0.7665, pixel accuracy 0.8693, mean class accuracy 0.9072. U-Net outperforms Random Forest baselines. External evaluation on Belgian EuroCrops, DACIA5, and PASTIS shows performance gaps under domain shifts, with reliable transfer for dominant/taxonomically-aligned classes but limited performance on minority classes and different taxonomies.
AI.conclusion: EuroCrops-derived supervision shows potential for Sentinel-2 crop segmentation but has clear limitations under domain shifts—especially for minority classes and datasets with different taxonomies or temporal organization—highlighting the need for better cross-dataset generalization strategies.

title: Same Payload, Different Channel: Measuring Trust Asymmetry in Tool-Using Language Models
summary: As language models take on agentic roles that span calling external APIs, reading tool outputs, and acting on instructions embedded in third-party content, their attack surface expands well beyond what users type. Whether a model treats a malicious instruction the same way regardless of where it arrives has not been systematically studied. We introduce the Safety Asymmetry Score (SAS), which measures how much a model's susceptibility to adversarial content shifts depending on whether that content arrives in the user message, tool metadata, or tool output, using matched payload pairs that keep the malicious text identical and vary only the context of delivery. Evaluated across 6 production LLMs and three attack families, we find a consistent and informative asymmetry: agent-native models are substantially more vulnerable when adversarial content arrives via tool descriptions than via user messages, while general-purpose models show the reverse. This asymmetry further inverts when the same content is delivered through tool outputs rather than descriptions, suggesting models implicitly treat tool metadata as trusted instructions and tool results as ordinary data. A mechanistic study on Llama 3.3 70B reveals that the safety-relevant representation is causally present at mid-to-late network depths but non-linearly encoded, explaining why linear probes fail to detect it. These findings expose a systematic, channel-dependent blind spot in how current tool-using models handle adversarial content.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Safety Asymmetry Score (SAS) to measure how LLMs' susceptibility to adversarial content shifts depending on whether the content arrives in user messages, tool metadata, or tool outputs. The authors find consistent asymmetry: agent-native models are more vulnerable via tool descriptions, while general-purpose models show the reverse. Mechanistic analysis on Llama 3.3 70B reveals safety-relevant representations are causally present but non-linearly encoded in mid-to-late network depths.
AI.motivation: As language models take on agentic roles involving external APIs, tool outputs, and third-party content, their attack surface expands beyond user-typed content. Yet whether a model treats malicious instructions differently based on where they arrive (user message, tool metadata, tool output) has not been systematically studied.
AI.method: The authors propose the Safety Asymmetry Score (SAS), which uses matched payload pairs that keep malicious text identical but vary the delivery context. They evaluated across 6 production LLMs and three attack families, and conducted a mechanistic study on Llama 3.3 70B.
AI.result: Agent-native models are substantially more vulnerable when adversarial content arrives via tool descriptions than user messages, while general-purpose models show the opposite. This asymmetry inverts when content is delivered through tool outputs vs. descriptions, suggesting models treat tool metadata as trusted instructions and tool results as ordinary data.
AI.conclusion: There is a systematic, channel-dependent blind spot in how current tool-using models handle adversarial content, revealing that safety alignment does not uniformly generalize across different input delivery channels.

title: Deft Scheduling of Dynamic Cloud Workflows with Varying Deadlines via Mixture-of-Experts
comment: This paper has been accepted by the Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Workflow scheduling in cloud computing demands the intelligent allocation of dynamically arriving, graph-structured workflows with varying deadlines onto ever-changing virtual machine resources. However, existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) schedulers remain limited by rigid, single-path inference architectures that struggle to handle diverse scheduling scenarios. We introduce \textbf{DEFT} (\textbf{D}eadline-p\textbf{E}rceptive Mixture-o\textbf{F}-Exper\textbf{t}s), an innovative DRL policy architecture that leverages a specialized mixture of experts, each trained to manage different levels of deadline tightness. To our knowledge, DEFT is the first to introduce and validate a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for dynamic cloud workflow scheduling. By adaptively routing decisions through the most appropriate experts, DEFT is capable of meeting a broad spectrum of deadline requirements that no single expert can achieve. Central to DEFT is a \textbf{graph-adaptive} gating mechanism that encodes workflow deadlines and DAGs, task states, and VM conditions, using cross-attention to guide expert activation in a fine-grained, deadline-sensitive manner. Experiments on dynamic cloud workflow benchmarks demonstrate that DEFT significantly reduces execution cost and deadline violations, outperforming multiple state-of-the-art DRL baselines.

AI.tldr: DEFT introduces a Mixture-of-Experts DRL architecture with a graph-adaptive gating mechanism for deadline-aware dynamic workflow scheduling in cloud computing, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines on cost and deadline violations.
AI.motivation: Existing DRL schedulers use rigid single-path inference architectures that cannot handle diverse scheduling scenarios with varying deadline requirements.
AI.method: A Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) DRL policy where each expert manages different deadline tightness levels, with a graph-adaptive gating mechanism using cross-attention to encode workflow deadlines, DAGs, task states, and VM conditions for fine-grained expert activation.
AI.result: Significant reduction in execution cost and deadline violations, outperforming multiple state-of-the-art DRL baselines on dynamic cloud workflow benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DEFT is the first to validate a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for dynamic cloud workflow scheduling, proving its effectiveness in meeting diverse deadline requirements that no single expert could achieve alone.

title: DisFlow: Scene Flow from Distance Field for Object Pose, Velocity Tracking, and Dynamic Object Reconstruction
summary: We present \emph{DisFlow}, a novel framework for online scene flow estimation from distance field that enables \emph{6DoF dynamic object pose estimation}, \emph{motion tracking}, and \emph{surface reconstruction}. The scene is represented by Gaussian Process Implicit Surfaces (GPIS), with surface normals serving as derivative constraints, enabling accurate signed distance computations near the surface and gradient queries with uncertainty. With this representation as a foundation, we compute a scene flow from the distance field that describes how surface points are transported over time in consecutive frames. Through our flow, we can estimate an object's pose and motion by incrementally registering a new observed point cloud via an elegant closed-form optimisation. Unlike prior methods that operate in the camera or world frame, our approach performs probabilistic fusion directly in the \emph{object frame}, where the object remains geometrically consistent over time. The tight coupling of the DisFlow method in space and time yields dense geometry, surface normals, object pose trajectories, velocities, and uncertainty, all at real-time rates. We evaluate DisFlow on dynamic object sequences and demonstrate that it achieves accurate pose and motion tracking while simultaneously reconstructing high-quality object surfaces. Code publicly available at \href{https://github.com/LanWu076/disflow_ros2}{https://github.com/LanWu076/disflow\_ros2}

AI.tldr: DisFlow is a real-time framework for 6DoF pose estimation, motion tracking, and surface reconstruction from online scene flow using Gaussian Process Implicit Surfaces (GPIS) with probabilistic fusion in the object frame.
