AI.motivation: To enable simultaneous dynamic object pose estimation, motion tracking, and surface reconstruction at real-time rates by tightly coupling spatial and temporal information through distance field-based scene flow.
AI.method: Represents scenes with GPIS using surface normals as derivative constraints; computes scene flow from distance fields describing how surface points transport across frames; performs closed-form optimization to register point clouds in the object frame for probabilistic fusion.
AI.result: Achieves accurate pose and motion tracking while simultaneously reconstructing high-quality object surfaces in real time, with outputs including dense geometry, surface normals, pose trajectories, velocities, and uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: DisFlow demonstrates that object-frame probabilistic fusion with GPIS-based scene flow enables robust dynamic object tracking and reconstruction at real-time rates, outperforming prior camera/world-frame approaches.

title: Shape-Prior-Based Point Cloud Completion for Single-Stage Fully Sparse 3D Object Detection
summary: Single-stage fully sparse 3D object detectors rely on point clouds data to detect objects in autonomous driving scenarios. However, the sparsity and incompleteness of point clouds significantly limit the performance of 3D object detection. To address this issue, this paper proposes a point clouds completion method specifically designed for single-stage fully sparse detectors. The entire shape-prior-based completion process consists of two consecutive steps. In the first step, we design a novel Instance Selection module, which is capable of identifying point clouds corresponding to foreground objects even when the baseline model does not generate proposals, while effectively ignoring the point clouds of background regions. In the second step, we introduce a novel Alignment-Based Point Completion module, which aligns the point clouds of foreground objects with prototypes in terms of both their centers and orientations. Subsequently, points are selected from the prototype to fill in the missing parts of the foreground object. We evaluated our method on two single-stage fully sparse detectors using the KITTI dataset. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the detection performance, confirming its effectiveness and generalizability.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a point cloud completion method for single-stage fully sparse 3D object detectors. It uses an Instance Selection module to identify foreground points and an Alignment-Based Point Completion module to fill missing parts using prototypes, improving detection performance on KITTI dataset.
AI.motivation: Point clouds are sparse and incomplete, which significantly limits the performance of 3D object detection in autonomous driving scenarios. Existing single-stage fully sparse detectors struggle with this issue.
AI.method: Two-step approach: (1) Instance Selection module identifies foreground object points even without proposals; (2) Alignment-Based Point Completion module aligns foreground points with prototypes (center and orientation) and selects points from prototypes to fill missing parts.
AI.result: Evaluated on two single-stage fully sparse detectors using the KITTI dataset. The method significantly improves detection performance, demonstrating effectiveness and generalizability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed point cloud completion method effectively enhances detection accuracy for single-stage fully sparse 3D detectors by completing sparse and incomplete foreground objects.

title: On the Recoverability of Causal Relations from Bulk Gene Expression Data
summary: Bulk gene expression profiling, which aggregates pooled RNA across cells within a biological sample, remains important in the single-cell era because it is typically less noisy, more sensitive, and more cost-effective than single-cell assays. Accordingly, a growing body of computational methods seeks to recover causal relations among genes from bulk expression data. However, aggregation is a lossy, non-invertible coarsening of the underlying cellular system, and it remains unclear whether and under what conditions causal relations are recoverable from aggregated bulk gene expression data. To answer this, we formalize recoverability under aggregation through two notions of consistency: functional-form consistency and conditional-independence consistency. We then derive necessary and sufficient conditions for recoverability, showing that these properties are preserved only under linear aggregations (e.g., sum/mean) coupled with affine structural equations. To assess the practical plausibility of these conditions, analyses of four bulk and four single-cell gene expression datasets further reveal that the estimated pairwise regulatory functions among genes deviate from linearity in both data types, providing limited empirical support for the linearity assumptions required for recoverability. Together, these results caution against recovering causal relations from aggregated bulk expression data without strong additional assumptions.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether causal relations among genes can be reliably recovered from bulk gene expression data (which averages signals across cells). The authors formalize recoverability through two consistency concepts and prove that causal relations are recoverable only under strict conditions: linear aggregation (e.g., sum/mean) combined with affine structural equations. Empirical analysis shows that real gene regulatory functions deviate from linearity in both bulk and single-cell data, suggesting limited support for these conditions. The paper concludes that recovering causal relations from bulk data is questionable without strong additional assumptions.
AI.motivation: Despite the rise of single-cell technologies, bulk gene expression profiling remains widely used due to its lower noise, higher sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness. As computational methods aim to recover causal gene regulatory networks from such data, it is critical to understand whether and under what conditions causal relations can be faithfully recovered after the lossy aggregation process inherent to bulk measurements.
AI.method: The authors define two formal notions of consistency (functional-form and conditional-independence) to characterize recoverability of causal relations under aggregation. They derive necessary and sufficient conditions mathematically. Empirically, they analyze four bulk and four single-cell gene expression datasets to estimate pairwise regulatory functions and assess their linearity.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows that causal relations are recoverable only under linear aggregation (e.g., sum/mean) combined with affine structural equations. Empirical analysis reveals that estimated pairwise regulatory functions deviate from linearity in both bulk and single-cell data, offering limited support for the required linearity assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Recovering causal relations from aggregated bulk gene expression data is not generally feasible without strong additional assumptions. Practitioners should exercise caution when applying causal inference methods to bulk expression data.

title: "Skill issues'': data-centric optimization of lakehouse agents
summary: Coding agents are becoming users of data infrastructure, but their success depends not only on model quality: it also depends on the skills and environment files that teach agents how to use a system. We study how to optimize these artifacts for agents operating on a branching lakehouse, Bauplan. In our setting, headless APIs and Git-like data primitives expose data workflows through code, branches, commits, and merges. Our central observation is that a branching lakehouse turns data-agent evaluation from an output-matching problem into a state-verification problem: agent-generated pipeline code induces concrete, inspectable lakehouse changes. We present a data-centric optimization pipeline that generates task-verifier pairs, executes candidate skills in isolated sandboxes, and scores trajectories using both trace-level signals and programmatic checks over lakehouse state. In a preliminary evaluation on 25 tasks, optimized skills improve accuracy by 31.9%. These results suggest that write-path data workflows provide a useful substrate for optimizing agent skills beyond read-only tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how to optimize coding agent skills for a branching lakehouse (Bauplan) by treating evaluation as state-verification rather than output-matching, achieving 31.9% accuracy improvement through a data-centric optimization pipeline.
AI.motivation: Coding agents' success depends not only on model quality but also on the skills and environment files that teach them how to use a system. The authors aim to optimize these artifacts for agents operating on a branching lakehouse.
AI.method: The authors propose a data-centric optimization pipeline that generates task-verifier pairs, executes candidate skills in isolated sandboxes, and scores trajectories using trace-level signals and programmatic checks over lakehouse state. They leverage the observation that branching lakehouses turn evaluation into a state-verification problem.
AI.result: In a preliminary evaluation on 25 tasks, optimized skills improved accuracy by 31.9%.
AI.conclusion: Write-path data workflows provide a useful substrate for optimizing agent skills beyond read-only tasks, suggesting that state-verification approaches have significant potential for improving coding agents in data infrastructure contexts.

title: The Lie We Tell: Correcting the Euclidean Fallacy in Vision Language Action Policies via Score Matching on Tangent Space
summary: Diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action policies achieve remarkable success in robotic manipulation, yet commit a fundamental geometric error we term the $\textbf{Euclidean Fallacy}$: representing SE(3) poses as flat $\mathbb{R}^{12}$ vectors. This approximation induces (1) manifold drift violating SO(3) constraints, (2) broken equivariance under coordinate transformations, and (3) non-geodesic trajectories with excessive kinematic cost. We introduce $\textbf{Lie Diffuser Actor (LDA)}$, a diffusion framework operating intrinsically on SE(3). Our method injects noise through left-invariant SDEs, predicts scores in the tangent space, and retracts samples via the exponential map. This formulation eliminates manifold drift by construction while guaranteeing coordinate-frame equivariance and geodesic optimality. On CALVIN ABC$\rightarrow$D, LDA improves average task length from $3.27$ to $3.51$ ($+7.3\%$). We further validate our method on real robot and the results show that our methodology outperforms the baseline on majority tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies the "Euclidean Fallacy" in diffusion-based robotic policies that incorrectly treat SE(3) poses as flat vectors, and proposes Lie Diffuser Actor (LDA), a diffusion framework operating intrinsically on SE(3) manifolds to eliminate drift, guarantee equivariance, and achieve geodesic optimality.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action policies represent SE(3) poses as flat ℝ¹² vectors, causing three fundamental problems: manifold drift violating SO(3) constraints, broken equivariance under coordinate transformations, and non-geodesic trajectories with excessive kinematic cost.
AI.method: LDA operates intrinsically on SE(3) manifolds by injecting noise through left-invariant SDEs, predicting scores in the tangent space, and retracting samples via the exponential map, which eliminates manifold drift by construction.
AI.result: On CALVIN ABC→D, LDA improves average task length from 3.27 to 3.51 (+7.3%), and outperforms baselines on real robot tasks.
AI.conclusion: Operating diffusion policies intrinsically on SE(3) manifolds is a principled and effective approach that fixes geometric errors inherent in Euclidean approximations.

title: Wavelet-Fusion Diffusion Model for Multimodal Brain MRI Synthesis with Modality and Metadata Conditioning
comment: 51 pages, 7 figures, including supplementary material. Submitted to Imaging Neuroscience
summary: Multimodal MRI provides complementary information for neuroimaging analysis, where different imaging modalities capture distinct anatomical, tissue, and pathological features that support the development and evaluation of downstream AI applications. Although large-scale structural MRI resources are increasingly available, their modality coverage is often uneven across public and pooled neuroimaging datasets. This uneven modality coverage is further complicated by heterogeneity across sites, scanners, and acquisition protocols, as well as demographic and clinical variables that are often sparse, inconsistently recorded, or unavailable across studies. Synthetic MRI generation can help address this imbalance by synthesizing target-modality volumes for dataset augmentation and controlled synthetic cohort creation. However, many existing MRI synthesis approaches are trained on narrow modality sets or relatively homogeneous cohorts, limiting their applicability to large pooled neuroimaging resources where modality availability, acquisition protocols, and metadata coverage vary substantially across datasets. Diffusion models have become an attractive approach for MRI synthesis because of their strong sample fidelity and diversity, but sampling directly in 3D voxel space is computationally expensive and slow at inference. Latent diffusion improves practicality by synthesizing MRI in a learned, 3D latent space, although generation quality depends on the autoencoder's reconstruction fidelity and the resulting latent distribution. Our approach combines a Wavelet-Fusion variational autoencoder (WF-VAE) latent compressor with a conditional 3D U-Net diffusion model trained in the learned latent space using explicit modality and metadata conditioning. Our proposed Wavelet-Fusion Diffusion Model (WFDM) achieved the strongest distributional alignment among the evaluated synthetic MRI generators.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Wavelet-Fusion Diffusion Model (WFDM) that combines a WF-VAE latent compressor with a conditional 3D U-Net diffusion model to synthesize multimodal MRI, addressing uneven modality coverage in large-scale neuroimaging datasets.
AI.motivation: Multimodal MRI datasets suffer from uneven modality coverage, heterogeneity across sites/scanners, and sparse/inconsistent metadata, limiting downstream AI applications. Existing synthesis methods are trained on narrow modality sets or homogeneous cohorts and don't generalize well to large pooled resources.
AI.method: WFDM combines a Wavelet-Fusion variational autoencoder (WF-VAE) for latent compression with a conditional 3D U-Net diffusion model trained in latent space using explicit modality and metadata conditioning.
AI.result: WFDM achieved the strongest distributional alignment among evaluated synthetic MRI generators.
AI.conclusion: The proposed latent diffusion approach with WF-VAE provides a practical and effective solution for multimodal MRI synthesis in heterogeneous pooled datasets.

title: On the Difficulty of Learning a Meta-network for Training Data Selection
summary: Synthetic data are increasingly used to train neural networks, yet distributional mismatch with real data limits their effectiveness when used indiscriminately. A common strategy is to learn data weights via bi-level optimization, which we refer to as Meta-learning for Training-data Selection (MTS). Interestingly, in practice, MTS often performs below expectation. We identify two obstacles in properly training MTS: a poor gradient signal-to-noise ratio (GSNR), which causes optimization difficulties, and lack of informative features that correlates with data quality. We present a mathematical analysis of MTS, which reveals the dynamics of normalized data weights and the relation between disparate data quality and poor GSNR. The analysis suggests a a simple yet effective solution: increasing the batch size. Further, we propose a set of informative features that capture the positions of training data in their distributions and training dynamics. Experiments across four benchmarks show consistent improvements, achieving average gains of 5.49% over training without selection and 2.89% over the strongest baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies two key obstacles in Meta-learning for Training-data Selection (MTS)—poor gradient signal-to-noise ratio (GSNR) and lack of informative features—and proposes increasing batch size plus using position-capturing features to improve selection, achieving ~5.5% average gains over no selection and ~2.9% over the strongest baseline.
AI.motivation: Synthetic data helps train neural networks but distributional mismatch with real data limits effectiveness. Meta-learning for Training-data Selection (MTS) is a common strategy that often underperforms, and the authors aim to identify and overcome the obstacles hindering its proper training.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of MTS dynamics revealing GSNR issues and data weight behavior; proposed solution: (1) increasing batch size to improve GSNR, (2) a set of informative features that capture training data positions in their distributions and training dynamics.
AI.result: Experiments across four benchmarks show consistent improvements, with average gains of 5.49% over training without selection and 2.89% over the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: Simple adjustments—increasing batch size and using better features—can significantly improve MTS performance, overcoming the identified obstacles of poor GSNR and uninformative features.

title: The Case for Model Science: Verify, Explore, Steer, Refine
summary: We argue that the AI community is now ready to move beyond benchmarking and consolidate scattered efforts in model analysis into a systematic discipline, a direction we term Model Science. Complex AI models now serve billions of users, yet our understanding of how they work lags far behind our ability to deploy them. Decades of benchmark-driven research have delivered remarkable progress: extensive leaderboards, a wide range of performance metrics, tracking capability gains across diverse tasks; yet this success has also revealed the limits of benchmarks as they tell us whether models perform but not why they succeed or fail, they miss critical failure modes, such as hallucinations or shortcuts. Precedents from established sciences point the way forward: cognitive science shows that understanding complex systems requires complementary levels of analysis; neuroscience demonstrates that deep study of single cases reveals what population studies miss; medicine teaches that specialised training must develop alongside research practice; and agriculture models how shared infrastructure and principles enable cumulative progress. These lessons inform three foundations for Model Science. First, we propose to consolidate research around four functional perspectives: Verify, Explore, Steer, and Refine that address complementary questions about model behaviour. Second, we discuss the required infrastructure for cumulative knowledge: catalogues of datasets, models and findings. Third, we highlight the need for deep analysis of individual model instances, not just model families, because single cases can reveal what population studies miss.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes moving beyond benchmarking toward a systematic "Model Science" discipline for understanding AI models, drawing lessons from cognitive science, neuroscience, medicine, and agriculture to establish four functional perspectives (Verify, Explore, Steer, Refine), shared infrastructure, and deep single-instance analysis.
AI.motivation: Current benchmark-driven research excels at measuring model performance but fails to explain why models succeed or fail, missing critical failure modes like hallucinations and shortcuts. There is a growing gap between our ability to deploy complex AI models and our understanding of how they work.
AI.method: The paper draws analogies from established sciences (cognitive science, neuroscience, medicine, agriculture) to derive three foundations: (1) four functional analysis perspectives—Verify, Explore, Steer, and Refine; (2) shared infrastructure for cumulative knowledge (catalogues of datasets, models, findings); (3) deep analysis of individual model instances rather than just model families.
AI.result: The paper presents a conceptual framework and roadmap for establishing Model Science as a systematic discipline, including consolidating research around four complementary perspectives and building infrastructure for cumulative progress.
AI.conclusion: The AI community should transition from benchmark-centric evaluation toward a more systematic Model Science that enables deeper understanding of model behavior, supported by shared infrastructure, complementary analysis perspectives, and case-level scrutiny.

title: PHASOR: Phase-Anchored Universal Action Representations for Humanoid Embodiments
summary: Learning a good action embedding space is fundamental to scalable robot policy learning, yet existing methods treat action latents as task-specific intermediates rather than first-class representations. The resulting latents are unstructured, embodiment-specific, and weakly tied to motion semantics, limiting interpretability, controllability, and transferability across robots. We position the action embedding space itself as a first-class design target, with downstream policy quality emerging from representation quality. Exploiting motion's intrinsic periodicity, we factorize it into a phase manifold that captures cyclic structure via FFT-parametric coefficients, together with a pose branch that conditions the manifold on non-periodic configuration detail. Combined with motion-semantic distillation, this factorized structure yields a cross-embodiment motion manifold that is interpretable and embodiment-agnostic by design. Anchoring multiple humanoid robots to a shared human-pretrained manifold then produces a unified action embedding space across diverse platforms, achieving strong cross-embodiment retrieval and consistent gains on downstream robot tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes making the action embedding space a first-class design target by factorizing motion into a periodic phase manifold (via FFT) and a non-periodic pose branch, combined with motion-semantic distillation, enabling cross-embodiment, interpretable, and transferable action representations for humanoid robots.
AI.motivation: Existing action embedding methods treat latents as task-specific intermediates, resulting in unstructured, embodiment-specific representations that lack interpretability, controllability, and cross-robot transferability.
AI.method: Factorizes motion into (1) a phase manifold capturing cyclic structure via FFT-parametric coefficients, and (2) a pose branch conditioning on non-periodic detail; combined with motion-semantic distillation to achieve a cross-embodiment, embodiment-agnostic manifold.
AI.result: Anchoring multiple humanoid robots to a shared human-pretrained manifold yields a unified action embedding space, achieving strong cross-embodiment retrieval and consistent downstream task gains.
AI.conclusion: Treating the action embedding space as a first-class design target, with periodic factorization and semantic distillation, produces interpretable and transferable representations that improve downstream robot policy learning across diverse platforms.

title: FROST-STA: Frozen Dense Features for the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation
summary: Short-term anticipation in egocentric video requires more than recognizing the current scene: a system must infer which object the camera wearer will contact, which action will follow, and how soon the contact will happen. This report describes FROST-STA, our submission to the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation (STA) Challenge at EgoVis 2026. For each query time, the model produces a ranked set of structured hypotheses containing an active-object box, noun label, verb label, time-to-contact (TTC), and confidence. FROST-STA builds on the V-JEPA 2.1 STA evaluation protocol, but adapts it to the challenge by using object-centric decoding, multi-head prediction, and a submission-oriented training and ensembling recipe. We keep the V-JEPA 2.1 ViT-G backbone fixed and extract two dense token streams: video tokens from a short clip resized to 384 pixels before the query, and image tokens from the last observed high-resolution frame. A compact alignment module, consisting of an attentive probe and frame-guided temporal pooling, maps the clip representation onto the spatial reference of the final frame before fusing it with image features. The fused maps are decoded by Faster R-CNN-style STA heads that estimate box offsets, nouns, verbs, TTC values, and interaction quality. For the final leaderboard entry, we train for 25 epochs with the official training split plus additional permitted validation annotations, and combine predictions across eight heads and checkpoints from epochs 15-25. FROST-STA obtains 5.13 Overall Top-5 mAP on the official test server, ranking second in the challenge and showing that frozen dense image-video features can serve as a strong basis for object-level interaction forecasting.

AI.tldr: FROST-STA achieves 2nd place in the Ego4D STA Challenge using frozen V-JEPA 2.1 features, object-centric decoding, and multi-head ensembling to anticipate object interactions in egocentric video.
AI.motivation: Short-term anticipation in egocentric video requires inferring which object will be contacted, what action will follow, and when contact occurs—going beyond simple scene recognition.
AI.method: Builds on V-JEPA 2.1 STA protocol; uses frozen ViT-G backbone, extracts two dense token streams (video from 384px clips; image from high-res frame), aligns via attentive probe + frame-guided temporal pooling, decodes with Faster R-CNN-style STA heads for box/noun/verb/TTC/confidence; trains 25 epochs with official+validation data; ensembles 8 heads across checkpoints.
AI.result: 5.13 Overall Top-5 mAP on official test server, ranking 2nd in the Ego4D STA Challenge.
AI.conclusion: Frozen dense image-video features provide a strong basis for object-level interaction forecasting.

title: Spatiotemporal Multi-Task Graph Transformer for Trip-Level Transit Prediction
comment: 25 pages, 7 figures, 11 tables, including appendix. Code available at https://github.com/Outsiders17711/SMTGraphFormer
summary: Passenger count data from public transit systems reveals urban mobility patterns and is essential for planning, operation, and optimisation. However, non-linear spatiotemporal interdependencies across stops and lines make modelling and prediction challenging. Existing approaches often rely on fixed temporal, spatial, or stop-level formulations, limiting their ability to capture within-trip evolution and network context. This study proposes SMT-GraphFormer, a spatiotemporal multi-task graph transformer that frames trip-level transit prediction as sequence-to-sequence modelling. Given a line's stop sequence and trip-level context, the model predicts successive boarding and alighting counts, with delay and dwell time treated as encoder-side surrogate tasks. Key components include graph embeddings for multi-relational stop similarity, a context encoder for weather and temporal information, and a multi-gate mixture-of-experts module that produces task-specific decoder representations for boarding and alighting predictions. Evaluation on public bus transit data from Trondheim, Norway, shows that SMT-GraphFormer outperforms stop-level tabular benchmarks, with ablation studies examining each component's contribution. The sequential formulation yields substantial gains on alighting prediction ($+$0.24 in $R^2$) and consistent improvements on boarding, delay, and dwell, confirming the value of explicit trip-level sequential bias and inter-target dependencies. These findings demonstrate the potential of transformer-based sequence modelling for capturing complex spatiotemporal dynamics in public transit and underscore the value of architectures tailored to transit data rather than off-the-shelf tabular models. The proposed framework provides a horizon-agnostic basis for scenario analysis in digital twin environments, supporting informed decision-making by planners and transit operators.

AI.tldr: SMT-GraphFormer is a transformer-based model that treats trip-level transit prediction as sequence-to-sequence learning, predicting boarding/alighting counts along a bus line while using delay/dwell time as auxiliary tasks. It outperforms stop-level models on Trondheim bus data, especially for alighting prediction (+0.24 R²).
AI.motivation: Existing transit prediction models rely on fixed stop-level or temporal formulations, failing to capture within-trip evolution and network context. Better modelling of non-linear spatiotemporal dependencies across stops and lines is needed for planning and optimisation.
AI.method: SMT-GraphFormer: a spatiotemporal multi-task graph transformer. It frames trip-level prediction as seq2seq, uses graph embeddings for multi-relational stop similarity, a context encoder for weather/temporal info, and a multi-gate mixture-of-experts module for task-specific boarding/alighting predictions. Delay and dwell time serve as encoder-side surrogate tasks.
AI.result: On Trondheim bus data, SMT-GraphFormer outperforms stop-level tabular benchmarks. Alighting prediction gains +0.24 in R²; boarding, delay, and dwell also improve consistently. Ablation studies confirm each component's contribution.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based sequence modelling effectively captures complex spatiotemporal dynamics in public transit. The trip-level sequential bias and inter-target dependencies are valuable. The framework provides a horizon-agnostic basis for scenario analysis in digital twin environments.

title: Can LLM Agents Sustain Long-Horizon Organizational Dynamics?
summary: Large language agents are increasingly used for social simulation, yet it remains unclear whether they can sustain coherent behavior in structured organizations, where goals must propagate through hierarchy, tasks depend on prior execution, and artifacts accumulate over long horizons. We formulate long-horizon organizational simulation as a memory-centered coordination problem and introduce TaskWeave, a hierarchical agentic framework that maintains planning states through a Formulate-Partition-Diagnose-Align cycle and grounds execution through dependency-aware trace memory. We evaluate TaskWeave in a year-long IT company simulation and compare it with other multi-agent frameworks on organizational coherence, execution grounding, and downstream enterprise NLP utility. Experiments show that TaskWeave supports coherent and long-horizon organizational dynamics while producing grounded artifacts and adapting to external environments. These findings suggest that structured simulation memory is a key mechanism for building reliable LLM-based organizational simulators.

AI.tldr: TaskWeave is a hierarchical agentic framework for long-horizon organizational simulation that uses a Formulate-Partition-Diagnose-Align cycle and dependency-aware trace memory to maintain coherent behavior in structured organizations, demonstrating effectiveness in year-long IT company simulations.
AI.motivation: Large language agents struggle to sustain coherent behavior in structured organizations over long horizons, where goals must propagate through hierarchy, tasks depend on prior execution, and artifacts accumulate over time.
AI.method: TaskWeave formulates long-horizon organizational simulation as a memory-centered coordination problem, using a Formulate-Partition-Diagnose-Align cycle for planning and dependency-aware trace memory for execution grounding.
AI.result: TaskWeave supports coherent, long-horizon organizational dynamics, produces grounded artifacts, adapts to external environments, and outperforms other multi-agent frameworks on organizational coherence, execution grounding, and downstream enterprise NLP utility.
AI.conclusion: Structured simulation memory is a key mechanism for building reliable LLM-based organizational simulators.

title: Set-Supervised Diffusion Policy: Learning Action-Chunking Diffusion through Corrections
summary: Diffusion policies have recently emerged as a powerful framework for robotic manipulation. However, like other behavior cloning methods, they remain vulnerable to distributional shift, often requiring human-in-the-loop interventions to correct failures during deployment. These interactions naturally provide paired supervision in the form of the robot's undesired actions and the human teacher's corrective actions. Yet existing data aggregation pipelines and standard behavior cloning losses largely ignore this negative signal from undesired actions, leading to overfitting to teacher's actions and an increasing reliance on costly expert data. To address this limitation, we propose Set-Supervised Diffusion Policy (SDP), a novel learning framework that utilizes contrastive action-chunk data to train diffusion policies from human corrections. From paired positive and negative action-chunks, SDP constructs a set of desired action-chunks and designs a training pipeline that encourages the diffusion policy to align with the set. Through extensive experiments across multiple robotic manipulation tasks, we demonstrate that SDP consistently improves policy performance, with particularly strong gains in robustness to noisy data. Moreover, SDP induces high-quality aggregated datasets, enabling more efficient and reliable policy learning from human-in-the-loop corrections. Our code is available at https://set-supervised-diffusion-policy.github.io/.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Set-Supervised Diffusion Policy (SDP), a framework that uses positive and negative action-chunks from human corrections to train diffusion policies for robotic manipulation, improving robustness and reducing reliance on expert data.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion policies for robotic manipulation suffer from distributional shift and overfitting to teacher actions. Human-in-the-loop corrections provide paired positive/negative signals, but standard methods ignore the negative signal, leading to costly reliance on expert data.
AI.method: SDP uses contrastive action-chunk data (paired positive and negative action-chunks from human corrections) to construct a set of desired action-chunks, then trains a diffusion policy to align with this set via a novel training pipeline.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple robotic manipulation tasks show SDP consistently improves policy performance, with strong gains in robustness to noisy data, and produces high-quality aggregated datasets.
AI.conclusion: SDP enables more efficient and reliable policy learning from human-in-the-loop corrections by leveraging negative signals that prior methods ignore.

title: VICR: Visual In-Context Restoration for Real-World Image Super-Resolution
summary: Real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR) requires balancing structural fidelity to degraded observations with realistic detail synthesis. However, existing generative Real-ISR methods often rely on entangled conditioning mechanisms, leading to structural drift or semantically inconsistent details. To address this issue, we propose Visual In-Context Restoration (VICR), a Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based framework that formulates Real-ISR as image completion. Specifically, we introduce a decoupled visual prior injection mechanism that derives local and global cues from the low-quality (LQ) image: local cues help recover image structures and support high-frequency detail synthesis, while global cues guide overall generation and promote semantic consistency. For ambiguous regions under severe degradation, VICR employs an inference-time agent to refine semantic prompts using visual evidence from the LQ input while keeping model parameters fixed. Experiments show that VICR achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple Real-ISR benchmarks with only 127M trainable parameters.

AI.tldr: VICR is a Diffusion Transformer framework that reformulates Real-ISR as image completion using decoupled local and global visual priors, achieving SOTA with only 127M parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing generative Real-ISR methods use entangled conditioning, causing structural drift and semantically inconsistent details.
AI.method: Proposes VICR with a DiT-based framework treating Real-ISR as image completion, featuring: (1) decoupled visual prior injection (local cues for structure/detail, global cues for semantic consistency), and (2) an inference-time agent that refines semantic prompts from LQ visual evidence without changing model parameters.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple Real-ISR benchmarks with only 127M trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling local and global conditioning in a completion-based DiT framework effectively balances structure fidelity and realistic detail in Real-ISR.

title: LASER: Loss-Aware Singular-value Decomposition and Rank Allocation for Efficient Low-Precision Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) deliver strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but their large computational cost and high parameter counts make deployment challenging on resource-constrained devices. Low-rank decomposition has emerged as a promising compression technique, yet existing methods often optimize local matrix reconstruction error, rely on uniform or heuristic rank allocation, and focus mainly on attention projections while leaving feed-forward networks underexplored. In this paper, we propose~\textit{LASER} (\textbf{L}oss-\textbf{A}ware \textbf{S}ingular-value d\textbf{E}composition and \textbf{R}ank allocation), a low-rank compression framework for efficient low-precision VLM inference. LASER derives a curvature-weighted SVD objective from a second-order approximation of the model loss and uses Kronecker-factored Fisher information to guide decomposition toward downstream performance rather than reconstruction alone. We further introduce a loss-aware cross-layer rank allocation strategy based on calibration gradients, enabling more effective parameter budgeting across layers. Finally, we extend low-rank compression to FFN layers through a hybrid scheme that combines SVD with quantization. The evaluation results show that LASER achieves more than $2.3\times$ decoding speedup over previous work while preserving strong accuracy under low-precision inference.

AI.tldr: LASER is a low-rank compression framework that uses loss-aware SVD and cross-layer rank allocation to achieve 2.3x speedup for VLM inference on resource-constrained devices while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: VLMs are computationally expensive and hard to deploy on resource-constrained devices. Existing low-rank compression methods optimize local reconstruction error, use heuristic rank allocation, and neglect feed-forward networks.
AI.method: (1) Curvature-weighted SVD derived from second-order loss approximation with Kronecker-factored Fisher information; (2) loss-aware cross-layer rank allocation using calibration gradients; (3) hybrid SVD+quantization scheme for FFN layers.
AI.result: LASER achieves over 2.3x decoding speedup compared to prior work while maintaining strong accuracy under low-precision inference.
AI.conclusion: Loss-aware low-rank compression can effectively accelerate VLM inference on resource-limited devices by jointly optimizing attention and FFN layers with principled rank allocation.

title: The Shape of Wisdom: Decision Trajectories in Language Models
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Code and derived artifacts: https://github.com/gut-puncture/The-Shape-of-Wisdom
summary: Language models do not simply choose an answer at the output layer. In a 9,000-trajectory MMLU study across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, the score of the answer moves across depth in structured ways. We describe each trajectory with three quantities: the current answer margin, the next-layer change in that margin, and the distance from a decision flip. The main empirical picture is that correctness and stability are different: the largest group is unstable-correct, not stable-correct. A traced subset then asks what moves the margin. In stable-correct cases, the average attention scalar points in the correct direction, while the average MLP scalar does not; span deletion shows that removing answer-supporting text hurts the margin and removing distractor-like text helps it. The result is not a full circuit explanation. It is a reproducible way to see which answers are settled, which remain fragile, and which measured sources move them.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how language models' answer margins evolve across internal layers during MMLU tasks, finding that correctness and stability are distinct properties, with most correct answers being unstable rather than stable.
AI.motivation: To understand that language models don't simply choose answers at the output layer, but exhibit structured internal dynamics where answer margins change across depth, revealing that correctness and stability are different phenomena.
AI.method: Traced 9,000 trajectories across three 7B-parameter models (Qwen2.5, Llama-3.1, Mistral) on MMLU, describing each trajectory using three quantities: current answer margin, next-layer change in margin, and distance from a decision flip. Analyzed attention and MLP scalar contributions, and performed span deletion experiments.
AI.result: The largest group of answers is unstable-correct (not stable-correct). In stable-correct cases, attention scalars point in the correct direction while MLP scalars do not. Removing answer-supporting text hurts the margin; removing distractor-like text improves it.
AI.conclusion: Provides a reproducible framework for identifying which answers are settled, which remain fragile, and what measured sources drive margin changes, though it does not constitute a full circuit explanation.

title: Closed-Form Pose Estimation of Endoluminal Medical Devices via Gradiometer-Based Electromagnetic Localization System
summary: Embedded magnetic tracking holds highly attractive prospects for remote navigation of endoluminal medical devices. However, existing six-degree-of-freedom pose recovery approaches often require pre-calibrated workspace field maps or iterative nonlinear optimization. This letter presents a Gradiometer-Based Electromagnetic Localization System (GELS), a closed-form tracking framework that uses a compact magnetometer array as an embedded quasi-gradiometer to estimate local magnetic fields and gradient tensors. These quantities are mapped by the Euler homogeneous relation to displacements between source and array, from which multi-source Procrustes registration recovers the array orientation and position using at least three non-collinear sources. The algorithm requires known source positions and array geometry, but no pre-calibrated workspace field maps, initial pose guesses, or calibrated excitation-source moments. The recovered pose also enables a proof-of-concept sub-level dipole localization task by serving as a mobile magnetic reference frame. Benchtop experiments across sensor-array configurations and excitation modes demonstrate sequence-averaged position errors of \SI{10.80}{\milli\meter}--\SI{15.57}{\milli\meter}, a fastest update rate of \SI{14.49}{\hertz}, and a median solver runtime of \SI{172.00}{\micro\second}. A perturbation-based error propagation analysis further identifies inter-sensor inconsistency and dipole-model mismatch as the dominant accuracy limits, thereby informing future sensor array and magnetic source design for further reducing pose-estimation error.

AI.tldr: A closed-form electromagnetic tracking system using a magnetometer array as a quasi-gradiometer to recover 6-DOF pose without pre-calibrated field maps or iterative optimization.
AI.motivation: Embedded magnetic tracking enables remote navigation of endoluminal devices, but existing methods require pre-calibrated field maps or iterative nonlinear optimization, limiting practicality.
AI.method: Uses a compact magnetometer array as a quasi-gradiometer to estimate local fields and gradient tensors, applies Euler homogeneous relation for displacement estimation, and multi-source Procrustes registration for orientation/position recovery using ≥3 non-collinear sources.
AI.result: Achieves 10.80–15.57 mm position errors, 14.49 Hz update rate, and 172 μs median solver runtime. Identifies inter-sensor inconsistency and dipole-model mismatch as dominant error sources.
AI.conclusion: GELS provides a fast, closed-form pose tracking framework without pre-calibration, and the error analysis informs future sensor/source design improvements.

title: CR-JEPA: Cross-Modal Joint-Embedding Predictive Learning for Remote Sensing Image Retrieval
summary: Cross-modal remote sensing image retrieval aims to retrieve semantically related scenes across heterogeneous sensing modalities. This remains challenging because paired observations may differ substantially in imaging physics, spatial resolution, spectral configuration, and visual appearance. Moreover, a single retrieval projection trained with one objective may be insufficient to jointly support cross-modal semantic alignment and same-modal neighbourhood preservation. We propose CR-JEPA, a Cross-modal Retrieval Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for dual-modality remote sensing retrieval. The model uses modality-specific stems, a shared transformer trunk, and JEPA-style predictive objectives to estimate masked latent target features within and across modalities. Inspired by LeJEPA, we apply Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization to raw retrieval projections to stabilize embeddings and mitigate collapse. CR-JEPA further employs a decoupled-head design with a unified retrieval head for same-modal retrieval and a cross-modal retrieval head for cross-modal search. We evaluate CR-JEPA on BEN-14K, CBRSIR_VS, and DSRSID. On BEN-14K, CR-JEPA improves S1 to S2 retrieval from 61.23% to 75.82% and S2 to S1 retrieval from 63.73% to 75.40% over X-JEPA, while also achieving competitive same-modal retrieval with fewer parameters.

AI.tldr: CR-JEPA is a cross-modal retrieval model for remote sensing that uses JEPA-style predictive objectives, modality-specific stems, shared transformer trunk, Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization, and decoupled heads to significantly improve cross-modal retrieval accuracy on BEN-14K, CBRSIR_VS, and DSRSID benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Cross-modal remote sensing image retrieval is challenging due to heterogeneous imaging physics, resolutions, spectra, and appearances. A single retrieval projection trained with one objective fails to jointly support cross-modal semantic alignment and within-modality neighborhood preservation.
AI.method: CR-JEPA uses modality-specific stems, a shared transformer trunk, and JEPA-style predictive objectives (masked latent feature prediction within/across modalities). It applies Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (inspired by LeJEPA) to stabilize embeddings and prevent collapse, plus a decoupled-head design: one unified head for same-modal retrieval and one cross-modal head for cross-modal retrieval.
AI.result: On BEN-14K, CR-JEPA improves S1-to-S2 retrieval from 61.23% to 75.82% and S2-to-S1 retrieval from 63.73% to 75.40% over X-JEPA, while also achieving competitive same-modal retrieval with fewer parameters. Evaluated on BEN-14K, CBRSIR_VS, and DSRSID.
AI.conclusion: CR-JEPA demonstrates that decoupled retrieval heads and JEPA-style objectives with regularization effectively address the dual challenge of cross-modal alignment and same-modal preservation in remote sensing retrieval.

title: Looped Transformers with Layer Normalization Provably Learn the Power Method
summary: Transformers have achieved remarkable success across a wide range of applications, and a growing body of work suggests that part of their strength comes from their ability to learn and execute algorithmic procedures. However, our understanding of how transformers learn such algorithms remains limited, especially in the presence of layer normalization (LN). In this work, we study principal component prediction as a concrete testbed for understanding the training dynamics of transformers with LN. We prove that a looped linear transformer with LN, trained by gradient descent, converges to a solution that implements the power method, with each self-attention layer performing one power iteration. Notably, the model is trained only for principal component prediction, rather than being explicitly supervised to implement the power method. Our finding thus reveals an "algorithmic implicit bias" of looped transformers with LN: principal-component prediction can in principle be achieved by many mechanisms, yet gradient descent selects one that realizes the power method. We further provide a concrete comparison between transformers with and without LN: even with layerwise guidance from power iterations, a transformer without LN cannot exactly learn the power method, whereas the corresponding transformer with LN can, leading to a provable performance gap in principal component prediction. Our results provide, to our knowledge, the first theoretical analysis of the training dynamics of looped and single-layer transformers with LN, and shed light on the role of LN in transformer models.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that looped linear transformers with layer normalization (LN), trained via gradient descent for principal component prediction, converge to a solution implementing the power method. It reveals an "algorithmic implicit bias" and shows LN is essential for successfully learning this algorithm, unlike transformers without LN.
AI.motivation: While transformers excel at algorithmic tasks, how they learn such algorithms—especially with layer normalization—remains poorly understood. This work aims to fill that gap by analyzing training dynamics in a controlled setting (principal component prediction).
AI.method: The authors study a looped linear transformer with LN trained by gradient descent for principal component prediction. They provide theoretical analysis showing convergence to the power method, and compare transformers with and without LN to demonstrate LN's critical role.
AI.result: The looped transformer with LN converges to implement the power method (one power iteration per layer). Transformers without LN cannot exactly learn the power method even with layerwise guidance, creating a provable performance gap.
AI.conclusion: This is the first theoretical analysis of training dynamics for looped and single-layer transformers with LN, revealing an "algorithmic implicit bias" and highlighting LN's crucial role in enabling algorithmic learning.

title: Advanced Mathematics Learning Behavior Prediction and Academic Early Warning Model Based on Multimodal Data Analysis
summary: Early detection of at-risk students and timely academic intervention pose major challenges in advanced mathematics education, where complex conceptual hierarchies and nonlinear learning trajectories often hold back students' academic performance. This study adopts multimodal data analytics to build a dynamic framework for learning behavior prediction and academic early warning. It constructs a hierarchical knowledge graph ontology, realizes adaptive edge weighting according to problem difficulty and student performance, and combines heterogeneous graph attention with temporal sequence modeling to capture students' evolving knowledge states. Empirical tests on semester-long multimodal datasets prove that this method can accurately identify high-risk students and effectively track error propagation. Targeted interventions greatly improve students' knowledge mastery and reduce academic risks. The results verify that integrating knowledge graph analytics with multimodal temporal modeling can deliver more efficient and personalized learning support for advanced mathematics education.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a dynamic framework using multimodal data analytics, knowledge graphs, and temporal modeling to predict at-risk students in advanced mathematics education and enable timely interventions.
AI.motivation: Early detection of at-risk students and timely intervention are major challenges in advanced mathematics education due to complex conceptual hierarchies and nonlinear learning trajectories.
AI.method: The authors build a hierarchical knowledge graph ontology with adaptive edge weighting, combine heterogeneous graph attention with temporal sequence modeling to capture evolving knowledge states.
AI.result: The method accurately identifies high-risk students, effectively tracks error propagation, and targeted interventions significantly improve knowledge mastery and reduce academic risks.
AI.conclusion: Integrating knowledge graph analytics with multimodal temporal modeling delivers more efficient and personalized learning support for advanced mathematics education.

title: Learning Action-Conditional and Object-Centric Gaussian Splatting World Models for Rigid Objects
summary: World models enable intelligent agents to predict the consequences of their actions on the environment. In this paper, we propose Multi Rigid Object Gaussian World Model (MRO-GWM), a novel model that learns action-conditional dynamics of rigid objects in 3D. By representing the scene by object-centric Gaussians, we can represent arbitrary object shapes and multi-object scenes. We develop a novel spatio-temporal transformer architecture that predicts future rigid body motion from a history of object Gaussians and future actions. Objects are represented by their Gaussians in a canonical frame, which allows for describing object motion as rigid body transformation. Our model is trained on reconstructions from multiple viewpoints, which requires the model to handle partial observations of objects due to occlusions. We analyze prediction performance of our approach on synthetic datasets composed of typical household objects with multi-object dynamics and interactions by a robot end effector. We also evaluate our model in model-predictive control for non-prehensile manipulation in simulation.

AI.tldr: MRO-GWM is a 3D world model that uses object-centric Gaussians and a spatio-temporal transformer to predict rigid object dynamics from multiple viewpoints, enabling action-conditional forecasting and non-prehensile manipulation control.
AI.motivation: To enable intelligent agents to predict consequences of actions on environments by learning action-conditional dynamics of rigid objects in 3D, handling partial observations due to occlusions, and representing arbitrary object shapes in multi-object scenes.
AI.method: Represents scenes with object-centric Gaussians in a canonical frame; uses a novel spatio-temporal transformer to predict future rigid body motion from history of object Gaussians and future actions; trained on multi-view reconstructions.
AI.result: Evaluated on synthetic datasets with household objects and multi-object dynamics/interactions by a robot end effector; also tested in model-predictive control for non-prehensile manipulation in simulation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MRO-GWM effectively models rigid object dynamics in 3D with object-centric Gaussian representations and spatio-temporal transformer, showing promise for manipulation tasks.

title: CASTLE2026 Team WDL Technical Report
summary: The CASTLE Challenge @ EgoVis 2026 evaluates long-form egocentric video question answering over 600+ hours of multi-perspective recordings. Each four-choice question requires evidence from videos, transcripts, auxiliary photos, people, days, rooms, and temporal context. We propose an evidence-aware multimodal reasoning pipeline based on Qwen. Our system parses question hints, retrieves ASR chunks, attaches auxiliary images, samples candidate video frames, and routes questions into static visual, speech/text, temporal, and mixed types with specialized prompts. Multiple inference passes are aggregated by confidence-weighted voting and converted into the official Codabench format. In ablation, LoRA improves the score from 0.21 to 0.50, and more sampled frames further raise it to 0.58. Our final system ranks first in the CASTLE Challenge @ EgoVis 2026.

AI.tldr: A Qwen-based multimodal pipeline, combining ASR, auxiliary images, video frames, and type-specific prompts with confidence-weighted voting, won the CASTLE Challenge @ EgoVis 2026 for long-form egocentric video QA over 600+ hours.
AI.motivation: The CASTLE Challenge @ EgoVis 2026 introduces a highly complex long-form egocentric video QA task with 600+ hours of multi-perspective data, requiring evidence from diverse sources (videos, transcripts, photos, people, days, rooms, temporal context). Existing methods struggle to integrate all these modalities.
AI.method: An evidence-aware pipeline based on Qwen: parses question hints → retrieves ASR chunks → attaches auxiliary images → samples candidate frames → routes questions into four types (static visual, speech/text, temporal, mixed) with specialized prompts → multiple inference passes aggregated by confidence-weighted voting → Codabench format output.
AI.result: LoRA fine-tuning improved score from 0.21 to 0.50; more sampled frames raised it to 0.58. The system ranked first in the CASTLE Challenge @ EgoVis 2026.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multimodal reasoning pipeline, with evidence-aware design and confidence-weighted voting, achieves state-of-the-art performance on long-form egocentric video QA, demonstrating the effectiveness of routing questions by type and leveraging LoRA and dense frame sampling.

title: CARE-RL: Capability-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Mitigating Cross-Domain Conflicts
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) with verifiable rewards has achieved strong progress in reasoning-oriented LLMs, but extending it to multi-domain RL remains challenging due to reward unreliability in non-verifiable tasks and capability interference across domains. We propose CARE-RL to combine protocol-aware reward generation with capability-aware optimization for mitigating cross-domain conflicts. For non-verifiable tasks, the Protocol-Aware Generative Reward Model (PA-GRM) constructs prompt-level evaluation protocols and schemas before producing trace-conditioned rewards, enabling task-adaptive yet comparable evaluation of open-ended responses. For multi-domain optimization, Direction-Aware Capability Subspace Projection (DACSP) extracts historical capability directions from previous RL stages and modulates later updates by amplifying aligned components, suppressing conflicting components, and preserving orthogonal updates. Experiments across math, chat, and instruction-following benchmarks show that CARE-RL consistently outperforms standard multi-domain RL baselines, achieving Total Avg scores of 47.9 and 50.7 on Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen3-4B, respectively.

AI.tldr: CARE-RL introduces protocol-aware reward generation (PA-GRM) for non-verifiable tasks and capability-aware optimization (DACSP) to mitigate cross-domain conflicts, achieving state-of-the-art multi-domain RL results on math, chat, and instruction-following benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Extending RL with verifiable rewards to multi-domain settings is challenging due to unreliable rewards in non-verifiable tasks and capability interference across domains.
AI.method: (1) PA-GRM: builds prompt-level evaluation protocols/schemas before generating trace-conditioned rewards for non-verifiable tasks. (2) DACSP: extracts historical capability directions from prior RL stages, then amplifies aligned components, suppresses conflicting ones, and preserves orthogonal updates.
AI.result: Outperforms standard multi-domain RL baselines; achieves Total Avg scores of 47.9 (Qwen2.5-7B) and 50.7 (Qwen3-4B) across math, chat, and instruction-following benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CARE-RL effectively enables multi-domain RL by jointly addressing reward generation for non-verifiable tasks and cross-domain capability conflicts.

title: Application of Algorithms in Energy-Efficient Design Platforms for Green Building
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures.2026 International Conference on Big Data Applications in Education and Engineering (ICBDAEE 2026)
summary: During green building design, computer-aided energy assessment is widely used to improve efficiency and achieve overall optimization. This paper presents a platform that combines Building Information Modeling (BIM), sensor operational data, and advanced simulation workflows using robust algorithms. The platform uses a multi-layer service architecture with dynamic energy simulation and evolutionary multi-objective optimization, connected via a high-performance C++ core and adaptive agent models. A mid-rise office building was selected as the case study. Five representative areas were chosen to collect data on building envelope characteristics and occupancy patterns. After preprocessing, missing sensor data accounted for 3.2% of annual records, and all variables were standardized using 15-minute interpolation. After 40 optimization rounds, annual energy consumption per square meter dropped by 29.3% from 315 kWh/m2 to 223 kWh/m2. The lifecycle cost increase for occupants was limited to 3.7%, and discomfort hours were reduced to under 70 hours per year. Analysis of Pareto optimal solutions shows that the envelope U-value ranges from 1.05 to 1.57 W/m2K, and nighttime ventilation rate ranges from 2.1 to 3.6 h-1, both closely linked to energy performance. The results confirm that the integrated algorithm framework offers good scalability, strong performance, and technical feasibility for green building design. This platform provides a reliable decision-support tool for design engineers and sustainability practitioners, enabling accurate, data-driven delivery of energy-efficient buildings.

AI.tldr: A platform integrating BIM, sensor data, and multi-objective optimization reduces building energy consumption by 29.3% (from 315 to 223 kWh/m²) with only 3.7% lifecycle cost increase and under 70 discomfort hours/year, validated on a mid-rise office building.
AI.motivation: To improve efficiency and achieve overall optimization in green building design through computer-aided energy assessment.
AI.method: A multi-layer service platform combining BIM, sensor operational data, dynamic energy simulation, evolutionary multi-objective optimization, a high-performance C++ core, and adaptive agent models, validated on a mid-rise office building with 15-minute interpolated sensor data.
AI.result: Annual energy consumption dropped 29.3% (315→223 kWh/m²), lifecycle cost increased only 3.7%, discomfort hours <70/year, Pareto-optimal U-value 1.05–1.57 W/m²K, nighttime ventilation 2.1–3.6 h⁻¹.
AI.conclusion: The integrated algorithm framework offers good scalability, strong performance, and technical feasibility, serving as a reliable decision-support tool for data-driven green building design.

title: Co-training with Ego-centric Video and Demonstration for Robot Navigation Task
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models are promising for diverse robotic tasks, but their performance heavily depends on large-scale high-quality training data, whose collection on real robots is costly and time-consuming. While prior work has explored augmenting manipulation datasets with egocentric human videos, applying such approaches to mobile robot navigation remains challenging due to viewpoint changes during locomotion. In this paper, we propose a framework that converts egocentric walking videos into datasets for mobile robot imitation learning. The proposed method estimates camera motion from human videos and transforms it into action representations compatible with ground mobile robots. By jointly training a VLA model on human-derived and robot-collected datasets, the model achieves improved language understanding and more robust action generation than training with either data source alone. Experiments on a fruit-search navigation task demonstrate that human egocentric videos provide an effective and scalable data source for mobile robot learning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework that converts egocentric walking videos into training datasets for mobile robot imitation learning by estimating camera motion and transforming it into compatible action representations, achieving improved performance through joint training with robot-collected data.
AI.motivation: VLA models require large-scale high-quality data, but real robot data collection is costly and time-consuming. Prior work using egocentric human videos for manipulation tasks faces challenges in mobile robot navigation due to viewpoint changes during locomotion.
AI.method: A framework that estimates camera motion from egocentric human walking videos and transforms it into action representations compatible with ground mobile robots, then jointly trains a VLA model on both human-derived and robot-collected datasets.
AI.result: Experiments on a fruit-search navigation task show that joint training on human-derived and robot-collected data achieves improved language understanding and more robust action generation than using either data source alone.
AI.conclusion: Human egocentric videos provide an effective and scalable data source for mobile robot learning, addressing the data scarcity problem in robotic navigation.

title: Scaling Parallel Sequence Models to Foundation-Scale Vision Encoders
summary:
Vision foundation models are bottlenecked by the quadratic cost of self-attention, which limits usable resolution and increases the cost of large-scale pretraining. Subquadratic alternatives such as linear attention and state-space models reduce this cost, but often serialize images into 1D token streams and weaken the 2D spatial structure important for vision. Generalized Spatial Propagation Networks (GSPN) instead propagate context directly on the 2D grid through line-scan recurrences, achieving near-linear complexity without positional embeddings, but have seen little use as foundation-scale encoders.
  We present C-GSPN, a foundation-scale vision encoder based on 2D spatial propagation. C-GSPN makes the operator practical through three improvements: (1) a fast GSPN CUDA kernel that fuses per-step launches into a single warp-specialized implementation with shared-memory tiling, coalesced access, and a compact multi-channel propagation, reaching over 90% of peak memory bandwidth and running up to 40--52x faster than the original GSPN implementation; (2) a compressed latent-space propagation block with fused normalization, which turns kernel-level speed into block- and model-level efficiency; and (3) a two-stage cross-operator distillation recipe that trains the new architecture from an attention teacher without the cost of from-scratch foundation-scale training. Distilled with 600M image-text pairs, C-GSPN matches an isomorphic ViT baseline with 15% fewer parameters, improves ADE20K segmentation by +2.1%, transfers to high resolution with a fraction of the data needed from scratch, and delivers a 4x end-to-end block speedup at 2K with single-pass, tiling-free inference.

AI.tldr: C-GSPN is a vision foundation model that replaces quadratic-cost self-attention with 2D spatial propagation (GSPN), achieving near-linear complexity. It introduces a fast CUDA kernel (40-52x speedup), compressed latent-space propagation, and a two-stage distillation recipe from attention teachers. It matches ViT with 15% fewer parameters, achieves +2.1% ADE20K segmentation improvement, and delivers 4x speedup at 2K resolution.
AI.motivation: Vision foundation models suffer from the quadratic cost of self-attention, limiting resolution and increasing pretraining cost. Subquadratic alternatives (linear attention, SSMs) often serialize images into 1D streams, weakening 2D spatial structure. GSPN propagates context on 2D grids with near-linear complexity but hasn't been used at foundation scale.
AI.method: Three improvements: (1) A fast GSPN CUDA kernel using warp-specialization, shared-memory tiling, and compact multi-channel propagation (40-52x faster); (2) compressed latent-space propagation block with fused normalization; (3) two-stage cross-operator distillation from an attention teacher using 600M image-text pairs.
AI.result: Matches isomorphic ViT with 15% fewer parameters; +2.1% on ADE20K segmentation; 4x end-to-end block speedup at 2K resolution with single-pass, tiling-free inference; kernel reaches over 90% peak memory bandwidth.
AI.conclusion: C-GSPN demonstrates that 2D spatial propagation networks can serve as efficient, high-performance vision foundation encoders, overcoming the quadratic bottleneck of attention while preserving 2D spatial structure, with practical speedups and competitive accuracy.

title: How Neural Losses Shape VAE Latents
summary: Modern VAEs are rarely trained with the pointwise likelihood implied by the standard $β$-VAE objective. In practice, pointwise reconstruction is often combined with perceptual and adversarial losses, despite a lack of understanding of how this changes the latent dynamics of the model. We show that the choice of reconstruction loss reshapes the rate-distortion problem itself, altering both the information content and the geometry of the learned latent space in ways that may be invisible from reconstructions alone. First, we prove and verify empirically that augmenting pointwise reconstruction with neural terms, such as perceptual and adversarial objectives, reduces the amount of information stored in the latent representations. Second, we show that neural reconstruction losses systematically change the geometry of the latent space: they make representations more isotropic and distribute uncertainty more evenly across latent dimensions, producing different posterior variance profiles. These findings highlight how the rate-distortion tradeoff is not a comprehensive lens to understand the behavior of VAEs, and we propose a more mechanistic approach to investigate how the choice of a distortion metric reshapes the optimization problem.

AI.tldr: Modern VAEs use perceptual and adversarial losses instead of standard pointwise reconstruction, which reduces latent information, makes latent representations more isotropic, and distributes uncertainty more evenly across dimensions; thus, the rate-distortion tradeoff is insufficient for understanding VAE behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand how replacing pointwise reconstruction with perceptual and adversarial losses changes the latent dynamics of VAEs, particularly regarding information content and latent space geometry, which is currently poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors prove theoretically and verify empirically that neural reconstruction losses (perceptual, adversarial) reduce information stored in latent representations and systematically alter latent space geometry toward isotropy and uniform uncertainty distribution across dimensions.
AI.result: Neural reconstruction losses reduce information content in latent representations, make latent space more isotropic, and distribute posterior variance more evenly across dimensions compared to standard pointwise reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The rate-distortion tradeoff is insufficient for understanding VAE behavior; a more mechanistic approach is needed to study how distortion metrics reshape the optimization problem.

title: HomeFlow: A Data Flywheel for Smart Home Agent Training with Verifiable Simulation
summary: Large language model agents are moving beyond text-only interaction toward physical-world control, with smart homes as a representative domain. Real domestic interaction requires understanding ambiguous intents, operating in dynamic environments, and performing multi-turn reasoning. However, existing methods struggle to generate high-quality training data for smart home agents. We propose HomeFlow, a verifiable data flywheel for this domain. HomeFlow uses HomeEnv as a unified simulation environment and HomeMaker to procedurally generate diverse home settings. Subsequently, Blueprint compiles open-ended user intents into executable state-based success conditions, while MCTS-Flow synthesizes diverse, verifiable multi-turn trajectories through environment-guided tree search. We then optimize the agents via supervised fine-tuning and step-wise RLVE, which facilitates iterative improvement through authentic physical feedback. We further construct SmartHome-Bench to evaluate the agent across various smart home tasks. On this benchmark, HomeFlow-RL-4B and HomeFlow-RL-8B achieve task success rates of 84.60% and 87.03%. It is worth noting that HomeFlow-RL-8B even surpasses the leading GPT-5.5 by 1.23 percentage points.

AI.tldr: HomeFlow proposes a verifiable data flywheel for training smart home agents, combining procedural environment generation, state-based intent compilation, MCTS-guided trajectory synthesis, and step-wise RLVE optimization. Their 8B model achieves 87.03% task success rate on SmartHome-Bench, surpassing GPT-5.5 by 1.23%.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle to generate high-quality training data for smart home agents that need to understand ambiguous intents, operate in dynamic environments, and perform multi-turn reasoning.
AI.method: HomeFlow consists of: (1) HomeEnv (unified simulation environment) + HomeMaker (procedural home generation); (2) Blueprint (compiles intents to state-based success conditions); (3) MCTS-Flow (synthesizes diverse multi-turn trajectories via tree search); (4) Supervised fine-tuning + step-wise RLVE (reinforcement learning with verifiable environment feedback).
AI.result: HomeFlow-RL-4B achieves 84.60% and HomeFlow-RL-8B achieves 87.03% task success rate on SmartHome-Bench. HomeFlow-RL-8B surpasses GPT-5.5 by 1.23 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: The verifiable data flywheel approach effectively generates high-quality training data and achieves state-of-the-art performance on smart home agent tasks, with smaller models outperforming larger general-purpose LLMs.

title: WALL-WM: Carving World Action Modeling at the Event Joints
summary: WALL-WM is a World Action Model that shifts video-action learning from chunk-centric optimization to event-grounded Vision-Language-Action pretraining, using semantically coherent action events as the atomic unit of learning. Existing WAMs commonly initialize from multimodal or video foundation models and then optimize fixed-length action chunks conditioned directly on the current observation and instruction. Although convenient, this chunk-centric formulation creates a fundamental granularity mismatch. Language describes semantic goals and events, vision evolves through continuous scene dynamics, and actions operate at control-level timescales; forcing all three into the same fixed-length prediction window turns VLA training into short-horizon correlation fitting. WALL-WM addresses this mismatch by organizing both supervision and data around semantic events. Specifically, it pairs event-grounded VLA pretraining with a data ecosystem built from event-level captions and cluster-balanced sampling, enabling scalable learning over diverse behaviors, scenes, and task structures. From the same event-pretrained backbone, WALL-WM supports two complementary inference modes. The event mode consumes next-event descriptions and enables variable-length execution chunks, while the unified mode uses a VLM with Staircase Decoding to condition conventional fixed-length chunk inference while preserving a gradient-continuous VLA path. Together with Muon-optimizer-based large-scale pretraining infrastructure, WALL-WM provides a practical scale-up recipe for general-purpose WAMs. Experiments show that WALL-WM generalizes broadly across language, scenes, and tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance in large-scale real-world generalization evaluation.

AI.tldr: WALL-WM is a World Action Model that replaces chunk-centric video-action learning with event-grounded Vision-Language-Action pretraining, achieving state-of-the-art real-world generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing WAMs use fixed-length action chunks conditioned on observations and instructions, creating a granularity mismatch between language (semantic events), vision (continuous dynamics), and actions (control-level timescales), leading to short-horizon correlation fitting.
AI.method: WALL-WM uses event-grounded VLA pretraining with event-level captions and cluster-balanced sampling. It supports two inference modes: (1) event mode with variable-length execution chunks, and (2) unified mode using VLM with Staircase Decoding for fixed-length chunks. Uses Muon-optimizer-based large-scale pretraining infrastructure.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in large-scale real-world generalization evaluation, generalizing broadly across language, scenes, and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Event-grounded VLA pretraining resolves the granularity mismatch in existing WAMs, providing a practical scale-up recipe for general-purpose World Action Models.

title: SkyShield: Occupancy as a Safety Interface for Low-Altitude UAV Autonomy
summary: For low-altitude Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) autonomy, 3D spatial understanding is not merely a perception objective, but the safety interface between human instructions and physical flight. In human-scale urban airspace below 20 meters, thin geometry, occlusions, vegetation, and urban clutter define whether an aerial agent can safely enter the space ahead. However, existing UAV datasets mainly provide 2D annotations or 3D boxes, while driving-oriented occupancy benchmarks assume stable ground-level sensor rigs. Both miss the defining regime of low-altitude flight: a front-facing monocular camera observing occupied and free space from a moving aerial body with frame-wise changing 6-DoF pose and camera extrinsics. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{SkyShield}, to the best of our knowledge the first front-view monocular semantic occupancy benchmark for urban UAV flight below 20 meters. Built on CARLA, SkyShield contains 36K front-view UAV samples across diverse urban scenes and weather conditions, pairing each image with frame-wise 6-DoF UAV pose, frame-wise dynamic camera geometry, UAV states, and front-frustum semantic occupancy labels. We further propose \textbf{KAR-mIoU}, a UAV-centric and dynamics-aware metric that re-weights voxel-level evaluation by kinematic reachability and time-to-collision, revealing safety-critical risks hidden by conventional mIoU. To tackle this challenging new setting, we provide \textbf{SkyOcc}, a geometry-first monocular baseline that integrates frame-wise UAV attitude into projection, fuses temporal occupancy features, and applies safety-prior optimization to preserve sparse collision-critical structures. Together, SkyShield, KAR-mIoU, and SkyOcc establish occupancy as a safety interface for low-altitude aerial autonomy. Code and dataset will be released publicly.

AI.tldr: SkyShield is the first front-view monocular semantic occupancy benchmark for low-altitude UAV flight (<20m), featuring 36K samples with 6-DoF pose, dynamic camera geometry, and occupancy labels.
