AI.tldr: A novel architecture with axis-anchored transformers and an auxiliary Soft-DTW regression task for text-based 3D human motion editing that achieves state-of-the-art results on MotionFix by understanding both temporal and joint-level edits.
AI.motivation: Previous works focus on when an edit should occur temporally, but fail to understand which specific joints are responsible for the change. The goal is to create a model that captures both temporal and joint-level aspects.
AI.method: Proposes two axis-anchored transformers (joint and time dimensions) with a cross-axis fusion block, plus an auxiliary task that trains the joint-anchored transformer to regress Soft-DTW distance between source and target joint rotations.
AI.result: Significantly improves semantic alignment with text instruction and source motion, and overall fidelity, achieving state-of-the-art on MotionFix dataset.
AI.conclusion: The joint-aware auxiliary task and axis-anchored architecture effectively enable the model to understand both which joints to modify and which to preserve, leading to superior text-based motion editing.

title: Task diversity produces systematic transfer but inhibits continual reinforcement learning
summary: Continual reinforcement learning aims to produce agents that learn not only to improve at their current tasks but also to adapt as task distributions change. Training an agent on many diverse tasks can induce zero-shot generalization, but previous work generally evaluates this generalization after training -- with frozen weights. Whether task diversity also improves an agent's ability to continue learning across distribution shifts remains unclear. We introduce Banyan, a GPU-accelerated continual RL domain in which task diversity factors into three independently controllable axes: the map layouts an agent must navigate, the objects it must interact with, and the hierarchical structures of sub-goal dependencies. Across individual distribution shifts, increasing diversity along each axis causes agents to begin training on the new tasks near the performance attained on the previous one, even when the shift changes the structure of the optimal policy. However, as the number of shifts increases, this local transfer does not by itself yield sustained continual learning: longer-horizon tasks plateau, and earlier task distributions are forgotten after later training. Banyan is a benchmark for studying when controlled task diversity produces transferable learning, when that transfer persists, and where it falls short of proper continual learning.

AI.tldr: Banyan is a GPU-accelerated continual RL benchmark with three controllable axes of task diversity (maps, objects, sub-goal structures). While task diversity improves local transfer (near-zero-shot adaptation to distribution shifts), it fails to produce sustained continual learning—long-horizon tasks plateau and earlier tasks are forgotten.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether task diversity improves an agent's ability to continue learning across distribution shifts, beyond just improving zero-shot generalization with frozen weights.
AI.method: Introduces Banyan, a GPU-accelerated continual RL benchmark with three independently controllable axes of task diversity: map layouts, interactable objects, and hierarchical sub-goal structures. The domain allows systematic study of how varying these factors affects agent behavior under distribution shifts.
AI.result: Increasing diversity along each axis improves local transfer (agents start near previous performance on new tasks), even when the optimal policy structure changes. However, as the number of shifts increases, longer-horizon tasks plateau and earlier distributions are forgotten—local transfer alone does not yield sustained continual learning.
AI.conclusion: Banyan is a benchmark for studying when controlled task diversity produces transferable learning, when that transfer persists, and where it falls short of proper continual learning.

title: EvoBrain: Continual Learning of EEG Foundation Models Across Heterogeneous BCI Tasks
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) is the cornerstone of non-invasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), yet conventional decoding relies on fragmented, task-specific architectures that severely limit cross-task scalability. While EEG foundation models pre-trained on massive corpora promise universal brain decoding, current post-training depends on task-isolated fine-tuning. This static paradigm restricts knowledge transfer across heterogeneous tasks, hinders model scalability, and incurs computational and storage overheads that scale linearly with task count. To overcome these bottlenecks, we formulate downstream adaptation as a cross-task continual learning problem and propose EvoBrain, a dynamic, task-aware continual learning framework for unified EEG decoding. EvoBrain addresses the plasticity-stability trade-off via two complementary components: (1) Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN) aligns incoming tasks with historical statistics while recalibrating spectral responses to handle distributional and neuro-spectral shifts; and (2) Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD), combined with time-dependent replay, preserves old-task response geometry and promotes selective knowledge transfer between spectrally compatible tasks, effectively mitigating forgetting. Extensive evaluations across six distinct BCI tasks demonstrate that EvoBrain consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods across diverse foundation backbones, optimally balancing plasticity and stability. To our knowledge, this work pioneers cross-task continual learning in the EEG domain, advancing the realization of a unified, one-for-all brain decoding system.

AI.tldr: EvoBrain is a continual learning framework that enables EEG foundation models to adapt to multiple tasks sequentially, using Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization and Response-Affinity Distillation to balance learning new tasks while retaining old knowledge, outperforming existing methods across six BCI tasks.
AI.motivation: Current EEG decoding relies on fragmented, task-specific architectures and isolated fine-tuning, which limits cross-task knowledge transfer, scalability, and incurs linearly growing computational/storage costs. The paper aims to overcome these bottlenecks by formulating downstream adaptation as a cross-task continual learning problem.
AI.method: EvoBrain consists of two components: (1) Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN) aligns new tasks with historical statistics and recalibrates spectral responses to handle distributional shifts; (2) Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD) with time-dependent replay preserves old-task response geometry and enables selective knowledge transfer between spectrally compatible tasks.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across six distinct BCI tasks show EvoBrain consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods across diverse foundation backbones, optimally balancing plasticity (learning new tasks) and stability (retaining old knowledge).
AI.conclusion: This work pioneers cross-task continual learning in the EEG domain, advancing the realization of a unified, one-for-all brain decoding system.

title: Learning Neural Deformation Representation for 4D Dynamic Shape Generation
summary: Recent developments in 3D shape representation opened new possibilities for generating detailed 3D shapes. Despite these advances, there are few studies dealing with the generation of 4D dynamic shapes that have the form of 3D objects deforming over time. To bridge this gap, we focus on generating 4D dynamic shapes with an emphasis on both generation quality and efficiency in this paper. HyperDiffusion, a previous work on 4D generation, proposed a method of directly generating the weight parameters of 4D occupancy fields but suffered from low temporal consistency and slow rendering speed due to motion representation that is not separated from the shape representation of 4D occupancy fields. Therefore, we propose a new neural deformation representation and combine it with conditional neural signed distance fields to design a 4D representation architecture in which the motion latent space is disentangled from the shape latent space. The proposed deformation representation, which works by predicting skinning weights and rigid transformations for multiple parts, also has advantages over the deformation modules of existing 4D representations in understanding the structure of shapes. In addition, we design a training process of a diffusion model that utilizes the shape and motion features that are extracted by our 4D representation as data points. The results of unconditional generation, conditional generation, and motion retargeting experiments demonstrate that our method not only shows better performance than previous works in 4D dynamic shape generation but also has various potential applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new 4D dynamic shape generation method using a neural deformation representation with disentangled shape and motion latent spaces, combined with conditional neural SDFs and diffusion models, achieving better generation quality and temporal consistency than prior work like HyperDiffusion.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D generation methods (e.g., HyperDiffusion) suffer from low temporal consistency and slow rendering due to entangled motion and shape representations. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a more efficient and higher-quality 4D generation approach.
AI.method: The authors propose a new neural deformation representation predicting skinning weights and rigid transformations for multiple parts, combined with conditional neural signed distance fields (SDFs). This architecture disentangles motion latent space from shape latent space. A diffusion model is trained on the extracted shape and motion features.
AI.result: The method outperforms previous works in unconditional generation, conditional generation, and motion retargeting experiments, demonstrating better performance and potential for various applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed disentangled deformation representation improves temporal consistency and generation quality for 4D dynamic shapes, enabling multiple downstream applications beyond generation.

title: Dive into Waves: Morlet Spectral Transformer for Cross-Subject Emotion Decoding from EEG
summary: We study cross-subject emotion recognition from EEG, a practically important yet challenging problem in brain-computer interfaces. Unlike tasks with clear waveform signatures, emotion-related EEG signals are primarily encoded in spectral power and are weak, noisy, and highly variable across subjects. Existing approaches rely either on large pretrained EEG foundation models, which require massive data yet still struggle with cross-subject variability, or frequency-domain encoders, which better reflect spectral structure but suffer from mismatched representations, drift-dominated tokenization, and lack of band-specific spatial modeling. In this article, we propose the Morlet Spectral Transformer (MST), built around three key components and integrated with a spatiotemporal Transformer backbone. First, Morlet wavelet tokenization provides a time-frequency representation that matches the multi-scale structure of brain rhythms, and extends classical differential entropy features to a form suitable for Transformers. Second, long-context baseline removal acts as a simple temporal normalization that removes subject-specific drift and redundancy across nearby windows. Third, frequency-specific spatial projection learns a separate channel mixer for each frequency band, capturing interpretable band-specific patterns and reducing cross-channel mixing. We show that, even without pretraining, MST consistently outperforms both large pretrained EEG foundation models and frequency-based methods across all SEED-family datasets. These results suggest that careful representation design can yield an accurate, cost-effective, and interpretable alternative to large-scale pretraining.

AI.tldr: The Morlet Spectral Transformer (MST) outperforms both large pretrained EEG foundation models and frequency-based methods on cross-subject emotion recognition, using Morlet wavelet tokenization, baseline removal, and frequency-specific spatial projections without pretraining.
AI.motivation: Cross-subject emotion recognition from EEG is challenging due to weak, noisy, and highly variable signals. Existing methods (large pretrained models or frequency-domain encoders) struggle with cross-subject variability, mismatched representations, drift-dominated tokenization, and lack of band-specific spatial modeling.
AI.method: MST integrates three components into a spatiotemporal Transformer backbone: (1) Morlet wavelet tokenization for time-frequency representation matching brain rhythms; (2) long-context baseline removal for subject-specific drift normalization; (3) frequency-specific spatial projection for per-band channel mixing and interpretable patterns.
AI.result: Without pretraining, MST consistently outperforms large pretrained EEG foundation models and frequency-based methods across all SEED-family datasets.
AI.conclusion: Careful representation design can yield an accurate, cost-effective, and interpretable alternative to large-scale pretraining for EEG-based emotion recognition.

title: Structure-Guided Adaptive Propagation for Protein-Protein Interaction Site Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of protein-protein interaction sites (PPIS) is essential for understanding cellular processes, disease mechanisms, and therapeutic target discovery. Graph-based deep learning has advanced PPIS prediction by incorporating residue-level structural context. However, most graph-based models still rely on fixed propagation schemes that treat all residues similarly, despite the structural and functional heterogeneity of protein interfaces. Such propagation may limit the ability to adapt information diffusion to local geometric environments, making it difficult to distinguish true interaction sites from structurally similar non-interacting neighbors. We present SGAP-PPIS, a structure-guided adaptive propagation model for PPIS prediction. Rather than using a fixed propagation mechanism, SGAP-PPIS leverages multi-scale geometric states from an equivariant graph neural network to generate residue-wise propagation coefficients. This design allows each residue to adaptively balance local feature preservation and neighborhood diffusion according to its geometric microenvironment. Experimental results show that SGAP-PPIS achieves competitive performance among the state-of-the-art methods on Test\_60. Ablation studies show that geometry-conditioned adaptive propagation, scale-aligned geometric guidance, and multi-step propagation-state representation jointly drive these improvements.

AI.tldr: SGAP-PPIS adaptively propagates information in graph neural networks based on local geometric environments for improved protein-protein interaction site prediction.
AI.motivation: Fixed propagation schemes in graph-based PPIS models treat all residues equally, failing to capture structural and functional heterogeneity, which limits the ability to distinguish true interaction sites from similar non-interacting neighbors.
AI.method: SGAP-PPIS uses multi-scale geometric states from an equivariant graph neural network to generate residue-wise propagation coefficients, enabling each residue to adaptively balance local feature preservation and neighborhood diffusion based on its geometric microenvironment.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance on Test_60 benchmark compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-conditioned adaptive propagation, scale-aligned geometric guidance, and multi-step propagation-state representation jointly drive the improvements.

title: ProductWebGen: Benchmarking Multimodal Product Webpage Generation
summary: Crafting a product display webpage from a source product image, along with layout and visual content instructions, holds significant practical value for domains such as marketing, advertising, and E-commerce. Intuitively, this task demands strict visual consistency across product displays and high-fidelity instruction following to jointly generate renderable HTML code. These requirements on controllability and instruction-following are closely aligned with the core features of advanced multimodal generative models, such as image editing models and unified models. To this end, this paper introduces ProductWebGen to systematically benchmark the product webpage generation capacities of these models. We organize ProductWebGen with 500 test samples covering 13 product categories; each sample consists of a source image, a visual content instruction, and a webpage instruction. The task is to generate a product showcase webpage including multiple consistent images in accordance with the source image and instructions. Given the mixed-modality input-output nature of the task, we design and systematically compare two workflows for evaluation -- one uses large language models and image editing models to separately generate HTML code and images (editing-based), while the other relies on a single UM to generate both, with image generation conditioned on the preceding multimodal context (UM-based). Empirical results show that editing-based approaches achieve leading results in webpage instruction following and content appeal, while UM-based ones may display more advantages in fulfilling visual content instructions. We also construct a supervised fine-tuning dataset, ProductWebGen-1k, with 1,000 groups of real product images and LLM-generated HTML code. We verify its effectiveness on the open-source UM BAGEL. The data and code are available at https://github.com/SJTU-DENG-Lab/ProductWebGen.

AI.tldr: ProductWebGen is a new benchmark (500 samples, 13 categories) for evaluating multimodal generative models on the task of generating renderable HTML product display webpages from a source image, visual content instructions, and webpage layout instructions, comparing editing-based (LLM + image editing) vs. UM-based (single unified model) workflows.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal generative models lack a systematic benchmark for evaluating their ability to jointly generate consistent product images and renderable HTML code while following both visual content and layout instructions — a task with high practical value for marketing, advertising, and E-commerce.
AI.method: The authors construct ProductWebGen with 500 test samples across 13 product categories, each with a source image, visual content instruction, and webpage instruction. They design and compare two workflows: (1) editing-based — using LLMs to generate HTML code and image editing models to generate consistent images; (2) UM-based — using a single unified model to generate both. They also build ProductWebGen-1k (1,000 groups) for supervised fine-tuning, validated on the open-source UM BAGEL.
AI.result: Editing-based approaches achieve leading results in webpage instruction following and content appeal, while UM-based approaches show more advantages in fulfilling visual content instructions. The ProductWebGen-1k dataset improves the open-source UM BAGEL's performance.
AI.conclusion: ProductWebGen provides a systematic benchmark for product webpage generation, revealing complementary strengths between editing-based and UM-based approaches; the dataset and code are publicly released for future research.

title: Memory-Efficient LLM Training with Dynamic Sparsity: From Stability to Practical Scaling
summary: Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) offers a promising paradigm for improving the training and inference efficiency of deep neural networks; however, we find that in large language model training, DST can suffer from optimization instability, manifested as loss spikes after topology updates. In this work, we show that the naive use of standard Adam-based optimizers leads to a cold-start issue for newly regrown parameters, resulting in excessively large updates and disrupted training dynamics. To address this issue, we propose Sparse Memory-Efficient Training (SMET), which stabilizes DST with optimizer warm-up and improves training progress through density-aware learning-rate scaling. SMET further reduces memory consumption by storing gradients and optimizer states only for active parameters. We provide a theoretical analysis of the update behaviors under SMET, showing improved optimization stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SMET enables stable, scalable, and memory-efficient sparse pre-training of LLMs, paving the way for sparse training as a practical alternative to dense training. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/QiaoXiao7282/SMET.

AI.tldr: SMET stabilizes Dynamic Sparse Training for LLMs by addressing optimizer cold-start issues and reducing memory through density-aware scaling and sparse-only gradient storage.
AI.motivation: Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) causes optimization instability (loss spikes) in LLM training due to Adam-based optimizers creating a cold-start issue for newly regrown parameters, leading to excessively large updates.
AI.method: SMET introduces optimizer warm-up for newly regrown parameters and density-aware learning-rate scaling, while storing gradients and optimizer states only for active parameters to reduce memory.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SMET enables stable, scalable, and memory-efficient sparse pre-training of LLMs, making sparse training a practical alternative to dense training.
AI.conclusion: SMET stabilizes DST for LLMs by solving the cold-start issue and reducing memory, paving the way for practical sparse training at scale.

title: Stochastic convergence of parallel asynchronous adaptive first-order methods
summary: A new class of asynchronous adaptive first-order optimization methods is introduced, comprising asynchronous variants of several popular algorithms. Versions of these methods using momentum and/or inexact normalization are also considered. The convergence of methods in the class on non-convex functions is analyzed in a fully stochastic setting, and is shown to be (up to logarithmic factors) of order O(1/sqrt{t}) under reasonable assumptions. Numerical experiments suggest that such asynchronous adaptive algorithms are very relevant in heterogeneous large-scale machine learning systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a class of asynchronous adaptive first-order optimization methods, including variants with momentum and inexact normalization, proves their O(1/√t) convergence on non-convex functions in a stochastic setting, and demonstrates their effectiveness in heterogeneous large-scale ML systems through numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To address the need for efficient optimization methods in heterogeneous large-scale machine learning systems, where asynchronous and adaptive algorithms can improve performance and scalability.
AI.method: The authors propose a new class of asynchronous adaptive first-order optimization methods, including asynchronous variants of popular algorithms with momentum and/or inexact normalization, and analyze their convergence theoretically.
AI.result: The methods achieve O(1/√t) convergence (up to logarithmic factors) on non-convex functions in a fully stochastic setting, and numerical experiments confirm their relevance in heterogeneous large-scale ML systems.
AI.conclusion: Asynchronous adaptive algorithms are effective and relevant for heterogeneous large-scale machine learning, with theoretical guarantees supporting their practical performance.

title: Data Collection for Training Quality-Control AI in Carpet Manufacturing
summary: Visual inspection remains the dominant quality-control practice in woven and tufted carpet production, yet it is slow, subjective, and inconsistent at the line speeds and widths of modern looms. We present a design proposal for an in-line machine-vision system whose primary purpose is twofold: to inspect the carpet web in real time and, equally importantly, to systematically collect and label images of defect patterns so that increasingly capable quality-control models can be trained over the life of the installation.The proposal is grounded in a concrete industrial setting: a Six Sigma (DMAIC) project at a woven-carpet production facility that anticipated a production bottleneck following the installation of additional weaving machines, with a substantial baseline defect rate and significant financial exposure associated with quality failures. We describe an imaging subsystem based on synchronized line-scan cameras with combined bright-field and grazing illumination, derive the resolution and throughput requirements needed to resolve fine structural defects across a multi-metre web, and define a carpet-specific defect taxonomy.We then lay out a staged modelling strategy that begins with unsupervised anomaly detection trained on defect-free material, following the paradigm exemplified by the carpet category of the MVTec Anomaly Detection benchmark, and matures through a human-in-the-loop annotation flywheel into supervised detection and segmentation models. Finally, we connect detection performance to the DMAIC objectives, showing how reductions in escaped defects translate into improved process quality and process sigma levels. The contribution is an end-to-end, deployable blueprint that treats data collection as a first-class engineering objective rather than an afterthought.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an in-line machine-vision system design for carpet quality inspection, integrating line-scan cameras with dual illumination, a staged modeling approach from unsupervised anomaly detection to supervised segmentation, and a DMAIC-driven framework that treats data collection as a core engineering objective for continuous model improvement.
AI.motivation: Current visual inspection in carpet production is slow, subjective, and inconsistent at industrial line speeds/widths, leading to high defect rates and financial risk—especially acute given an anticipated bottleneck from adding new weaving machines.
AI.method: The system uses synchronized line-scan cameras with bright-field and grazing illumination, defines a carpet-specific defect taxonomy, and employs a staged modeling strategy: starting with unsupervised anomaly detection (e.g., MVTec AD paradigm), then evolving through human-in-the-loop annotation into supervised detection and segmentation models.
AI.result: Derives resolution and throughput requirements for detecting fine structural defects across multi-metre webs, and connects defect detection performance to DMAIC quality metrics, showing how escaped defect reductions improve process sigma levels.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is an end-to-end, deployable blueprint that treats data collection as a first-class engineering objective, enabling continuous model improvement over the installation's lifespan.

title: An Exploratory Study into using Machine-Learning for Fast Step-by-step Emulation of Numerical Mechanical Thrombectomy Simulations for Ischemic Stroke
comment: 40 pages, 16 figures, master thesis artificial intelligence
summary: The treatment of ischemic stroke using mechanical thrombectomy involves difficult decisions under intense time constraints. Numerical physics simulations can in theory inform operators to make better decisions regarding treatment approaches and device selection, but are too slow to do so in practice. In this thesis, we investigate if current machine learning based surrogates can accurately emulate these simulations in a step-by-step manner while making them significantly faster. To do this we train three surrogate models on two simulations that involve a simplified aspiration procedure, with varying levels of geometric complexity. Our results show that two of our models accurately predict singular simulation steps and provide substantial speedups, especially when combined with specific data augmentations. However, the models showed a lack of stability when emulating simulations with complex geometries over longer time periods. Overall, this work provides a foundation for future studies to develop stable methods that scale to realistic numerical physics simulations of mechanical thrombectomy.

AI.tldr: This thesis explores using machine learning surrogates to speed up numerical physics simulations of mechanical thrombectomy for ischemic stroke treatment. Three models were trained on simplified aspiration simulations, showing accurate single-step predictions with significant speedups, but instability over longer timeframes in complex geometries.
AI.motivation: Mechanical thrombectomy for ischemic stroke requires rapid decision-making under time pressure. Numerical physics simulations could help guide operators, but they are too slow for real-time clinical use.
AI.method: Three machine learning surrogate models were trained on two simplified aspiration simulations with varying geometric complexity. Data augmentations were employed to improve performance.
AI.result: Two models accurately predicted single simulation steps with substantial speedups. However, models showed instability when emulating longer simulations with complex geometries.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundation for developing stable ML-based surrogates that could scale to realistic numerical physics simulations of mechanical thrombectomy in the future.

title: Consistency evaluation of benchmarks used for causal discovery
summary: In graphical causal model, causal discovery aims to construct a causal graph based on numerical data and domain knowledge in plain text. However, the evaluation of causal discovery methods remains a challenge in the area as the progress of domain researches often makes benchmark causal graphs contain mis-aligned knowledge. This problem especially affects the evaluation of large language model (LLM) based causal discovery methods as they are sensitive to the new discoveries in the literature. This work is the first to systematically study the quality of benchmark causal graphs. Specifically, we design a pipeline that automatically retrieves relevant research papers from scientific databases, and prompts LLMs to check the consistency between the benchmark causal graphs and domain research papers. We evaluate 11 popular real-world benchmarks, for which our pipeline in total proceeds 38,081 domain papers. Our results show that popular benchmarks vary significantly in their consistency with domain research, with clear implications for causal discovery research.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates the quality of benchmark causal graphs used in causal discovery research, finding significant inconsistencies between these benchmarks and domain research literature, which especially impacts LLM-based causal discovery methods.
AI.motivation: Benchmark causal graphs often contain mis-aligned knowledge due to evolving domain research, and this problem is particularly acute for LLM-based causal discovery methods that are sensitive to new literature. No prior work has systematically studied benchmark quality.
AI.method: The authors design an automated pipeline that retrieves relevant research papers from scientific databases and prompts LLMs to check consistency between benchmark causal graphs and domain research papers. They evaluate 11 popular real-world benchmarks using 38,081 domain papers.
AI.result: Popular benchmarks vary significantly in their consistency with domain research, with clear implications for causal discovery research.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark causal graphs have substantial quality issues that should be considered when evaluating causal discovery methods, especially LLM-based approaches.

title: Ask4VG: Risk-Aware Question Selection for Reducing Prior-Driven Answers in Medical VQA
summary: Medical visual question answering requires models to ground their responses in image evidence, because visually unsupported answers can mislead downstream interpretation. However, many medical VQA questions are generic, template-like, or highly similar in form, which can encourage models to learn question-answer shortcuts instead of image-dependent reasoning and thereby increase the risk of hallucinated responses. We propose Ask4VG, a label-free pilot framework for risk-aware question selection. Ask4VG estimates question-induced hallucination risk through counterfactual visual probing: the same question is asked under the original image, a perturbed image, a blank image, and a mismatched image, and the resulting answer relations are converted into weak supervision for a counterfactual risk estimator. The learned estimator then reranks candidate question rewrites to favor intent-preserving questions that are less invariant to missing or mismatched visual evidence before final answer generation. On VQA-RAD with Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct, prompt-only rewriting increases counterfactual risk, whereas predicted-risk reranking reduces held-out risk from 0.658 to 0.623 and improves exact accuracy from 0.337 to 0.356. A 300-sample PMC-VQA external check shows the same direction of risk reduction with a small accuracy gain. These results suggest that question selection is a promising complement to response-level hallucination mitigation for reliable medical VQA.

AI.tldr: Ask4VG proposes a label-free framework that estimates question-induced hallucination risk via counterfactual visual probing (original, perturbed, blank, mismatched images) and reranks question rewrites to favor those that depend on visual evidence, reducing hallucination risk and improving accuracy in medical VQA.
AI.motivation: Medical VQA models often exploit question-answer shortcuts and hallucinate responses due to generic or template-like questions. The authors aim to mitigate this by selecting questions that genuinely depend on visual evidence, not by fixing answers but by proactively filtering risky questions.
AI.method: Ask4VG uses counterfactual visual probing: the same question is asked under original, perturbed, blank, and mismatched images. The answer relations are used as weak supervision to train a counterfactual risk estimator, which reranks candidate question rewrites to favor intent-preserving but visually-grounded variants before generating final answers.
AI.result: On VQA-RAD with Qwen2-VL-2B-Instruct, prompt-only rewriting increases counterfactual risk, while predicted-risk reranking reduces held-out risk from 0.658 to 0.623 and improves exact accuracy from 0.337 to 0.356. A 300-sample PMC-VQA external check shows consistent risk reduction with a small accuracy gain.
AI.conclusion: Question selection is a promising complement to response-level hallucination mitigation for reliable medical VQA, as addressing question-induced hallucination risk at the input stage can improve both grounding and accuracy.

title: Accuracy, Stability, and Repeated-Run Reliability of Large Language Models on Deterministic Programming Tasks
summary: Run-level pass rate overstates retry-free coverage by up to 17.8 percentage points -- and the gap is largest precisely for mid-performing systems. We investigate this accuracy--stability relationship in large language model (LLM) evaluation for deterministic text-conditioned generation, using programming tasks as a concrete testbed. Standard code-generation benchmarks emphasize single-run accuracy or eventual success under repeated sampling, but many deployment settings also require stability: consistent outcomes across repeated invocations under the same task description. We present a repeated-run evaluation protocol with metrics for run-level accuracy, retry-free coverage, and per-problem variability. On a recency-based benchmark of 100 LeetCode-style problems, we evaluate 16 models from five provider families under two prompt templates with five repeated runs per problem, yielding 16,000 evaluation instances. Although run-level pass rate and perfect stability rate are strongly correlated (r=0.985), pass rate consistently exceeds retry-free coverage -- a gap that reaches 17.8 percentage points and reverses model rankings even among closely matched systems. Prompt effects are model-dependent rather than uniformly beneficial. These results suggest that repeated-run stability analysis is a necessary complement to conventional accuracy reporting for deterministic text-conditioned generation tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that standard single-run pass rates overestimate LLM code generation reliability by up to 17.8 percentage points, especially for mid-performing models, and proposes repeated-run stability analysis as a necessary complement to conventional accuracy metrics.
AI.motivation: Standard code-generation benchmarks focus on single-run accuracy or eventual success under repeated sampling, but many deployment settings also require consistent outcomes across repeated invocations under the same task description (stability). The authors investigate this accuracy-stability gap in LLM evaluation.
AI.method: The authors present a repeated-run evaluation protocol with metrics for run-level accuracy, retry-free coverage, and per-problem variability. They evaluate 16 models from five provider families on 100 LeetCode-style problems using two prompt templates with five repeated runs per problem (16,000 evaluation instances).
AI.result: Run-level pass rate and perfect stability rate are strongly correlated (r=0.985), but pass rate consistently exceeds retry-free coverage by up to 17.8 percentage points. The gap reverses model rankings even among closely matched systems. Prompt effects are model-dependent rather than uniformly beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Repeated-run stability analysis is a necessary complement to conventional accuracy reporting for deterministic text-conditioned generation tasks.

title: OctoT2I: A Self-Evolving Agentic Text-to-Image Router
summary: The explosive growth of Text-to-Image (T2I) models, from large-scale versions to lightweight, real-time ones, now faces diminishing marginal returns from single-model scaling. Agentic T2I methods emerged to alleviate this bottleneck by using multiple models. However, existing agentic T2I methods suffer from three key challenges: reliance on expensive handcrafted priors or human annotations, rigid single-path decision mechanisms, and a neglect of inference efficiency. To address these challenges, we introduce OctoT2I, a novel agentic framework that reformulates the T2I task as a joint optimization of generation quality and inference efficiency. OctoT2I implements a stateful, multi-round routing strategy that adaptively selects the most suitable tool based on its knowledge and memory. This strategy is enabled by a knowledge base built from scratch by our novel Self-Evolving Mechanism. This mechanism, which requires no human supervision, first autonomously defines foundational Conceptual Dimensions (eg, style, color, count) and then intelligently explores their combinations via an iterative" Propose--Solve--Evaluate--Learn"(PSEL) loop. The PSEL loop efficiently discovers each tool's capability frontier, driving continuous improvement without external guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OctoT2I achieves competitive performance (0.96) on GenEval while delivering a 90.3% inference speedup and a 56.6% energy-efficiency gain over the leading baseline (Flow-GRPO), striking an exceptional balance between performance and efficiency. Code and models will be made available.

AI.tldr: OctoT2I is a novel agentic framework for Text-to-Image generation that jointly optimizes generation quality and inference efficiency via a self-evolving, multi-round routing strategy, achieving competitive GenEval scores (0.96) with 90.3% speedup and 56.6% energy gain over Flow-GRPO.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic T2I methods suffer from expensive handcrafted priors/human annotations, rigid single-path decision mechanisms, and neglect of inference efficiency. OctoT2I aims to address these bottlenecks.
AI.method: OctoT2I uses a stateful, multi-round routing strategy with a knowledge base built via a Self-Evolving Mechanism that autonomously defines Conceptual Dimensions (style, color, count) and explores their combinations through an iterative PSEL (Propose-Solve-Evaluate-Learn) loop.
AI.result: Achieves 0.96 on GenEval, 90.3% inference speedup, and 56.6% energy-efficiency gain over Flow-GRPO.
AI.conclusion: OctoT2I strikes an exceptional balance between performance and efficiency, eliminating the need for human supervision in agentic T2I pipelines.

title: Decoupled Residual Denoising Diffusion Models for Unified and Data Efficient Image-to-Image Translation
summary: We propose Decoupled Residual Denoising Diffusion models (DRDD) for unified and data-efficient image-to-image (I2I) translation. While diffusion models have advanced I2I translation in terms of quality and diversity, we uncover a previously under-explored property in diffusion models. Crucially, beyond its conventional role of manifold lifting (i.e., moving data off low-dimensional manifolds), injecting Gaussian noise facilitates domain harmonization by implicitly aligning feature distributions across domains, a property particularly advantageous for unified I2I translation. However, existing diffusion models prematurely erode this harmonization effect, as noise and residuals are simultaneously removed in a single coupled diffusion process. To address this, DRDD decouples the diffusion process into two sequential and independent diffusion stages: (1) a stochastic noise diffusion for domain harmonization and manifold lifting, and (2) a deterministic residual diffusion that learns the core semantic mapping entirely within the fixed-noise domain. This decoupling preserves harmonization and manifold lifting effects throughout the transformation, substantially simplifying the learning of unified mappings across diverse tasks and domains. Notably, the noise diffusion stage is trained exclusively on abundant, unpaired target-domain images, greatly improving data efficiency. Comprehensive theoretical and empirical analysis demonstrates that DRDD is broadly compatible with mainstream diffusion models and consistently delivers robust, unified I2I translation, even under limited paired data. Our code is available at https://github.com/HKU-HealthAI/DRDD.

AI.tldr: DRDD proposes a decoupled diffusion process (noise diffusion + residual diffusion) for unified I2I translation, achieving better domain harmonization, data efficiency, and performance under limited paired data.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion models for I2I translation prematurely erode the domain harmonization effect because noise and residuals are removed together in a single coupled process. The authors identify that Gaussian noise injection, beyond manifold lifting, also aligns feature distributions across domains—a property under-exploited in prior work.
AI.method: DRDD decouples the diffusion into two stages: (1) stochastic noise diffusion for domain harmonization and manifold lifting (trained on unpaired target-domain images), and (2) deterministic residual diffusion for learning core semantic mappings entirely within the fixed-noise domain.
AI.result: DRDD is broadly compatible with mainstream diffusion models, delivers robust and unified I2I translation, and shows strong data efficiency even with limited paired data.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling noise and residual diffusion preserves harmonization and simplifies unified mapping learning across diverse tasks and domains, offering a data-efficient paradigm for I2I translation.

title: Task Structure Reverses Layerwise State Encoding in Sequence Models
summary:
Mechanistic studies of sequence models often treat layerwise state encodings as architectural traits: recurrent models concentrate readable state, attention-based models distribute it. We find that the same architecture reverses this profile when the task changes. Across Transformers, Mamba, Mamba-2, LSTMs, and GRUs, Parity is concentrated late in Mamba and the recurrent baselines and built gradually by Transformer; on bounded-depth Dyck-k the pattern flips. The same flip appears in fine-tuned Mamba-130M and Pythia-160M, and the Pythia Dyck bottleneck persists at 410M. Two explanations are conflated in the literature: algebraic structure (commutativity) versus computational structure (prefix update vs. stack). To separate them we add a third task: non-commutative S_3 permutation composition. S_3 groups with Parity, not Dyck, on layerwise probing across all five architectures and on Mamba-specific Conv1D attribution, so the grouping tracks computational structure rather than commutativity.
  Causal interventions show that, in the 4-layer formal models, linearly readable directions are often functionally necessary and can remain important at out-of-distribution lengths on Parity and Dyck. At pretrained scale the picture splits. Fine-tuned Pythia Dyck has a strong middle-layer bottleneck (L6-L7 ablation drops accuracy by roughly 81% at 160M; broader L4-L18 plateau at 410M), far weaker at the best-probe layer. Pretrained Mamba shows the complementary failure mode: its final layer is highly readable, no single probe direction breaks the task on Parity, Dyck, or S_3, yet mid-position activation patching there recovers about 97-98% of the clean-corrupted logit gap. Probing localizes where state is linearly available, not always where the computation is bottlenecked. Mechanistic signatures are properties of architecture and task together.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that whether models concentrate readable state information in early or late layers depends on the task, not just the architecture. The authors disentangle commutative algebra vs. computational structure (prefix update vs. stack) by adding a non-commutative S₃ task, finding that computational structure drives the pattern. Causal interventions reveal that probing localizes linearly available state but not necessarily computational bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: To resolve conflated explanations for why different architectures show different patterns of layerwise state encoding—specifically whether the grouping is driven by algebraic commutativity or computational structure (prefix update vs. stack).
AI.method: Layerwise probing across Transformers, Mamba, Mamba-2, LSTMs, and GRUs on three tasks: Parity (commutative, prefix-update), bounded-depth Dyck-k (non-commutative, stack-like), and non-commutative S₃ permutation composition (prefix-update but non-commutative). Causal interventions (ablation, activation patching) at both small formal models and pretrained scale (Mamba-130M, Pythia-160M/410M).
AI.result: Parity concentrates state late in Mamba/recurrent baselines but builds gradually in Transformer; Dyck-k flips this pattern. S₃ groups with Parity (not Dyck) across all architectures, showing computational structure drives the profile. Causal interventions show probe-visible directions are often functionally necessary in small models but at pretrained scale, probing and bottlenecks misalign: Pythia has a strong middle-layer bottleneck for Dyck; Mamba's final layer is highly readable but no single probe direction is critical; mid-layer patching recovers ~97-98% of logit gap.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic signatures are jointly determined by architecture and task. Probing reveals where state is linearly available, not necessarily where computation bottlenecks. Computational structure (prefix update vs. stack) better explains layerwise encoding patterns than commutativity.

title: Token Predictors Are Not Planners: Building Physically Grounded Causal Reasoners
summary: Current benchmarks for embodied vision-language planning often favor linguistic next-token prediction over physically grounded next-state reasoning. This rewards models that mimic statistical language priors rather than track causal dependencies, reducing physical planning to shallow sequence modeling. We argue that reliable physical autonomy requires a shift from linguistically grounded token prediction toward physically grounded causal reasoning. To this end, we introduce Causal-Plan-Bench, a high-fidelity diagnostic suite curated through multi-stage verification to evaluate embodied planning across four causal dimensions. We also construct Causal-Plan-1M, a million-scale corpus of explicit reasoning traces produced by a four-stage annotation pipeline over egocentric videos. Extensive evaluation shows that leading models still struggle to demonstrate genuine physical agency, with Gemini 3 Pro reaching only 38.18 on our benchmark. In contrast, our training recipe enables Causal Planner, built on Qwen3-VL-8B, to internalize physical logic for more accurate next-state estimation. The model achieves strong in-domain performance and cross-benchmark generalization, and reveals a Causal Scaling Law: scaling causal training data to one million instances yields a 36.3% relative gain, from 33.22 to 45.28. Overall, our work provides a concrete step toward turning agents from superficial token predictors into physically grounded causal reasoners.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that embodied vision-language planning benchmarks over-reward linguistic pattern matching rather than genuine physical reasoning. To fix this, the authors introduce Causal-Plan-Bench (a benchmark testing four causal dimensions) and Causal-Plan-1M (a million-scale reasoning trace dataset). Their model, Causal Planner (built on Qwen3-VL-8B), achieves strong results and reveals a Causal Scaling Law: scaling data to 1M instances yields a 36.3% relative gain.
AI.motivation: Current embodied planning benchmarks favor linguistic next-token prediction over physically grounded causal reasoning, causing models to mimic statistical language priors instead of understanding true causal dependencies. This reduces physical planning to shallow sequence modeling, hindering genuine physical autonomy.
AI.method: The authors introduce Causal-Plan-Bench (a diagnostic suite with multi-stage verification across four causal dimensions) and Causal-Plan-1M (a million-scale corpus of explicit reasoning traces from egocentric videos via a four-stage annotation pipeline). They train Causal Planner on Qwen3-VL-8B using this data.
AI.result: Leading models struggle, with Gemini 3 Pro reaching only 38.18 on the benchmark. Causal Planner achieves strong in-domain and cross-benchmark performance. A Causal Scaling Law is observed: scaling to 1M instances yields a 36.3% relative gain (from 33.22 to 45.28).
AI.conclusion: The work provides a concrete step toward transitioning agents from superficial token predictors into physically grounded causal reasoners, emphasizing the need for causal reasoning over linguistic imitation in embodied planning.

title: TextFake: Benchmarking AI-Generated Image Detection on Text-Rich Images
summary: Recent AI-generated image (AIGI) detectors perform well on natural-image benchmarks, but their behavior on text-rich forgeries, such as fabricated screenshots, documents, and news pages prevalent in misinformation, remains untested. We introduce TextFake, a 20,000-image benchmark for text-rich AIGI detection spanning 28 languages, 4 topic categories, and 2 scene modalities. Fake images are synthesized via a four-stage pipeline that annotates real images along three controlled dimensions and generates counterparts through distribution-aligned structured prompting, ruling out covariate shortcuts. Zero-shot evaluation of 14 specialized detectors and 3 frontier VLM APIs reveals a large systematic gap: no method exceeds 80% accuracy, with some dropping over 60% from natural-image benchmarks. Diagnostic evaluations identify three failure modes: the Text Density Curse, where dense glyphs overwhelm low-level detectors; Cloaking via Rendering Fidelity, where stronger text rendering suppresses enerative artifacts; and Threshold Collapse, where routine perturbations drive detectors toward chance-level performance.

AI.tldr: TextFake benchmark reveals that current AI-generated image detectors severely underperform (≤80% accuracy) on text-rich forgeries across 28 languages, with three identified failure modes: text density, rendering fidelity, and perturbation vulnerability.
AI.motivation: Current AIGI detectors are evaluated on natural images but their effectiveness on text-rich forgeries (screenshots, documents, news pages) used in misinformation remains unknown, creating a critical blind spot.
AI.method: Constructed TextFake (20K images, 28 languages) via a four-stage pipeline: real image annotation along three controlled dimensions → distribution-aligned structured prompting → synthetic generation → covariate shortcut removal. Evaluated 14 specialized detectors + 3 frontier VLMs under zero-shot settings.
AI.result: No detector exceeds 80% accuracy; some drop >60% from natural-image benchmarks. Three failure modes identified: (1) Text Density Curse, (2) Cloaking via Rendering Fidelity, (3) Threshold Collapse under routine perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Existing AIGI detectors have a systematic blind spot for text-rich forgeries, necessitating new detection paradigms beyond natural-image-focused benchmarks.

title: Cellular Sheaf Neural Operators for Structure-Preserving Surrogate Modeling of Constrained PDEs
summary: Neural operators provide fast surrogate models for PDE simulations, but standard architectures often treat geometry and discretization as secondary to field data. Physical states are usually represented as grid-channel stacks, even when different quantities naturally belong on vertices, edges, faces, cells, boundaries, or interfaces and must satisfy compatibility constraints. We propose Cellular Sheaf Neural Operators, a discretization-aware framework for structure-preserving neural PDE surrogates. The method represents PDE states on oriented cell complexes, couples local feature spaces through learned restriction maps, and uses incidence/Hodge-informed message passing to follow computational geometry. Learned update heads pass through coboundary or flux maps, allowing selected constraints to arise from cell-complex structure rather than only from loss penalties. For magnetohydrodynamics, this yields face-based magnetic-flux updates driven by edge electromotive fields and finite-volume-style fluid updates driven by learned face fluxes and cell sources. On turbulent MHD and fusion-equilibrium surrogate tasks, the method improves structure-sensitive diagnostics, including rollout behavior, divergence control, spectral error, and equilibrium-regression accuracy. These results indicate that cellular-sheaf structure is a useful inductive bias for neural PDE surrogates in constrained multiphysics systems.

AI.tldr: Cellular Sheaf Neural Operators are proposed as a discretization-aware, structure-preserving framework for neural PDE surrogates that uses oriented cell complexes and Hodge-informed message passing to enforce compatibility constraints, demonstrating improved diagnostics on turbulent MHD and fusion-equilibrium tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard neural operators treat geometry and discretization as secondary, representing physical states as grid-channel stacks even when quantities belong on different mesh elements (vertices, edges, faces, cells) and must satisfy compatibility constraints.
AI.method: Represents PDE states on oriented cell complexes, couples local feature spaces via learned restriction maps, uses incidence/Hodge-informed message passing, and employs coboundary/flux map-based update heads to embed structure constraints.
AI.result: On turbulent MHD and fusion-equilibrium surrogate tasks, the method improves rollout behavior, divergence control, spectral error, and equilibrium-regression accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Cellular-sheaf structure provides a useful inductive bias for neural PDE surrogates in constrained multiphysics systems.

title: CAPF: Guiding Search-Agent Rollouts with Credit-Attenuated Privileged Feedback
summary: Recent LLM search agents use reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to learn search-augmented reasoning from outcome rewards. On hard problems, these agents rarely sample end-to-end successful rollouts, leaving outcome-only RLVR with few positive-reward trajectories. We argue that improving learning on such problems requires additional guidance during training, and RLVR already contains verifier-side information that can provide it. This information can identify errors or omissions in the agent's submitted answer and guide revision within the rollout. We propose a training-time mechanism called \textbf{Credit-Attenuated Privileged Feedback} (CAPF), which makes this verifier-side information available through a Privileged Feedback call during training. CAPF lets the policy revise zero-reward attempts into positive-reward repair trajectories and attenuates credit for the feedback call and earlier actions to accommodate deployment without this call. Empirical research demonstrates that CAPF improves Qwen3-4B's average exact-match score from 44.7% under outcome-only RLVR to 48.5% on seven open-domain QA benchmarks.

AI.tldr: CAPF improves RLVR training for LLM search agents by providing verifier-side feedback to guide revision of zero-reward rollouts, boosting Qwen3-4B's average exact-match score from 44.7% to 48.5% on seven open-domain QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: On hard problems, LLM search agents trained with outcome-only RLVR rarely sample successful end-to-end rollouts, leaving the reward signal too sparse to learn effectively. The authors argue that additional guidance during training is needed.
AI.method: CAPF (Credit-Attenuated Privileged Feedback) makes verifier-side information available through a Privileged Feedback call during training, allowing the policy to revise zero-reward attempts into positive-reward repair trajectories. Credit is attenuated for the feedback call and earlier actions to maintain consistency with deployment when this call is absent.
AI.result: On seven open-domain QA benchmarks, CAPF improves Qwen3-4B's average exact-match score from 44.7% (outcome-only RLVR) to 48.5%.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging verifier-side information during training via CAPF significantly enhances LLM search agents' performance on hard problems where outcome-only RLVR struggles due to sparse reward signals.

title: 3DCodeBench: Benchmarking Agentic Procedural 3D Modeling Via Code
comment: Project Page: https://www.3dcodebench.com/; 11 pages (main), with appendix
summary: Procedural 3D modeling through code is emerging as a versatile paradigm, offering deterministic, engine-ready, and precisely editable assets that neural 3D generators inherently lack. Authoring such procedural content, however, demands deep expertise in 3D software APIs, parametric design, and code-level geometric reasoning. In this paper, we propose 3DCodeBench, a systematic benchmark for evaluating vision-language model (VLM) agents for procedural 3D generation in 3D modeling software. Specifically, 3DCodeBench evaluates how effectively 12 advanced VLMs can serve as procedural 3D modelers by translating text and image references into procedural code for 3D modeling software. Recognizing that automated metrics may not fully capture the perceptual quality of 3D shapes, we build 3DCodeArena, a ranking platform based on pairwise human preferences over generated 3D outputs. From extensive evaluations and results, we observe that: (1) Failures mostly arise from API mismatches, while successful renders still suffer from disconnected or floating 3D geometric components. (2) Test-time scaling, such as higher thinking budgets and multi-turn refinement, improves performance overall. Our findings highlight a critical need for high-quality procedural coding data to advance commercial VLMs. Furthermore, effective procedural 3D modeling requires a robust execution environment that provides high-fidelity feedback for iterative refinement. We release 3DCodeBench, including the curated large-scale dataset of multimodal (text/image) prompts, procedural code, 3D object triplets, evaluation protocol, and the public 3DCodeArena platform as a foundational toolkit for exploring VLM-based procedural 3D modelers.

AI.tldr: 3DCodeBench is a benchmark evaluating 12 VLMs on procedural 3D modeling via code generation, with a human-preference ranking platform (3DCodeArena). Key findings: failures mainly from API mismatches and geometric issues; test-time scaling improves performance; need for high-quality coding data and robust execution feedback.
AI.motivation: Procedural 3D modeling through code offers deterministic, editable assets that neural methods lack, but authoring such content requires deep expertise. The paper aims to systematically evaluate whether VLMs can serve as procedural 3D modelers.
AI.method: Proposes 3DCodeBench (a benchmark with multimodal prompts, procedural code, and 3D object triplets) and 3DCodeArena (a human-preference ranking platform). Evaluates 12 advanced VLMs on translating text/image references into procedural modeling code.
AI.result: Failures mainly from API mismatches; successful renders still have disconnected/floating geometry. Test-time scaling (higher thinking budgets, multi-turn refinement) improves overall performance.
AI.conclusion: High-quality procedural coding data is critical for advancing commercial VLMs; effective modeling needs robust execution environments with high-fidelity feedback for iterative refinement. Release of 3DCodeBench and 3DCodeArena as foundational toolkits.

title: PRISM: Gauge-Invariant Tangent-Space Differentially Private LoRA
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026) as an oral presentation
summary: Applying differential privacy (DP) via DP-SGD to Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is a natural approach for privacy-preserving fine-tuning. However, LoRA's low-rank parameterization poses a fundamental challenge. In LoRA, each trainable update is represented as a low-rank matrix $Z = AB^\top$, but this factorization is inherently non-identifiable: many factor pairs $(A,B)$ represent the same update $Z$. As a result, applying DP-SGD directly to the factors induces gauge-dependent perturbations on $Z$, and we show that this naive DP-LoRA can lead to unbounded noise amplification. We propose PRISM, an intrinsic DP mechanism for LoRA that is gauge invariant by construction, avoids bilinear noise amplification, and admits an efficient low-dimensional noise sampler. Moreover, PRISM yields a closed-form characterization of the effective intrinsic noise induced on $Z$, enabling stable privacy-utility trade-offs through bounded, gauge-invariant perturbations. We establish standard $(ε,δ)$-DP guarantees for PRISM and introduce a DP-aware, gauge-invariant adaptive update rule that prevents adaptive optimization from amplifying injected privacy noise, improving numerical stability in practice.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a gauge-invariant DP mechanism for LoRA that avoids noise amplification by applying perturbations directly to the intrinsic low-rank update Z rather than its non-identifiable factor pairs (A,B), with closed-form noise characterization and DP-aware adaptive optimization.
AI.motivation: Naive DP-LoRA (applying DP-SGD to LoRA's factor matrices A,B) suffers from unbounded noise amplification due to the non-identifiable nature of low-rank factorization, where many (A,B) pairs represent the same update Z, causing gauge-dependent perturbations.
AI.method: PRISM injects DP noise directly into the intrinsic low-rank matrix Z in a gauge-invariant manner, avoiding perturbations on the factor pairs. It uses an efficient low-dimensional noise sampler and derives a closed-form characterization of the effective noise on Z.
AI.result: PRISM achieves standard (ε,δ)-DP guarantees with bounded, gauge-invariant perturbations, eliminating the unbounded noise amplification problem. The DP-aware adaptive update rule improves numerical stability.
AI.conclusion: PRISM provides a principled and practical solution for private LoRA fine-tuning by addressing the fundamental gauge-dependence issue, leading to stable privacy-utility trade-offs.

title: Evaluation of Baseline Methods for IDD-based SSD External Memory Search
comment: accepted to The 19th International Symposium on Combinatorial Search (SoCS2026)
summary: Many difficult search problems cannot be solved by algorithms such as A* using only RAM. Search algorithms which use external memory such as SSDs and HDDs with much higher capacity than RAM have been proposed in previous work, but previous work has focused on delayed duplicate detection approaches, as well as complex immediate duplicate detection (IDD) methods, and relatively simple methods for IDD have not been systematically studied. In addition, the effect of OS-level mechanisms for managing and speeding up accesses to external memory, such as page caches, has not been studied. This paper addresses these gaps in the literature by evaluating and analyzing the performance of simple baseline approaches for IDD-based A*.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates simple baseline methods for immediate duplicate detection (IDD) in A* search using external memory, and studies the impact of OS-level page caching mechanisms, filling gaps left by prior work that focused on complex IDD or delayed duplicate detection.
AI.motivation: Previous work on external-memory search algorithms focused on delayed duplicate detection and complex immediate duplicate detection methods, while simple IDD approaches and the impact of OS-level page caching mechanisms were not systematically studied.
AI.method: The paper evaluates and analyzes the performance of simple baseline approaches for immediate duplicate detection (IDD) in A* search when using external memory (SSDs, HDDs), and studies the effect of OS-level page caches on performance.
AI.result: Performance evaluation of simple IDD baseline methods for A* with external memory, including analysis of OS page cache effects.
AI.conclusion: Simple baseline methods for IDD in external-memory A* and OS page cache mechanisms merit systematic study, as they address gaps left by prior work.

title: A Multiscale Network with Supervised Contrastive Learning for Real-Time Facial Emotion Recognition
summary: Real-time emotion recognition from facial expressions is a challenging task, particularly in video-based scenarios where multiple emotional states may occur over time. The difficulty increases further due to the fact that each emotional state is associated with facial expressions that vary significantly across individuals. The change of facial expressions portraying emotional state is not discrete, but rather continuous, which is very challenging to represent through computational aids. A system with the ability to detect variations in facial expressions can have a significant impact on determining the emotional state of an individual. Such a system can be very beneficial for psychologists during counseling by providing additional insights into the emotional state of a subject. In this paper, a deep learning-based system is presented to detect emotional changes in real-time video of a person by modeling the change in facial expressions. The current study is conducted on a standard dataset for training of the deep learning system and the system has provided very satisfactory outcomes in this respect.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a deep learning-based system for real-time emotion recognition from facial expressions in video, designed to model continuous emotional changes across individuals, achieving satisfactory results on a standard dataset.
AI.motivation: Real-time emotion recognition from facial expressions is challenging due to: (1) multiple emotional states occurring over time in video, (2) high variability in facial expressions across individuals, and (3) the continuous (not discrete) nature of emotional expression changes. Such a system could benefit psychologists during counseling.
AI.method: A deep learning-based system that models changes in facial expressions to detect emotional changes in real-time video.
AI.result: The system provided very satisfactory outcomes when trained and evaluated on a standard dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning approach effectively detects emotional changes in real-time video, offering potential benefits for psychological counseling applications.

title: Silent Failures in Federated Personalization of Foundation Models
summary: Foundation models are increasingly personalized on decentralized private data through federated learning and are now deployed at scale under growing regulatory requirements for post-market monitoring. We argue that this convergence creates a distinct and under-recognized class of trustworthiness failures, which we term "Silent Failures." These include amplified bias, fairness collapse, and alignment erosion that may remain difficult to detect because federated learning's privacy constraints limit visibility into model behavior. A landscape analysis of existing benchmarks reveals a structural divide. Federated benchmarks evaluate system performance but provide limited insight into model behavior, whereas centralized trustworthiness benchmarks assess behavior but require model access incompatible with federated privacy. We introduce a taxonomy of six silent failure modes arising from the interaction of foundation model personalization, dataset shift, and core federated constraints. Our analysis shows that privacy-preserving training alone is insufficient for trustworthy deployment. We conclude with a research agenda for privacy-preserving behavioral evaluation and propose that silent failures become a standard diagnostic category for trustworthy federated artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "Silent Failures"—undetected trustworthiness issues (bias, fairness collapse, alignment erosion) that arise when foundation models are personalized via federated learning on decentralized private data. The authors highlight a divide between federated benchmarks (system-focused) and centralized trustworthiness benchmarks (behavior-focused, but privacy-incompatible). They propose a taxonomy of six silent failure modes and call for privacy-preserving behavioral evaluation methods.
AI.motivation: The convergence of personalized foundation models via federated learning and growing regulatory requirements for post-market monitoring creates an under-recognized class of trustworthiness failures that remain difficult to detect due to privacy constraints.
AI.method: Landscape analysis of existing benchmarks revealing a structural divide; introduction of a taxonomy of six silent failure modes arising from foundation model personalization, dataset shift, and federated constraints.
AI.result: Identifies that privacy-preserving training alone is insufficient for trustworthy deployment. Reveals a gap where federated benchmarks assess system performance but not behavior, and centralized benchmarks assess behavior but require incompatible model access.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a research agenda for privacy-preserving behavioral evaluation and recommends that "silent failures" become a standard diagnostic category for trustworthy federated AI.

title: Does Compression Preserve Uncertainty? A Unified Benchmark for Quantized and Sparse LLMs via Conformal Prediction
summary: Model compression techniques such as quantization and pruning are widely used to reduce the deployment cost of large language models (LLMs), with existing evaluations focusing almost exclusively on accuracy preservation. However, in safety-critical applications, a model's ability to reliably quantify its own uncertainty is equally important. We ask: does compression preserve this ability? To answer this question, we benchmark 12 LLMs under various compression configurations across five NLP tasks, using conformal prediction to provide a rigorous, distribution-free measure of uncertainty. Our experiments reveal that: (I) compression frequently decouples accuracy from uncertainty; (II) larger models absorb compression-induced uncertainty far more effectively than smaller ones; and (III) uncertainty inflation is often threshold-like rather than gradual. These results suggest that accuracy-only evaluation is insufficient for assessing the deployment readiness of compressed LLMs, and that uncertainty-aware benchmarking should be a standard component of model compression pipelines.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether compressing LLMs (via quantization/pruning) preserves their ability to quantify uncertainty, finding that compression often decouples accuracy from uncertainty, larger models handle compression better, and uncertainty inflation is threshold-like—concluding accuracy-only evaluation is insufficient for deployment readiness.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of compressed LLMs focus almost exclusively on accuracy preservation, ignoring whether compression preserves the model's ability to reliably quantify its own uncertainty—a critical capability for safety-critical applications.
AI.method: Benchmarked 12 LLMs under various compression configurations (quantization and pruning) across five NLP tasks, using conformal prediction to provide a rigorous, distribution-free measure of uncertainty.
AI.result: (I) Compression frequently decouples accuracy from uncertainty; (II) larger models absorb compression-induced uncertainty more effectively than smaller ones; (III) uncertainty inflation is often threshold-like rather than gradual.
AI.conclusion: Accuracy-only evaluation is insufficient for assessing deployment readiness of compressed LLMs; uncertainty-aware benchmarking should be a standard component of model compression pipelines.

title: Chameleon: Style-Content Disentangled Framework for Cross-Domain Object Compositing
comment: The last two authors are co-corresponding authors. Please visit our project page at https://cmlab-korea.github.io/Chameleon/
summary: Image compositing aims to seamlessly insert a foreground object into a background image, and recent advances in diffusion models have significantly enhanced the quality, especially when the foreground and background images come from the same domain (e.g., natural images). However, cross-domain compositing, where the foreground and background come from different domains, is relatively underexplored and remains challenging because the model must preserve the foreground object's identity while stylizing it to match the background domain. Existing cross-domain compositing approaches largely rely on training-free blending and refinement strategies. This is partly due to the lack of large-scale paired datasets for cross-domain compositing, limiting the development of training-based solutions. As a result, they are limited to tone-level alignment and often produce style-inconsistent or overstylized results. To overcome such limitations, we construct ChameleonDataset, the first large-scale training dataset for cross-domain compositing, with a comprehensive evaluation benchmark, built through a scalable data construction pipeline. Building on this, we propose Chameleon, a novel two-stage training-based cross-domain compositing framework. In the first stage, we propose Joint Hard Contrastive Learning (JHCL) to train ChameleonEncoder, which effectively disentangles style and content representations. In the second stage, we introduce Spatio-Temporal Attention Gating (STAG) into a diffusion transformer for effective stylization, adaptively regulating how style tokens from the first-stage encoder are injected across spatial and temporal dimensions. Our method outperforms state-of-the-art in-domain and cross-domain compositing models, sequential pipelines and commercial models, achieving improvements in both compositional plausibility and stylistic fidelity.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses cross-domain image compositing by constructing the first large-scale training dataset (ChameleonDataset) and proposing a two-stage framework (Chameleon) with Joint Hard Contrastive Learning (JHCL) for disentangling style/content and Spatio-Temporal Attention Gating (STAG) for adaptive stylization, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Cross-domain compositing (foreground and background from different domains) is underexplored and challenging due to lack of large-scale paired training datasets. Existing training-free methods are limited to tone-level alignment and produce style-inconsistent or overstylized results.
AI.method: 1) Constructs ChameleonDataset, the first large-scale training dataset for cross-domain compositing via a scalable data pipeline. 2) Proposes Chameleon framework with two stages: Stage 1 uses Joint Hard Contrastive Learning (JHCL) to train ChameleonEncoder for disentangling style and content; Stage 2 introduces Spatio-Temporal Attention Gating (STAG) into a diffusion transformer to adaptively regulate style token injection across spatial and temporal dimensions.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art in-domain and cross-domain compositing models, sequential pipelines, and commercial models, with improvements in both compositional plausibility and stylistic fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and two-stage training framework effectively address cross-domain compositing limitations, enabling better style-content disentanglement and adaptive stylization for high-quality results.

title: Explainable deep reinforcement learning reveals energy-efficient control strategies for turbulent drag reduction
summary: We propose a method combining Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MARL) and eXplainable Deep Learning (XDL) to reduce drag in wall-bounded turbulent flows. Taking as a baseline the results of training agents directly targeting wall-shear stress and opposition control, three SHAP-guided approaches are compared. In the first, the reward is computed from SHAP attributions of a U-net predicting the future velocity field; in the second, from SHAP attributions of a U-net predicting the skin-friction coefficient; in the third, from a combination of SHAP attributions of two U-nets predicting the skin-friction coefficient and the wall pressure fluctuations, respectively. The combined SHAP strategy based on skin-friction coefficient and wall-pressure fluctuations achieves the best overall performance, achieving a DR of 34.44% and a NES of 34.01% with only 0.43% normalized input power. Relative to opposition control, drag reduction and net energy saving increase by 49.41% and 48.52%, respectively. Compared with the direct wall-shear-stress baseline, the proposed strategy simultaneously improves performance while reducing the normalized actuation cost from 5.90% to 0.43%. Analysis of the results reveals that the energetically efficient policy is consistent with pressure-gated actuation, activating predominantly at near-zero wall pressure, and operates on a temporal timescale comparable to the lifetime of the near-wall turbulent structures.

AI.tldr: A MARL + XDL (SHAP-guided) method for turbulent flow drag reduction achieves 34.44% DR and 34.01% NES with only 0.43% normalized input power, using combined SHAP attributions from skin-friction and wall-pressure U-net predictors.
AI.motivation: To reduce drag in wall-bounded turbulent flows more efficiently than existing opposition control methods, by leveraging interpretable deep learning to guide reinforcement learning agents.
