AI.motivation: Synchronous RL methods like GRPO suffer from stragglers—single slow rollouts that delay the entire group, worsening with larger group sizes. This creates a trade-off between group-size benefits and synchronization stall costs.
AI.method: SAGC formulates group-size selection as an online constrained optimization problem, dynamically adjusting the training group size based on observed rollout behavior to control straggler events while retaining larger-group benefits.
AI.result: SAGC consistently reduces straggler incidence and improves wall-clock efficiency across GRPO and DAPO training, on both vanilla and engineered baselines, achieving competitive or better training reward and downstream reasoning benchmark performance, often with shorter outputs.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic group control is a practical way to make synchronous on-policy RL more efficient and robust, improving both training speed and final model quality.

title: Multi-modal Video Representation Alignment for Robust Self-supervised Driver Distraction Detection
summary: Robust self-supervised learning of multi-modal video representations is critical for real-world applications such as driver distraction detection, where multiple sensors provide complementary but noisy signals. Conventional contrastive objectives, such as InfoNCE, assume all negatives are equally informative and all positives are reliable. However, this assumption is frequently violated in multi-modal data due to viewpoint changes, occlusions, or semantic overlap across modalities. In this work, we propose a novel framework for multi-modal global alignment that addresses these challenges by jointly modeling faulty negatives and unreliable or faulty positives. We introduce soft targets derived from cycle-consistency scores to relax the hard-negative assumption, and a weighting mechanism based on similarity distributions to mitigate the impact of noisy or faulty positives. Our approach extends traditional pairwise alignment to a principled global multi-modal setting, aggregating alignment information across all modality pairs. We evaluate our method on the Drive&Act dataset, demonstrating that it consistently outperforms both pairwise and existing global alignment baselines across RGB, IR, Depth, and Skeleton modalities. Cross-view ablation studies further show strong generalization to unseen camera perspectives, highlighting the robustness of our representations. Overall, our framework provides a scalable and effective solution for self-supervised global multi-modal representation learning, enabling reliable driver distraction detection and pioneering in real-world multi-modal video understanding. Our code will be published on GitHub.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised multi-modal video representation learning framework that handles faulty negatives and unreliable positives via cycle-consistency-based soft targets and similarity-distribution weighting, outperforming baselines on the Drive&Act dataset for driver distraction detection.
AI.motivation: Conventional contrastive learning (e.g., InfoNCE) assumes all negatives are equally informative and all positives are reliable, which is violated in multi-modal data due to viewpoint changes, occlusions, and semantic overlap across modalities.
AI.method: (1) Soft targets derived from cycle-consistency scores to relax the hard-negative assumption; (2) A weighting mechanism based on similarity distributions to mitigate noisy/faulty positives; (3) Global alignment aggregating information across all modality pairs rather than pairwise only.
AI.result: Outperforms pairwise and existing global alignment baselines on Drive&Act across RGB, IR, Depth, and Skeleton modalities; cross-view ablation shows strong generalization to unseen camera perspectives.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, effective solution for self-supervised global multi-modal representation learning, enabling reliable driver distraction detection and advancing real-world multi-modal video understanding.

title: Network Learning with Semi-relaxed Gromov-Wasserstein
summary: Estimating the generative mechanism of large-scale networks is a fundamental challenge in statistical machine learning. It requires the identification of the latent connectivity structure, which is in general an NP-hard combinatorial problem due to the absence of canonical node labels. We address this challenge by allowing for probabilistic couplings, thereby relaxing the assignment problem. Our estimation framework can be formulated as a semi-relaxed Gromov-Wasserstein objective and provides a low-dimensional representation of the generative structure. We solve this via a block-coordinate conditional gradient algorithm. Despite the relaxation, the resulting solution is typically deterministic: in fact, we show that the optimality gap between the relaxed solution and the deterministic assignment vanishes at rate $O(1/n)$, where $n$ is the number of nodes. This allows for tractable recovery of the underlying model and enables rigorous statistical analysis: we establish consistency and minimax-optimal convergence rates for both stochastic block models and Holder-smooth graphons. Our implementation scales efficiently with $n$, as demonstrated on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a semi-relaxed Gromov-Wasserstein framework for estimating latent generative mechanisms in large-scale networks, solving the NP-hard node assignment problem via probabilistic relaxation. The method achieves deterministic recovery with O(1/n) optimality gap, establishes consistency and minimax-optimal rates for SBMs and graphons, and scales efficiently.
AI.motivation: Estimating generative mechanisms of large-scale networks is fundamentally challenging due to the NP-hard combinatorial problem of identifying latent connectivity structure without canonical node labels.
AI.method: The authors formulate the estimation as a semi-relaxed Gromov-Wasserstein objective, allowing probabilistic couplings to relax the assignment problem, and solve it via a block-coordinate conditional gradient algorithm.
AI.result: The optimality gap between the relaxed solution and deterministic assignment vanishes at O(1/n) rate. The method achieves consistency and minimax-optimal convergence rates for both stochastic block models and Hölder-smooth graphons, with efficient scaling demonstrated on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed relaxation-based framework enables tractable, statistically rigorous recovery of generative network models while overcoming the NP-hard combinatorial assignment problem.

title: Do Multimodal Agents Really Benefit from Tool Use? A Systematic Study of Capability Gains
summary: Tool-augmented multimodal agents show strong benchmark gains, often taken as evidence that agents have learned to use tools. We argue that this interpretation can be premature: a tool-call trace alone does not show whether the tool supplied answer-critical information. We study two representative ``thinking with images'' agents, Thyme and DeepEyesV2, across real-world understanding, OCR, chart understanding, and mathematical reasoning. Each agent is compared with its Tool-Free counterpart and with a Pure-Text Reasoner trained from the same source pool without tool-calling trajectories. Tool access yields little consistent aggregate improvement, does not reliably reduce generated-token cost, and leaves only a small tool-only solved set: 93% of DeepEyesV2's tool-solved problems and 96% of Thyme's are also solved by at least one non-tool setting. Mechanism ablations further show that the full tool-use loop does not consistently outperform either the tool-call format or the returned execution result alone. In the settings we study, the analyzed agents appear to learn tool-calling patterns more reliably than tool-contributed capabilities, suggesting that evaluation should distinguish tool availability from whether tools actually expand what agents can solve.

AI.tldr: Tool-augmented multimodal agents show benchmark gains, but analysis of Thyme and DeepEyesV2 reveals that tool access yields little consistent improvement, with 93-96% of tool-solved problems also solved without tools, suggesting agents learn tool-calling patterns rather than genuine tool-contributed capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations interpret tool-call traces as evidence that agents have learned to use tools effectively, but this assumption may be premature since a tool-call trace does not reveal whether the tool actually supplied answer-critical information.
AI.method: The authors study Thyme and DeepEyesV2 across real-world understanding, OCR, chart understanding, and mathematical reasoning tasks, comparing each with Tool-Free counterparts and Pure-Text Reasoners. They conduct mechanism ablations to isolate tool-call format effects from actual tool-contributed information.
AI.result: Tool access yields little consistent aggregate improvement, does not reliably reduce generated-token cost, leaves only a small tool-only solved set (93% of DeepEyesV2's and 96% of Thyme's tool-solved problems are also solved by non-tool settings), and the full tool-use loop does not consistently outperform the tool-call format or execution result alone.
AI.conclusion: Evaluations should distinguish tool availability from whether tools actually expand what agents can solve, as analyzed agents appear to learn tool-calling patterns more reliably than genuine tool-contributed capabilities.

title: A Doeblin-Anchored Contrastive Chart for Learning Markov Transition Kernels
summary: Learning a Markov transition model is not merely conditional density estimation: the learned object must be a valid transition kernel before it is iterated in downstream dynamics. This paper introduces a Doeblin-anchored contrastive chart, a statistical-to-dynamical coordinate framework for learning transition kernels from contrastive objectives. Given a restart law and an anchor strength, the chart mixes the target transition with the restart law. The resulting anchored kernel is simultaneously a Doeblin-minorized Markov kernel, the positive conditional law in a binary contrastive experiment, and an explicitly invertible coordinate for the original transition law. We prove that the anchored contrastive risk identifies the anchored transition density and calibrates excess risk to density error. Since inversion of a learned score may produce a signed or unnormalized object, we introduce a measurable Markovization operator that restores kernel validity while preserving integrated $L^1$ accuracy up to a constant factor. Oracle inequalities and Hölder--ReLU approximation bounds yield nonparametric rates for independent transition pairs. For stationary geometrically $β$-mixing trajectories, a conservative thinning-and-coupling extension yields the same reconstruction interface with an effective sample size. Occupancy-weighted perturbation bounds transfer one-step kernel error to finite-horizon marginal, path-law, and occupation-measure errors under explicit coverage.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Doeblin-anchored contrastive chart framework for learning Markov transition kernels that guarantees kernel validity, with theoretical guarantees on excess risk calibration, Markovization correction, and finite-horizon error bounds under mixing conditions.
AI.motivation: Learning a Markov transition model is not merely conditional density estimation; the learned object must be a valid transition kernel before it is iterated in downstream dynamics.
AI.method: The paper proposes a Doeblin-anchored contrastive chart, which mixes the target transition with a restart law to create an anchored kernel that is simultaneously a Doeblin-minorized Markov kernel, the positive conditional law in a binary contrastive experiment, and an invertible coordinate for the original transition law. A measurable Markovization operator restores kernel validity while preserving L1 accuracy.
AI.result: Oracle inequalities and Hölder-ReLU approximation bounds yield nonparametric rates for independent transition pairs. For stationary geometrically β-mixing trajectories, a thinning-and-coupling extension achieves the same reconstruction interface with an effective sample size. Occupancy-weighted perturbation bounds transfer one-step kernel error to finite-horizon errors under explicit coverage.
AI.conclusion: The anchored contrastive risk identifies the anchored transition density and calibrates excess risk to density error, providing a principled framework for learning valid Markov transition kernels from contrastive objectives.

title: PRIMA: Boosting Animal Mesh Recovery with Biological Priors and Test-Time Adaptation
summary: We present PRIMA (*PRI*ors for *M*esh *A*daptation), a framework for robust 3D quadruped mesh recovery under severe species and pose imbalance. Existing animal reconstruction methods often regress toward mean shapes and poses due to limited 3D supervision and long-tailed species distributions, resulting in poor generalization to underrepresented animals and rare articulations. PRIMA addresses this challenge through three key contributions. First, we incorporate BioCLIP embeddings as biological priors to inject semantic and morphological knowledge into the reconstruction process, enabling more accurate and generalizable shape prediction across diverse quadrupeds. Second, we introduce a test-time adaptation (TTA) strategy that refines SMAL predictions using 2D reprojection constraints together with auxiliary keypoint guidance, improving pose and shape estimation while enabling the generation of high-quality pseudo-3D annotations from existing 2D datasets. Third, leveraging this TTA framework, we construct Quadruped3D, a large-scale pseudo-3D dataset that covers diverse species and pose variations to systematically improve model performance. Extensive experiments on Animal3D, CtrlAni3D, Quadruped2D, and Animal Kingdom demonstrate that PRIMA achieves state-of-the-art results, with particularly strong improvements on underrepresented species and challenging poses. Our results highlight the importance of biological priors and adaptation-driven data expansion for scalable and generalizable animal mesh recovery. Code is available at https://github.com/AdaptiveMotorControlLab/PRIMA.

AI.tldr: PRIMA introduces BioCLIP biological priors, test-time adaptation (TTA), and a large pseudo-3D dataset (Quadruped3D) to improve 3D quadruped mesh recovery, especially for underrepresented species and rare poses.
AI.motivation: Existing animal reconstruction methods suffer from mean-shape/pose regression due to limited 3D supervision and long-tailed species distributions, leading to poor generalization on underrepresented animals and rare articulations.
AI.method: (1) BioCLIP embeddings as biological priors for semantic/morphological knowledge; (2) Test-time adaptation (TTA) refining SMAL predictions via 2D reprojection + keypoint guidance; (3) Construction of Quadruped3D, a large-scale pseudo-3D dataset from existing 2D data.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on Animal3D, CtrlAni3D, Quadruped2D, and Animal Kingdom, with significant improvements on underrepresented species and challenging poses.
AI.conclusion: Biological priors and adaptation-driven data expansion are crucial for scalable and generalizable animal mesh recovery.

title: Why Are DMD Students Lazy? Understanding the Copying Behavior in Few-Step Distillation
summary: Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) compresses pretrained diffusion models into efficient few-step generators by aligning their noised distributions across all scales. In principle, such distribution-level supervision remains agnostic to specific noise-data pairings of the teacher; this provides the student the freedom to remap latent noise, a behavior consistently observed in low-dimensional settings. Surprisingly, we find that in high-dimensional settings, distilled students spontaneously reproduce the original noise-data pairings of the teacher, a phenomenon we term copying. We demonstrate that copying is neither a byproduct of adversarial objectives nor a result of teacher memorization. Instead, our evidence suggests that copying is an emergent property arising from the limited geometric freedom of the student model during high-dimensional distillation.

AI.tldr: Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) compresses diffusion models into efficient generators. In high-dimensional settings, distilled students spontaneously copy the teacher's noise-data pairings, an emergent property from limited geometric freedom, not adversarial objectives or memorization.
AI.motivation: To understand why distilled student models in high-dimensional settings reproduce the original noise-data pairings of the teacher model, despite distribution-level supervision being agnostic to such pairings.
AI.method: The paper analyzes DMD behavior across low and high-dimensional settings, comparing noise-data pairing reproduction patterns and investigating whether copying stems from adversarial objectives, teacher memorization, or geometric constraints of the student model.
AI.result: In high-dimensional settings, distilled students spontaneously copy the teacher's noise-data pairings, an effect not caused by adversarial objectives or teacher memorization.
AI.conclusion: Copying is an emergent property arising from the limited geometric freedom of the student model during high-dimensional distillation.

title: Honey, I Shrunk the Arc de Triomphe!
summary: Metric scale monocular geometry estimation has seen significant progress through large-scale data aggregation, yet current foundation models suffer from a persistent ''scale-collapse'' phenomenon: distant landmarks and vast landscapes are metrically underestimated. We hypothesize that this performance gap stems from a training data bottleneck, where existing metric-scale datasets are hardware-constrained to homogenous vehicle-captured LiDAR or short-range indoor scans, or consist of synthetic data that lacks the semantic complexity of the physical world. To bridge this gap, we curate a new metrically-grounded, in-the-wild dataset that we call MetricScenes, gathered from a variety of sources including Internet photo collections and stereo imagery. We estimate camera poses and initial depth maps for each scene using off-the-shelf methods, and recover absolute scale from geo-tagged metadata as well as known stereo camera baselines. We also improve the quality of depth maps derived from MetricScenes via a new two-stage Poisson completion method. Fine-tuning MoGe-2 on our dataset significantly mitigates scale-collapse and achieves superior metric accuracy in unconstrained, open-domain scenes while maintaining state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MetricScenes, a new metric-scale dataset collected from diverse sources (internet photos, stereo imagery) to address the "scale-collapse" problem in monocular depth estimation. Fine-tuning MoGe-2 on this dataset improves metric accuracy in open-domain scenes while maintaining benchmark SOTA.
AI.motivation: Current monocular geometry estimation foundation models suffer from "scale-collapse" (underestimating distant landmarks/landscapes), hypothesized to stem from a training data bottleneck: existing metric-scale datasets are either hardware-constrained (vehicle LiDAR, short-range indoor scans) or synthetic (lacking real-world semantic complexity).
AI.method: The authors curate MetricScenes, a metrically-grounded in-the-wild dataset from internet photo collections and stereo imagery. They estimate poses and initial depth maps using off-the-shelf methods, recover absolute scale from geo-tagged metadata and stereo baselines, and improve depth quality via a two-stage Poisson completion method. MoGe-2 is fine-tuned on this dataset.
AI.result: Fine-tuning MoGe-2 on MetricScenes significantly mitigates scale-collapse and achieves superior metric accuracy in unconstrained, open-domain scenes while maintaining state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and fine-tuning approach successfully addresses the scale-collapse problem in metric monocular depth estimation, enabling more accurate metric geometry in diverse real-world scenes without sacrificing benchmark performance.

title: BlockGen: Flexible Blockwise Sequence Modeling with Hybrid Samplers
summary: Is the uniform-state diffusion framework a more powerful paradigm for discrete diffusion? Recent studies indicate that this may be the case. In combination with predictor-corrector samplers, uniform-state diffusion models (USDMs) produce samples of higher-quality than masked diffusion models (MDMs), and USDMs equal or outperform MDMs in downstream tasks, even though they exhibit greater perplexity. Two issues remain unresolved. First, existing work compares uniform and masked diffusion with un-informed correctors that re-inject noise at random positions, rather than targeting tokens most likely to be wrong. Second, prior work compares full-sequence diffusion models, so we do not know whether the same conclusion holds when tokens are generated block by block. To address these issues, we introduce BlockGen, a blockwise sequence model that we instantiate with both masked and uniform diffusion. BlockGen trains on a mixture of block sizes and its likelihood interpolates between AR and pure diffusion more finely than models with a fixed block size. BlockGen enables AR-informed predictor-corrector sampling (ARPC), which combines AR and diffusion predictions to re-generate unlikely tokens without an auxiliary verifier. Under ancestral sampling, uniform outperforms masked in the block-by-block setting, especially in the few-step regime. Under ARPC, the gap closes and reverses at high NFE. With block size $16$ on GSM8K, MDMs reach slightly higher accuracy than USDMs, and we observe a similar trend in Generative Perplexity on OpenWebText. Find our code at https://github.com/jdeschena/blockgen.

AI.tldr: BlockGen introduces blockwise sequence generation combining masked and uniform diffusion, with a new AR-informed predictor-corrector sampler (ARPC) that outperforms uniform diffusion in block-by-block settings, especially at high NFE.
AI.motivation: Two unresolved issues in comparing uniform-state vs masked diffusion: (1) existing correctors re-inject noise at random positions rather than targeting likely-wrong tokens; (2) prior work only compares full-sequence diffusion, not block-by-block generation.
AI.method: Proposes BlockGen, a blockwise diffusion model trained on a mixture of block sizes. Introduces AR-informed predictor-corrector sampling (ARPC) that combines AR and diffusion predictions to re-generate unlikely tokens without an auxiliary verifier.
AI.result: Under ancestral sampling, uniform outperforms masked in block-by-block setting. Under ARPC, the gap closes and reverses at high NFE. With block size 16 on GSM8K, MDMs slightly outperform USDMs. Similar trend in Generative Perplexity on OpenWebText.
AI.conclusion: The relative performance of uniform vs masked diffusion depends on sampling method and NFE; with informed correctors like ARPC, masked diffusion can match or exceed uniform diffusion in blockwise generation.

title: Information-Theoretic Lower Bounds for Bit-Constrained Stochastic Optimization via a Reduction to Compressed Gaussian Mean Estimation
summary: Low-precision pretraining (FP8, MXFP4, NVFP4) is now standard for frontier language models, yet the literature is almost entirely achievability -- algorithms and empirical scaling laws -- with no matching characterization of what is information-theoretically possible. We study a B-bit quantized stochastic first-order oracle: an optimizer interacts for T rounds and receives, each round, a B-bit adaptive public-coin description of its stochastic gradient. Our main contribution is an exact reduction from optimizing a strongly convex quadratic family to interactively compressed Gaussian mean estimation -- under the B-bit oracle the query carries no information, so optimization collapses exactly onto a sequential distributed-estimation problem. This yields two unconditional lower bounds, a communication bound TB = Omega(d) and a statistical bound T = Omega(sigma^2 d / eps^2), and the sharp product-form bound T = Omega((sigma^2 d / eps^2) max{1, d/B}). The product form is also unconditional: a B-bit transcript carries at most O(TB / sigma^2) of Fisher trace about the mean, so bits rather than dimension limit the recoverable information, and combined with the multivariate van Trees inequality this gives the bound directly, without bounded-likelihood-ratio truncation. We give a near-matching achievability result with exact per-round bit accounting under a bounded-dynamic-range oracle, tight up to a logarithmic factor; the lower bound is for truly Gaussian (unbounded) gradients, and closing this oracle gap is left open. A sequential rate-distortion perspective extends the reduction to correlated and drifting oracles and corrects an earlier conjecture: positive noise correlation raises the bound by (1+rho)/(1-rho) rather than relaxing it. The bounds give an information-theoretic baseline for any low-bit gradient path, not an optimality claim about deployed FP4 systems.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first information-theoretic lower bounds for low-precision (B-bit) stochastic gradient optimization, showing that the product-form bound T = Ω((σ²d/ε²)·max{1, d/B}) is unconditional and near-matching achievability is possible under bounded-dynamic-range oracles, with implications for correlated and drifting gradients.
AI.motivation: Despite the widespread use of low-precision pretraining (FP8, MXFP4, NVFP4) for frontier language models, the literature only covers achievability (algorithms and empirical scaling laws) without characterizing what is information-theoretically possible. This paper aims to fill that gap by establishing fundamental limits.
AI.method: The authors propose an exact reduction from optimizing a strongly convex quadratic family to interactively compressed Gaussian mean estimation. They derive unconditional lower bounds using Fisher information and the multivariate van Trees inequality, and provide a near-matching achievability result with exact per-round bit accounting under a bounded-dynamic-range oracle.
AI.result: Key results include: (1) communication bound TB = Ω(d), (2) statistical bound T = Ω(σ²d/ε²), and (3) sharp product-form bound T = Ω((σ²d/ε²)·max{1, d/B}). The product form is unconditional, showing bits rather than dimension limit recoverable information. They also show positive noise correlation raises the bound by (1+ρ)/(1-ρ), correcting an earlier conjecture.
AI.conclusion: The bounds provide an information-theoretic baseline for any low-bit gradient path, though the authors note these are not optimality claims about deployed FP4 systems. The sequential rate-distortion perspective extends the reduction to correlated and drifting oracles, and a gap remains between lower bounds (truly Gaussian gradients) and achievability (bounded-dynamic-range oracles).

title: Explainable Forensics of Manipulated Segments in Untrimmed Long Videos
summary: The rapid advancement of AI-driven video generation has transformed content creation, while simultaneously increasing the risk of misinformation through localized manipulations in long-form videos. Existing video forensic methods predominantly operate on short, independent clips, and thus fail to capture realistic scenarios where AI-generated content is sparsely embedded within otherwise authentic footage. To bridge this gap, we formulate the task of Temporal AI-Generated Segment Localization and Explanation, which targets authenticity detection, temporal localization, and interpretable analysis of manipulated segments in untrimmed long videos. We further introduce TASLE, a large-scale benchmark comprising 12,472 untrimmed videos with diverse manipulation patterns and rich annotation signals, including temporal boundaries, authenticity labels, and segment-level rationales. In addition, we propose MSLoc, a coarse-to-fine forensic baseline that combines a boundary-sensitive proposal generation module for efficient long-video scanning with an MLLM-based refinement module for precise boundary localization and interpretable reasoning. Experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed baseline, highlighting the importance of segment-level explainable forensics for long-form AI-generated video analysis. Our dataset and code are publicly available at https://debby-0527.github.io/TASLE.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TASLE, a large-scale benchmark (12,472 untrimmed videos) and MSLoc, a coarse-to-fine baseline method for detecting, localizing, and explaining AI-generated segments within long authentic videos, addressing the gap in temporal video forensics for realistic misinformation scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing video forensic methods work on short, independent clips and fail to capture realistic scenarios where AI-generated content is sparsely embedded within otherwise authentic long-form videos, leaving a critical gap in combating misinformation.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) TASLE benchmark with 12,472 untrimmed videos annotated with temporal boundaries, authenticity labels, and segment-level rationales; and (2) MSLoc, a coarse-to-fine forensic baseline combining boundary-sensitive proposal generation for efficient long-video scanning with an MLLM-based refinement module for precise localization and interpretable reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed MSLoc baseline on the TASLE benchmark, demonstrating its capability for temporal localization and interpretable explanation of AI-generated segments in long videos.
AI.conclusion: The work highlights the importance of segment-level explainable forensics for long-form AI-generated video analysis, providing both dataset and code publicly available.

title: ArrythML: An Autoencoder-Based TinyML Approach for On-Device Arrhythmia Detection on Resource-Constrained Embedded Systems
summary:
Our work presents a method for ECG segmentation and arrhythmia detection using Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) models for real-time, on-device inference on resource-constrained embedded systems. We develop INT8 quantized autoencoder-based TinyML models with minimal layers and parameters for embedded deployment. These models are evaluated using a custom dataset derived from the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database and validated in both PC-based simulations and on-device environments.
  For the evaluations, over 95,000 ECG segments are processed on an ESP32-S3 microcontroller running the TensorFlow Lite Micro runtime. Post-evaluation, detailed analysis, including annotation-wise and record-wise failure analysis, is conducted to characterize model behavior across diverse ECG morphologies and rhythm patterns and to explain missed detections. In several cases, apparent misclassifications may correspond to early or subtle anomaly patterns labeled as normal in the reference annotations, highlighting the model's sensitivity. A refined evaluation by filtering out ambiguous cases in the dataset shows that the best-performing DNN-based autoencoder achieves a recall of 84%, an F1-score of 79%, a model size of approximately 180 KB, and an inference latency of 9 ms on-device. These results demonstrate the feasibility of low-power, privacy-preserving embedded wearable systems capable of performing accurate arrhythmia detection entirely on-device.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a TinyML-based autoencoder approach for real-time ECG segmentation and arrhythmia detection on resource-constrained embedded devices (ESP32-S3), achieving 84% recall and 79% F1-score with ~180 KB model size and 9 ms inference latency.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time, on-device arrhythmia detection on resource-constrained embedded systems for low-power, privacy-preserving wearable healthcare applications.
AI.method: INT8 quantized autoencoder-based TinyML models with minimal layers/parameters, deployed on ESP32-S3 using TensorFlow Lite Micro, evaluated on ~95,000 ECG segments from MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database.
AI.result: Best DNN-based autoencoder achieved 84% recall, 79% F1-score, ~180 KB model size, and 9 ms inference latency on-device after filtering ambiguous cases.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of low-power, privacy-preserving embedded wearable systems for accurate on-device arrhythmia detection.

title: Edge Prediction for Roof Wireframe Reconstruction with Transformers
comment: Presented at the 3rd Urban Scene Modeling (USM3D) Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: This paper presents a competitive solution to the S23DR Challenge 2026, which aims to reconstruct 3D house roof wireframe models from sparse SfM point clouds and ground-level semantic segmentations and depth maps. Our proposed method utilizes an end-to-end Transformer encoder-decoder architecture inspired by DETR. To effectively process the geometric and semantic data, the sparse SfM point cloud input is dynamically subsampled based on semantic priority and augmented with Gestalt and ADE20k class features. To further increase segmentation context, we fuse the point features with additional Gestalt feature encodings which are obtained by projecting the points into latent feature maps produced by a frozen autoencoder. Learned query embeddings are then decoded directly into 3D wireframe edges via cross-attention mechanisms. Evaluated on the "HoHo 22k" dataset, our approach significantly outperforms both handcrafted and learned baselines, achieving a Hybrid Structure Score (HSS) of 0.6476 and securing the second-highest position on the challenge's private leaderboard.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a Transformer-based method for reconstructing 3D house roof wireframes from sparse SfM point clouds and ground-level data, achieving second place in the S23DR Challenge 2026 with a Hybrid Structure Score of 0.6476.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of reconstructing 3D house roof wireframe models from sparse SfM point clouds combined with ground-level semantic and depth information.
AI.method: Uses an end-to-end Transformer encoder-decoder architecture (inspired by DETR). Input point clouds are dynamically subsampled based on semantic priority and augmented with Gestalt and ADE20k class features. Point features are fused with Gestalt feature encodings from a frozen autoencoder. Learned query embeddings are decoded into 3D wireframe edges via cross-attention.
AI.result: Achieved a Hybrid Structure Score (HSS) of 0.6476 on the HoHo 22k dataset, outperforming both handcrafted and learned baselines, and secured second place on the private leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Transformer-based method effectively combines geometric and semantic cues for 3D roof wireframe reconstruction, demonstrating significant improvement over existing baselines.

title: A combination of noise and bilateral filters achieve supralinear and scalable adversarial robustness in CNNs
comment: Main: 8 pages, 3 figures, 2 Tables. Supplement: 10 pages, 7 figures, 6 Tables
summary: The vulnerability of deep neural networks to adversarial examples poses a significant challenge for real-world deployment. Existing techniques to enhance deep network robustness rely on adversarial training, an approach that is powerful but computationally intensive and typically tailored to specific attack types. To address these limitations, existing works have explored techniques such as adding gaussian noise or filtering images, both of which can boost the network robustness to various adversarial attacks, albeit modestly. Here, we theoretically demonstrate that these two approaches enhance robustness against adversarial attacks through complementary mechanisms, resulting in supralinear robustness when combined. Building on this insight, we experimentally show that a simple preprocessor combining Gaussian noise and bilateral filtering yields supralinear improvements in adversarial robustness with minimal computational cost. Next, we combine our preprocessor with adversarial training and test on RobustBench to assess its supralinear improvement over state-of-the-art defenses. First, this combination ranks second on AutoAttack and third overall, while using only $\sim$35% of the training FLOPs, using a model with $\sim$50% less parametets, trained with $\sim$33% of the epochs and $\sim$15% the data compared to state-of-the-art defenses. Second, our method scales efficiently, matching the accuracy of competing models with roughly 2-8x less total compute across 3 orders of magnitude. Overall, our approach provides a principled and easily integrable framework for enhancing adversarial robustness, offering negligible computational overhead and a simple yet theoretically grounded design.

AI.tldr: A simple preprocessor combining Gaussian noise and bilateral filtering yields supralinear adversarial robustness improvements, and when combined with adversarial training, achieves state-of-the-art results using significantly fewer compute, parameters, epochs, and data.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial examples, and existing defenses like adversarial training are computationally intensive and attack-specific. There is a need for efficient, general robustness methods.
AI.method: The authors theoretically show Gaussian noise and bilateral filtering have complementary robustness mechanisms. They build a simple preprocessor combining both, and then combine it with adversarial training for evaluation on RobustBench.
AI.result: Combined with adversarial training, the method ranks 2nd on AutoAttack and 3rd overall on RobustBench while using ~35% training FLOPs, ~50% fewer parameters, ~33% epochs, and ~15% data. It scales efficiently with 2-8x less total compute across 3 orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled, theoretically grounded, and easily integrable framework for enhancing adversarial robustness with negligible computational overhead.

title: GC-MoE: Genomics-Guided Cell-Type-Specific Mixture of Experts for Histology-Based Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics
summary: Histology-based single-cell spatial transcriptomics (ST) estimation aims to predict gene expression for individual cells from histopathological images and cell locations, reducing the need for costly single-cell ST measurements. Unlike existing histology-to-ST methods that mainly predict spot-level profiles for local regions containing multiple cells, this task requires modeling cell-to-cell expression variability, which is strongly structured by cell type. We propose Genomics-Guided Cell-Type-Specific Mixture-of-Experts (GC-MoE), which estimates cell-type probabilities with a routing network and softly combines cell-type-specific experts for gene expression prediction. To further encode cell-type-dependent gene programs, we introduce the Cell-Type-Specific Co-Expression-Aware Predictor (CAP), together with a lightweight Cell-to-Cell Interaction Attention (C2CA) module for neighboring-cell context. Experiments and ablations on public single-cell ST datasets show consistent improvements over existing single-cell and adapted spot-level baselines.

AI.tldr: GC-MoE uses cell-type-specific mixture-of-experts with co-expression-aware prediction and cell interaction attention to estimate single-cell gene expression from histology images and cell locations.
AI.motivation: To reduce the cost of single-cell spatial transcriptomics by predicting gene expression per cell from histopathological images and cell locations, while capturing cell-to-cell expression variability structured by cell type.
AI.method: Proposes GC-MoE with a routing network for cell-type probability estimation, cell-type-specific experts, Cell-Type-Specific Co-Expression-Aware Predictor (CAP), and Cell-to-Cell Interaction Attention (C2CA) module.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over existing single-cell and adapted spot-level baselines on public single-cell ST datasets.
AI.conclusion: GC-MoE effectively models cell-type-specific gene expression variability for histology-based single-cell ST estimation.

title: CityTrajBench: A Unified Benchmark for City-Scale Vehicle Trajectory Generation
summary: Urban trajectory generation is a fundamental task for transportation simulation, urban planning, and mobility analytics. However, systematic comparison across trajectory generation methods remains difficult because existing studies often rely on different datasets, preprocessing pipelines, trajectory representations, and evaluation metrics. This fragmentation makes it unclear whether reported performance differences arise from the generation mechanism itself or from inconsistent experimental protocols. To address this issue, we present CityTrajBench, a unified benchmark framework and protocol for city-scale vehicle trajectory generation. CityTrajBench standardizes data ingestion, trajectory normalization, feature construction, model adaptation, map-aware post-processing, model selection, and multi-level evaluation under a common setting. It supports heterogeneous generators, including statistical baselines, VAE-based, GAN-based, diffusion-based, and flow-matching-based models, and evaluates them on three real-world urban trajectory datasets. The benchmark measures global spatial realism, trip-level distribution fidelity, trajectory-level geometric similarity, conditional mobility consistency, and efficiency. Experiments reveal clear trade-offs across model families: DiffTraj is strongest on trajectory-level geometric fidelity, DiffRNTraj is competitive on structure-sensitive global realism, and TrajFlow provides a strong balance across realism, quality, conditional consistency, and efficiency. Meanwhile, a simple Markov baseline remains competitive on coarse-grained trip and local-movement statistics. These findings show that urban trajectory generation quality is inherently multi-objective, that no single model dominates all criteria equally, and that CityTrajBench provides a reproducible benchmark protocol and testbed for future research on urban mobility generation.

AI.tldr: CityTrajBench is a unified benchmark for city-scale vehicle trajectory generation that standardizes datasets, preprocessing, models, and evaluation across five criteria. Experiments show no single model dominates all metrics: DiffTraj excels at trajectory-level geometry, DiffRNTraj at global realism, and TrajFlow offers the best overall balance.
AI.motivation: Systematic comparison of trajectory generation methods is difficult due to inconsistent datasets, preprocessing, representations, and evaluation metrics across studies, making it unclear whether performance differences stem from the generation method or experimental protocols.
AI.method: CityTrajBench standardizes data ingestion, trajectory normalization, feature construction, model adaptation, map-aware post-processing, model selection, and multi-level evaluation. It supports statistical baselines, VAE, GAN, diffusion, and flow-matching models, evaluated on three real-world datasets across five criteria.
AI.result: DiffTraj achieves best trajectory-level geometric fidelity; DiffRNTraj excels at structure-sensitive global realism; TrajFlow provides the strongest balance across realism, quality, consistency, and efficiency; a simple Markov baseline remains competitive on coarse-grained statistics.
AI.conclusion: Urban trajectory generation is inherently multi-objective; no single model dominates all criteria. CityTrajBench provides a reproducible benchmark protocol for future research.

title: Geometry-Aware Implicit Memory for Video World Models
summary: Video world models aim to simulate controllable visual environments, but long-horizon rollouts depend on what the model remembers after observations leave its native context window. Explicit memories retain frames or online 3D reconstructions, which can suffer from heuristic retrieval errors, redundant appearance storage, or reconstruction artifacts. Implicit memories compress history into a compact state, but existing designs are not explicitly constrained to encode cross-view scene geometry. We propose GIM-World, a geometry-aware implicit memory framework for video world models. A lightweight transformer encoder compresses variable-length history into fixed-size memory tokens, a camera-queryable geometry head distills 3D scene structure from a frozen foundation model into the memory during training, and an information-guided pruning rule keeps encoding cost bounded as history grows. The geometry teacher is discarded at inference, leaving a lightweight memory module. Experiments on MIND show that GIM-World better preserves long-horizon geometric and visual consistency than both explicit- and implicit-memory baselines.

AI.tldr: GIM-World introduces a geometry-aware implicit memory framework for video world models that compresses history into compact memory tokens with geometry supervision from a teacher model, achieving better long-horizon visual and geometric consistency than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon video world models suffer from memory limitations: explicit memory (frames, 3D reconstructions) has retrieval errors and artifacts, while implicit memory lacks explicit geometric constraints. The authors aim to design an implicit memory that encodes cross-view scene geometry.
AI.method: GIM-World uses (1) a lightweight transformer encoder to compress variable-length history into fixed-size memory tokens, (2) a camera-queryable geometry head that distills 3D structure from a frozen foundation model into the memory during training, and (3) an information-guided pruning rule to keep encoding cost bounded.
AI.result: On MIND benchmark, GIM-World outperforms both explicit-memory and implicit-memory baselines in preserving long-horizon geometric and visual consistency.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware implicit memory is effective for long-horizon video world modeling, and the geometry teacher can be discarded at inference, leaving a lightweight module that retains strong performance.

title: Massive Spikes in LLMs are Bias Vectors: Mechanistic Uncovering and Spike-Free Quantization
summary: Massive activation spikes in Large Language Models (LLMs) severely degrade quantization by stretching dynamic ranges. While prior hypotheses characterize these as high-level scalar biases, we argue that they are merely the scalar intermediates of rigid, structural vector biases in the spike-carrying tokens. We show that these tokens converge to constant vectors after normalization that drive the attention sink and value-state drain mechanisms. We geometrically substantiate this by analyzing the coordination of projection weights: $W_K$ contrastively amplifies the vector, $W_Q$ aligns semantic tokens toward it, and $W_V$ projects it into the spectral null-space. Furthermore, we reveal that the model actively preserves these structural biases against Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) perturbations by localizing them in "zones of rotational stability" utilizing low-frequency bands and coherent channel pairs. Leveraging this, we propose INSERTQUANT, a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that clamps spikes and restores their function via pre-computed template vectors. This renders activations strictly spike-free, enabling robust low-bit quantization with high fidelity. INSERTQUANT achieves parity with state-of-the-art per-tensor quantization methods on LLMs and uniquely generalizes beyond text to other modalities such as ViTs.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit structural vector biases (not scalar spikes) causing quantization issues; INSERTQUANT clamps spikes and restores function via pre-computed templates, achieving spike-free activations for robust low-bit quantization.
AI.motivation: Massive activation spikes in LLMs severely degrade quantization by stretching dynamic ranges; existing hypotheses inadequately characterize these as high-level scalar biases.
AI.method: Geometric analysis of projection weights (W_K, W_Q, W_V) revealing structural vector biases; identification of RoPE stabilization zones; INSERTQUANT framework that clamps spikes and restores function via pre-computed template vectors.
AI.result: INSERTQUANT achieves parity with SOTA per-tensor quantization methods on LLMs and generalizes beyond text to ViTs.
AI.conclusion: Spike-carrying tokens are structural vector biases, not scalar; INSERTQUANT enables robust low-bit quantization with high fidelity across modalities.

title: Spatial-Temporal Decoupled Reference Conditioning for Identity-Preserving Text-to-Video Generation
summary: Identity-preserving video generation (IPVG) aims to synthesize high-fidelity videos that follow text prompts while faithfully preserving a reference identity. Despite recent progress, existing IPVG methods still struggle to balance high-level semantic control and low-level identity fidelity. To bridge this gap, we propose ST-DRC, an effective Spatial-Temporal Decoupled Reference Conditioning framework for identity-preserving text-to-video generation. At the framework level, ST-DRC performs latent in-context feature injection by encoding the reference image with the video VAE and concatenating it with noisy video latents, enabling rich low-level identity details to be accessed without additional adapters. To separate identity-aware reference retrieval from appearance copying, we introduce TASS-RoPE, a Temporal-Adjacent Spatial-Shifted RoPE scheme that places reference tokens near the video sequence in time but shifts them in space, allowing reference information to flow through spatio-temporal attention while suppressing pixel-level copy-paste shortcuts. To further prevent shortcut learning and strengthen the otherwise diluted identity supervision in the diffusion objective, we combine appearance-invariant reference augmentation with face-guided identity objectives, encouraging the model to preserve identity under variations in color, pose, and layout. At inference time, we introduce a three-stream reference classifier-free guidance strategy that independently controls text adherence and reference fidelity. Experiments demonstrate that ST-DRC achieves strong identity preservation, prompt alignment, temporal consistency, and video quality with a lightweight design built on LTX-2.3. Our method ranks among the top submissions in the facial identity-preserving video generation track, validating the effectiveness of spatial-temporal decoupled reference conditioning.

AI.tldr: ST-DRC is a lightweight framework for identity-preserving video generation that uses spatial-temporal decoupled reference conditioning (via TASS-RoPE and latent in-context injection) and three-stream CFG to balance text control and identity fidelity, achieving top-tier results on LTX-2.3.
AI.motivation: Existing IPVG methods struggle to balance high-level semantic control (text adherence) and low-level identity fidelity (preserving the reference identity). The paper aims to bridge this gap with a decoupled conditioning approach.
AI.method: 1) Latent in-context feature injection (concatenating reference image latents with noisy video latents at VAE level). 2) TASS-RoPE (Temporal-Adjacent Spatial-Shifted RoPE) to separate identity-aware retrieval from appearance copying. 3) Appearance-invariant reference augmentation + face-guided identity objectives to prevent shortcut learning. 4) Three-stream reference CFG for independent control of text and identity.
AI.result: Strong identity preservation, prompt alignment, temporal consistency, and video quality with a lightweight design on LTX-2.3. Ranks among top submissions in the facial identity-preserving video generation track.
AI.conclusion: Spatial-temporal decoupled reference conditioning effectively balances identity fidelity and text control, validated by competitive benchmark performance.

title: Regularized Large Neighborhood Search
summary: Operations research practitioners typically tackle NP-hard combinatorial problems using large neighborhood search (LNS), a scalable heuristic that iteratively refines a current solution by locally re-optimizing subsets of its variables. In contrast, most existing approaches for integrating combinatorial optimization layers into neural networks still assume access to an exact global solution, which is computationally intractable. We bridge this gap by introducing regularized LNS (RLNS). By regularizing or perturbing local subproblems, we turn the LNS heuristic into an efficient MCMC sampler over the combinatorial set of feasible solutions, with associated Fenchel-Young losses. Under entropic regularization, we prove that RLNS performs exact block Gibbs sampling. Furthermore, adjusting the number of RLNS iterations allows us to interpolate between pseudolikelihood and exact maximum likelihood estimation, for end-to-end learning without global solvers. We demonstrate our approach on $k$-subset selection, generalized assignment, and stochastic vehicle scheduling problems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Regularized Large Neighborhood Search (RLNS), which transforms the LNS heuristic into an efficient MCMC sampler via regularization/perturbation, enabling integration of combinatorial optimization layers into neural networks for end-to-end learning without requiring exact global solvers.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for integrating combinatorial optimization into neural networks require exact global solvers, which are computationally intractable for NP-hard problems. The authors aim to bridge this gap by leveraging the scalable LNS heuristic.
AI.method: The authors propose RLNS, which regularizes/perturbs local subproblems within LNS to create an MCMC sampler over feasible combinatorial solutions. Under entropic regularization, RLNS performs exact block Gibbs sampling. The number of RLNS iterations allows interpolation between pseudolikelihood and exact MLE.
AI.result: The approach is demonstrated on k-subset selection, generalized assignment, and stochastic vehicle scheduling problems.
AI.conclusion: RLNS enables end-to-end learning for combinatorial optimization without global solvers, with theoretical guarantees (exact block Gibbs sampling under entropic regularization) and practical interpolation between approximation and exact likelihood.

title: Reason-Then-Retrieve for CoVR-R with Structured Edit Prompts and Dense-Sparse Fusion
summary: CoVR-R studies reason-aware composed video retrieval: given a reference video and an edit instruction, the system must retrieve the target video that satisfies the edit. The main difficulty is that the target is not described directly; it must be inferred from fine-grained changes in object identity, action order, final state, hand interaction, and scene transition. We build a zero-shot reason-then-retrieve pipeline around Qwen3.5-27B. For each gallery video, the model generates a retrieval-oriented structured description and a dense embedding by pooling generated-token hidden states with token-dependent weights. For each query, the model first performs edit reasoning over the reference video and instruction, then generates a target-video description whose hidden states serve as the query embedding. We complement dense retrieval with a TF-IDF branch over the generated texts and fuse the two rankings with split-specific weights. On validation, the current best submission reaches 80.81 at R@1, 94.86 at R@5, 97.11 at R@10, and 98.59 at R@50. On the blind test split, it reaches 89.73 at R@1, 95.79 at R@5, 96.63 at R@10, and 97.98 at R@50.

AI.tldr: CoVR-R introduces a zero-shot reason-then-retrieve pipeline for composed video retrieval, using Qwen3.5-27B to generate structured descriptions and dense embeddings, fused with TF-IDF rankings, achieving strong results on both validation and blind test splits.
AI.motivation: Composed video retrieval (CoVR) is challenging because the target video is not directly described; it must be inferred from fine-grained changes such as object identity, action order, state transitions, hand interactions, and scene transitions.
AI.method: A zero-shot pipeline around Qwen3.5-27B: (1) For each gallery video, generate a retrieval-oriented structured description and dense embedding via pooled token hidden states with token-dependent weights. (2) For each query, perform edit reasoning over the reference video and instruction, then generate a target-video description whose hidden states serve as the query embedding. (3) Complement dense retrieval with a TF-IDF branch over generated texts, fusing rankings with split-specific weights.
AI.conclusion: The proposed reason-then-retrieve approach with dual ranking fusion effectively handles fine-grained composed video retrieval in a zero-shot manner, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

title: Measurement Geometry and Design for Trustworthy Generative Inverse Problems
summary: Generative models are increasingly used as priors for inverse problems, but their ability to produce realistic images creates a basic trust problem: a plausible reconstruction may be supported by the measurements, or it may be filled in by the prior along unobserved directions. This distinction is especially important in medical imaging, where acquisition operators are designed under scan-time, dose, and calibration constraints. We study generative inverse problems from a measurement-geometry perspective. The central question is whether a fixed measurement operator can distinguish nearby images that are plausible under the generative prior, and whether this relationship can guide better measurements. We introduce a local measurement-manifold compatibility measure that quantifies how well the operator observes prior-relevant tangent directions. Under local regularity assumptions, we prove that this quantity controls the stable part of the reconstruction error, while the generative prior controls off-manifold drift. This worst-direction certificate motivates practical fixed and sequential acquisition rules based on overall local volume preservation, including a posterior-cloud design that adapts measurements at test time without training a sampling policy. Across row-sampling, tomographic, and MR acquisition settings, the proposed scores predict failure modes, explain measurement-induced hallucinations, and guide better sampling. In fastMRI Cartesian sampling, posterior-cloud measurement design improves over strong non-learned ACS-preserving baselines, including variable-density and Poisson-like masks.

AI.tldr: This paper studies inverse problems with generative priors from a measurement-geometry perspective. It introduces a local measurement-manifold compatibility measure that quantifies how well measurement operators observe prior-relevant tangent directions, linking this to reconstruction error. The theory motivates practical fixed and sequential acquisition rules, validated across imaging modalities including fastMRI where posterior-cloud design outperforms standard baselines.
AI.motivation: Generative models used as priors for inverse problems create a trust problem: realistic reconstructions may be supported by measurements or hallucinated by the prior. This is especially critical in medical imaging where acquisition is constrained by scan-time, dose, and calibration. The authors aim to understand whether measurement operators can distinguish nearby plausible images and how this relationship can guide better measurements.
AI.method: The authors introduce a local measurement-manifold compatibility measure to quantify observation of prior-relevant tangent directions. Under local regularity assumptions, they prove this controls stable reconstruction error while the prior controls off-manifold drift. This leads to practical fixed and sequential acquisition rules based on local volume preservation, including a posterior-cloud design adapting measurements at test time without training sampling policies.
AI.result: Across row-sampling, tomographic, and MR acquisition settings, proposed scores predict failure modes, explain measurement-induced hallucinations, and guide better sampling. In fastMRI Cartesian sampling, posterior-cloud design improves over strong non-learned ACS-preserving baselines including variable-density and Poisson-like masks.
AI.conclusion: The measurement-geometry perspective provides theoretical guarantees and practical tools for designing acquisition strategies that mitigate hallucination risks from generative priors, with demonstrated improvements in medical imaging settings.

title: Initialization is Half the Battle: Generating Diverse Images from a Guidance Potential Posterior
summary: Despite the remarkable fidelity of generative models, they frequently suffer from mode collapse. Existing strategies for enhancing diversity predominantly focus on intervening during the generation trajectory. We identify a critical oversight that the standard Gaussian initialization often causes trajectories to collapse into dominant modes because it is agnostic to the guidance potential landscape. In this work, we formulate selecting the initial noise from a guidance potential posterior, which effectively re-weights the prior towards diversity-rich regions. To sample from this distribution efficiently, we introduce Diversity-inducing Initialization (DivIn), which leverages Langevin dynamics to actively navigate the initialization landscape, steering initial noise away from collapsing regions while anchoring them to the valid data manifold. Our method serves as an inference-time diversity enhancement compatible with both diffusion and flow matching models. Extensive experiments show that DivIn exhibits a superior performance in both class-to-image and text-to-image scenarios. Furthermore, we highlight that as DivIn is orthogonal to trajectory-based methods, combining them significantly expands the diversity-quality Pareto frontier beyond what either achieves in isolation.

AI.tldr: DivIn enhances generative model diversity by selecting initial noise via Langevin dynamics from a guidance potential posterior, preventing mode collapse at inference time without modifying generation trajectories.
AI.motivation: Standard Gaussian initialization is agnostic to the guidance potential landscape, causing trajectories to collapse into dominant modes. Existing diversity methods only intervene during generation trajectory, overlooking this initialization issue.
AI.method: DivIn formulates initial noise selection from a guidance potential posterior, using Langevin dynamics to steer noise away from collapsing regions while staying on the valid data manifold, compatible with both diffusion and flow matching models.
AI.result: Superior performance in class-to-image and text-to-image scenarios. When combined with trajectory-based methods, DivIn significantly expands the diversity-quality Pareto frontier beyond either approach alone.
AI.conclusion: Initialization selection is a critical yet overlooked aspect for diversity. DivIn is an effective, orthogonal inference-time enhancement that complements existing trajectory-based diversity methods.

title: Repurposing Adversarial Perturbations for Continual Learning: From Defense to Active Alignment
summary: In dynamic environments, large language models need to keep adapting to new tasks, but continual learning often suffers from forgetting, limited transfer, and vulnerability to adversarial perturbations. To address this, we present AdvCL, which repurposes adversarial perturbations as a geometric control signal for stable continual adaptation. AdvCL combines three plug-in modules: Intra-Smooth promotes local smoothness via small adversarial perturbations; Proto-Clip uses similarity clipping to prevent excessive alignment to current task prototype; and Inter-Align applies directional alignment toward previous task prototype to reduce representational gaps. Experiments show consistent gains in both standard performance and robustness, with lower forgetting and stronger transfer. We further analyze key mechanisms by quantifying the sensitivity of Intra-Smooth to perturbation settings and the effect of Inter-Align on task similarity and geometric distance. In summary, the modules provide complementary gains when combined, and each can also be integrated individually into diverse CL paradigms, including replay, regularization, and dynamic architectures, thereby offering a geometric control mechanism for continual learning.

AI.tldr: AdvCL uses adversarial perturbations as a geometric control signal for continual learning in LLMs, combining three modules (Intra-Smooth, Proto-Clip, Inter-Align) to improve stability, reduce forgetting, and enhance transfer learning across diverse CL paradigms.
AI.motivation: LLMs need to continually adapt to new tasks in dynamic environments, but suffer from catastrophic forgetting, limited knowledge transfer, and vulnerability to adversarial perturbations.
AI.method: AdvCL repurposes adversarial perturbations as a geometric control signal with three plug-in modules: (1) Intra-Smooth for local smoothness via small adversarial perturbations; (2) Proto-Clip using similarity clipping to prevent excessive alignment to the current task prototype; (3) Inter-Align applying directional alignment toward previous task prototypes.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in both standard performance and robustness, with lower forgetting and stronger transfer. The modules provide complementary gains and can be integrated into replay, regularization, and dynamic architecture paradigms.
AI.conclusion: AdvCL offers a flexible geometric control mechanism for continual learning, where each module can be used individually or combined, providing robustness against forgetting and adversarial vulnerability in dynamic environments.

title: Active Exploring like a Pigeon: Reinforcing Spatial Reasoning via Agentic Vision-Language Models
summary: Enabling Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to perform spatial reasoning remains challenging. Existing approaches treat VLMs as passive observers, which is difficult for real-world applications. Moreover, reinforcement learning methods rely on sparse rewards, limiting their effectiveness for complex reasoning tasks. Inspired by pigeons' building and exploiting cognitive maps for navigation, we propose a novel agentic pipeline for spatial reasoning. First, we introduce a new \emph{dynamic cognitive map} parameterizing scene layout as object positions and orientations, serving as persistent memory for new observations. Second, we propose a novel \emph{Spatial Assertion Codes (SAC)}, Python expressions programmatically describing spatial relationships. By collaborating with the dynamic cognitive map, SAC enables verification of intermediate reasoning steps, providing dense reward signals. We optimize the model via supervised and reinforcement finetuning. Experiments on the MindCube benchmark demonstrate state-of-the-art performance with \emph{80.5\%} overall accuracy, outperforming the best current method by \emph{29.5} accuracy points (a relative improvement of \emph{53.2\%}) on the challenging \textsc{Rotation} subset. Our code and data are open-sourced at https://github.com/dw-dengwei/active-spatial-reasoning.git.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel agentic pipeline for spatial reasoning in VLMs, featuring dynamic cognitive maps and Spatial Assertion Codes (SAC) for dense reward signals, achieving 80.5% accuracy on MindCube benchmark (53.2% relative improvement on Rotation subset).
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs struggle with spatial reasoning as they are passive observers without persistent memory, and RL methods suffer from sparse reward signals, limiting their effectiveness for complex spatial reasoning tasks in real-world applications.
AI.method: The method consists of (1) a dynamic cognitive map that parameterizes scene layout as object positions/orientations serving as persistent memory, and (2) Spatial Assertion Codes (SAC) — Python expressions programmatically describing spatial relationships — that provide dense reward signals by verifying intermediate reasoning steps. The model is optimized via supervised and reinforcement finetuning.
AI.result: Achieved 80.5% overall accuracy on MindCube benchmark, outperforming the best current method by 29.5 accuracy points (53.2% relative improvement) on the challenging Rotation subset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed agentic pipeline significantly advances spatial reasoning in VLMs by leveraging biologically-inspired cognitive maps and programmatic spatial assertions, demonstrating substantial improvements over existing methods.

title: Riemannian Gradient Descent for Low-Rank Architectures
summary: We explore Riemannian optimization techniques for rank-factored matrix parameters, targeting contemporary deep learning applications. We examine ten points in the algorithm design space: two geometries for rank-$r$ matrices, three geometries for rank-$r$ partial isometries, and block-matrix variants of these five, where factors are shared across block-rows and block-columns. We apply our methods to the multihead attention parameters in small language models. After tuning learning rates, our methods do not conclusively outperform an AdamW baseline. Our implementations are available online.

AI.tldr: This paper explores Riemannian optimization for rank-factored matrices in deep learning, testing five geometries (including block-matrix variants) on multihead attention in small language models, but finds these methods do not consistently outperform AdamW after tuning.
AI.motivation: To explore whether Riemannian optimization techniques can improve rank-factored matrix parameter learning in contemporary deep learning, particularly for attention mechanisms in language models.
AI.method: The authors examine ten algorithm design points: two geometries for rank-r matrices, three geometries for rank-r partial isometries, plus block-matrix variants of all five. They apply these to multihead attention parameters in small language models and compare against AdamW.
AI.result: After hyperparameter tuning, the Riemannian optimization methods do not conclusively outperform the AdamW baseline.
AI.conclusion: Riemannian optimization, while principled, does not provide clear benefits over standard AdamW for rank-factored attention parameters in small language models. Code is released for reproducibility.

title: MASER: Modality-Adaptive Specialist Routing for Embodied 3D Spatial Intelligence
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026 Foundation Models Meet Embodied Agents Workshop
summary: In 3D environments, Embodied Agents answer spatially relevant questions through reasoning from a mixture of modalities including natural language, RGB images, point clouds, depth maps and camera poses. Existing Vision-Language models (VLMs) are fine-tuned over a single modality. This completely ignores the question semantics which may favor a different modality than the finetuned modality. To address this, we propose MASER (Modality-Adaptive SpEcialist Routing), a lightweight framework that trains five different modality adapters of a shared VLM backbone and learns a neural routing policy that selects the best adapter based on the question during inference. We encode each question with a frozen sentence transformer and pass the embedding through a small Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) trained on oracle adapter-accuracy labels. We evaluate our methodology over the Open3D-VQA benchmark and our evaluations show that no single modality is universally optimal -- point-cloud answers are best in 51.5% of cases. MASER routes with 51.3% oracle agreement, outperforming a Random-Forest ablation (43.5%), with only a single adapter call per question.

AI.tldr: MASER is a lightweight framework that learns to route questions to the best modality adapter (among five modalities) for a VLM backbone, achieving 51.3% oracle agreement and outperforming random-forest baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs are fine-tuned over a single modality, ignoring question semantics that may favor different modalities for different spatial queries in 3D environments.
AI.method: MASER trains five modality adapters (shared VLM backbone) and a neural routing policy (sentence transformer + MLP) that selects the best adapter per question using oracle adapter-accuracy labels.
AI.result: No single modality is universally optimal; point-cloud answers are best in 51.5% of cases. MASER achieves 51.3% oracle agreement, outperforming Random-Forest (43.5%), with only one adapter call per question.
AI.conclusion: Modality-adaptive routing improves embodied VQA by dynamically selecting the most suitable modality per question, offering an efficient alternative to fixed-modality fine-tuning.

title: Entropy Minimization without Model Collapse: Mitigating Prediction Bias in Medical Imaging
summary: Entropy minimization (EM) is the dominant objective for test-time adaptation, yet its failure mode, model collapse, remains poorly understood. In this work, we show that distribution shifts can cause feature clusters corresponding to distinct classes in the model's representation space to merge, while the decision boundary remains fixed. This induces a systematic skew in the predicted class distribution, referred to as prediction bias. Prediction bias refers to a shift in the predicted class distribution, with some classes overrepresented and others suppressed. We show that entropy minimization amplifies this prediction bias by tightening the existing clusters, reinforcing the incorrect groupings until all predictions collapse to a trivial solution. Next, to demonstrate the significance of prediction bias and mitigate it, we further propose Distribution Shift Bias Reduction (DSBR), a bias-correcting objective that specifically targets this failure mode by equalizing the contribution of each predicted class to the unsupervised entropy minimization loss. To study this failure mode, we design suitable adaptation settings using four medical-imaging datasets and additionally evaluate on ImageNet-C. We find that DSBR consistently stabilizes test-time adaptation, prevents model collapse, and matches or outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, DSBR operates solely at test-time.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies prediction bias as a key failure mode in entropy minimization for test-time adaptation, where distribution shifts cause class clusters to merge and EM amplifies this bias leading to model collapse. They propose DSBR, a bias-correcting objective that equalizes class contributions to prevent collapse, achieving stable adaptation across medical and ImageNet-C datasets.
AI.motivation: Entropy minimization is widely used for test-time adaptation but suffers from model collapse, a failure mode that is poorly understood. The authors aim to formally analyze this collapse mechanism and develop a targeted solution.
AI.method: The authors first analyze how distribution shifts cause feature cluster merging and prediction bias. They then propose DSBR (Distribution Shift Bias Reduction), which modifies the EM objective by equalizing each predicted class's contribution to the loss, counteracting the systematic skew that leads to collapse.
AI.result: DSBR consistently stabilizes test-time adaptation, prevents model collapse, and matches or outperforms state-of-the-art methods on four medical-imaging datasets and ImageNet-C.
AI.conclusion: Prediction bias is a root cause of model collapse in EM-based test-time adaptation, and DSBR effectively mitigates this issue as a simple, test-time-only bias-correcting objective.

title: Retrieve What's Missing: Coverage-Maximizing Retrieval for Consistent Long Video Generation
summary: Maintaining long-term geometric consistency remains challenging for long-horizon autoregressive video generation. Memory-augmented generative models address this by retrieving historical frames, but their effectiveness depends on two key design choices: what 3D-geometric evidence should represent past observations, and how memory frames should be selected from this evidence. Existing methods often rely on camera poses or field-of-view overlap, which are lightweight but too coarse to reason about pixel-wise visibility, or use explicit 3D reconstruction, which provides fine-grained evidence but is costly to maintain over long rollouts. We propose Coverage-Maximizing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (COVRAG), a depth-based memory retrieval framework that uses pretrained 3D priors to construct a target-view coverage map as lightweight 3D memory evidence. For frame selection, COVRAG maximizes residual coverage gain, iteratively retrieving frames that explain target-view regions not covered by the current context or previously selected memories. To improve scalability in long-video generation, we introduce sliding-window depth caching for efficient geometry estimation. Experiments on RealEstate10K and DL3DV10K show that COVRAG improves long-horizon geometric consistency while maintaining low latency compared to baselines.

AI.tldr: COVRAG is a depth-based memory retrieval framework for long-horizon autoregressive video generation that uses pretrained 3D priors to create target-view coverage maps for lightweight memory selection, improving geometric consistency over long rollouts.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon autoregressive video generation suffers from geometric inconsistency. Existing memory-augmented models rely on either coarse lightweight cues (camera poses/FoV overlap) that lack pixel-wise visibility reasoning, or costly explicit 3D reconstruction that is impractical for long rollouts.
AI.method: COVRAG uses pretrained 3D priors to construct target-view coverage maps as lightweight 3D memory evidence. It maximizes residual coverage gain for iterative frame selection, retrieving frames covering target-view regions not yet explained. A sliding-window depth caching mechanism improves scalability for long videos.
AI.result: Experiments on RealEstate10K and DL3DV10K demonstrate improved long-horizon geometric consistency with low latency compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Depth-based coverage maximization offers an effective middle ground between lightweight but coarse methods and costly 3D reconstruction, enabling practical long-horizon video generation with geometric consistency.

title: Local Preferential Bayesian Optimization
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a popular and effective approach for tuning expensive, noisy experiments, but requires the formulation of an explicit objective function. Preferential BO (PBO) removes this requirement by learning from pairwise human feedback, yet existing methods struggle to efficiently optimize beyond low- and medium-dimensional problems due to their global search approaches. We address this limitation by developing a family of local PBO methods that transfer key ideas from high-dimensional BO to the preferential setting. In particular, we introduce local PBO methods which adapt trust-region and derivative-informed local search to pairwise preference feedback, where the latter exploits first- and second-order derivatives of the Laplace-approximated GP posterior. Our benchmark on GP sample paths, standard optimization benchmark functions, and policy-search tasks shows that local PBO methods are especially effective in high-dimensional and complex landscapes with steep optima.
