Compared with global preference-based baselines, they can substantially reduce cumulative regret, making them particularly useful for real-world preference-based optimization tasks such as policy search.

AI.tldr: This paper develops local Preferential Bayesian Optimization (PBO) methods that adapt trust-region and derivative-informed local search to pairwise preference feedback, significantly improving optimization efficiency in high-dimensional and complex landscapes compared to global PBO approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing PBO methods struggle to efficiently optimize beyond low- and medium-dimensional problems due to their global search approaches, limiting their practical applicability.
AI.method: The authors introduce local PBO methods that adapt trust-region and derivative-informed local search to pairwise preference feedback, exploiting first- and second-order derivatives of the Laplace-approximated GP posterior.
AI.result: Local PBO methods outperform global preference-based baselines on GP sample paths, standard benchmark functions, and policy-search tasks, especially in high-dimensional and complex landscapes with steep optima, substantially reducing cumulative regret.
AI.conclusion: Local PBO methods are particularly useful for real-world preference-based optimization tasks such as policy search, where high-dimensional and complex optimization landscapes are common.

title: Places in the Wild: A Large, High-Resolution RAW Photograph Dataset for Ecologically Valid Vision Research
summary: Large image datasets have accelerated progress in cognitive neuroscience and computer vision. However, most datasets are low-resolution, internet-sourced JPEGs with unknown capture conditions and limited spatial context. Places in the Wild is a dataset of 67,574 high-resolution photographs collected in situ across 810 physical locations spanning 260 basic-level scene categories, including indoor, urban, and natural environments. At each location, a 45-megapixel Canon EOS R5 mounted on a panoramic tripod captured 72 images at 5-degree horizontal intervals plus 12 images at varying elevations, yielding dense 360-degree viewpoint sampling. All images were recorded simultaneously as 14-bit RAW (CR3) files and compressed JPEGs, preserving sensor-level detail for analyses of luminance, contrast, color, and other image statistics. The dataset is accompanied by complete EXIF metadata and a suite of image-quality metrics. Places in the Wild supports research on viewpoint-dependent recognition in humans and models, training and evaluation of scene-understanding systems under realistic conditions, characterization of natural scene statistics, and experiments requiring near-full-field visual displays.

AI.tldr: Places in the Wild is a high-resolution 360-degree panoramic dataset of 67,574 photographs from 810 real-world locations across 260 scene categories, captured with a 45-megapixel camera including RAW and JPEG formats, supporting research in scene understanding, viewpoint-dependent recognition, and natural scene statistics.
AI.motivation: Existing large image datasets are predominantly low-resolution, internet-sourced JPEGs with unknown capture conditions and limited spatial context, lacking the sensor-level detail and dense viewpoint sampling needed for rigorous cognitive neuroscience and vision research.
AI.method: 67,574 high-resolution photographs were collected in situ across 810 physical locations spanning 260 scene categories using a 45-megapixel Canon EOS R5 on a panoramic tripod, capturing 72 images at 5-degree horizontal intervals plus 12 elevation-varying images per location, recorded as both 14-bit RAW (CR3) and compressed JPEG.
AI.result: A dataset with dense 360-degree viewpoint sampling, complete EXIF metadata, and a suite of image-quality metrics, preserving full sensor-level detail for luminance, contrast, color, and other statistical analyses.
AI.conclusion: Places in the Wild enables research on viewpoint-dependent recognition, realistic scene understanding, natural scene statistics, and near-full-field visual display experiments for both human and model-based studies.

title: Minimax-Optimal Policy Regret in Partially Observable Markov Games
summary: We study sequential decision-making in partially observable environments against strategic, adaptive opponents, modeled as partially observable Markov games (POMGs). The central challenge is to learn latent dynamics from partial observations while facing an adversary whose behavior depends on the learner's strategy, making standard regret notions inadequate. We prove that an epoch-based optimistic maximum-likelihood algorithm achieves $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ policy regret for fixed problem parameters, with explicit dependence on the horizon, adversary memory, confidence radius, and the aggregate Eluder dimension of the observable-operator class. The algorithm selects one policy per geometrically growing epoch using confidence sets built cumulatively from past data, which keeps the cost of comparing adversary responses across policies logarithmic in $T$. We also prove a lower bound matching the $\sqrt{T}$ and aggregate-Eluder-dimension dependence, up to problem-dependent and logarithmic factors. Finally, we extend the framework to horizon-adaptive guarantees and adversaries with geometric fading memory.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an epoch-based optimistic maximum-likelihood algorithm that achieves $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ policy regret in partially observable Markov games against strategic, adaptive opponents, with matching lower bounds and extensions to horizon-adaptive and fading-memory adversaries.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of sequential decision-making in partially observable environments against strategic, adaptive opponents, where standard regret notions fail because the adversary's behavior depends on the learner's strategy.
AI.method: An epoch-based optimistic maximum-likelihood algorithm that selects one policy per geometrically growing epoch using confidence sets built cumulatively from past data, keeping the cost of comparing adversary responses across policies logarithmic in T.
AI.result: Achieves $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ policy regret with explicit dependence on horizon, adversary memory, confidence radius, and aggregate Eluder dimension of the observable-operator class. Also provides a matching lower bound.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm provides provably efficient learning in POMGs, with the framework extendable to horizon-adaptive guarantees and adversaries with geometric fading memory.

title: X-Stream: Exploring MLLMs as Multiplexers for Multi-Stream Understanding
summary: While video streaming understanding has made significant strides, real-world applications, such as live sports broadcasting, autonomous driving, and multi-screen collaboration, inherently demand continuous, multi-stream interactions. However, existing benchmarks are confined to single-stream paradigms, leaving a critical gap in evaluating online, cross-stream reasoning. To bridge this, we introduce X-Stream, the first benchmark dedicated to multi-stream streaming understanding. Comprising 4,220 rigorously curated QA pairs across 932 videos, X-Stream evaluates 11 subtasks across multi-window, multi-view, and multi-device scenarios. Crucially, our dataset is constructed using a novel dual-verification pipeline that prevents over-reliance on a single stream. Furthermore, we pioneer the conceptualization of multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) as naive multiplexers, systematically evaluating their performance through the lens of Signal Multiplexing Theory. Our extensive online inference experiments reveal a stark reality: state-of-the-art MLLMs struggle significantly with concurrent streams, achieving only about 50% score and exhibiting poor proactive ability. Ultimately, X-Stream exposes the trade-off of current multiplexing schemes, providing both a practical evaluation protocol and empirical guidance for next-generation multi-stream agents.

AI.tldr: X-Stream is the first benchmark for multi-stream video streaming understanding, evaluating MLLMs across 11 subtasks with 4,220 QA pairs, revealing that state-of-the-art models struggle (~50% accuracy) with concurrent stream reasoning.
AI.motivation: Real-world applications (sports broadcasting, autonomous driving, multi-screen) require continuous multi-stream reasoning, but existing benchmarks are limited to single-stream paradigms, leaving a critical evaluation gap.
AI.method: Constructed 4,220 QA pairs across 932 videos covering 11 subtasks in multi-window, multi-view, and multi-device scenarios. Uses a novel dual-verification pipeline to prevent over-reliance on a single stream. Frames MLLMs as "naive multiplexers" via Signal Multiplexing Theory.
AI.result: State-of-the-art MLLMs achieve only ~50% accuracy on concurrent stream reasoning and exhibit poor proactive ability, exposing significant limitations.
AI.conclusion: X-Stream reveals the trade-offs of current multiplexing schemes, providing a practical evaluation protocol and empirical guidance for next-generation multi-stream agents.

title: FOAM: Frequency and Operator Error-Based Adaptive Damping Method for Reducing Staleness-Oriented Error for Shampoo
summary: Shampoo is attracting considerable attention for its superior performance on large-scale optimization benchmarks; yet it faces a significant practical bottleneck: the prohibitive computational overhead of matrix inversion. To mitigate this, practitioners typically rely on stale preconditioner updates, creating a fundamental trade-off between computational efficiency and optimization fidelity. In this work, we provide a theoretical study of staleness through the complementary lenses of convergence and stability. While staleness improves computational efficiency, it inherently degrades performance and introduces numerical instability. Crucially, we identify that damping, acting as a numerical stabilizer, can effectively suppress these negative effects. Guided by this analysis, we propose FOAM, an adaptive algorithm that stabilizes training by dynamically controlling both the damping factor and the eigendecomposition frequency based on an approximation of the staleness-oriented error. Experimental results demonstrate that FOAM reduces wall-clock time compared to standard Shampoo while maintaining robust convergence.

AI.tldr: Shampoo optimization's staleness trade-off between efficiency and fidelity is analyzed; FOAM algorithm dynamically adjusts damping and eigendecomposition frequency to reduce computational overhead while maintaining convergence.
AI.motivation: To address the prohibitive computational cost of matrix inversion in Shampoo optimizer, which forces practitioners to use stale preconditioner updates, creating a fundamental trade-off between efficiency and optimization quality.
AI.method: Provides theoretical analysis of staleness through convergence and stability lenses; identifies damping as a numerical stabilizer that suppresses negative effects of staleness; proposes FOAM algorithm that adaptively controls damping factor and eigendecomposition frequency based on staleness-oriented error approximation.
AI.result: FOAM reduces wall-clock time compared to standard Shampoo while maintaining robust convergence.
AI.conclusion: Staleness inherently degrades performance and introduces instability, but damping can effectively counteract these issues; adaptive control of both damping and update frequency enables more efficient Shampoo optimization.

title: MORPHOS: Autoregressive 4D Generation with Temporal Structured Latents
summary: We present MORPHOS, a novel autoregressive framework that generates dynamic 3D assets from videos across diverse representations, including meshes, 3D Gaussians, and radiance fields. Existing methods are typically limited to a single representation, struggle to model topological changes, or fail to maintain temporal consistency over long videos. To address these limitations, we introduce the Temporal Structured Latents (T-SLAT), a unified 4D representation that jointly encodes geometry and appearance along the temporal dimension. Leveraging T-SLAT, MORPHOS autoregressively generates dynamic 3D assets via causal attention, conditioning each frame on its preceding history to ensure temporal consistency while handling evolving topologies. We also propose a temporal-structural augmentation to mitigate error accumulation in autoregressive generation. MORPHOS achieves state-of-the-art performance in appearance and competitive results in geometry across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating superior generalization across various representations and robustness in long-horizon generation.

AI.tldr: MORPHOS is an autoregressive framework that generates temporally consistent dynamic 3D assets from videos across multiple representations (meshes, 3D Gaussians, radiance fields) using a novel unified 4D representation called Temporal Structured Latents (T-SLAT).
AI.motivation: Existing methods are limited to single representations, struggle with topological changes, and fail to maintain temporal consistency over long videos.
AI.method: Introduces Temporal Structured Latents (T-SLAT), a unified 4D representation encoding geometry and appearance over time. Uses causal attention for autoregressive generation and temporal-structural augmentation to mitigate error accumulation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art appearance and competitive geometry performance across multiple benchmarks, with superior generalization across representations and robustness in long-horizon generation.
AI.conclusion: MORPHOS effectively addresses key limitations of dynamic 3D generation by unifying representations and ensuring temporal consistency, achieving strong generalization and long-horizon robustness.

title: When Do Attention Circuits Form? Developmental Trajectories of Capability and Attention-Sink Emergence Across Three 1B-ClassArchitectures
summary:
We track the developmental trajectory of attention-head circuit formation across three 1B-class language models spanning two architecture families (dense transformer, mixture-of-experts) and two pretraining corpora (The Pile, DCLM): Pythia 1B, OLMo 1B-0724-hf, and OLMoE 1B-7B-0924. At each of 10 log-spaced revisions per model -- 30 mechanistic-interpretability runs in total -- we apply a participation-ratio (PR) spectral signal and an all-head capability-specific selectivity screen to track induction, previous-token, and BOS-attractor heads as they emerge.
  Five findings. (F1) Layers 0 and 1 produce zero BOS-classified heads at every revision in every model: the L0/L1 zero-BOS floor is an architectural property, not a learned outcome. (F2) The whole-model BOS-attractor fraction follows three distinct emergence shapes -- a gradual ramp in Pythia 1B, a sharp phase transition in OLMo 1B (7% to 70% between adjacent checkpoints), and a gradual ramp in OLMoE 1B-7B. (F3) In DCLM models, induction-circuit formation precedes BOS-attractor formation by 10-20x in tokens; capability-circuit formation and attention-sink formation are two transitions, not one. (F4) The capability-specific screen converges to the final induction circuit within 0.3-2% of total training tokens -- circuit identification does not require the final model. (F5) For every final-checkpoint induction head sampled across all three models, per-head PR is elevated at or before the first revision at which that head crosses its capability-selectivity threshold.
  The results refine the induction-phase-transition framing: in 1B-class models trained on DCLM, the induction transition and the attention-sink transition are separated by an order of magnitude in tokens and have qualitatively different shapes.

AI.tldr: Tracking 30 runs across three 1B-class language models reveals that attention heads form at different rates: induction circuits form rapidly (within 0.3-2% of training), BOS-attractor heads show distinct emergence shapes per model, and layers 0-1 never produce BOS heads—an architectural invariant. The induction and attention-sink transitions are separate phenomena, separated by 10-20x in tokens on DCLM.
AI.motivation: To understand how different functional attention-head circuits (induction, previous-token, BOS-attractor) emerge during training, and whether they form as a single phase transition or multiple distinct processes.
AI.method: Applied participation-ratio (PR) spectral signal and capability-specific selectivity screens across 10 log-spaced checkpoints per model (30 total runs) on Pythia 1B, OLMo 1B, and OLMoE 1B-7B trained on The Pile and DCLM.
AI.result: Five findings: (1) L0/L1 never produce BOS heads (architectural property). (2) BOS-attractor shapes differ across models. (3) Induction circuits form 10-20x earlier than BOS-attractor heads in DCLM models. (4) Induction circuits converge within 0.3-2% of training. (5) Per-head PR correlates with capability-selectivity timing.
AI.conclusion: The induction phase transition and attention-sink transition are separate phenomena, differing by an order of magnitude in tokens and having qualitatively different shapes, refining prior understanding of a single "induction phase transition."

title: GloResNet: A lightweight 3D CNN with global topological features for preterm brain injury prediction
summary: This study introduces an automated deep learning framework for predicting brain injury (BI) in preterm infants from T2-weighted MRI (dHCP dataset). We propose GloResNet, a lightweight 3D CNN based on ResNet-10, pretrained on MedicalNet to address data scarcity. A global manifold mapping strategy first resamples each 3D volume to 128x128x128 and then applies subject-wise z-score intensity normalization, thereby preserving global topology while standardizing appearance. Training integrates mixup, class weighting, and test-time augmentation for robustness. In 5-fold cross-validation, GloResNet achieved 75.18% average accuracy (peak 81.82%), with specificity 0.81 and sensitivity 0.76. Results demonstrate that a topology-aware lightweight CNN has the capability to effectively predict neonatal BI, offering a non-invasive screening tool. The source code of this paper can be obtained from the GitHub repository: https://github.com/ICL-SUST/GloResNet-Preterm-Brain

AI.tldr: GloResNet, a lightweight 3D CNN based on ResNet-10 with MedicalNet pretraining, predicts brain injury in preterm infants from T2-weighted MRI, achieving 75.18% accuracy on the dHCP dataset.
AI.motivation: To develop a non-invasive, automated screening tool for brain injury detection in preterm infants, addressing data scarcity challenges in medical imaging.
AI.method: Proposes GloResNet, a ResNet-10-based 3D CNN pretrained on MedicalNet, using global manifold mapping (resampling to 128x128x128 + subject-wise z-score normalization), with mixup, class weighting, and test-time augmentation for robustness.
AI.result: 75.18% average accuracy (peak 81.82%), specificity 0.81, sensitivity 0.76 in 5-fold cross-validation on dHCP T2-weighted MRI.
AI.conclusion: A topology-aware lightweight CNN can effectively predict neonatal brain injury, offering a promising non-invasive screening tool for clinical use.

title: TabPrep: Closing the Feature Engineering Gap in Tabular Benchmarks
summary: Progress in tabular machine learning has largely focused on increasingly sophisticated model architectures. At the same time, feature engineering remains a critical yet underexplored component of real-world modeling pipelines that is entirely absent from modern benchmarks, which creates an unquantified evaluation gap. In this work, we introduce TabPrep, a lightweight preprocessing pipeline composed of feature generators that are carefully designed to target three specific structural data patterns. We show that many widely used model classes exhibit predictable blind spots to these patterns and that systematic feature engineering alone can establish new peak performance. Across the TabArena benchmark, integrating TabPrep into model training and tuning consistently improves performance for tree-based, neural, linear, and foundation models, often surpassing gains achieved by model-centric innovations alone. TabPrep outperforms previous automated feature engineering approaches in performance, efficiency, and applicability across datasets, enabling integration into large-scale benchmarks. By releasing TabPrep (see https://github.com/atschalz/tabprep), we enable researchers to integrate feature engineering into their benchmarking setup, filling a longstanding gap in tabular evaluations.

AI.tldr: TabPrep is a lightweight feature engineering pipeline that fills the evaluation gap in tabular ML benchmarks by systematically generating features targeting structural data patterns, consistently improving performance across model classes beyond what model-centric innovations achieve.
AI.motivation: Feature engineering is critical yet underexplored and absent from modern benchmarks, creating an unquantified evaluation gap in tabular machine learning.
AI.method: TabPrep is a lightweight preprocessing pipeline with feature generators targeting three specific structural data patterns, integrated into model training and tuning.
AI.result: TabPrep consistently improves performance across tree-based, neural, linear, and foundation models on TabArena, often surpassing gains from model-centric innovations alone, and outperforms prior automated feature engineering approaches.
AI.conclusion: Systematic feature engineering alone can establish new peak performance; TabPrep fills a longstanding gap in tabular evaluations and enables integration into large-scale benchmarks.

title: Question-Aware Evidence Ledgers for Video Relational Reasoning
comment: Technical report for the VRR Challenge at the VideoLLMs Workshop, CVPR 2026
summary: The VRR-QA challenge evaluates visual relational reasoning in videos, where answers often depend on implicit spatial relations, event boundaries, target identity, and dialogue context rather than a single salient frame. We present a test-time reasoning pipeline built around a strong GPT-5.5 video QA solver and a set of question-aware evidence ledgers. The initial solver answers each question from a uniform video representation, while routed ledgers are prompted to make the required targets, count units, reference frames, and temporal or spatial scope explicit for counting, spatial, endpoint, viewpoint, and dialogue reasoning. External tools such as open-vocabulary detection, depth cues, pair crops, ASR, and scene-graph ledgers are used only as evidence sources. A conservative gate keeps the current answer unless independent evidence uniquely supports a different option. The final evidence-gated pipeline achieves 92.95% overall accuracy and 93.79% macro accuracy on the challenge test split.

AI.tldr: A test-time reasoning pipeline using GPT-5.5 and question-aware evidence ledgers achieves 92.95% accuracy on the VRR-QA video relational reasoning challenge by incorporating spatial, temporal, and dialogue evidence through a conservative gating mechanism.
AI.motivation: Video relational reasoning (VRR-QA) is challenging because answers depend on implicit spatial relations, event boundaries, target identity, and dialogue context rather than a single salient frame, requiring a more sophisticated reasoning approach.
AI.method: A pipeline built around GPT-5.5 as a video QA solver, with question-aware evidence ledgers that make targets, count units, reference frames, and temporal/spatial scope explicit. External tools (open-vocabulary detection, depth cues, pair crops, ASR, scene-graph ledgers) serve as evidence sources. A conservative gate retains the current answer unless independent evidence uniquely supports a different option.
AI.result: 92.95% overall accuracy and 93.79% macro accuracy on the VRR-QA challenge test split.
AI.conclusion: The evidence-gated pipeline with question-aware ledgers and a conservative gating mechanism significantly improves video relational reasoning performance.

title: Policy and World Modeling Co-Training for Language Agents
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) improves large language model (LLM) agents by teaching them which actions lead to high rewards, but provides little supervision on what those actions do to the environment. World modeling (WM) can fill this gap, yet existing approaches often require separate simulators, extra training stages, or additional inference-time computation. We observe that on-policy RL rollouts already contain the needed signal: each transition pairs an action with its resulting next observation. Based on this observation, we propose PaW, a Policy and World modeling co-training framework that adds auxiliary WM supervision to the same policy during RL, without changing the inference paradigm. To make auxiliary WM supervision informative and stable, PaW introduces three components: action-entropy-based WM data selection, noise-tolerant WM loss, and reward-adaptive loss balancing. Experiments on three agentic task benchmarks show consistent improvements over strong RL baselines across models and RL algorithms. These results suggest that standard RL rollouts are a practical source of WM supervision for language-agent training.

AI.tldr: PaW co-trains policy and world model during on-policy RL by using rollout transitions as free supervision, improving LLM agent performance without extra simulators or inference changes.
AI.motivation: RL teaches agents which actions yield high rewards but provides little supervision on what actions actually do to the environment. World modeling could fill this gap, but existing WM approaches need separate simulators, extra training stages, or additional computation.
AI.method: PaW adds auxiliary world modeling supervision to the same policy during on-policy RL, using three components: (1) action-entropy-based WM data selection, (2) noise-tolerant WM loss, and (3) reward-adaptive loss balancing.
AI.result: Experiments on three agentic task benchmarks show consistent improvements over strong RL baselines across different models and RL algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Standard RL rollouts are a practical source of world modeling supervision for language-agent training, enabling dual learning without extra inference cost.

title: A Local Perturbation Theory for Cross-Domain Interference and Recovery in Multi-Domain RL
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training improves large language models (LLMs) on individual domains such as mathematical reasoning, code generation, question answering, and creative writing (CW), but training on one domain often degrades performance on others. Existing explanations based on catastrophic forgetting or global gradient conflict are incomplete: substantial interference can occur even when full-model gradients are nearly orthogonal. We show that single-domain RL produces sparse, small-magnitude parameter edits with weak overlap among top-changed neurons, while different domains still share substantial active computation routes on which update directions determine whether they act synergistically or conflict. Guided by this observation, we prove under a local perturbation model of multi-domain RL that later-domain training harms an earlier domain mainly through a second-order damage term, which under the observed sparse route structure concentrates in a low-dimensional shared conflict subspace. Moreover, a short domain refresh contracts the harmful component on this subspace, enabling selective recovery with limited collateral damage. Consistent with the theory, a brief Re-Math refresh after Code $\rightarrow$ Math $\rightarrow$ QA $\rightarrow$ CW recovers Math from 57.66 to 66.04 while largely preserving performance on the other domains, yielding the best average score of 66.39. Beyond refresh, a training-free rollback on a sparse proxy conflict coordinate set for the Math-QA pair partially restores Math, providing direct proxy-level evidence for localized damage. These results provide a localized mechanistic account of interference and recovery in multi-domain RL.

AI.tldr: Multi-domain RL post-training for LLMs causes interference between domains. This paper shows that damage is localized in a low-dimensional conflict subspace, and brief domain refresh can selectively recover performance with minimal collateral damage.
AI.motivation: Existing explanations based on catastrophic forgetting or global gradient conflict are incomplete; substantial interference occurs even when full-model gradients are nearly orthogonal. The paper aims to provide a more precise mechanistic account of interference and recovery in multi-domain RL.
AI.method: The paper proves under a local perturbation model that later-domain training harms earlier domains via a second-order damage term concentrated in a low-dimensional shared conflict subspace. It also shows that a short domain refresh contracts the harmful component on this subspace, enabling selective recovery.
AI.result: A brief Re-Math refresh after Code→Math→QA→CW training recovers Math from 57.66 to 66.04 while largely preserving other domains, achieving best average score of 66.39. A training-free rollback on sparse proxy conflict coordinates also partially restores Math.
AI.conclusion: Multi-domain RL interference is localized and mechanistic, operating through a low-dimensional conflict subspace. Brief domain refresh or targeted rollback can selectively recover performance, providing practical strategies for improving multi-domain post-training.

title: ToolFG: Towards Well-Grounded Fine-Grained Image Classification
summary: Fine-grained image classification (FGIC) has broad applications and has attracted significant research attention. In this paper, we explore a novel paradigm for solving FGIC by proposing \textbf{ToolFG}, the first tool-integrated MLLM-based framework tailored to FGIC. ToolFG enables MLLMs to autonomously and flexibly use external tools during the reasoning process, actively interact with images, and collect verifiable visual cues for distinguishing highly similar categories in a more \textit{reliable} and \textit{well-grounded} manner. To equip the model with such tool-use ability, we design a novel \textbf{MCTS-guided tool-use knowledge distillation mechanism}, which effectively mines tool-use- and FGIC-relevant knowledge from advanced proprietary MLLMs for model training. Furthermore, we propose a \textbf{model-tool co-evolution mechanism} that jointly refines the toolset and the model's tool-use policy, driving them toward a mutually adapted and FGIC-specialized state. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework.

AI.tldr: ToolFG is a novel framework integrating external tools with MLLMs for fine-grained image classification, using MCTS-guided knowledge distillation and model-tool co-evolution to improve reliability and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained image classification (FGIC) is challenging due to highly similar categories. Existing methods lack reliability and grounded reasoning. The authors aim to leverage MLLMs with external tools to collect verifiable visual cues for more accurate classification.
AI.method: (1) ToolFG framework: integrates external tools with MLLMs for autonomous tool-use during reasoning. (2) MCTS-guided tool-use knowledge distillation: mines tool-use knowledge from proprietary MLLMs. (3) Model-tool co-evolution mechanism: jointly refines the toolset and the model's tool-use policy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of ToolFG for fine-grained image classification.
AI.conclusion: ToolFG is the first tool-integrated MLLM-based framework for FGIC, enabling more reliable and well-grounded classification through autonomous tool use, knowledge distillation, and co-evolution of models and tools.

title: On the Scaling of PEFT: Towards Million Personal Models of Trillion Parameters
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) is usually treated as a cheaper alternative to full fine-tuning. We study a broader role: small trainable adapters as persistent local state on top of strong shared foundation models. In this framing, the base model provides shared competence while adapters carry instance-specific behavior such as preferences, skills, tool habits, and memory-like updates. We organize the problem around three scaling axes: Scale Up, where stronger shared priors make small local updates more useful; Scale Down, where we study how small adapters can be while remaining reliable; and Scale Out, where many persistent adapted instances coexist. MinT provides one infrastructure example for managing adapter identity, revision, provenance, evaluation, and serving residency. Together, the results suggest that PEFT can be a compact substrate for persistent personal models rather than only a budget substitute for full fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: This paper reconsiders PEFT not as a cheaper alternative to full fine-tuning but as a mechanism for persistent, instance-specific local state on shared foundation models, exploring scaling axes (Up, Down, Out) and introducing MinT infrastructure for managing adapter identity and provenance.
AI.motivation: To reframe PEFT beyond cost-saving — positioning small adapters as persistent, personalized local state on top of shared foundation models, enabling instance-specific behavior (preferences, skills, memory).
AI.method: Organizes the problem around three scaling axes: Scale Up (stronger shared priors), Scale Down (minimal reliable adapter size), and Scale Out (many coexisting adapters). Introduces MinT infrastructure for managing adapter identity, revision, provenance, evaluation, and serving.
AI.result: Stronger shared priors make small local updates more useful; adapters can be very small yet reliable; many persistent adapted instances can coexist effectively.
AI.conclusion: PEFT can serve as a compact substrate for persistent personal models rather than only a budget substitute for full fine-tuning.

title: Moment-Video: Diagnosing Temporal Fidelity of Video MLLMs on Momentary Visual Events
summary: Video multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made rapid progress on general and long-form video understanding, yet their ability to preserve brief answer-critical visual evidence remains underexplored. Many practical questions are determined by momentary visual events: localized actions or state transitions that may last only a few frames. Such evidence can be skipped by sparse frame sampling, suppressed by visual-token compression, or diluted by coarse temporal aggregation, causing failures that language-side reasoning cannot reliably recover. We introduce Moment-Video, a benchmark for diagnosing the temporal fidelity of video MLLMs through momentary visual event understanding. Each question is grounded in a localized, visually observable, and sampling-sensitive event, requiring models to notice, count, describe, or reason about transient evidence rather than rely on persistent objects, global scene context, or language priors. Moment-Video contains 1,000 human-verified video-QA pairs across 7 domains and 25 fine-grained subcategories, covering four task types: Temporal Occurrence, Temporal Counting, Action Description, and Temporal Reasoning. We evaluate 33 proprietary and open-source MLLMs on Moment-Video. The best-performing model, Seed-2.0-Pro, achieves only 39.6% overall accuracy, while most open-source models remain below 25%, revealing a substantial gap in momentary visual event understanding. Diagnostic analyses show that denser frame sampling improves some models but does not eliminate the bottleneck, and longer videos introduce stronger temporal-localization challenges. These findings suggest that current video MLLMs still lack temporally faithful representations for capturing, preserving, and using brief but decisive visual evidence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Moment-Video, a benchmark to evaluate video MLLMs on understanding momentary visual events (brief, critical visual evidence). Tests show the best model achieves only 39.6% accuracy, and most open-source models stay below 25%, revealing a significant gap in temporal fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current video MLLMs excel at general and long-form video understanding, but their ability to preserve brief answer-critical visual evidence (momentary events lasting only a few frames) remains underexplored. Such evidence is often lost due to sparse sampling, token compression, or coarse aggregation, causing failures that language reasoning cannot fix.
AI.method: The authors create Moment-Video, a benchmark with 1,000 human-verified video-QA pairs across 7 domains and 25 subcategories. It covers four task types: Temporal Occurrence, Temporal Counting, Action Description, and Temporal Reasoning. Each question targets localized, visually observable, sampling-sensitive events. They evaluate 33 proprietary and open-source MLLMs.
AI.result: The best model (Seed-2.0-Pro) achieves 39.6% overall accuracy; most open-source models score below 25%. Denser frame sampling helps some models but does not eliminate the bottleneck, and longer videos increase temporal-localization difficulties.
AI.conclusion: Current video MLLMs lack temporally faithful representations for capturing, preserving, and using brief but decisive visual evidence, highlighting a fundamental gap in momentary visual event understanding.

title: Speculative Sampling For Faster Molecular Dynamics
comment: Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 32 pages, 14 figures, 8 tables
summary: Molecular dynamics (MD) is a key tool for simulating the dynamical behavior of atomic systems. However, MD is inherently serial, which makes it difficult to increase single-system throughput with concurrent compute. To address this, we introduce Langevin Speculative Dynamics (LSD), a distributed and model-agnostic speculative sampler for accelerating MD without adding relative error. Inspired by speculative methods in language and diffusion modeling, LSD uses a draft model to propose fast simulation steps and verifies them in parallel with a slower target model, applying a transport map from the draft to the target distribution. We extend speculative sampling to second-order Langevin dynamics, derive the achievable speedup as a function of physical parameters, show that LSD generalizes across different systems and draft-target combinations with a 3-9x speedup, and confirm theoretically and empirically that LSD samples trajectories from its target model distribution.

AI.tldr: Langevin Speculative Dynamics (LSD) accelerates molecular dynamics simulations by using a draft model to propose steps and a target model to verify them in parallel, achieving 3-9x speedup without added error.
AI.motivation: MD is inherently serial, making it difficult to increase single-system throughput with concurrent compute.
AI.method: LSD extends speculative sampling to second-order Langevin dynamics, using a draft model for fast proposals and a target model for parallel verification, with a transport map aligning distributions.
AI.result: Achieves 3-9x speedup across different systems and draft-target combinations, with theoretical and empirical confirmation of correct target distribution sampling.
AI.conclusion: LSD generalizes across systems, provides provable speedup, and samples trajectories faithfully from the target model distribution.

title: Why Not Hyperparameter-Friendly Optimisation? A Monotonic Adaptive Norm Rescaling Approach For Long-Tailed Recognition
summary: Long-tailed recognition poses a significant challenge for deep learning. The two-stage decoupling paradigm, which separates representation learning from classifier retraining, offers a promising solution. During the classifier retraining stage, adaptive norm rescaling is a popular technique. It adjusts the per-class weight norms via parameter regularization, which inevitably introduces hyperparameters. However, many studies report that long-tailed recognition is sensitive to these hyperparameters, as their setup significantly impacts performance. In this paper, we first provide a class-conditional distribution perspective to support norm rescaling methods. Furthermore, we propose a simple but effective approach called Self-Adaptive Monotonic Normalization (SAMN). SAMN avoids the need for parameter regularization. It directly enforces monotonicity on per-class weight norms using the Pool Adjacent Violators Algorithm, making the method hyperparameter-friendly. SAMN is a universal strategy that integrates seamlessly with other methods for enhanced performance. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method significantly boosts long-tailed recognition performance, often achieving state-of-the-art results.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Self-Adaptive Monotonic Normalization (SAMN), a hyperparameter-free method that enforces monotonicity on per-class weight norms using the Pool Adjacent Violators Algorithm to improve long-tailed recognition performance.
AI.motivation: Existing norm rescaling methods for long-tailed recognition rely on parameter regularization with hyperparameters that are sensitive and significantly impact performance.
AI.method: SAMN directly enforces monotonicity on per-class weight norms using the Pool Adjacent Violators Algorithm (PAVA), eliminating the need for parameter regularization and hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: SAMN significantly boosts long-tailed recognition performance on benchmark datasets, often achieving state-of-the-art results, and integrates seamlessly with other methods.
AI.conclusion: The class-conditional distribution perspective theoretically supports norm rescaling, and SAMN provides a simple, hyperparameter-friendly, and universal solution for long-tailed recognition.

title: Expressivity of congruence-based architectures for DNNs on positive-definite matrices
summary: This work studies neural architectures for classifying symmetric positive-definite matrices, focusing on congruence-like layers, in which the input matrix is multiplied on the left and right by a (possibly rectangular) weight matrix $W$ and its transpose. Such layers lie at the core of the celebrated SPDNet and have also been employed independently for dimensionality reduction on positive-definite data. We show that the (semi)-orthogonality constraint commonly imposed on $W$ limits the expressivity of these layers: for certain activation functions, the resulting architecture collapses to a one-hidden-layer equivalent. This lack of expressivity follows from a loss of spectral diversity in congruence-like layers for semi-orthogonal $W$ and is a direct consequence of Poincaré's separation theorem. We then examine the choice of the final classifier, comparing several Riemannian classifiers and discussing their compatibility with the feature maps produced by congruence-like layers.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that semi-orthogonality constraints on weight matrices in SPDNet's congruence layers limit expressivity by collapsing deep architectures to shallow equivalents, due to spectral diversity loss (Poincaré's separation theorem). It also compares Riemannian classifiers for compatibility with such features.
AI.motivation: To understand the limitations of semi-orthogonal constraints in congruence-like layers used for SPD matrix classification, and to analyze expressivity bottlenecks in deep SPD networks.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using Poincaré's separation theorem to show spectral collapse; examination of congruence layers with/without semi-orthogonal constraints; comparative study of Riemannian classifiers.
AI.result: Semi-orthogonal W causes loss of spectral diversity, leading deep architectures to collapse to one-hidden-layer equivalents for certain activations. This fundamentally limits expressivity.
AI.conclusion: Semi-orthogonality constraints should be relaxed to preserve depth benefits; classifier choice significantly affects compatibility with congruence-based feature maps.

title: Improving Combined Detection and Classification of TEM Defects via Mask-Conditioned Latent Diffusion Augmentation
summary: Analyzing microstructural defects in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, particularly in irradiated metal alloys, is often limited by the availability of high-quality, labeled data. To address this, we introduce a generative data augmentation approach using a mask-conditioned latent diffusion model (LDM) for synthesizing realistic TEM images with controllable, automatically labeled multi-class defect masks. Without requiring manual annotations for generation, our method enables the creation of synthetic image-mask pairs by sampling distributions learned from experimental masks. These generated data were used to augment small experimental datasets of varying sizes (10, 50, and 100 labeled experimental images) to train a Mask Regional Convolutional Neural Network (R-CNN) model for defect detection and classification. Our results show that generative augmentation yields small overall model performance improvements, with up to a 0.02 gain in the harmonic mean of detection and classification F1 scores. However, we also find that the relative contributions to detection and classification improvement depend on the specific train/test data split. These findings highlight the potential of targeted generative models to enhance deep learning performance in data-scarce microscopy-based image quantification tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a mask-conditioned latent diffusion model for generating synthetic TEM image-mask pairs to augment small experimental datasets, achieving modest performance gains (up to 0.02 F1 score improvement) for defect detection and classification using Mask R-CNN.
AI.motivation: Microstructural defect analysis in TEM images is limited by the scarcity of high-quality labeled data, especially for irradiated metal alloys.
AI.method: A mask-conditioned latent diffusion model is used to synthesize realistic TEM images with controllable, automatically labeled multi-class defect masks, without requiring manual annotations for generation. Synthetic data augments small experimental datasets (10, 50, 100 labeled images) to train Mask R-CNN.
AI.result: Generative augmentation yields up to 0.02 gain in harmonic mean of detection and classification F1 scores, with contributions varying depending on train/test data splits.
AI.conclusion: Targeted generative models show potential for enhancing deep learning performance in data-scarce microscopy image quantification, though improvements are modest and split-dependent.

title: A Biconvex Formulation for Stable Transport of Mixture Models with a Unique Solution
summary: Optimal transport (OT) provides a principled framework for mapping between probability distributions. Despite extensive progress, applying OT to large-scale data remains computationally demanding, and the resulting pointwise transport plans are often difficult to interpret. We introduce Optimal Mixture Transport (OMT), a scalable framework that shifts the transport paradigm from individual samples to mixtures of subpopulations, reformulating the transport problem as a strictly biconvex optimization with a unique global minimizer. We further establish theoretical guarantees on the stability of the OMT map, showing that bounded perturbations of the underlying distributions lead to bounded changes in the transport plan. By formulating subpopulations as exponential-family distributions, OMT decouples computational complexity from the sample size, scaling solely with the number of mixture components. We demonstrate the effectiveness and practicality of OMT on a wide range of synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets, including image data and large-scale single-cell RNA sequencing measurements.

AI.tldr: Optimal Mixture Transport (OMT) reformulates optimal transport from pointwise mapping to mixture-of-subpopulations transport, enabling scalable computation with theoretical stability guarantees and practical effectiveness on large-scale data.
AI.motivation: Existing optimal transport methods are computationally demanding for large-scale data and produce pointwise transport plans that are difficult to interpret.
AI.method: OMT shifts transport from individual samples to mixtures of subpopulations, formulated as a strictly biconvex optimization with a unique global minimizer, using exponential-family distributions to decouple complexity from sample size.
AI.result: OMT scales only with the number of mixture components, demonstrated effective on synthetic benchmarks, image data, and large-scale single-cell RNA sequencing data.
AI.conclusion: OMT provides a scalable, interpretable, and theoretically stable alternative to classical optimal transport for large-scale applications.

title: LL-Bench: Rethinking Low-Level Vision Evaluation in the Era of Large-Scale Generative Models
summary: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across image generation and editing tasks. However, their performance in low-level vision tasks, which require pixel-wise control, remains insufficiently studied. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{LL-Bench}, a comprehensive \textbf{Benchmark} for evaluating the capabilities of large-scale generative models on \textbf{L}ow-\textbf{L}evel vision tasks. The benchmark comprises 2,469 real-world degraded images covering 16 low-level degradation tasks, and 28,919 restored images produced by 10 state-of-the-art large-scale generative models and 21 conventional restoration models, which are annotated with 152,020 expert-level pairwise human preferences and 28,334 quality scores. Built upon LL-Bench, we present a systematic diagnosis that reveals the performance boundaries and unique failure modes of large-scale generative models across diverse low-level vision tasks, compared with conventional representative restoration approaches. Moreover, we investigate the effectiveness of current quality evaluation metrics on LL-Bench, which exhibit significant discrepancy with human ratings. To better align restored-image quality assessment with human preferences, we further propose \textbf{LL-Score}, an MLLM-based evaluator that captures both restoration quality and hallucination existence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LL-score not only outperforms existing image quality assessment metrics, but also serves as a promising reward model for training generative models on low-level vision tasks.

AI.tldr: LL-Bench is a benchmark with 2,469 degraded images across 16 low-level vision tasks, evaluating 10 generative and 21 conventional models using 152K human preferences and 28K quality scores. It reveals generative models' limitations and proposes LL-Score, an MLLM-based evaluator that aligns better with human preferences than existing metrics.
AI.motivation: Large-scale generative models show strong performance in high-level image tasks, but their capabilities in low-level vision tasks requiring precise pixel-wise control are insufficiently studied. The authors aim to systematically evaluate and diagnose these models.
AI.method: Construct LL-Bench with 2,469 real degraded images across 16 tasks, 10 generative + 21 conventional models, 152K pairwise human preferences, and 28K quality scores. Then propose LL-Score, an MLLM-based evaluator assessing both restoration quality and hallucination presence.
AI.result: Existing quality metrics show significant discrepancy with human ratings on LL-Bench. LL-Score outperforms existing IQA metrics and can serve as a reward model for training generative models on low-level tasks.
AI.conclusion: LL-Bench provides a systematic diagnosis of generative models' boundaries and failure modes in low-level vision, while LL-Score offers better human-aligned evaluation and potential use as a reward model.

title: Drifting Preference Optimization for One-Step Generative Models
summary: One-step text-to-image generators are attractive for deployment because they generate an image with a single forward pass, but preference finetuning them remains difficult: standard alignment methods often rely on policy likelihoods, denoising trajectories, differentiable reward gradients, or test-time optimization. We propose Drifting Preference Optimization (DrPO), an online preference-finetuning method for deterministic one-step generators. For each prompt, DrPO samples candidates from the current generator, ranks them with a target reward, and uses high- and low-scoring samples to synthesize a feature-space update direction. The update is a non-parametric dipole preference field plus a reference drift estimated from the frozen base generator, and is optimized through a detached feature-space regression target. The target reward is used only for ranking, so DrPO can train with large, black-box, or non-differentiable rewards while inference remains a single generator call. We evaluate DrPO on SD-Turbo and SDXL-Turbo with multiple target rewards and benchmarks, including HPSv3 and GenEval. DrPO improves alignment over reward-gradient-free one-step preference baselines and reduces HPSv3 training computation by $3.51\times$ under the matched effective-batch setting by removing reward-model backpropagation. Initial offline experiments suggest that sample-based gradient synthesis can also be used beyond online reward ranking.

AI.tldr: Drifting Preference Optimization (DrPO) is an online preference-finetuning method for deterministic one-step text-to-image generators. It samples candidates, ranks them with a target reward, and synthesizes a feature-space update direction using a non-parametric dipole preference field plus a reference drift. DrPO improves alignment over baselines on SD-Turbo/SDXL-Turbo with multiple reward benchmarks and reduces training computation by 3.51× by removing reward-model backpropagation.
AI.motivation: Standard preference alignment methods (e.g., relying on policy likelihoods, denoising trajectories, differentiable reward gradients, or test-time optimization) are difficult to apply to one-step text-to-image generators. There is a need for an efficient, reward-gradient-free method that works with black-box or non-differentiable rewards.
AI.method: DrPO samples candidates from the current one-step generator, ranks them with a target reward, then synthesizes a feature-space update direction. The update combines a non-parametric dipole preference field (based on high/low-scoring samples) and a reference drift from a frozen base generator. Optimization is done via a detached feature-space regression target.
AI.result: DrPO outperforms reward-gradient-free one-step preference baselines on SD-Turbo and SDXL-Turbo across benchmarks (HPSv3, GenEval). It reduces HPSv3 training computation by 3.51× under matched effective-batch settings by eliminating reward-model backpropagation.
AI.conclusion: DrPO provides an effective and computationally efficient preference finetuning approach for one-step generators, and preliminary offline experiments suggest broader applicability of sample-based gradient synthesis beyond online reward ranking.

title: Modeling Depth Ambiguity: A Mixture-Density Representation for Flying-Point-Free Depth Estimation
summary: Despite advances in depth estimation, flying points remain a persistent failure mode: near object boundaries, depth estimators often predict spurious 3D points in the empty space between foreground and background surfaces. We trace this artifact to a standard modeling choice: assigning each pixel a single depth hypothesis. At boundaries, a pixel can straddle a foreground and a background surface, so its true depth is ambiguous between the two. A model that predicts a single depth cannot keep both possibilities, so training instead pulls the prediction toward an intermediate depth that lies on neither surface. We address this with MDA, a mixture-density representation that lets the model predict multiple depth hypotheses and their associated probabilities for each pixel. Near boundaries, different hypotheses can align with different surfaces, and the decoded depth is selected from one of these hypotheses rather than placed in the empty space between them. Across different backbones, MDA substantially improves boundary reconstruction and largely removes flying-point artifacts even under severe input blur, while adding negligible runtime overhead. The same mixture-density framework naturally extends to transparent objects, where it predicts multiple depth layers at transparent pixels, and to sky regions, where a dedicated component separates the unbounded sky from finite-depth regions, producing flying-point-free skylines. Project Page: https://biansy000.github.io/mda-site/.

AI.tldr: MDA replaces single-depth-per-pixel prediction with a mixture-density representation that allows multiple depth hypotheses per pixel, eliminating "flying points" (spurious depth predictions in empty space) at object boundaries, transparent objects, and sky regions with negligible runtime overhead.
AI.motivation: Flying points (spurious 3D points in empty space between foreground and background) are a persistent artifact in depth estimation, caused by the standard practice of assigning each pixel a single depth hypothesis even when the pixel straddles multiple surfaces at boundaries.
AI.method: MDA (Mixture-Density depth representation) allows each pixel to predict multiple depth hypotheses with associated probabilities. Near boundaries, different hypotheses can align with different surfaces, and the decoded depth is selected from a hypothesis rather than placed in empty space. The framework naturally extends to transparent objects (multiple depth layers) and sky regions (dedicated component for unbounded sky).
AI.result: Across different backbones, MDA substantially improves boundary reconstruction and largely removes flying-point artifacts even under severe input blur, while adding negligible runtime overhead.
AI.conclusion: The mixture-density framework effectively solves flying points at boundaries, transparent objects, and sky regions, offering a simple yet powerful fix to a persistent depth estimation failure mode.

title: IntraShuffler: A Privacy Preserving Framework for Heterogeneous DP Federated Learning
summary:
Heterogeneous Differential Privacy (HDP) in Federated Learning (FL) allows clients to select individual privacy budgets ($\varepsilon_i$) according to institutional policies and data sensitivity. In practice, many HDP-FL systems employ $\varepsilon$-aware server aggregation to improve model utility by re-weighting client updates according to their declared privacy budgets. However, gradient updates in FL retain structural patterns induced by non-independent and identically-distributed (non-IID) data, and these additional signals exposed by $\varepsilon$-aware aggregation create new opportunities for inference by an honest-but-curious server. In this work, we first show that a server equipped with gradient denoising and surrogate modeling can mount a \emph{Privacy Inference Attack} that infers distributional attributes of clients and links updates from the same client across training rounds, measured via surrogate inference accuracy and linkage success, under realistic knowledge constraints.
  The Shuffle-Model has been widely studied as a defense against such inference risks by anonymizing update sources, but it is fundamentally incompatible with HDP-FL $\varepsilon$-aware aggregation. To address this challenge, we propose \textbf{IntraShuffler}, a middleware defense framework designed for HDP-FL systems. IntraShuffler introduces a privacy-aware shuffling mechanism that groups clients into privacy-compatible buckets and performs parameter-level shuffling within each bucket to disrupt persistent gradient structure while preserving $\varepsilon$-aware aggregation. Experiments across four different datasets show that IntraShuffler reduces gradient recoverability by over 60% and decreases surrogate inference accuracy from 0.78 to 0.33 while maintaining comparable model utility across multiple FL aggregation rules.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that ε-aware aggregation in Heterogeneous Differential Privacy FL systems can leak client distributional attributes and linkability, then proposes IntraShuffler—a middleware that groups clients into privacy-compatible buckets with parameter-level shuffling to disrupt persistent gradient structure while preserving ε-aware aggregation—achieving over 60% reduction in gradient recoverability and dropping surrogate inference accuracy from 0.78 to 0.33.
AI.motivation: HDP-FL systems use ε-aware aggregation to improve utility, but gradient updates retain structural patterns from non-IID data that can be exploited by honest-but-curious servers for privacy inference attacks. The Shuffle-Model defense is incompatible with ε-aware aggregation, creating a need for a compatible defense mechanism.
AI.method: The authors introduce IntraShuffler, a middleware defense framework that groups clients into privacy-compatible buckets based on their privacy budgets and performs parameter-level shuffling within each bucket to disrupt persistent gradient structure while maintaining ε-aware aggregation capability.
AI.result: Across four datasets, IntraShuffler reduces gradient recoverability by over 60%, drops surrogate inference accuracy from 0.78 to 0.33, and maintains comparable model utility under multiple FL aggregation rules.
AI.conclusion: IntraShuffler effectively mitigates privacy inference risks in HDP-FL systems while preserving the utility benefits of ε-aware aggregation, offering a practical defense that fills the gap left by the Shuffle-Model's incompatibility.

title: LongLive-RAG: A General Retrieval-Augmented Framework for Long Video Generation
summary: Autoregressive (AR) video diffusion enables variable-length synthesis, but long-horizon generation often suffers from accumulated errors and identity drift. For efficiency, existing methods commonly adopt sliding-window attention during generation. This creates an irreversible generation trajectory: once the active window accumulates appearance errors, subsequent generations can only condition on this degraded trajectory and drift further away. We address this limitation by formulating long video generation as a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) problem. Rather than relying solely on the recent window, we treat previously generated latents as a dynamic, searchable history. We propose LongLive-RAG, a general retrieval framework for AR video generation. At each new block, LongLive-RAG uses a query embedding to retrieve relevant historical latents. This lightweight retrieval step adds only a small overhead relative to generation and lets the generator condition on non-local context instead of only the recent window. To make retrieval more discriminative, we introduce the Window Temporal Delta Loss that suppresses redundant local similarity and encourages embeddings to capture meaningful temporal changes. Together, these components help reduce error accumulation caused by sliding-window attention. Experiments across multiple AR backbones and generation lengths show improved long-video quality and the best average VBench-Long rank. To our knowledge, among open-ended AR long video generation methods, LongLive-RAG is the first to formulate self-generated latent history as content-addressable retrieval memory. Code is available at https://github.com/qixinhu11/LongLive-RAG.

AI.tldr: LongLive-RAG is a retrieval-augmented generation framework for autoregressive video diffusion that treats previously generated latents as a searchable history to reduce error accumulation and identity drift in long video generation, achieving state-of-the-art VBench-Long rank.
AI.motivation: Existing sliding-window attention in AR video diffusion creates irreversible error accumulation and identity drift for long-horizon generation, as the generator can only condition on a degraded recent window.
AI.method: Formulates long video generation as a retrieval-augmented generation problem. Uses query embeddings to retrieve relevant historical latents from a dynamic searchable memory. Introduces Window Temporal Delta Loss to suppress redundant local similarity and enhance discriminative temporal change embeddings.
AI.result: Improves long-video quality across multiple AR backbones and generation lengths; achieves the best average VBench-Long rank; lightweight retrieval adds minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: LongLive-RAG is the first open-ended AR long video generation method to formulate self-generated latent history as content-addressable retrieval memory, effectively mitigating error accumulation from sliding-window attention.

title: VLMs are Good Teachers for Video Reasoning via Adaptive Test-Time Optimization
summary: The recent "Reasoning with Video" paradigm utilizes Video Generation Models (VGMs) to generate temporally coherent visual trajectories to complete reasoning tasks. Although state-of-the-art VGMs excel at visual quality, they often struggle to understand and follow task-specific rules, leading to logical failures across diverse reasoning scenarios. Existing efforts try to utilize Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as problem pre-solvers to produce or refine textual guidance for the VGM. However, textual descriptions fail to capture intricate spatiotemporal details, and VGMs often struggle to faithfully execute fine-grained or long-tail instructions even with a valid plan. While VLMs struggle as solvers, they possess strong perception capabilities to evaluate process-constraint satisfaction and final-goal achievement. Leveraging this strength, we introduce a paradigm shift that transitions the role of VLMs to "teachers". Specifically, a VLM teacher extracts task-specific rules to formulate differentiable rewards, guiding a VGM Reasoner via test-time online optimization of a lightweight LoRA module. This strategy enables adaptive test-time optimization and extends the reasoning capabilities beyond the VGM's intrinsic boundaries. Evaluations on symbolic (VBVR-Bench) and general-purpose (RULER-Bench) video reasoning benchmarks show that the proposed method yields a 16.7-point average performance gain, outperforming the VLM-as-Solver paradigm (+0.4 points) and Best-of-N scaling (+2.2 points) by a large margin at comparable test-time cost. These findings reveal that integrating VLMs as test-time teachers offers a promising paradigm for achieving generalizable video reasoning. Project Page: https://VLM-as-Teacher.github.io/

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a paradigm shift where VLMs act as "teachers" (not solvers) to guide VGMs via test-time optimization of a LoRA module, achieving 16.7-point average gains on video reasoning benchmarks over existing paradigms.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art VGMs excel at visual quality but fail to follow task-specific rules, leading to logical failures. Existing VLM-as-Solver approaches use textual descriptions that miss spatiotemporal details, and VGMs struggle with fine-grained instructions.
AI.method: A VLM teacher extracts task-specific rules to formulate differentiable rewards, guiding a VGM Reasoner via test-time online optimization of a lightweight LoRA module, enabling adaptive optimization beyond VGM's intrinsic boundaries.
AI.result: 16.7-point average performance gain on VBVR-Bench and RULER-Bench, significantly outperforming VLM-as-Solver (+0.4) and Best-of-N scaling (+2.2) at comparable test-time cost.
AI.conclusion: Integrating VLMs as test-time teachers offers a promising paradigm for achieving generalizable video reasoning, transitioning VLMs from solvers to evaluators/teachers.

title: Policy-based Foveated Imaging and Perception
summary: Ultra-high-resolution image sensors offer the potential to capture fine spatial details critical for many visual perception tasks, but acquiring and processing all pixels at full resolution is often infeasible under realistic bandwidth, latency, and power constraints. Existing approaches address this challenge through acquisition strategies such as spatial or temporal downsampling, which irrevocably discard information before task relevance can be assessed. In this work, we introduce a real-time, predictive, and task-aware foveated imaging system that operates directly at image acquisition time. Leveraging emerging dual-stream sensor architectures, our method dynamically allocates limited pixel bandwidth to task-relevant regions of interest while maintaining a low-resolution global context. We formulate foveated acquisition as a sensor attention policy-learning problem, in which past observations guide actions that determine future measurements, closing the perception-acquisition loop. Through extensive simulation across multiple perception tasks, we demonstrate that our approach achieves high task performance under strict pixel budgets and significantly outperforms relevant baselines operating at the same bandwidth. We further validate our system on a 200-megapixel dual-stream sensor, capturing real-world videos under realistic bandwidth and latency constraints, demonstrating the practical feasibility of task-driven, acquisition-time foveated imaging.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a real-time, task-aware foveated imaging system that uses dual-stream sensor architectures to dynamically allocate limited pixel bandwidth to task-relevant regions while maintaining low-resolution global context, achieving high performance under strict pixel budgets.
AI.motivation: Ultra-high-resolution sensors can capture fine details, but acquiring/processing all pixels at full resolution is infeasible under realistic bandwidth, latency, and power constraints. Existing downsampling approaches discard information before task relevance can be assessed.
AI.method: Formulates foveated acquisition as a sensor attention policy-learning problem where past observations guide actions determining future measurements, closing the perception-acquisition loop. Uses emerging dual-stream sensor architectures.
AI.result: Achieves high task performance under strict pixel budgets, significantly outperforms baselines at same bandwidth, and validates on a real 200-megapixel dual-stream sensor under realistic constraints.
AI.conclusion: Task-driven, acquisition-time foveated imaging is practically feasible and effective for real-world deployment under bandwidth/latency constraints.

title: AdaCodec: A Predictive Visual Code for Video MLLMs
summary: Video is temporally redundant: adjacent frames usually share most objects, background, and layout. Yet existing video multimodal large language models (video MLLMs) usually encode each sampled frame as an independent RGB image, causing visual tokens to repeat content already present in earlier frames. This suggests a more direct video interface: send a full reference frame only when the scene cannot be predicted well from prior context, and otherwise transmit a compact description of inter-frame changes. We call this interface a \emph{predictive visual code}, and instantiate it for video MLLMs as \textbf{AdaCodec}. AdaCodec spends full visual tokens on a reference frame only when its conditional predictive cost is high; otherwise, it encodes inter-frame changes, including motion and prediction residuals, as compact P-tokens. Across all eleven benchmarks, AdaCodec improves over the Qwen3-VL-8B per-frame RGB baseline at a matched visual-token budget. Even at $1/7$ the budget, AdaCodec with 32k tokens surpasses the 224k baseline on all long-video benchmarks; on five general-video benchmarks, it raises the average score while substantially cutting time-to-first-token from 9.26s to 1.62s.

AI.tldr: AdaCodec reduces video redundancy for MLLMs by using a predictive visual code: full reference frames only when predictive cost is high, otherwise compact P-tokens for inter-frame changes. Achieves better scores with 1/7 token budget, cutting latency from 9.26s to 1.62s.
AI.motivation: Video frames are temporally redundant; existing video MLLMs waste tokens encoding repeated content across adjacent frames, leading to inefficiency.
AI.method: AdaCodec uses a predictive visual code interface: transmits full reference frames only when conditional predictive cost is high; otherwise encodes inter-frame changes (motion + prediction residuals) as compact P-tokens.
AI.result: Outperforms Qwen3-VL-8B RGB baseline across 11 benchmarks at matched token budget. At 1/7 budget (32k tokens), surpasses 224k baseline on long-video benchmarks; improves average score on general-video benchmarks while reducing time-to-first-token from 9.26s to 1.62s.
AI.conclusion: Predictive visual coding is an effective, efficient interface for video MLLMs, drastically reducing token redundancy and latency without sacrificing performance.

title: VISReg: Variance-Invariance-Sketching Regularization for JEPA training
summary: Self-supervised learning methods prevent embedding collapse via modeling heuristics or explicit regularization of the embedding space. Among the latter, VICReg decomposes regularization into variance and covariance objectives, offering flexibility and interpretability. However, covariance captures only second-order statistics -- encouraging decorrelation but failing to enforce the full distributional shape needed for stable training. Sketching-based methods such as SIGReg address this by aligning embeddings to an isotropic Gaussian, but lack flexibility and suffer from vanishing gradients under collapse. We propose Variance-Invariance-Sketching Regularization (VISReg), which replaces covariance with a Sliced-Wasserstein-based sketching objective that enforces full distributional shape, while retaining a variance term for scale control. By decoupling scale and shape, VISReg combines VICReg's flexibility with the distributional rigor of sketching methods, providing robust gradients even under collapse. We show that VISReg scales linearly, outperforms existing regularization on low-quality datasets, and is resilient to long-tailed and low-rank regimes. Pre-trained on ImageNet-1K, VISReg achieves state-of-the-art performance on out-of-distribution datasets. Pre-trained on ImageNet-22K, it matches DINOv2's OOD performance despite the latter using 10x more data (LVD-142M). Project and code: https://haiyuwu.github.io/visreg.

AI.tldr: VISReg replaces covariance regularization in VICReg with a Sliced-Wasserstein-based sketching objective to enforce full distributional shape, combining flexibility with distributional rigor, achieving SOTA OOD performance and matching DINOv2 with 10x less data.
AI.motivation: VICReg's covariance regularization only captures second-order statistics, failing to enforce full distributional shape for stable training. SIGReg aligns embeddings to isotropic Gaussian but lacks flexibility and suffers from vanishing gradients under collapse.
AI.method: VISReg replaces covariance with a Sliced-Wasserstein-based sketching objective for full distributional shape enforcement, retains variance term for scale control, and decouples scale and shape regularization.
AI.result: Scales linearly, outperforms existing regularization on low-quality datasets, resilient to long-tailed and low-rank regimes. SOTA OOD on ImageNet-1K pretraining. Matches DINOv2's OOD performance on ImageNet-22K despite DINOv2 using 10x more data (LVD-142M).
AI.conclusion: VISReg effectively combines VICReg's flexibility with sketching methods' distributional rigor, offering robust gradients even under collapse and achieving strong performance with less data.

title: HumanNOVA: Photorealistic, Universal and Rapid 3D Human Avatar Modeling from a Single Image
summary: In this paper, we present HumanNOVA, a photorealistic, universal, and rapid model for generating 3D human avatars from a single RGB image. Achieving both photorealism and generalization is challenging due to the scarcity of diverse, high-quality 3D human data. To address this, we build a scalable data generation pipeline that follows two strategies. The first one is to leverage existing rigged assets and animate them with extensive poses from daily life. The second strategy is to utilize existing multi-camera captures of humans and employ fitting to generate more diverse views for training. These two strategies enable us to scale up to 100k assets, significantly enhancing both the quantity and the diversity of data for robust model training. In terms of the architecture, HumanNOVA adopts a feed-forward, token-conditioned avatar modeling framework that allows fast inference in less than one second and requires no test-time optimization. Given an input image and an estimated simplified human mesh (SMPL) without detailed geometry or appearance, the model first encodes both inputs into compact token representations. These tokens then act as conditioning signals and are fused through cross-attention to construct a triplane-based 3D avatar representation. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of our approach, both quantitatively and qualitatively, as well as its robustness under diverse input image conditions. Project page at https://HumanNOVA.github.io .

AI.tldr: HumanNOVA generates photorealistic 3D human avatars from a single RGB image in under one second using a feed-forward, token-conditioned framework trained on 100k assets with diverse poses and views.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of diverse, high-quality 3D human data makes it challenging to achieve both photorealism and generalization in single-image 3D avatar generation.
AI.method: Builds a scalable data pipeline (rigged assets animated with daily poses + multi-camera captures with fitting) to scale to 100k assets; uses a feed-forward, token-conditioned avatar modeling framework with cross-attention to fuse input image and SMPL tokens into triplane-based 3D representations.
AI.result: Superior quantitative and qualitative performance over baselines on multiple benchmarks, with robust results across diverse input conditions and inference under one second without test-time optimization.
AI.conclusion: HumanNOVA effectively addresses data scarcity and achieves fast, photorealistic, and generalizable 3D human avatar generation from a single image.

title: From Zero to Hero: Training-Free Custom Concept Spawning in World Models
summary: Autoregressive world models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for interactive video generation, allowing users to navigate dynamically generated environments through actions. These models are typically conditioned on a text prompt and/or a single reference frame, from which the entire world is generated. Yet the moment the user navigates beyond what is visible in that frame, the unseen regions are populated by the base model's priors, with no mechanism for the user to specify what should appear and where. This is a fundamental limitation for applications such as gaming, interactive storytelling, and simulation, where controllable scene composition is essential. We refer to this missing capability as concept spawning; introducing a user-specified visual concept into a world model, analogous to spawning in a game engine. We introduce SPAWN (Swapping Pinned Anchor with Windowed iNjection), a training-free method for concept spawning. SPAWN exploits a structural property of image-to-video backbones: the first slot of the context memory is pinned to the reference frame and acts as a foundational anchor for every generated chunk.
